Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 13 SOP 1.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 1.2 UNIFORMS AND PERSONA L HYGIENE Implementation Date 16 February 2021 Purpose To provide guidance on HMCAS uniform requirements and presenting an easily identifiable and professional appearance. Responsibility ALL HMCAS GROUP STAF F 1. Purpose: 1.1 To provide guidance on HMCAS uniform requirements and presenting an easily identifiable and professional appearance . 2. Scope: 2.1 This SOP applies to all HMCAS Group Staff. 3. Definitions: 3.1 None 4. Procedure: 4.1 The HMCAS Executive Director or designee may modify various provisions of the dress code for special functions or specific work assignments . 4.2 A high standard of personal appearance and hygiene is imperative to the maintenance of a positive personal, professional, and public image and ensuring appropriate safety standards in the work environment . 4.3 Based on t he relevant policy, s upervisors have the right and duty to judge the appropriateness of the dress and personal hygiene of an employee . 4.4 This SOP does not alter the re

quirement for staff to wear appropriate PPE in hazardous environments as stated in HMCAS SOP 2.1 . 4.5 All HMCAS staff entitled for uniform shall be issued with the HMCAS approved uniform item s according to their duty requirements . Uniform entitlement is non - accumulative and is given per eligibility period or when required (Refer to Appendix 1.2 - 1: Uniform Entitlement ) . 4.6 Uniform Dress Code : 4.6.1 Always w ear corporate identification name tag and DHP identification. 4.6.2 Wear only the designated colour shirt with shoulder badges and rank epaulettes ( Appendix 1.2 - 2: Approved Rank Epaulettes ). 4.6.3 Press uniforms to deliver a neat appearance. 4.6.4 Polish and keep boots in a good state of repair. 4.6.5 Staff shall remain responsible to care for their uniform and personal issued equipment. 4.6.6 Staff should take care of their uniform as guided by the basic uniform care and washing rule s ( Appendix 1.2 - 3: Washing Instructions ) . 4.6.7 Undergarments are provided by the staff and should not be transparent through shirts or trouser. Staff should be guided by HMC HR Policy 3008 (3.7). 4.6.8 Socks (black) and swe aters (black or navy blue under sky blue shirt, white u
nder white shirts) is supplied by staff and should be worn according to HMCAS Policy AS 9035 (3.11.8 & 3.11.9).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 14 4.6.9 Staff wanting to cover their arms may use navy blue long sleeve T - Shirts under their sky blue HMCAS shirts or a white long sleeve T - Shirt under a white HMCAS shirt purchased at their own cost. 4.6.10 Wear uniforms ONLY to and from duty, or whilst operating in an official capacity. 4.6.11 Do not wear non - HMCAS approved pins, insignia, or hats. 4.6.12 Female staff may wear a dark blue head scarf, neatly tucked in to avoid interference with work performance. 4.6.13 Non - clinical staff and senior personnel may wear clothing that is appropriate to the nature of their work. In HMCAS offices, this will usually be business attire (suit, shirt and tie, Qatari national dress, or female equivalent). 4.6.14 Administration and Communicati on staff that perform clinical duties for licensure purposes will be issued two sets of uniform according to their designated duties. 4.6.15 Retrieval Service personnel shall wear the following orders of dress: 4.6.15.1 Ground Based Retrieval: AS Uniform . 4.6.15.2 Aero SHAARP: Fli ght suit with Retrieval Service badge . 4.6.15.3 Commercial on call/Clinical Coordinator: AS Uniform . 4

.6.15.4 Commercial Flights: Khaki pants and navy - blue polo shirt without badges or logos. 4.6.16 Staff who will cover events should wear their standard/primary uniform. 4.7 Personal Appearance and Hygiene : 4.7.1 Neatly groom and maintain a conservative hair appearance. 4.7.2 Facial hair must not interfere with the appropriate fit of a N95 mask. 4.7.3 Draw back hair that is shoulder length or longer. 4.7.4 Wash hands with soap and water or use han d gel between patient contacts, upon returning to the work area from the restroom, and after eating. 4.7.5 Keep fingernails clean, neatly trimmed, and must always be kept short to prevent injury to clients . 4.7.6 Conservative use of cosmetics, fragrances or makeup i s permitted. 4.7.7 One plain wedding ring is allowed, however, rings with stones, or are not smooth, can compromise the integrity of gloves and should not be worn 4.7.8 A wrist watches with a second hand is required . 4.7.9 Wearing of loose or dangling jewelry is not perm itted. 4.7.10 Deodorant or antiperspirant should be used following a daily bath or shower to avoid body odor . 4.7.11 Daily oral hygiene (brushing of teeth) is required . 4.8 Uniform Replacement : 4.8.1 Un

iform is supplied to the Staff as listed on Appendix 1.2 - 1 , however full responsibility for maintenance and cleanliness will remain with the wearer, including replacement of damaged/lost uniform. 4.8.2 Damaged uniform while performing work duties:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 15 4.8.2.1 When uniform needs replacement within the entitlement period; Staff reports the incident to his Immediate Supervisor, submits OVA, waits feedback and approval from the Committee through the Ambulance Service Quality Section to process replacement. 4.8.3 Lost, Faded, Ripped or Torn, Physical changes resu lting from gaining or losing of weight or Pregnancy: 4.8.3.1 When uniform needs replacement within the entitlement period, Staff submits OVA, waits feedback and approval from the Committee through the Ambulance Service Quality Section to process replacement. 4.8.3.2 This extra uniform can be acquired from the vendor at employee’s expense with written authorization from the Management. 4.8.4 Lost ID tags/badges must be reported to HMC Security without delay and shall be replaced according to HMC procedure (HR Policy 3010). 4.9 Unifo rm Requirements for External Services/Institutions : 4.9.1 All external services/institutions will be accountable to supply uniform to their staff/students scheduled to perform observatory duties in HMCAS that is inline and conforms to the HMCAS operational and s afety requirements. 4

.9.2 All external services/institutions are liable to supply uniform to their staff/students scheduled to perform clinical observatory duties in the HMCAS vehicles at their own costs which must be conform to safety standards uniformity as re quired by the HMCAS. 4.9.3 Observers will not be allowed to work on HMCAS vehicles if safety standards are not met and provided to the individual. 4.10 Uniform Request : 4.10.1 All HMCAS staff shall request uniforms by emailing a Uniform Request Form to the AS Uniform Secti on at ss - uniform@hamad.qa . 4.10.2 Staff should know their correct uniform sizes. Ensure size is correct before completing the Uniform Request Form . If unsure of size, Staff should try on a sample uniform before submitting a uniform request . 4.10.3 Approval of uniform request is based on eligibility and stock availability. 4.11 End of Service/Transfer to other HMC Department 4.11.1 Staff who resigns or transfers to other department within HMC should process End of Service clearance. 4.11.2 Uniform Section issues and verifies the End of Service Uniform Checklist. Staff should return Personal Issued Equipment, Reflective Vest, Life Patrol Hel met, and other items issued wi

thin 6 months prior to the date of resignation or transfer. See Appendix 1.2 - 5: End of Service Uniform Checklist . 5. Attachments: 5.1 Appendix 1.2 - 1: Uniform Entitlement 5.2 Appendix 1.2 - 2: HMCAS Rank Insignia 5.3 Appendix 1.2 - 3: Washing Instructions 5.4 Appendix 1.2 - 4: Uniform Request Form 5.5 Appendix 1.2 - 5: End of Service Uniform Checklist
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 16 6. References: 6.1 None

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 17 SOP 1.3 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Production Procedure 1.3 LEAVE AND SHIFT SWOP S Implementation Date 1 April 2017 Purpose To outline staff procedures for leave and shift swop requests Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Leave request Procedure: 1.1 Requests for Casual Leave: 1.1.1 Casual leave may be requested after a probation period of 3 months following initial employment date. 1.1.2 A maximum of 2 consecutive days leave (3 for Qatari staff) may be requested per leave application. 1.1.3 Complete and sign a leave application form ( see attachment ). 1.1.4 Request a signature and approval from immediate supervisor. 1.1.5 Hand the application form into the scheduling section for final approval. 1.1.6 Upon approval, hand in the original document to the administration section. 1.1.7 Hand in a copy of the original document to the scheduling section. 1.2 Requests for Annual Leave: 1.2.1 Staff are eligible for annual leave after at least 10 months of service. 1.2.2 Requests for leave prior to the 10 - month period may be grante d as unpaid

leave at the discretion of the immediate supervisor. 1.2.3 Complete online Oracle leave application form. 1.2.4 Await automated Oracle approval notification from immediate supervisor. 2. Return from leave: 2.1 Complete online Oracle “Return from Leave” application as soon as possible, not exceeding 11 days after returning to duty. 2.2 Await automated Oracle approval from immediate supervisor within 3 days. 3. Emergency leave within HMCAS: 3.1 In the case of a family or other unforeseen emergency, a request for emer gency leave may be made and shall be approved at the discretion of the Senior Operations Manager. 4. Sick Leave: 4.1 Sick whilst on duty: 4.1.1 Inform PTL and immediate supervisor. 4.1.2 The PTL shall book the affected unit out of service. 4.1.3 Proceed to the staff clinic during office hours or to HGH Accident & Emergency unit outside office hours. 4.1.4 Obtain a sick leave form from Romeo or immediate supervisor in order to present to examining doctor.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 18 4.1.5 Ensure a completed a sick leave form is returned from the examining doctor at the st aff clinic or hospital. 4.1.6 At earliest opportunity, present the completed sick leave form to the Staff Clinic for verification stamp. 4.1.7 Inform the PTL and the immediate supervisor if sick leave has been granted by the doctor. 4.1.8 Upload a copy of the completed sick leave form including Staff Clinic stamp, with the application for sick leave request on the Oracle online system for Immediate Supervisor approval. 4.1.9 PTL shall inform Scheduling to get a replacement in place of the affected staff member. 4.2 Sick leave before the start of shift: (Minutes before start of shift) 4.2.1 Contact immediate supervisor and inform accordingly. 4.2.2 The immediate supervisor shall inform the scheduling section. 4.2.3 Scheduling shall contact staff for overtime to replace the affected staff member. 4.2.4 Report to the designated hub in uniform. 4.2.5 Follow steps 4.1.3. to 4.1.7 4.2.6 Return home 4.3 Sick leave a day before start of shift: 4.3.1 Inform the scheduling section and immediate supervisor. 4.3.2 Proceed to staff clinic and

obtain a completed sick leave form signed by the examining doctor. 4.3.3 Upload a copy of the completed sick leave form including Staff Clinic stamp, with the application for sick leave request on the Oracle online system for Immediate Supervisor approval. 4.3.4 Scheduling shall contact staff for overtime to re place the affected staff member. 5. Shift swop procedure: 5.1 Inform immediate supervisor on duty of intention to swop a shift. 5.2 All requests will be completed electronically using the staff HMCAS Corporate email address. 5.3 The form below (Figure 1) is to be comple ted by both parties prior to the proposed date of the shift swop. 5.4 The First Party [Requesting Staff] is to fill in the electronic form with his/her details and rostered shift in the “From” block. 5.5 The First Party the emails the electronic form to the Second Party [Accepting Staff] for completion . 5.6 The Second Party is to fill in the electronic form with his/her details and rostered shift in the “From” block. 5.7 The Second Party is to fill in the electronic form with his/her accepted shift swop in the “To” block. 5.8 The completed electronic form is then emailed by the Second Party to the Line Mana
ger for approval and electronic signature.
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 19 5.9 Only 3 shift swop requests are permitted per month.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 20 SOP 1.5 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 1.5 RED RULES IN AMBULAN CE SERVICE Implementation Date 28 March 2021 Purpose To identify Red Rules in HMC Ambulance Service which must be followed at all times, without exception. Responsibility ALL HMC AMBULANCE SERVICE STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 To identify Red Rules in HMC Ambulance Service which must be followed at all times, without exception. 2. Scope: 2.1 This SOP applies to all HMCAS Group staff who operate a vehicle within the HMCAS fleet. 3. Definitions: 3.1 Red Rules are used to reduce the probability of harm to patients and employees. 3.2 Red Rules in Ambulance Service are associated with acts that have the highest level of risk to patient or employee safety . 4. Procedure: 4.1 As a result of learnings drawn from the ongoing review of incidents and associated risks related to the work of HMCAS, the following set of explicit, no exception, safety ‘Red Rules’ have been implemented. 4.2 Due to the significant safety implications behind these rules, any contraventions will result in a mini

mum of three days’ pay being withheld, in addition to any related disciplinary action . 4.3 Red Rules : 4.3.1 Never speed above 140 km/h in any location in any HMCAS vehicle. Priority 1 driving excluded. 4.3.2 Never drive without a seatbelt. 4.3.3 Never talk on, or hold a mobile phone, while driving in any 2 - manned unit. 4.3.4 Never cross a red light without being on a Priority 1 call. 4.3.5 Never work on a public road without wearing a reflective safety jacket/vest. 4.4 Red Rule Violation Manag ement Process : 4.4.1 Support Service Control Center (SSCC) will send an email with violations recorded on the ACETECH vehicle system to Senior Operations Managers (SOM) each Thursday by 1200 hours. 4.4.2 SOMs will review the list to ensure that the concerned vehicle a nd registered staff was operational when the violation occurred. SOMs must return their findings to HMCAS Quality Department no later than the following Thursday by 1200 hours. If a reply is not received within the timeframe it will be considered that SOMs concur with the recorded violation. 4.4.3 Violations confirmed by SOMs, as per para 2, will be sent via the HMCAS Quality Department to the Office of the Executive Direct
or (ED) HMCAS. The list must be sent to the designated person by 1400 hours on Thursday

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 21 4.4.4 Vio lations that are contested by SOMs will be sent back to the SSCC for a review of technology or to check if there has been a mistake in the allocation of an individual to a vehicle. Contested violations will be received by SSCC before 1400 hours each Thursd ay and the SSCC will have 7 calendar days to investigate the query. 4.4.5 By Tuesday the following week the office of the Executive Director HMCAS will: 4.4.5.1 Prepare a letter, to be signed by the ED HMCAS, issuing a written warning and notification of 3 days salary d eduction in accordance with the Red Rules . 4.4.5.2 Send the warning letter to the relevant AED of the service where the person works. The AED is responsible for speaking to the staff member and issuing the warning letter. 4.4.6 For multiple offences the staff member wil l meet with the Executive Director HMCAS and respective AED. This will be conducted in a timely fashion and a Performance Improvement Plan will be initiated accordingly . 4.4.7 In all instances, copies of the following documents will be sent to Corporate HR for inclusion on the staff member’s file: 4.4.7.1 Warning Letter 4.4

.7.2 Record of meeting between staff member and the AED or AED and ED. 4.4.7.3 Performance Improvement Plan with dates for review . 4.4.8 At the end of each calendar month a list of all staff to receive salary deductions is to be prepared, together with a memo from the Ambulance Service Group Chief Executive and sent to the Chief of Human Resources. A copy of this memo is sent to the HR Manager for inclusion on staff files. 5. Attachments: 5.1 Figure 1 : HMCAS Red Rule Violation Management Process 6. References: 6.1 None

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 22 Figure 1 : HMCAS Red Rule Violation Management Process Each Thursday before 12:00 Support Control Center will send a list of violations, which occurred that week, to Senior Operations Managers SOM will review the list of violations and send email confirmation to HMCAS Quality Department before Thursday 12:00. If no reply is received from the SOM, the list of violations is considered validated by the SOM. Are any violations Contested? Yes Any violations that are contested will be returned to the Support Control Center for additional analysis (7 - day turnaround) No By Thursday, no later than 14:00, confirmed violations are sent to Office of Executive Director HMCAS for processing. By Tuesday of the following week, the Office of Executive Director HMCAS will prepare all necessary documents, which will be issued to the staff member, then sent to Corporate HR Beginning of process Day 7 of Process Day 14 of Process Day 19 of Process

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 23 SOP 1.6 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Production Procedure 1.6 SANCTIONS FOR VI OLATING RULES AND RE GULATIONS AND ACTS O F NEGLIGENT BEHAVIOR Implementation Date 1 October 2016 Purpose To ensure that all operational personnel managed by HMCAS will be sanctioned fairly, appropriately, and equally, when violating regulations or behaving negligently. Responsibility ALL HMCAS AMBULANCE SERVICE STAFF 1. General Processes pertaining to Sanctions: 1.1 The HMCAS Quality Department, with full support from the relevant operational departments, shall investigate all violations of rules or regulations and acts of negligent behaviour by HMCAS staff. 1.2 All investigations shall be initiated by submitting an OVA report as per SOP 4.9 . B . After an initial investigation, a more detailed investigation may be recommended. 1.3 Staff members or managers directly involved with the incident shall not participate in the investigation. 1.4 Upon completio n, the investigating team shall present a report with recommendations of sanctions to be imposed. 1.5 All reports and r

ecommendations for sanctions shall be reviewed by the HMCAS Executive Team who shall decide the outcome and enforce and implement sanctions a s necessary. 1.6 Depending on the level of sanction, the Executive Team may elect to engage HMC Corporate HR to impose the requested staff sanction. 2. Levels of sanctions for HMCAS staff: 2.1 Additional mandatory training 2.2 Verbal warning 2.3 Written warning 2.4 Written warni ng combined with suspension from function, role, or duty (including restriction of paid overtime and possible promotion) . 2.5 Deduction of salary 2.6 Suspension from duty 2.7 Recommendation of dismissal

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 24 SOP 1.7 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Support Services Procedure 1.7 AMBULANCES DRIVE N BY NON - MEDICAL PER SONNEL Implementation Date 01 February 2017 Purpose To ensure that non - medical personnel is properly guided when driving ambulances to any location across Qatar. Responsibility ALL NON - MEDICAL STAF F DRIVING ANY TYPE O F EMERGENCY VEHICLE 1. General Rules: 1.1 Vehicle can only be driven by staff holding a Qatari Driving License. 1.2 An Ambulance is driven by Non - Medical Staff e.g. Support Services Staff when transporting ambulance to any workshop location across Qatar. 1.3 A sign “NOT IN SERVICE” must be placed in front and rear side of ambulance and must be visible to the public. 2. Approaching Road Traffic Accident (RTA): 2.1 In the eve nt of any Ambulance or Support Vehicle approaching or witnessing incident such as RTA, the Support Services Staff should stop and park the vehicle in a safe location. 2.2 The Support Services Staff must quickly assess the scene. 2.3 After assessing the scene, imme diately call 999. 2.4 The Support Servic
es Staff must be familiar with “Know the Five to Save a Life” . 2.5 The vehicle must remain on scene until the responding Emergency Ambulance arrives.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 25 SOP 1.8 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Support Services Procedure 1.8 VEHICLES RUNNING DURING SUMMER SEASO N Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure vehicle safety maintenance are considered when temperatures begin to rise in Qatar. Responsibility ALL HMC AMBULANCE SE RVICE STAFF 1. Basic Safety Precautions: 1.1 Daily vehicle check using Vehicle Safety Checklist . 1.1.1 Ensure fluids, emergency system, lights, mirrors, air conditioning, tires, brake system and all safety items are visually checked before driving the vehicle. 1.1.2 Staff must report to Production Supervisor or Fleet Section, if any issues are found. 1.2 Tire Check: 1.2.1 Regularly check tires and ensure proper tire pressure. 1.2.2 As temperatures rise, under or over inflated tires may put you at risk of tire bursts and blowouts. 1.2.3 Staff must report to Production Supervisor or Fleet Section, if any damage or excessive wear to tires is noticed. 1.2.4 All tires must be at the same brand on each axle assembly. 1.3 Air Conditioning (AC) System: 1.3.1 Check AC system to ensu

re that it is giving correct amount of air flow. 1.3.2 When switching AC on; give ample time for AC to cool down. 1.3.3 Pay attention to any strange noises or odours. 1.3.4 Staff must report to Production Supervisor or Fleet Section if AC is not working correc tly. 2. Shoreline Facility: 2.1 Emergency and Non - Emergency Ambulances: 2.1.1 All ambulances must not be left running at any location unless no shoreline available, the driver must position the gear in the Park position and engage the hand break to activate the Eco Idle mode . 2.1.2 All ambulances must be plugged into the shoreline when on standby at a location when shoreline is available . Vehicle engine should be switched off. 2.1.3 Engine must remained off when connected to the shoreline so the safety ejection system can be act ivated when switching on the engine . 2.1.4 Staff must report immediately to Production Supervisor if shoreline is defective, damaged or any related issue. 2.1.5 All units ending their shift at AS parking areas where shoreline cables are available must ensure to connec t their vehicles for charging . 2.1.6 Before moving make sure the shoreline is secured in place to avoid driving over it .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 26 2.2 Marked Prado : 2.2.1 All marked Prado carrying medical equipment can be kept with the engine running at the standby location. 2.2.2 All marked Prado must allow ambulances to have access to the shoreline when standby at any location where shoreline is available.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 27 SOP 1.9 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Support Services Procedure 1.9 UNMARKED RAPID RESPO NSE VEHICLE (RRV) AL LOCATION Implementation Date 01 November 2016 Purpose To ensure that Rapid Response Vehicle is allocated as required Responsibility ALL STAFF UTILIZING UNMARKED RRV 1. General Rules: 1.1 Vehicle can only be driven by staff holding a Qatari Driving License or other nationality license approved by the Traffic Department. 1.2 A vehicle is allocated to a staff member as part of an emergency response on - call rota. 1.3 No vehicle will be permanently allocated to any member of staff without the written approval of the Group CEO. 1.4 In the event of other operational requirements, vehicle can be taken from staff. 1.5 If Staff goes on leave for five consecutive days and above, it is his/her responsibility to return the vehicle to the Fleet Section. 1.6 Fleet Section reallocates vehicle as required. 2. On - Call Vehicle Allocation: 2.1 On - Call Staff is allocated with an appropriate vehicle for his/her duty. 2.2 Depending upon the on - call profile,

vehicle may be shared between staff. 2.3 If Staff changes his/her on - call status, it is his/her responsibility to inform the Production Team Leader and FMS Coordinator. 2.4 On - Call rota is updated and circulated by Sunday 10:00 of every week. 2.5 FMS updated with vehicle and driver details by 11:00 each Sunday. 2.6 FMS Coor dinator informs PTL by email on updated on - call vehicle list by 11:15 each Sunday. 3. Documentation Responsibilities: 3.1 All vehicle check sheets to be completed as required. 3.2 Staff is responsible for ensuring that refueling takes place at an approved petrol station. 3.3 Petty cash claims for fuel will not be accepted without written approval of the Executive Director. 3.4 For accident reporting, it is the Staff responsibility to report to Fleet Section as soon as practical after the accident takes place. 4. Reference: 4.1 Vehicle Usage Policy

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 28 SOP 1.1 1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 1.1 1 PHOTO/VIDEO RECORDI NG Implementation Date 11 September 2018 Purpose Actions to be taken by HMCAS should a member of the public, patient , or a patient’s family member video /photograph and/or record voice of the staff without their permission Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Introduction : 1.1 Social media is an established and accepted part of modern society. However, it can be intrusive to patient care, privacy, and dignity. HMCAS have a duty to protect patient ’ s dignity at all costs. However, staff are reminded to always act in a professional and courteous manner when challenging members of the public and/or family who are filming patient care as those who see the subsequently shared footage will lack the situational awareness of the events and will only observe the behaviour of HMCAS staff. 2. Recording of a patient with their consent : 2.1 Where patients are happy to be record ed then this is acceptable behavior. 2.1.1 This should be documented on your ePCR . 2.2

Sta ff members have the right to refuse being filmed . S ee point 4 . 3. Recording of a pa tient without the patient permission : 3.1 Respectfully request those recording clinical care to stop . 3.2 Should they refuse: 3.2.1 Shield patient from being recorded . 3.2.2 Up date National Command Centre . 3.2.2.1 Ask the patient if they wish to have police requested . 3.2.3 Document incident on the ePCR . 3.2.4 Avoid conflict as this will escalate events . 3.2.5 Remain professional, polite, and non - confrontational . 4. Staff not wishing to be recorded : 4.1 Should staff note that they are being recorded and they wish it to stop , but it involves the patient filming their own care then: 4.1.1 Politely and professionally request that recording be stopped . 4.1.2 If able, without affecting patient care, move away from the immediate environment e.g. sit out of shot if the patient is taking self - portraits . 4.1.3 If the pati ent and/or the person recording the incident refuse to stop filming : 4.1.3.1 Remain professional at all times . 4.1.3.2 Report incident to National Command Centre and direct supervisor . 4.1.3.3 Continue managing the patient in an appropriate manner . 4.1.3.4 Avoid
escalating the incident.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 29 SOP 1.12 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Group: Support Services Procedure 1.12 W OQOD E - FUELING SYSTEM Implementation Date 19 November 2019 Purpose To ensure that all staff driving any type of vehicle is properly guided on the WOQOD e F ueling system. Responsibility ALL HMCAS STAFF 1. General Information: 1.1 The WOQOD RFID chip is installed and activated in all Ambulance Service vehicles. 1.2 The reader automatically recognizes the required type and quantity of the fuel. 1.3 Staff are responsible for ensuring that refueling takes place only at WOQOD station. 2. Use of Fuel Voucher: 2.1 The fuel voucher is no longer accepted in any WOQOD station except on the following circumstances: 2.1.1 Unregistered vehicle used as replacement vehicle. 2.1.2 WOQOD e - System failure; and 2.1.3 Refueling of Command and Control Vehicle generators. 2.2 The fuel voucher that can be used in those circumstances should ONLY be from the book voucher issued to every Department. 2.3 The voucher number that is being issued must be logged and submitted to Support Services Pro

curement Sect ion through ASContracts@hamad.qa on monthly basis. 3. WOQOD e - System Failure: 3.1 If the WOQOD chip is damaged and not working, staff must immediately inform his immediate supervisor. 3.2 The supervisor ensures that a single voucher is delivered immediately to the vehicle (at the fuel station). 3.3 The supervisor reports to Fleet S ection through SS - Fleet@hamad.qa with the vehicle registration number and full details of the prob lem. 3.4 The Fleet Section will liaise with WOQOD for rectification.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 30 SOP 1.13 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 1.13 MANAGEMENT NOTI FICATION PROCESS Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide guidelines and a set of rules for the Management Notification process in the Ambulance Service. Responsibility HMCAS MANAGEMENT STA FF 1. MAJOR INCIDENT ON CALL WEEKLY ROSTER will be sent via email to all HMCAS Management staff every Sunday. 2. HMCAS Managers are responsible for posting the roster at NCC, HCC, and all HUBS. 3. NCC On - Duty Officers must use the Incident of Interest Group on Microso ft Teams to send out Notifications. 3.1 All information shared on the Incident of Interest Group is confidential . 3.2 This is not a primary activation channel for any such incidents. Fact 24 will continue to be used as the primary activation channel. 3.3 All Incidents of Interest should be posted in the Incident s of Interest Group by the NCC On - Duty Officer, including a brief description of the incident and NAJM pin location . 3.4 Appropriate updates should also be posted . 3.5 Information will be sha

red in real time at all hours of the d ay and night. If you need to not be disturbed , it is your resp onsibility to temporarily mute the notifications. 3.6 In the interest of minimizing necessary disturbance s , please do not 'acknowledge' messages (copy, noted , etc. should n ot be used) . 3.7 If you are instructed to take an action or provided information, report only when the action is completed or with the information requested . 4. Operation Command Desk and Clinical Desk must be informed by NCC On - Duty Officer fo r any Incident list ed in Point 7 .5. 5. Operation Command Desk On - Duty is the person to communicate any related information from scene to Clinical Desk after being briefed by On - Duty NCC Officer or Distribution Team Leader. 6. The Incidents of Interest listed in points 7.1 to 7.14 require the following actions by the NCC On - Duty Officer: 6.1 Send notification to Incidents of Interest Group in Microsoft Teams. 6.2 Inform the Operation Officers On - Duty. 6.3 A ctivate On - Call Silver Command 6.3.1 Silver Command at his discretion, choose to notify Gold Co mmand. 6.4 When required, NCC On - Duty Officer will ensure the Team Leaders have notified their In - L

ine Managers respectively. 7. Incidents of Interest include, but are not limited to: 7.1 Incidents where on initial call are greater than 5 patients in total ( 10 patient s** ) . 7.2 Incidents where there are more than 3 patients classified as either Yellow or Red ( 5 patient s** ) . 7.3 Incidents with prolonged scene times , i.e.>60 minutes .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 31 7.4 Significant Mecha nism of Injury (i.e. shootings, explosive related, sign of child abuse, sign of criminal activity and chemical/radiological spills). 7.5 Incidents which attract media interest** (incidents in very crowded areas, incidents of public unrest, significant injury, or illness of a patient involved in a major sporting or entertainment event). 7.6 Incidents which involve HMCAS staff (i.e. any on duty ambulance resource collisions with other road users and/or where there are injuries). 7.7 Incidents which may reflect negatively on HMC** 7.8 Incidents occurring at sensitive locations (Ministry buildings, military installations, embassies, AUAB, DIA, HIA). 7.9 Any airport incident inv olving whole aircraft, life threatening injury or illness, as well a s any potential cases of infection with potential public health consequences. 7.10 Any VIP, VVIP case 7.11 Activation of Major Incident Response (MIR) Plan 7.12 Any significant service failure (in the field or in the control room). 7.13 Other incidents may be included at the discretion of the Operation Officer On - Duty at the NCC. 7.14 **Incidents that should

be escalated by Silver Communication to Silver Command & Gold Command as listed on the Major Incident On - Call Roster. 8. If d em and exceeds resource capability, the On - Duty NCC Off icer will escalate to Gold Command as listed on the Major Incident On - Call Roster . 9. Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 32 SOP 1.14 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Staff Procedure 1.14 ABSENTEEISM MANAGEME NT Implementation Date 01 September 2020 Responsibility ALL HMCAS STAFF 1. Purpose : 1.1 To manage attendance within HMCAS in order to maintain operational readiness, staff health and wellness, ensure staff are fit to care for patients, and to minimize the impact of sick leave absence on work colleagues. 2. Scope : 2.1 All HMCAS Staff. 3. Definitions : 3.1 The Bradford Factor: a trigger tool used to identify patterns of absence related to sickness. 3.2 Bradford Factor Score: a formula used to calculate a numeric score based on the number of unrelated absence periods (S) squared, multiplied by the total number of days absent (D), equals the Bradford Score (B). The formula is S 2 x D = B . 3.3 Bradford Factor Table: a matrix with the calculated scores based on the Bradford Factor formula. 4. Procedure : 4.1 The Ambulance Service will be utilizing the Bradford Factor S core as a trigger tool to assist Supervisors in identifying staff that require more comprehensive support t

o reduce their sick leave . 4.2 Following the issuance of the previous quarter’s sickness report, the supervisor will note the following : 4.2.1 Number of separate occasions the staff member has been absent due to sickness . 4.2.2 Total number of days sick during the period . 4.3 Using the Brad ford Factor Table, the supervisor will then determine the Bradford Factor Score and corresponding colour attributable of the staff member . 4.4 The score and colour dictate which stage of the management program should be followed in addressing the staff member’ s absenteeism due to sicknes s. 4.5 Special considerations related to staff on light dut y : 4.5.1 All decisions to place a staff member on light duties will be supported by a report from the Staff Clinic. The report will clearly indicate the start and review date of t he light duty period. The report will be shared with the Senior Operations Manager and the Assistant Executive Director for Group Business Services . 4.5.2 During a period of light dut y, all staff members will/will not be deployed in accordance with t he guidance from the Staff Clinic. Further more, during the period of light duties , the staff member is not eligible f
or overtime, nor promotion into any acting position.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 33 The staff member will remain an employee of the cost centre that they are substantively employed unde r. 4.5.3 Upon reaching the light dut y review date , the staff member will return to the Staff Clinic for follow up evaluation. T he resulting report and decision on whether they are fit to pract ice in their substantive job will be decided. If it is decided that the staff member is unable to practice as their substantive job, then they will be transferred to an administrative role depending on vacancy and the recruitment selection process by the r ecruiting department: their pay and benefits may be affected . 5. Attachments : 5.1 Table 1: Bradford Factor Grid 5.2 Table 2: Summary of Actions 5.3 Appendix 1: Green 5.4 Appendix 2: Yellow 5.5 Appendix 3: Orange 5.6 Appendix 4: Red 5.7 Appendix 5: Purple 6. References : 6.1 None

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 41 SOP 1.15 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 1.15 PERFORMANCE IMPROVE MENT PROCESS Implementation Date 28 March 2021 Responsibility ALL HMCAS GROUP STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 To describe how the Performance Improvement Process will be implemented in the Ambulance Service. 2. Scope: 2.1 Applicable to all staff, clinical and non - clinical, except physicians, within the Ambulance Service. 3. Definitions: 3.1 Poor Performanc e: W here the level of job performance does not meet the requirements and standards described in the Job Description , and/or where the employee has received an annual performance evaluation score below Meets Expectations/Good . 3.2 SMART O bjective s: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Realistic, and Timebound. 4. Procedure: 4.1 Managers and supervisors will utilize this process for ad dressing poor performance of HMCAS staff. 4.2 Where persistent poor performance is noted, this SOP should be applied to assist managers in order to support staff and address concerns . 4.3 Table 1 describes a 3 - step process , starting with

a face - to - face meeting between the immediate supervisor and employee, where an action plan is agreed and documented. SMART objectives must be utilized . 4.4 If the desired improvement is achieved, then no further action may be required . If improve ment is not achieved, managers should move to Step 2, which will require further supervision and support . 4.5 If the desired outcome is still not achieved, Step 3 will require the engagement of HR to review the documentation and consider the individuals future employment . 4.6 Managers should ensure that sufficient supervision and support is available for staff to achieve the desired outcome, and that objectives are clear. It is recommended that employees meet with their immediate supervisor weekly to monitor perfor mance, and provide feedback as needed. 4.7 This SOP is linked to the appraisal process , and episodes of poor performance must be reflected in the appraisal grading and comments . 5. Attachments: 5.1 Table 1: Guidelines for Managing Poor Performance 5.2 Figure 1 : Performance Improvement Plan Template 6. References: 6.1 HR 3020: Employee Performance Improvement (Non - Physician Staff) 6.2 HR 304 9: Performance Management
, Appraisal and Evaluation

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 42 Table 1: Guidelines for Managing Poor Performance STEP PROCESS Step 1: Initial Notification Initiate Process: Face - to - face meeting between employee and immediate supervisor to discuss the performance shortfall and the background to why it may have occurred. Action Corrective Action documented with recommended timeframe of 1 - 3 months . If there is successful improvement in performance: Acknowledgement memo to employee. Meet the employee in person to acknowledge the improvement and encourage ongoing high levels of work. Advise if there is a repeat occurrence of unsatisfactory performance for the same or different reason, the process may resume at the next step as a continuous proc ess. If no successful improvement occurs: Go to Step Two Step 2: Second Notification Initiate Process: Face - to - face meeting between employee and immediate supervisor to discuss the performance shortfall and the background to why improvement has not occurred. Inform employee that failure to improve performance may result in disciplinary action through HR. Action Corrective Action documented. Recommended timeframe of 2 months. If there

is successful improvement in performance: Acknowledgement memo to employee. Advise if there is a repeat occurrence of unsatisfactory performance for the same or different reason, counseling may resume at the next step as a continuous process. If no successful improvement occurs: Go to Step Three Step 3: Third Notification Department shall forward the issue with all supporting documents to HR. HR in coordination with Legal Department (if applicable) shall verify and finalize the case in accordance with HR Management Law (15) 2016 and HMC By Laws .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 44 SOP 1.16 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 1.16 HMCAS PROCUREMENT AN D CONTRACT PROCESS Implementation Date 09 April 2020 Purpose T o familiarize department employees with department purchasing practices . Responsibility ALL HMCAS MANAGERS A ND EXECUTIVES 1. Purpose: 1.1 The purpose of this document, hereafter referenced as a Procurement SOP, is to familiarize department employees (whether an Ambulance Service Procurement Coordinator, SCM buyer or an AS staff) with department purchasing practices in a clear, common understanding of goals, benefits and policies as well as what t he department expects with regard to performance and conduct. The document provides an overall description of the department’s purchasing program. 2. Responsibility : 2.1 Ambulance Service Procurement Section is responsible for developing their procurement plans i n cooperation with the HMC Supply Chain Management and the Ambulance Service. 2.1.1 Project/Procurement Coordinator is the personnel with access rights to the HMC's ERP System (Oracle). They have the

relevant authority to assign a requisition for a specific Cost Centre or a range of Cost Centers, as appropriate. They are responsible for coordinating all procurement related activities, including contacting the supplier (through SCM) and communicating the receipt/update to the specific end - user (authorized requesto r or a person appointed on his/her behalf). 2.1.2 Senior Supervisor is the personnel who closely monitor/assist project coordinator’s assignment. He is authorized to assign project coordinator for PR Creation. He manages HMC Ambulance Service’s contracts. He is responsible for arranging evaluation meetings with executives for high value tenders. 2.1.3 Project Manager – Procurement and Tender is the personnel responsible for managing the core business operations of the procurement team and all its purchases. He is respo nsible for delegating tasks to the entire team and for any escalation as when required. 3. Guideline/Procedure : 3.1 HMCAS Standard Procurement Process : 3.1.1 The Authorized Requestor (Manager/Executive) must submit a general request by email to the Procurement Section. The requestor must make sure AS Stores/Facilities Section does not have the same i

tem in their stock. Authorized Requestor is responsible for providing accurate specifications to the AS Procurement Section. 3.1.2 The request can be clubbed with other requests a nd clearly stated in the Oracle System. 3.1.3 Procurement Coordinator creates the Oracle PR (follows the iProcurement Work Instructions). 3.1.4 IT Contracts Management, projects, applications, software/hardware equipment requests may be subjected to further approval c ycle, both internal to HMC (HICT portfolio) and external to Ministry of Transport and Communications (MOTC) whether your request is OPEX or CAPEX.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 45 3.1.4.1 The following documentation is required for all IT requests: 3.1.4.1.1 IT change request 3.1.4.1.2 Business Case 3.1.4.1.3 To be submitted to HMCAS IT delivery team, who will review before submitting to HICT portfolio. 3.1.4.2 The following documentation is required for all IT requests above 500,000 QAR. 3.1.4.2.1 IT change request 3.1.4.2.2 Business Case 3.1.4.2.3 Request for a proposal (RFP) 3.1.4.2.4 a set of MOTC documentatio n 3.1.4.2.5 To be submitted to HMCAS IT delivery team, who will review before submitting to HICT portfolio. 3.2 HMCAS Group Oracle PR Approval Process : 3.2.1 Once approved by the Finance Business Partner through email, then the Incomplete PR is submitted in the Oracle system by the Procurement Coordinator. While submitting the PR, a Manager/AED is added as “First Approver”. 3.2.2 The chain of approvals for PR value is as follows Requestor > Project Manager - Procurement > HMC Finance > AED (First Approver) > ED > CEO (Final Approver ) 3.2.3 The Approver can “Request Information” from anyone in HMC through the Oracle system by clicking the search tab and typing in the relevant name. 3.

2.4 The approver must log into the Oracle system, as they would for the HR Self Service. On the home page they wil l see all pending notifications with regards to the approvals of PR/requested information. 3.2.5 Click on the notification and the approver can choose to approve/reject or request information to anyone in HMC. 3.2.6 The Final Approver (EDs) should confirm the followin g before approving the PRs: 3.2.6.1 Is the quantity requested in line with the current/agreed consumption rates? 3.2.6.2 Is an alternative available or has been considered if the price per unit seems excessive? 3.2.6.3 Is the spend aligned with the Service Delivery Plan (SDP)? 3.2.6.4 SD P includes the ten - year CAPEX budget and the yearly OPEX budget sent to the respective EDs for their respective departments. 3.2.6.5 If yes – approve PR 3.2.6.6 The approver can “Request Information” from Finance Business Partner (Damien Britton) to ensure the coding. 3.2.6.7 If the spend is outside the agreed SDP/budget, he/she must “Request Information” from Department Chief to agree on additional spend. 3.2.7 Once approved, the PR is sent to SCM for further process. If it requires specifications form Engineering/Bio
- Engine ering, then it must be sent to the relevant department before forwarding it to SCM.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 46 3.2.8 If PR amount is QAR 200,000 or above, please send it to Minor Tender Committee and if it is above QAR 1,000,000 it is sent to the Tender Committee Secretariat along with th e appropriate specifications and RFP. 3.2.9 For any PR above QAR 200,000, a Tender Request (TR) has to be created by the AS Procurement Coordinator. 3.2.10 SCM/TCS Manager will need to assign a Buyer for processing the request. This must be followed - up by the AS Procur ement Coordinator to avoid delay. 3.2.11 Buyer will send out RFQs to suppliers/service providers and closing date is set. Procurement Coordinator communicates all updates to the end - user. 3.2.12 Quotes are received by the Buyer on the closing date and are provided to AS by way of evaluation memo. 3.2.13 Procurement Coordinator documents and files the evaluation memo before forwarding it to the end - user for their evaluation. 3.2.14 Acceptance memo/Rejection memo is created as a reply to the evaluation memo. Price, quality, and delivery time should be taken into consideration while evaluating. If approval is required from other departments, it must be addressed to the releva

nt department and then forwarded to SCM/TCS. 3.2.15 The Buyer will create a PO based on the acceptance memo or go for re - q uotation depending on AS Procurement Section’s reply. 3.2.16 For some TCS requests, the Buyer might request for considerations from HMC Internal Audit and HMC Legal Affairs departments before releasing the PO. 3.2.17 The PO will be released, and the vendor/service provi der will collect it from SCM. 3.2.18 Any changes in PO, a memo is sent to Head of Expediting Section detailing the change and its justification. 3.3 HMCAS Procurement Process (for inks, cartridges, toners, water coolers, water coupons, business cards, catering) : 3.3.1 The Authorized Requestor (Stores/Facilities/Executive Secretaries’ Office) submits a request to the Procurement Section. 3.3.2 For business cards, the business card request form must be signed and approved by the Executive before the PR is created. A memo must be se nt to the Chief of Business Services for approval. 3.3.3 All PRs (except for catering) is catalogued in the Oracle system and is automatically generated as a PO as they are Blanket Purchase Agreement. 3.3.4 As these are BPA POs (except for catering), they are assigne

d a PO number along with a release number (E.g.: 118130 - 731). 3.4 Documentation : 3.4.1 This must be filed as individual files and numbered using the PR number. Subsequent specifications, additional information, memos, and email threads must be attached to the file. 3.4.2 T he receiving copies of all PRs and its attaching documents must be scanned and stored in the HMC AS Production server ( \ \ hmcfs03 \ ASProduction). Must have PR number in the file name so it can be identified or searched easily. 3.5 Contracts Management :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 47 3.5.1 All contracts are to be mentioned in the Outlook Calendar with the reminders kept 8 months/6 months/3 months before expiry. All parties under ASContracts@hamad.qa are notified of the same. 3.5.2 HMCAS Procurement must ensure meetings are conducted on a monthly, quar terly, biannually, or annually (based on the total value of the contracts) with each vendor; 3.5.2.1 QAR 10,000,000 and more (in total contracts value) = quarterly meetings. 3.5.2.2 QAR 1,000,000 and more (in total contracts value) = biannual meetings. 3.5.2.3 QAR 500,000 and mor e (in total contracts value) = annual meetings. 3.5.3 During these scheduled meetings, performance is evaluated based on KPIs or other indicators set by the respective contracts. 3.5.4 HMCAS Procurement must ensure all previous payments have been processed before proc essing the latest invoice. 3.5.5 HMCAS Procurement must ensure a contract excel sheet is updated every month with the latest incoming and outgoing contracts. Contracts amendments must also be reflected here. 4. Attachments : 4.1 PR Procedure 4.2 Contract/Procurement Evaluat ion Process

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 50 SOP 1 . 17 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 1.17 DISCLOSURE OF H MCAS S TAFF I DENTIFICATION TO E XTERNAL S ECURITY A GENCIES Implementation Date 1 8 July 20 23 Purpose To establish guidelines and procedures for disclosing staff information to the external security agencies while ensuring the protection of sensitive employee data and compliance with legal requirements. Responsibility ALL HMC AS STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 To establish guidelines and procedures for disclosing staff information to the external security agencies while ensuring the protection of sensitive employee data and compliance with legal requirements. 2. Scope: 2.1 The SOP applies to all HMCAS staff . 3. De finitions: 3.1 HMCAS Staff information: Personal and confidential information (for example, but not limited to, HMC ID, QID, Qatar Driving License). 3.2 External Security Agencies: Authorized law enforcement agencies, National Security agencies responsible for enforcing laws, maintaining public safety, traffic s afety or conducting investigations i.e., Qatar Traffic

department, Lekhwiya, Al Fazaa, Criminal Investigation Department, ISF etc. 4. Procedure: 4.1 HMCAS staff must communicate calmly and politely when asked for their identification. 4.2 Politely ask the reason for requesting ID card. 4.3 When an external security agency requests HMCAS staff information, the request should be verified for authenticity and legitimacy. Ensure the requester belongs to the authorized external security agency. Politely state that HMCAS Policy requires the requestor: 4.3.1 To be in uniform 4.3.2 Verify reason for requesting the identification 4.3.3 If not in uniform, request verification by their official identification. Without this, no HMCAS staff can provide identification details . 4.4 Before disclosing any information please make sure the requester is eligible to receive that HMCAS staff identification . 4.5 If HMCAS staff are in doubt about the authenticity of the requester, please make sure to request for your immediate supervisor via NCC notification. 4.6 Provide the requested staff information promptly, ensuring accuracy and completeness. 4.7 If the requester is police/law enforcement team HMCAS staff can show their HMC ID and/or QID. 4.8 If the re
quester is CID staff can display their HMC ID and/or QID.
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 51 4.9 HMCAS staff whilst driving Ambulance unit can share their driving license to the traffic patrol team on request .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 52 SOP 1 . 19 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operat ing Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Group Quality, Patient Safety and Risk Management Procedure 1.19 CONDUCTING A Q UALITY I MPROVEMENT A CTIVITY IN HMCASG Implementation Date 18 September 2024 Purpose To ensure that all quality improvement projects are conducted in compliance with the organisation’s policies and that all data usage and publication rights are clearly understood . Responsibility ALL HMC AS STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 Ensure that all quality improvement projects are conducted in compliance with HMCASG policies. 1.2 To adhere with the Corporate Quality and Patient Safety (QPS) program AS 9040. 1.3 Assist the HMCASG staff in ensuring that their Quality Improvement (QI) projects are conducted properly. 1.4 All data usage and publication rights are clearly understood. 2. Scope: 2.1 All HMCASG staff. 3. Definitions: 3.1 Quality Improvement Advisor (QIA): a professional who provides guidance and advice to individuals within an organisation aiming to improve their operational effectiveness and quality of service. The key roles of a QIA are: 3.

1.1 Advise on the development and implementation of quality improvement strategies and plans. 3.1.2 Collaborate with management to set quality expectations and goals. 3.1.3 Encourage the dissemination of a continuous improve ment culture. 3.2 Quality Improvement Mentor (QIM): a professional who reports to the QIA to promote and facilitate the application of quality improvement principles within an organisation by supporting improvement teams and providing them with the necessary c oaching and training to foster a culture of continuous quality improvement. The key roles of QIM are: 3.2.1 Assists in identifying areas of potential improvement and implementing improvement strategies and plans within an organisation. 3.2.2 Provides mentoring and coaching for the improvers when conducting their QI projects. 3.2.3 Facilitates training and development to equip improvers with the necessary skills and knowledge to maintain quality standards. 3.2.4 Promotes a positive quality culture within the organi sation, fostering engagement and enthusiasm for continuous improvement efforts. 4. QIA and QIM are responsible for ensuring that the HMCAS Executive Director is informed about all the ongoing QI
projects. 4.1 Service Delivery Unit (SDU) reviewer: A reviewer asses ses the QI proposals to comply with SDU priorities, requirements and HMCASG policy.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 53 4.2 ASGQPSRM reviewer: T he Ambulance Service Group Quality and Patient Safety reviewer who coordinates the QI review process and assesses the QI methodology. 5. Procedure: 5.1 Submiss ion to the Ambulance Service Group Quality, Patient Safety and Risk Management: 5.1.1 All quality improvement projects must be submitted to the Ambulance Service Group Quality, Patient Safety and Risk Management at the e - mail address HMCAS - QI@hamad.qa for approval prior to commencement. 5.1.2 The submission should include a proposal detailing the purpose, methods, and expected outcomes using the Proposal Form (Appendix 1). 5.1.3 Where MRC approved a study as a quality improvement stud y, the Improver must forward the proposal and MRC decision letter to HMCAS - QI@hamad.qa to ensure receiving the required HMCASG approval to initiate the QI project. 5.1.4 The QIA and QIM are responsible for ensuring that a ll QI projects approved by the HMCASGQPSRM are logged in the Life QI platform. 5.1.5 The improvers are responsible for executing the appropriate steps and carrying out required analyses in line with QI processes under the guidance of

QIA and QIM. 5.1.6 The QIA and QIM are not directly tasked with conducting the analysis or synthesising project outcomes. Their roles primarily involve providing guidance and support, while the actual execution of the improvement tasks is the responsibility of the designated team or indivi dual. 5.1.7 HMCASG QPS commit to supporting the Improver in preparing the outcome of their projects to be presented in national and international events and publications. 5.1.8 The project outcome is owned by the HMC. The Improver has to obtain approval for publicatio n/presentation in international and national forums by sending a request to HMCAS - QI@hamad.qa . 5.2 Compliance with Terms: 5.2.1 All quality improvement projects must be conducted in accordance with the terms outlined below. 5.2.2 These terms include, but are not limited to, ethical considerations, patient safety, data security, and confidentiality: 5.2.2.1 Institution Policies and Patient Safety: The project must be conducted in adherence to the institution’s policies and procedures, including but not limited to those rel ated to patient safety, confidentiality, data handling, and quality control. It is crucial that respect for

patient rights, privacy, and well - being be maintained at all times during the project. 5.2.2.2 Publication and Data Usage: As outlined in the Project Data U sage and Publication Agreement (Appendix 2), all data generated from this project is owned by HMCASG. It is critical the Improver does not publish, disclose, or disseminate any project findings without the express written permission from HMCASG. The Improv er is required to sign this agreement and return it within three (3) working days from the receipt of the approval memo. 5.3 Non - Compliance: 5.3.1 Failure to comply with any of the points mentioned in this SOP could lead to disciplinary measures.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 54 6. Benefits of conducting a Quality Improvement initiative: 6.1 Staff who submit QI initiatives will be given priority for nomination to attend QI courses offered by Hamad Healthcare Quality Institute and other relevant institutions. 6.2 Participants will receive a formal thank you letter acknowledging their contribution to improving healthcare quality. 6.3 The thank you letter can be used to support claims for CPD points with the Department of Healthcare Professions (DHP). 6.4 Successful QI initiatives may lead to opportunities for writ ing and publishing findings in professional journals. 7. Attachments: 7.1 Appendix 1: Quality Improvement Activity Proposal . (if a

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 62 SOP 2. 1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.1 PERSONAL PROTECT ION & SAFETY Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To direct and ensure the use of appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) • To ensure both patient and practitioner safety in hazardous environments Responsibilit y ALL HMC AMBULANCE SE RVICE STAFF 1. General considerations for PPE: 1.1 Wear only HMCAS Approved PPE 1.2 Before operational duties, remove hand jewelry that could damage surgical gloves. 1.3 Use hygienic techniques for donning and removing medical gloves. 1.4 Perform hand hygiene before and after donning gloves. 1.5 Don disposable medical gloves prior to arriving at the patient’s side. 1.6 Do not wear medical gloves whilst driving. 1.7 Replace torn or punctured disposable gloves immediately. 1.8 Dispose of disposable PPE at th e point of care after endorsing the patient. 1.9 Dispose of disposable PPE in designated waste containers. 1.10 Thoroughly clean non - disposable PPE prior to next use. 1.11 Seek advice from senior staff if unsure about the

use of PPE. 1.12 Notify a senior HMCAS staff member i f a staff member becomes injured . 2. Highly infectious disease situations: 2.1 Notify the communications center 2.2 Avoid any unnecessary exposure of self and others to the patient. 2.3 Wear a second pair of gloves plus eye protection (goggles/face - shield) for additional protection. 2.4 Wear a “N95 Mask” or equivalent respiratory mask in the presence of air - borne diseases. 2.5 Wear specialized full body PPE for uncommon highly infectious diseases, as directed by a senior clinical HMCAS staff member. See Fig. 2.1 - 1 & Figure 2.1 - 2 for donning/doffing PPE. 2.6 Follow national recommendations on wearing PPE during outbreaks and in the presence of new emerging and re - emerging diseases . 3. PPE for RTA scenes, Major Incidents, and Industrial Incidents: 3.1 ALWAYS don a high - visibility vest BEFORE arrival on the scene . 3.2 Wear a firmly secured helmet if there is a perceived head injury risk . 3.3 Use face and eye protection in the form of an appropriate face - shield and/or safety goggles. 3.4 Wear rescue gloves during special access o perations. 3.5 Use appropriate hearing protection (e.g. earmuffs /inserts) in high -
noise environments (e.g. helicopter, industrial machines, etc.) . Enhanced situational and spatial awareness is required since the ability to communicate and concentrate may becom e diminished.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 63 4. Lighting and Safety : 4.1 Position HMCAS vehicles so that the lights could provide light in a dark environment. 4.2 Carry HMCAS - issued torch at all times , including during the day. 4.3 Do not shine the torch light into other people’s faces. 4.4 Do not attempt to access a patient in confined/hazardous situations (e.g. crushed vehicle, building collapse, etc.) until: 4.4.1 You have donned appropriate PPE 4.4.2 It safe to do so; and 4.4.3 You receive support from other appropriate stakeholders (Civil Defence/Police on the s cene) . 4.5 Shield the patient from potential debris during extrication from hazardous environments which may include broken glass, exposed sharp metal, non - deployed airbags, construction material and waste, falling debris, hazardous gas, hazardous chemicals, h oles, heights, electrocution, and fire.
