Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 66 SOP 2.1.A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.1. A MANAGEMENT OF AMBUL ANCE IN THE EVENT OF FIRE Implementation Date 23 October 2024 Purpose To ensure the safety of ambulance personnel, patients, and bystanders in the event of a fire occurring in an ambulance during operations . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 The purpose of this SOP is to establish clear, detailed procedures to ensure the safety of ambulance personnel, patients, and bystanders in the event of a fire occurring in an ambulance during operations. This includes outlining emergency response protocols, evacuation procedures, preventive measures, and post - incident actions. 2. Scope: 2.1 This SOP applies to all ambulance operations where a fire - related incident may occur, whether during patient transport, emergency response, or stationary operations. It covers actions to be taken by all ambulance personnel in the event of a fire. 3. Definitions: 3.1 Ambulance Fire : A fire occurring in the ambulance, including fires originating from the engine

, e lectrical components, or medical equipment, which can pose significant risks to both personnel and patients. 3.2 Oxygen Tank : Mounted and portable Oxygen accelerates combustion, making these tanks extremely dangerous in the event of a fire. 3.3 RACE Procedure : ( R a is e the alarm, A ssist with the evacuation of patient/s, C lose any doors or windows and E xtinguish the fire ONLY if you are confident or safe to do so). 3.4 PASS Method : ( P ull the pin, A im the nozzle, S queeze the handle, and S weep the extinguisher from side to side). 4. Procedure: 4.1 Ensure Personal and Patient Safety: 4.1.1 Stop the Vehicle Immediately: If the ambulance is in motion when the fire starts, your priority is to pull over to a safe location away from traffic, structures, or flammable materials (if possible). Do not wait to assess the fire; any sign of smoke or fire should be taken seriously. Turn off the engine vehicle. 4.2 Call for Emergency Services: 4.2.1 Notify NCC/HCC via current channel by pressing the emergency button on the radio and including the “ break, break, b reak – control [vehicle call sign]”. Refer to SOP 4.5.C to give a message that the vehicle is on fire and help is requir

ed and evacuation of the staff and patient should take priority. 4.2.2 Provide your exact locatio n and describe the situation, including whether there are hazardous materials on board, like oxygen tanks or flammable medical supplies. This will allow responders to arrive fully prepared. 4.3 Evacuate All Occupants: 4.3.1 Get yourself, your partner, and any patients or passengers out of the ambulance as quickly and safely as possible. Time is of the essence because fires in enclosed

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 67 spaces can spread rapidly. If you have a patient, follow your training to ensure they are removed carefully but swiftly. 4.3.2 ( Note : Your safety and the patient’s safety are the highest priority. If the fire is progressing quickly, you need to prioritize getting people out over saving equipment ). 4.4 Move to a Safe Distance: 4.4.1 Maintain a Safe Distance : Once everyone is out of the ambula nce, move at least 100 meters away. This is crucial, as vehicle fires can escalate, leading to fuel and oxygen tank explosions or the release of toxic fumes from burning materials such as plastic, chemicals, and medical equipment. 4.4.2 Keep Bystanders Away: Ens ure bystanders are kept at a safe distance as well. Ambulances carry flammable materials like oxygen tanks, which can explode if the fire reaches them. 4.5 Use a Fire Extinguisher (Only If Safe to Do So): 4.5.1 Assess the Fire: If the fire is small and localized and the engine fire hasn't spread, you may consider using a fire extinguisher. However, your first duty is to evaluate whether using the extinguisher is safe. 4.5.2 If it’s safe to do so, aim the extinguisher at

the base of the fire, not the flames, and follow the PASS method. 4.5.3 ( Note : If the fire has spread to the rear of the ambulance or is growing rapidly, do not attempt to extinguish it. Furthermore, avoid returning to the ambulance if the fire has started near oxygen tanks or medical supplies, as these can pose s ignificant explosive or hazardous risks ). 4.6 Medical Assessment: 4.6.1 Once the situation is under control, everyone involved should be checked for injuries or smoke inhalation. Even brief exposure to a vehicle fire can result in respiratory issues due to toxic fum es. 4.7 Documentation: 4.7.1 Before the end of the shift staff shall: 4.7.1.1 Complete OVA report 4.7.1.2 Obtain the staff incident investigation report 4.7.1.3 ( Note : Police report and Staff Investigation report should be completed within 48 hours ) . 4.8 Post Incident procedure: 4.8.1 Follow SOP 4.6 D Section 5 5. Attachments: 5.1 FIRE AND EXPLOSION HAZARDS CAUSED BY OXYGEN CYLINDERS. 6. References: 6.1 BOŻENA et al., 2018. FIRE AND EXPLOSION HAZARDS CAUSED. Wit press, 174(7), pp. 149 - 151 .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 68 SOP 2.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.2 MEDICAL WASTE HA NDLING Implementation Date 23 June 2024 Purpose To direct and ensure the appropriate handling and disposal of medical waste Responsibility ALL HMCAS STAFF 1. Purpose : 1.1 To give clear direction about the segregation, storage, handling, transportation, and disposal of waste in order to ensure the safety of healthcare workers and to minimize the potential risk it presents to the general public and the environment. 2. Scope : 2.1 All HMCAS Staff 3. Definitions : 3.1 Healthcare Waste: any material produced by the health c are establishment that is no longer wanted and requires disposal. 3.2 Infectious Waste/Clinical Waste: has the potential for transmitting infectious diseases, it includes: 3.3 Sharp Waste: These are the wastes that contain sharp items such as sharps, intravenous n eedles, blood vials, glass vials and needles, scalpels, lancets, sutures, razors, broken glass or any other sharp objects that cut or sting the body. 3.4 Waste from Human blood, blood products and

body fluids: includes human blood or blood components (serum o r plasma). Containers with free - flowing blood or blood components or discarded saturated material containing free flowing blood, blood component and body fluids. Soiled surgical dressings, swabs etc. 4. Procedure : 4.1 All HMCAS staff shall comply with this SOP . 4.2 Operational staff shall ensure that vehicles are equipped with colour - coded bags for proper segregation . 4.3 It is necessary to use the appropriate colour coded bags and labels for collection of wastes and shall be colour coded or have a biohazard label. 4.4 Iden tification, segregation, and packaging of waste shall be done at the point and time of origin by the generator of that waste. 4.5 Any spillage of clinical waste (e.g. content of yellow bag) must be dealt with immediately. 4.6 Temporary Storage of Infectious Waste : 4.6.1 The TEMPORARY STORAGE area for Infectious Waste shall have the following provisions: 4.6.1.1 The specified location for the collection of infectious waste should be known to all h ealthcare staff. 4.6.1.2 This waste shall be placed in closed and secured containers or plastic bags prior to storage.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 69 4.6.1.3 Waste will be temporarily stored in closed containers withi n the designated area until the scheduled pick - up time. The storage location shall be appropriate and cause no pollution or harm against human health or the environment . 4.6.1.4 Storage times for infectious waste (e.g. the time gap between generation and treatment ) should normally not exceed 24 hours but can extend up to 48 hours during the cool season . 4.6.1.5 Legible stickers should be placed on the storage location stating the nature of the contents in the location . 4.7 Temporary Storage of Other Healthcare (Non - Infectious) Waste : 4.7.1 The holding site for domestic waste black bag collection shall be covered and fly proof. 4.7.2 Domestic waste storage area must be separated from infectious waste areas. 4.8 Handling of Waste : 4.8.1 In order to prevent inappropriate disposal, infectious waste shal l be segregated from non - risk (domestic waste). Clear information, instruction, and training about identifying specific categories of waste should be provided to Staff. 4.8.2 Correct colour coded bags shall be used to segregate waste: TYPE OF WA

STE COLOR CODED BAG Domestic Waste (Paper tissues and face mask used by the patient, gloves, gowns, apron, face shield, head cover, shoe cover, and face masks including N95 mask used). Black bag Infectious Waste (Visibly soiled waste includes human blood or bloody fluids) Blood stained PPE such as: Gloves, face shield, gowns. Yellow bag labeled with Universal Biohazard sign Sharps (needles, syringes etc.) Yellow Sharp Box with the Universal Biohazard sign Amputa ted body part (e.g. hand, leg etc.) Transparent plastic bag covered with bed sheet Pathological Waste – including Placenta. Yellow bag Presence of emerging and re - emerging disease. Follow Corporate and MOPH recommendations for waste segregation and disposal . 4.8.3 Waste bags shall be packed three fourth full (3/4), or as required, and securely closed by tying the top of each bag. 4.9 Segregation of Waste :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 70 4.9.1 Segregation and packaging of waste shall be done using waste bags and boxes according to the colour code and labelling scheme. 4.9.2 Yellow bags with contaminated clinical waste shall not be mixed with black bags 4.9.3 Double bagging is recommended when the weight/mass of the waste warrants added packing strength. Infectious waste shall be p acked in single bags only. 4.9.4 Bags shall be protected from puncture and tearing by preventing contact with external sharp objects. 4.9.5 Sharps c ontainers shall be securely closed when three fourth full (3/4), put into yellow plastic bags with the required identifi cation tag and sent for autoclaving. 4.9.6 Sharps Containers in response bags shall be always securely closed between uses . 4.9.7 Body fluid drains shall be placed in a yellow plastic bag. 4.9.8 For used Intravenous set, discard by tying the tube around the empty plastic ba g that contained the intravenous fluid and place in the domestic black bin – no cutting. If the IV tubing is connected to glass, cut the IV tubing by retaining the spike inside the bottle and discard in the rigid container for empty bottles

. However, all t ypes of blood contaminated tubing should be placed in Yellow Bags to be sent for autoclaving . 4.10 Linen [Disposable] : 4.10.1 Place dirty linen not soiled with blood or body fluids in domestic bags . 4.10.2 Place linen soiled with blood or body fluids in yellow bags . 5. Referenc es : 5.1 Management of Infectious Waste CL 7249 .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 71 SOP 2.3 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Production Procedure 2.3 SOCIAL CLEANING AND SIMPLE DECONTAMI NATION OF VEHICLES A ND NON - CRITICAL EQUIPME NT Implementation Date 23 June 2024 Purpose To ensure that operational vehicles and non - critical equipment are socially clean and disinfected . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Social cleaning and simple decontamination may be performed at Hubs, Sheltered Spokes and Health Facilities. 2. At the shift start, ensure that vehicles and equipment are socially clean : 2.1 Assess the vehicle and equipment for soiling . 2.2 Where there is soiling from body - substances, the vehicle or equipment should be exchanged, and the soiled equipment sent for Deep Cleaning. 2.3 Cleaning and disinfection should be performed for minor visible soiling. 2.4 Visible non - body - substance soiling should b e cleaned. 2.5 External vehicle cleaning should be done as required 3. Cleaning products and tools : 3.1 Social cleaning should be carried out with soap, cloths, vacuum cleaners (inside ), mops , soft brooms, and/

or high - pressure cleaners (outside) . 3.1.1 Brooms cloths and mo ps should be rinsed and allowed to dry naturally . 3.2 Cleaning and disinfection should be performed with AS approved disinfectant disposable wipes . 3.2.1 Wipes should be discarded in a black bin . 4. Personal protective equipment (PPE) : 4.1 The following personal protective equipment should be worn: 4.1.1 Latex or non - latex examination gloves, doubled - up . 4.1.2 Safety glasses , if required 4.2 Following cleaning, disposable PPE must be discarded in a black bin and non - disposable PPE should be wiped with alcohol - based disposable wipes . 5. Cleaning and disinfection must be performed after every patient encounter . 5.1 Assess equipment and work surfaces for significant visible body - substances soiling; where found the vehi cle and/or equipment should be exchanged for a pool unit or pool equipment. 5.2 Where there is no significant visible soiling, perform cleaning and disinfection on all non - critical equipment used and working surfaces. This includes, but is not limited to, the following items : 5.2.1 Monitor - defibrillator and attachments 5.2.2 Stethoscopes and other diagnostic equipment 5.2.3 Immobil
ization equipment 5.2.4 Stretcher and cabinet handles

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 72 6. Exchanging of equipment or vehicle should be done when there is significant visible soiling with body - substances . 6.1 If at HGH , exchange the ambulance with pool ambulance unit . 6.2 If near Hub 1 , exchange with a pool ambulance unit at the main stores . 6.3 At other h ubs exchange with a pool unit at that specific h ub . 7. References: 7.1 Cleaning and Disinfection and S ter ilization CL 7261 7.2 Infection Control in Housekeeping Department CL 7242 7.3 Infection Control in A mbulance S ervices CL 9041

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 73 SOP 2.3.A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.3.A M AINTENANCE AND C LEANING OF HMCAS R ESPONSE AND M EDICATION B AGS Implementation Date 01 June 2023 Purpose To provide guidelines for maintaining and cleaning HMCAS bags, as well as preventing and controlling the spread of microorganisms . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF, HMCAS SUPPO RT SERVICES STAFF, HMCASG PHARMACY STAF F AND SUB - CONTRACTOR S . 1. Definitions: 1.1 Dirty/Contaminated Bag : Bag that has been used for patient care and is visibly unclean but is not soiled with blood and/or body fluids . 1.2 Soiled / Contaminated Bag : Bag that is soiled, stained, or soaked with blood and/or other body fluids, as well as other potentially infectious materials . 2. S torage of Bags in HMCAS Stores: 2.1 Clean bags shall be kept in a dust bag and stored on a clean shelf, away from direct sunlight and possible sources of contamination . 2.2 Shelves used to store clean bags shall be free of dust and vermin . 3. Procedure /Process: 3.1 Contents of the Bag : 3.1.1 All HMCAS staff

are responsible for preparing the HMCAS bag s according to the attached checklist, with any additional items added as per patient requirements . 3.1.2 HMCAS staff shall replace all medical supplies that have been used on a daily basis and as needed . 4. Cleaning of Bags: 4.1 Routine decontamination of Bags 4.1.1 Social cleaning of HMCAS Bags: 4.1.1.1 The outer surface of the bag shall be cleaned after each use with HMC - approved disinfectant wipes . 4.1.1.2 Hand hygiene should be performed prior to cleaning the inside of bag . 4.1.1.3 The bag’s inner pouches shall be kept clean at all times; end - u sers shall clean the bag's interior pouch using HMC - approved disinfectant wipes during routine checks and as needed . 4.1.1.4 Following manufacturer’s recommendations, all equipment shall be cleaned after each use and prior to being placed in the bag . 4.1.1.5 Prior to rest ocking medications, pharmacy staff shall clean the outer surface and inner pouches of the bag using HMC - approved disinfectant wipes . 5. Cleaning of Soiled/Contaminated Bags: 5.1 Washable Medication Bags : 5.1.1 Unused medications shall be removed by the end user and pla ced in an orange bag before being return
ed to the pharmacy .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 74 5.1.2 The end user shall return dirty and/or soiled medication bags in an orange bag for manual washing . 5.2 Controlled Medication Cases : 5.2.1 The end user shall place the whole dirty and/or soiled bags without removing their controlled medications in an orange bag and return them to the pharmacy . 5.3 Other w ashable Bags : 5.3.1 Packs and equipment shall be placed in an orange bag and returned to the Stores . 5.3.2 Dirty and/or soiled bags shall be placed in an orange bag and surrendered for manual washing . 5.4 Non - washable Bags : 5.4.1 Packs and equipment shall be placed in an orange bag and returned to the Store . 5.4.2 Non - washable bags that are dirty and/or soiled shall be placed in a yellow bag and discarded . 5.5 Deep Cleaning an d Disinfection of HMCAS Bags : 5.5.1 Cleaning and disinfection shall be performed by a sub - contractor in accordance with the manufacturer’s guidelines for each type . 5.5.2 Sodium hypochlorite or HMC - approved disinfectant solution shall be used for manual decontaminatio n .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 75 SOP 2.3.B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.3.B INFECTION C ONTROL IN HMCAS S UPPLY C HAIN Implementation Date 19 March 2024 Purpose To provide guidelines for maintaining and cleaning HMCAS bags, as well as preventing and controlling the spread of microorganisms . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF, HMCAS SUPPO RT SERVICES STAFF, HMCASG PHARMACY STAF F AND SUB - CONTRACTOR S . 1. Definitions : 1.1 HMC AS Supply Chain (HMCASSC) : The section responsible for packing and re - packing medical supplies. This process involves disassembling, sorting, cleaning, packing, transporting, and distributing the processed items. 1.2 HMC AS Warehouse (HMC ASW) – Area where materials are received, stored, and processed before distribution to customers. This includes, but is not limited to: 1.2.1 HMCAS Stores in the hubs and facilities . 1.2.2 Other on - site facilities (for example, pop - up supply outlets for events, conference s, and drive - thru initiatives) . 1.3 Outsourced Staff – A contract employee who has training and expertise on a

certain work - related task or role. 1.4 Cleaning – The removal of visible dust, soil, or any other foreign material. 1.5 Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) – A specialized equipment worn by staff for protection. 1.6 First - in, First - out (FIFO) – An asset - management and valuation method in which the assets produced or acquired first are sold, used or disposed of first. 1.7 First - Expiry - First - Out (FEFO) – The product whose expiration date is due first shall move out of the storage first and the rest shall follow in the same order. 1.8 Shelf life – The amount of time that a product can be stored and still be considered safe and effective for use, the length o f time a material may be stored without deterioration, and the length of time it remains usable. 1.9 Pre - pack Kits – Pre - assembled medical consumable items. 1.10 Reprocessing – The cleaning, disinfecting, and repacking of an item that was not used on a patient but has not had its packing breached or damaged . 2. Responsibilities : 2.1 HMC AS Infection Prevention and Control Committee - responsible for reviewing, revising, and approving the policy. 2.2 HMC AS Operational Staff - responsible for implementing the po

licy. 2.3 HMC AS Supp ly Chain Staff - responsible for disseminating, educating and implementing the policy. 2.4 Outsourced HMCAS Supply Chain Staff - responsible for implementing the policy. 2.5 Production Supervisors – responsible for overseeing the proper delivery, collection, and tr ansportation of returned items in the hubs and other on - site facilities . 3. Physical Design of the Supply Chain : 3.1 The HMCASSC Warehouse shall be divided into the following areas: 3.1.1 Receiving Area for returned items

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 76 3.1.1.1 Initial sorting 3.1.2 Sorting Area 3.1.3 Cleaning/Decontami nation Area 3.1.4 Packaging Area 3.1.5 Storage Area 3.1.6 Distribution Area 3.2 Each work area shall be identified with a signage specifying the type of work conducted and its corresponding step within the process. 3.3 Work tables shall be made of nonporous materials (e.g., stainless steel). 3.4 Separate work areas shall be provided for processing clean and dirty items. 3.5 Safety measures (e.g., eye wash station) shall be available for staff use. 3.6 Hand washing facilities shall be easily accessible in the sorting and decontamination areas. 3.7 Alcohol hand rubs shall be readily available . 4. Procedure/Process : 4.1 Standard Precautions : 4.1.1 All supplies used for patient care shall be considered high - risk for potentially infectious material and shall be handled following standard precautions. 4.1.2 The common occupational injuries that occur in the supply chain include, but are not limited to: 4.1.2.1 Exposure to Blood and Body Fluids 4.1.2.2 Exposure to hazardous materials and chemicals . 4.1.3 Staff exposure to blood and body fluids shall be

managed accordin g to CL 7251 Occupational Exposure to Blood and Body Fluids . 4.1.4 Hands shall be washed thoroughly, or alcohol hand rub shall be used if hands are not visibly soiled (Refer to Policy CL7241 Hand Hygiene). 4.2 Collection and Handling of Opened and Unused Pre - pack Ki ts and Other Medical Consumable Items: 4.2.1 HMCAS Operational Staff/End - User 4.2.1.1 Partially used or opened pre - pack kits shall not be discarded unless they are soiled. 4.2.1.2 End - users shall conduct an initial risk assessment of used and opened pre - packs prior to returnin g them to the Stores: 4.2.1.2.1 Contaminated items shall not be stored in the same bag as clean and unused medical items. 4.2.1.2.2 Promptly discard contaminated items in accordance with waste management procedures (Refer to CL 7249 Management of Infectious Waste). 4.2.1.2.3 Clean, unused, or damaged pre - pack items shall be placed in designated collection boxes in the Stores for re - packing . 4.2.1.2.4 Collection boxes shall be labelled and stored in a clean area, away from exposure to dust and direct sunlight. 4.2.1.2.5 Opened packages must be ins pected by the concerned user. Promptly discard items if there are dou
bts regarding their integrity including, but not limited to:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 77 4.2.1.2.5.1 Tears 4.2.1.2.5.2 Holes in packages 4.2.1.2.5.3 Water/liquid marks 4.2.1.2.5.4 Item is visibly soiled 4.2.1.2.6 If the safety seal is breached or damaged, the end - user shal l return the pre - pack items to the Stores for replacement. 4.3 Transportation of Opened and Unused Prepack Kits and other Medical Consumable Items: 4.3.1 The assigned staff shall be responsible for collecting returned pre - pack items from the Stores and other on - site facilities. 4.3.2 Containers used for collecting returned items shall be labelled clearly as ‘for used pre - packs only’. 4.3.3 Storekeepers shall ensure that collection boxes are lined with transparent or white plastic bags to facilitate the transfer of returned items and prevent the contamination of said items. 4.3.4 Returned items shall be sent to the HMCASSC Warehouse in a transparent or white plastic bag and placed in closed container tightly knotted. 4.3.5 Unused pre - pack items shall be transported in a clean vehicle. 4.3.6 All it ems transported to the H MC ASSC Warehouse shall have a trolley dedicated for transporting returned items. This trolley shall be cl

eaned and washed along with the transporting vehicle in the ambulance wash bay at the end of each shift and when visibly dirty in between usage. 4.4 Sorting of Opened and Unused Prepack Kits and other Medical Consumable Items: 4.4.1 Sharps containers, infectious waste bins, and general waste bins shall be provided in the initial sorting area. 4.4.2 Staff assigned in the sorting areas shall: 4.4.2.1 Obse rve hand hygiene before and after wearing proper PPE. 4.4.2.2 Wear appropriate PPE and follow standard precautions while handling returned medical items. 4.4.2.3 Remove and properly discard PPE before leaving the work area. 4.4.2.4 Returned items shall be inspected for the following but not limited to: 4.4.2.4.1 Cleanliness 4.4.2.4.2 Integrity 4.4.2.4.3 Presence of tears in packaging 4.4.2.4.4 Discoloration and watermarks 4.4.2.4.5 Expiration date not discernible 4.4.3 Items found with compromised integrity during the inspection process shall be promptly discarded if contaminated, adhering to the established waste management policy (Refer to CL 7249 Management of Infectious Waste ). 4.4.4 Containers used for collecting and sorting returned items shall be cleaned and disinfected in b

etween usage. 4.4.5 Conduct an initial risk assess ment of opened and unused pre - pack items: 4.4.5.1 Promptly discard contaminated items in accordance with waste management procedures (Refer to CL 7249 Management of Infectious Waste).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 78 4.4.5.2 Damaged, non - contaminated items (for example, opened but unused syringes, O2 kit s, cannula, etc) can be cleaned and sent to the Training department for teaching purposes. 4.5 Cleaning of Work Areas: 4.5.1 The cleaning/decontamination area shall be separated by a physical barrier from other work areas. 4.5.2 Observe hand hygiene before and after wea ring proper PPE. 4.5.3 Wear appropriate PPE and follow standard precautions while handling returned medical items. 4.5.4 Cleaning shall be conducted using hospital approved disinfectant. 4.5.5 All warehouse staff, in cooperation with the outsourced housekeeping staff, shall observe proper housekeeping to ensure that: 4.5.5.1 Risk of fire is mitigated by proper disposal of waste. 4.5.5.2 Damaged or unused pallets and cartons are immediately removed if considered to be not part of the active operations. 4.5.5.3 Transportation carts shall be disinfect ed after each use. 4.5.5.4 Cleaning of potential dust collecting areas (for example, air conditioning vents, filters, and shelves) shall be scheduled. 4.6 Repacking of Medical Items: 4.6.1 Ensure that returned and unuse

d medical items are clean and dry prior to repacking. 4.6.2 P re - pack kits shall be clearly labelled with the following: 4.6.2.1 Name of item 4.6.2.2 Item Code and Lot No. 4.6.2.3 Production Date 4.6.2.4 Expiration Date 4.6.3 Where a pre - pack kit consists of items with differing expiration dates, the package label shall reflect the earliest expiration d ate among the included items. 4.7 Storage of Prepack Kits and other Medical Consumable Items in the HMCASSC Warehouse and Sub - stores (Hubs and Spokes): 4.7.1 Storage area specification and maintenance should be as follows: 4.7.1.1 Maintained at a temperature no higher than 25°C (75ºF) and relative humidity of 30 - 70%. Temperature and humidity readings should be recorded at least once daily. 4.7.1.2 Exterior shipping cartons s hall not be brought into the storage area. 4.7.1.3 Floors and shelving racks shall be cleaned as necessary by housekeeping staff. 4.7.1.4 Storage shelves should be labeled to maintain adequate inventories of clean and sterile products. 4.7.1.5 Medical items shall not be kept near sinks or stored under plumbing valves and traps. 4.7.1.6 All medical items shall be stored 8 - 10 inches from the floor and 18
- 20 inches from the ceiling. 4.7.1.7 Packed items shall be placed in properly labeled containers. 4.7.1.8 The storekeeper shall do the following:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 79 4.7.1.8.1 Ensure th at the integrity of the package is not compromised. 4.7.1.8.2 Ensure the proper storage of clean and sterile items: 4.7.1.8.2.1 Clean and sterile items shall not be crushed or grouped with rubber bands. 4.7.1.8.2.2 All packed items shall be preferably stored on shelves, in a dedicated cont ainer with labels. 4.7.1.8.2.3 Clean medical items shall preferably be stored in transparent plastic bags for additional protection. 4.7.1.8.2.4 Expiration dates of the stored sterile items shall be checked and recorded on a monthly basis. 4.7.1.8.2.5 Medical supplies shall be arranged to fa cilitate stock rotation and arranged according to the principles, “first in, first out” (FIFO) and First - Expiry - First - Out (FEFO). 4.7.1.9 The Production Supervisor shall maintain stock levels in accordance with the maximum and minimum levels designated by the Ope rations Officer. 4.8 Single - Use Disposable Items: 4.8.1 Single - use medical items shall not be re - sterilized as the re - sterilization processes may compromise the safe function (Refer to CL 7278 Management of Single Use Device and Expired Supplies). 4.8.2

Items that have e xpired or have been recalled from circulation due to manufacturer issues and are intended for teaching purposes shall be: 4.8.2.1 Decontaminated 4.8.2.2 Stored in a designated area 4.8.2.3 Labeled clearly as “for training use only” 4.9 Distribution of Clean Medical Items: 4.9.1 A dedicate d trolley shall be used for clean and sterile items. This trolley shall be cleaned at the beginning and end of each shift and when visibly dirty between usage. 4.9.2 All clean and sterile supplies shall be placed in covered or enclosed carts with solid bottom shelves and arranged in a manner that prevents crushing, bending, or compressing enclosed items. 4.9.3 A vehicle shall be dedicated solely for the transportation of medical items: 4.9.3.1 Vehicles shall be cleaned at the beginning and end of each shift, and when visibly dirty between usage. 4.9.3.2 Containers used for collecting and transporting returned and unused pre - pack kits and other medical consumable items shall not be stored directly on the vehicle’s flooring. Staff shall utilize plastic pallets or shelving racks to elevate said items. 4.9.3.3 Vehicles shall be kept at an appropriate temperature whenever possible b

y ensuring that the air conditioning system is switched on during loading and transportation. 4.9.4 Container Systems shall be designed to: 4.9.4.1 Prevent compromise of steri lity such as protection from moisture/ condensation, insects, vermin, dust and dirt.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 80 4.9.4.2 Facilitate easy loading, unloading, and removal of items without contamination. 4.9.4.3 The container shall be of an appropriate size relative to the items and shall be secured in the vehicle for transportation. 4.9.4.4 Environments with high humidity or extreme temperature changes shall be considered and mitigated when transporting medical items to reduce any risk of mishandling. 4.10 Transportation and Handling of Dirty/Used Items for Washing and Disinfection 4.10.1 Contaminated items shall include, but not be limited to the following: 4.10.1.1 Combi - Carrier 4.10.1.2 Backboards 4.10.1.3 Scoops 4.10.1.4 Stretcher and safety belts 4.10.1.5 Bags 4.10.1.6 Lifepak 15 4.10.1.7 Other immobilization devices such spider straps and head blocks . 4.10.2 If the orange bag can accommodate the item, contaminated items shall be placed in the orange bag. 4.10.3 The assigned staff shall wear proper PPE such as gloves and gown when handling contaminated items. 4.10.4 A dedicated vehicle shall be used for transporting contaminated items: 4.10.4.1 Clean item s shall not be transported in the same vehicle as contaminated items.

4.10.4.2 Deep cleaning shall be done on vehicles at the end of each shift, or when there is a visible splash, spillage, or contamination of the vehicle. 4.11 Waste Disposal: 4.11.1 All waste shall be managed according to Waste Management Policy CL 7249 Management of Infectious Waste. 4.12 Elements of a Recall System: 4.12.1 HMCASSC shall adhere to system processes for recalling items in the presence of evidence of risks, such as contamination, manufacturer failure, etc. 4.13 References: 4.13.1 HMC Occupational Exposure to Blood and Body Fluids Policy CL 7251 4.13.2 HMC Hand Hygiene Policy CL 7241 4.13.3 HMC Management of Single Use Device and Expired Supplies Policy CL 7278 4.13.4 HMC Management of Infectious Waste Policy CL 7249

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 81 SOP 2.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Production Procedure 2.4 DEEP CLEANING OF PATIENT TRANSPORTIN G VEHICLES Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure that patient transporting vehicles 1 are deep cleaned appropriately and safely on a routine basis. Responsibility SUPPORT SERVICES STA FF 1. Deep cleaning may only be performed in the dedicated wash bay at AS - hubs by trained housekeeping staff or trained ambulance attendants. 2. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): 2.1 The following personal protective equipment should be worn: 2.1.1 Tyvek coveralls 2.1.2 Heavy duty gloves 2.1.3 Safety goggles 2.1.4 Ordinary mask 2.1.5 Waterproof boots 2.2 Following deep cleaning, disposable PPE must be discarded in a black bin and non - disposable PPE should be washed by the end - user with sodium hypochlorite or HMC - approved cleaning solutions. 2.3 PPE may be worn for multiple vehicle deep cleans if a break is not being taken between vehicles. 2.3.1 Bottled water may be consumed while wearing PPE, but gloves must be removed and hands thoroughly washe

d prior to drinking. 2.3.2 Food should not be consumed while in PPE. 3. Deep Cleaning Tools and Products: 3.1 HMC - approved disinfectant solutions such as Surfanios and sodium hypochlor ite are to be used for manual decontamination. 3.1.1 Sodium hypochlorite solution concentration should be used as per HMC policy, as follows: 3.1.1.1 For routine surface cleaning and small blood spills use 0.05% (500 ppm). 3.1.1.2 For large blood and body fluid spills use 0.5% (5000 ppm). 3.1.2 Any remaining solution should be discarded after 24 hours. 3.2 Mops: 3.2.1 Should only be washed with HMC - approved disinfectant solution, never just with water, and kept dry when not in use. 3.2.2 Mop heads should be replaced once per week, on a Sunday mo rning. 3.2.3 Old mop heads must be discarded in a yellow bin. 1 Patient transporting vehicles are Alpha, PTS, Bariatric and 4x4 ambulances, and the “green busses”

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 82 3.2.4 Mop handles should be washed with HMC - approved disinfectant at the end of shift. 3.3 Hydrogen Peroxide Disinfection Machine : 3.3.1 Should only be used according to manufacturer’s instructions by staff trained i n its use. 3.3.2 A notice must be placed on the doors of any vehicle undergoing Hydrogen Peroxide disinfection. 3.3.3 Hydrogen Peroxide Disinfection Machine should be cleaned after each use. 4. Frequency of Deep Cleaning of Patient - carrying Vehicles: 4.1 Routine deep cleanin g of patient - carrying vehicles shall be conducted on a monthly basis, specifically every 30 days, to ensure optimal hygiene and safety standards. 4.2 Immediate deep cleaning is required: 4.2.1 Following the transportation of any cases involving emerging or re - emerg ing infectious diseases or outbreaks. 4.2.2 Presence of significant blood and body fluid spills. 4.2.3 As per Infection Control risk assessment and recommendations. 4.3 Any vehicle that fails the Adenosine Triphosphate (ATP) test after deep cleaning must be re - cleaned and disinfected until it meets passing criteria for the ATP test. 4.4 Deep cleaning staff must do

cument all vehicles that have been cleaned, ensuring effective tracking and accountability. 5. Cleaning and Disinfection Process: 5.1 Wipe down the interior surfaces of the patient compartment and driver’s cab with HMC - approved disinfectant solution. HMC - approved disinfectant solutions, such as Surfanios and sodium hypochlorite, should be used as per HMC policy, as follows: 5.1.1 For routine surface cleaning and small blood spills use 0.05% (500 ppm). 5.1.2 For large blood and body fluid spills use 0.5% (5000 ppm). 5.1.3 This includes: 5.1.3.1 Removing and cleaning stretchers and equipment. 5.1.3.2 Moving supplies so cabinet interiors can be cleaned. 5.1.3.3 Ceilings, walls, and surfaces. 5.1.3.4 Webbing straps, seatbe lts, and response bags. 5.2 Clean any visible body substance - soiling with soap and water, then use the disinfectant solution. 5.3 Floors are mopped with HMC - approved disinfectant solution. 5.4 The electrical machines should be cleaned in a dedicated place according to manufacturer’s instruction. 5.5 Replace all equipment and supplies in the vehicle, according to checklist. 6. Sodium Hypochlorite Disinfection : 6.1 When disinfecting surfaces and equipment with

sodium hypochlorite, ensure a minimum contact time of 10 min utes before wiping. 6.2 After disinfection, use towels soaked in water to wipe off any remaining solution. This prevents surface damage and discoloration.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 83 6.3 If HMC - approved disinfectant wipes are not available, use disposable towels soaked with Sodium Hypochlori te 0.05% solution to wipe electronic devices. 6.4 Avoid direct spraying on electronic devices, including the driver’s compartment: 6.5 Apply solution to a cloth or towel, then gently wipe surfaces. 6.6 Ensure the towels are not excessively wet to prevent damage to t he devices. 7. Disinfection with Surfanios Solution: 7.1 Follow manufacturer’s instructions for preparing solutions. 7.2 When disinfecting surfaces and equipment with Surfanios Solution, ensure a minimum contact time of 5 minutes before wiping. 7.3 Avoid direct spraying on electronic devices, including the driver’s compartment: 7.3.1 Apply solution to a cloth or towel, then gently wipe surfaces. 7.3.2 Ensure the towels are not excessively wet to prevent damage to the devices. 8. Hydrogen Peroxide Disinfection: 8.1 Hydrogen Peroxide disinfection is preferred after the deep cleaning process; however, this step can be omitted if the machine is unavailable due to maintenance or stock of consumables. 8.1.1 Open all cabinets and lockers and the interior

window. 8.1.2 Ensure all surfaces are dried. 8.1.3 Ret urn the cleaned medical equipment and bags inside the vehicle, except the linen. 8.1.4 Close all windows and doors. 8.1.5 Place the machine in patient compartment of ambulance and start program. 8.1.6 Keep vehicle closed for 30 minutes after starting the disinfection proces s. 9. Clean the exterior of the vehicle with powdered soap, soft brooms or cloths, and a hose pipe or high - pressure cleaner. 10. The exterior of the vehicle may be cleaned while the Hydrogen Peroxide disinfection machine is underway. 11. Do ATP testing to check the e ffectiveness of cleaning. 12. Complete and submit vehicle deep clean report. 13. References: 13.1 Cleaning and Disinfection and Sterilization CL 7261 13.2 Infection Control in Housekeeping Department CL 7242 13.3 Infection Control in Ambulance Service CL 9041

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 84 SOP 2.5 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Group Procedure 2.5 MANAGEMENT OF OCCUPA TIONAL EXPOSURE TO B LOOD AND BODY FLUIDS Implementation Date 23 June 2024 Purpose To provide guidance on the actions to be taken by staff who have been exposed to blood or body fluids. Responsibility ALL HMCAS GROUP STAFF 1. Blood and body fluid exposure includes contact with blood and/or body fluids, whether an infectious disease is suspected or not, and includes any of: 1.1 Pa renteral/percutaneous exposure (i.e. needle sticks, contaminated sharp items, human bites ) . 1.2 Mucous membrane exposure (i.e. splashes into the eyes or mouth) . 1.3 Cutaneous exposure (i.e. exposure to non - intact skin). 2. This SOP does not replace but rather compl i ments HMC Policy CL7251 – Management of Occupational Exposures to Blood and Body Fluids. 3. Immediate Actions on Exposure by Employee : 3.1 Immediately wash/irrigate wounds and skin sites that have been in contact with blood or body fluids with water and/or soap and water irrespective if there are cuts or abrasions present (avoid

alcohol - based products). 3.2 Apply a sterile dressing as necessary and apply pressure through the dressing if bleeding is still occurring. 3.3 Do not squeeze or rub the in jury site. 3.4 Exposed mucous membranes and eyes should be flushed thoroughly with water (remove contact lenses if worn). 3.5 If blood or body fluids are splashed in the mouth, spit them out and then rinse the mouth thoroughly with water. 3.6 If clothing is contaminat ed, remove clothing and shower if necessary. 4. Employee Reporting of Exposure Events : 4.1 The exposed employee shall inform their direct supervisor immediately after exposure. 4.2 The exposed employee shall report to the Emergency Department of the hospital where th e patient is being transported. 4.3 If staff reported to HGH/ED, the Romeo on duty will then liaise with the ED Head Nurse (55847803)/ED Nurse Lead (55814439) to facilitate the process of management . 4.4 The Romeo and all immediate supervisors shall inform the ASG Infection Control Practitioner and relay the source patient’s health card number. 4.5 The employee may alternatively report immediately to the Staff Medical Center during working hours (0700 hours to 1500 hours), such as i

n the case where the patient was not transported to an ED. 4.6 It is the responsibility of the exposed employee to follow any post - exposure management and treatment recommendations made by the treating physician and ASG Infection Control Practitioner and to attend all follow - up appointments.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 85 4.7 The employee shall complete an OVA and Blood and Body Fluids Exposure Form as soon as reasonably possible after the exposure. 4.8 The Quality Department shall forward the OVA and Blood and Body Fluids Exposure Forms to the ASG Infection Control Practitioner. 4.9 T he exposed employee shall report to the Staff Medical Center the next day for follow - up. 5. References : 5.1 HMC Policy 7251 - Management of Occupational Exposures to Blood and Body Fluids. 5.2 CDC Guidance for Evaluating Health - Care Personnel for Hepatitis B Virus P rotection and for Administering Post Exposure Management. 5.3 World Health Organization (WHO), Recommendations on Exposure t o Blood and Body Fluids, Chap 8.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 86 SOP 2.6 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.6 OXYGEN MANAGEMENT BY AMBULANCE SERVICES Implementation Date 15 May 201 8 Purpose Management, control, and storage of oxygen gases contained in pressurized cylinders . Responsibility HMCAS OPERATIONAL AN D SUPPORT PERSONNEL 1. Regulations Storing Oxygen and Pressurized Cylinders: 1.1 Oxygen is a colourless, odourless medical gas which is supplied in pressurized cylinders and is considered hazardous by the OSHA Hazard Communication Standard (29 CFR 1910.1200). 1.2 Care is needed when handling and using compressed medical oxygen cylinders. 1.3 Compressed oxygen cylinder potential hazards and precautions: 1.3.1 May ca use or intensify fire, Oxidizer 1.3.2 Contains gas under pressure, may explode if heated. 1.3.3 Store away from combustible materials 1.3.4 Keep valves and fittings free from grease and oil . 1.3.5 In case of fire: Stop leak, if safe to do so. 1.3.6 Store in well ventilated area 1.4 Rupture d cylinders may rocket 1.5 All pressurized cylinders are to be routinely inspected. 1.6 Th

e end user must carry out a physical cylinder inspection of integrity before and after use. 1.7 Pin index valve(s) integrity check must be carried out regularly. 1.8 All damaged and suspected damaged cylinders in storage must be reported immediately to line management. 1.9 Standard oxygen cylinder colour orientation is standardised white Ratio 1:5 black cylinders, or green Ratio 1:5 silver cylinders. 2. Types of Oxygen Cylinders, Refilling, Collection and Distribution : 2.1 All new cylinders are GCE Integrated Valve with the following sizes: 2.1.1 CD Luxfer – 2 Liter 2.1.2 HX Luxfer – 10 Liter 2.2 All old cylinder’s sizes are the following: 2.2.1 Size D (ALCAN) – 2 Liter 2.2.2 Size M60 (ALCAN) – 10 Liter 2.2.3 Size M6 (ALCAN) – 1 Liter (Bike) 2.2.4 L45X (Luxfer) – 4.7 Liter (LifeFlight) 2.2.5 MOST 30 - A1 – 17,000L (MIR) 2.3 The refilling, collection, and distribution of oxygen to all required Ambulance Service hub locations are carried out by the third - party contractor.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 87 3. HMCAS Oxygen Storage Area/Cabinet : 3.1 The oxygen storage area/cabinets are supervised daily by the Support Service Team to maintain availability of oxygen for immediate deployment within the Ambulance Service operations. 3.2 All HMCAS oxygen storage area/cabinet must m aintain a standardised maximum and minimum quantity of full and empty oxygen cylinders. 3.2.1 The temporary storage area at Bu Garn has a maximum quantity of: 3.2.1.1 40 x HX Luxfer 10 Liter and 3.2.1.2 48 x CD Luxfer 2 Liter cylinders 3.2.2 The oxygen storage cabinets at hub locat ions always maintain measurable cylinder quantities against the actual requirements of the current operations as required. 3.3 The oxygen storage area/cabinets must contain appropriate securing devices to ensure the stability of free - standing cylinders. 3.4 The o xygen storage area/cabinets have the appropriate racking to secure non - free - standing cylinders. 3.5 All full and empty cylinders are clearly separated and defined. 3.5.1 Empty cylinders must be placed in RED - labelled area. 3.5.2 Full cylinders must be positioned in GREEN - labelled area. 3.6 Available oxy

gen storage cabinets are to be utilised where appropriate securing devices in specific storage facilities are not available. 3.7 The oxygen storage cabinets should be filled only to their maximum capacity. Overstocking storage cabin ets is strictly prohibited. 3.8 When taking cylinder from the oxygen storage area/cabinet, ensure that entire tag is present. 3.8.1 All new cylinders : 3.8.1.1 Full cylinder is sealed with shrink plastic wrap and the ENTIRE tag is attached. 3.8.1.2 Empty cylinder is unsealed, has NO tag and/or EMPTY tag is attached. 3.8.1.3 If cylinder is found UNSEALED, consider cylinder is empty thus NOT to be taken. 3.8.2 All old cylinders : 3.8.2.1 Full cylinder has the ENTIRE tag attached. 3.8.2.2 Empty cylinder has NO tag and/or EMPTY tag is attached. 3.8.2.3 If cylinder is found WITHOUT TAG, consider cylinder is empty thus NOT to be taken. 3.9 The oxygen storage area/cabinet must be easy to access/egress with emergency access. 3.10 The oxygen storage area/cabinet is to be well ventilated and have adequate lighting. 3.11 The oxygen storage area/cabinet is to be kept clean and tidy all times. 3.12 No smoking or naked flame within the vicinity of the oxygen storage

area/cabinet. 3.13 No oils or fuel or lubricants store in and proximity of the oxygen storage area/cabinet. 3.14 HMCAS oxygen storage area/cabin et are to be marked with appropriate and approved signage. 3.15 The oxygen storage area/cabinet must comply with HMC corporate fire regulations.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 88 4. HMCAS EMERGENCY GUIDE – CYLINDER MAINTENANCE : 4.1 In case of fire, follow the normal evacuation procedures and ensure th at emergency services are informed that medical oxygen cylinders are in the premises. 4.2 Use fire extinguisher suitable for type of surrounding fire, if only trained to do so. 4.3 Oxygen equipment should be isolated from potential sources of combustion. 4.4 Move cylinders/containers from fire area if you can without risk. 4.5 Evacuate the immediate surrounding area. Isolate up to 800 metres radius. 4.6 Shelter behind building to avoid rocketing pressurized cylinders in a fire risk. 4.7 Always stay away from tanks engulfed in fire. 4.8 Allow compressed oxygen gas to vent under control if cylinder valve is damaged. 5. HMCAS EMERGENCY GUIDE – FIRST AID : 5.1 Move injured personnel away and into fresh air. 5.2 Call 999 5.3 Give CPR if required 5.4 Keep warm 5.5 Ensure all personnel are aware of material(s) involved and take precautions to protect .
