A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations and Production Procedure 2.7 . A USE OF SPOKE AND H UB FACILITIES Implementation Date 1 October 2016 Purpose To ensure cleanliness, safety and security of spokes and hubs . Responsibility OPERATIONAL CLINICAL PERSONNEL (E.G. ALP HA, BRAVO, CHARLIE, DELTA, OSCAR, MIKE, AND EVENT STAFF) 1. Access to Spoke and Hub facilities : 1.1 Use PIN 4349 # to open the door. 1.2 Once you enter the spoke, randomly scan for any damage and cleanliness. 1.3 Switch off and lock your vehicle. 1.4 If you notice any garbage, property damage or any variance, immediately notify immediate production/distribution supervisor. 2. Standby responsibilities at Spoke or Hub building : 2.1 Always keep your medication bag, portable radio, and MDT with you when on standby inside the spoke or hub building. 2.2 Plug your MDT and portable radio into wall charger while on standby inside the spoke or hub building. 2.3 Tag your refrigerated food with your name and date . Refrigerated food items more than 24 hours old shall be discarded with

out any notification. 2.4 Maintain cleanliness inside refrigerator and microwave oven. 3. Responsibilities when leaving a hub or spoke building : 3.1 Remember to take along all your personnel belongings, MDT, radio, and medication bag along with you when you are leaving the spoke or hub building. 3.2 Throw all domestic waste into domestic bin located at the hub or outside the spoke. 3.3 If you are the last person leaving the building, close and lock all windows and doors. 4. Safety & Security of the Sp oke and Hub : 4.1 Smoking is strictly Prohibited any HMC Compound and property . ( SOP 2.8 ). 4.2 No unauthorized persons are allowed to enter or remain inside the spoke or hub. 4.3 No unauthorized vehicles are allowed to park in the parking area of spoke and hub facilities. 4.4 Immediately report any violations, variance or concerns to your immediate supervisor (Production, Distribution) and the NCC via Production - 1 radio channel.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 93 SOP 2.7.B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Critical Care Services (CCS) and Scheduled Ambulance Service (SAS) Procedure 2.7.B HELIPAD BASE S ECURITY Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure Helipad Base Security and Safety . Responsibility ALL CRITICAL CARE SE RVICES AND SCHEDULED AMBULANCE SERVICE STAFF 1. Background : 1.1 Critical Care Services and Scheduled Ambulance Service use the offices and storage space located at the Hamad General Hospital rooftop helipad. Security of the helipad is to be afforded the highest priority for the following reasons: 1.1.1 Use of helipad by LifeFlight, QEAF, and other helicopters. 1.1.2 Physical security of Retrieval Services capital equipment items and consumables. 1.1.3 Integrity of information security and data protection. 2. Open Up Procedure: 2.1 At the start of each working day, the following actions are to be taken by the first person reporting for duty. 2.1.1 A visual check is to be conducted of the area around the offices to rule out the presence of any suspect per sonnel, objects, or packages. 2.1.2 Once unlocked

, keys must be secured in the lockable key press on the wall outside the office. 3. Working Day: 3.1 During the course of the working day, the following actions are to be taken as necessary: 3.1.1 Visitors to offices are requi red to show their ID. Contractors and non - HMCAS personnel are to be escorted at all times. 3.1.2 Suspect personnel must be challenged. If a person has no valid reason to be at the Helipad, security must be called to escort them from the premises. 3.1.3 Unnecessary mov ement outside while aircraft are landing and taking off is to be avoided. 3.1.4 Access to the Store will be by a combination lock. The store is to be locked when unoccupied. 4. Close Down : 4.1 The following action is to be taken at the end of each working day and when the compound is to be unoccupied for any period of time: 4.1.1 All lights are to be switched off. 4.1.2 All computer systems are to be locked or shut down. 4.1.3 Portable electric appliances (e.g. kettles, etc.) are to be unplugged. 4.1.4 Air - conditioning units are to be swi tched off in offices. In areas where temperature sensitive items are stored, air - conditioning must be set to LOW fan speed with a minimum air temperatur
e of 22 degrees Celsius. 4.1.5 All internal doors are to be closed and external doors are to be locked.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 94 4.1.6 Keys a re to be placed in the key press and secured using the combination lock. 5. Combination Codes: 5.1 Combination codes for the key press and the store are to be changed every three months. 6. Security Breach : 6.1 Suspected security breaches of any nature are to be reporte d immediately to the following personnel: 6.1.1 Operations Manager 6.1.2 Site Security Manager 6.1.3 Senior Operations Manager

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 95 SOP 2 . 7.C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Group Procedure 2.7.C T RAKA K EY M ANAGEMENT S YSTEM Implementation Date 10 October 2023 Purpose To give clear guidance on the Usage of Traka Key Management System for HMCAS . Responsibility ALL HMCAS GROUP STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 This Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) outlines the steps for the proper usage of the Traka Key Management System to ensure the secure and efficient management of keys and assets within the HMCAS Group. 2. Scope : 2.1 This SOP applies to all authorized personnel who are responsible for using the Traka Key Management System within the HMCAS Group . 3. Responsibility : 3.1 Support Control Center : 3.1.1 Responsible for the overall management and administration of the Traka system . 3.1.2 Ensure that all authorized personnel receive proper training on how to use the Traka Key Ma nagement System with biometric access effectively and securely . 3.2 Location Supervisor : 3.2.1 Responsible for overall usage of the Traka System . 3.2.2 Should send details of the new staff to Control Support

Center for giving access to the Hub . 3.2.3 For coordinating with Suppo rt Control Center for reporting malfunction or issues regarding the Traka System key cabinet . 3.2.4 For safe keeping and operation of Emergency Keys . 3.3 Authorized Users: Individuals who are granted access to keys or assets through the Traka system . 3.4 All the above U sers: Responsible for informing Support Control Center (SCC) by calling +974 4032 8300 or by emailing ( SS - SupportControlCenter@hamad.qa ) for Emergency Key Access and for informing malfunction or issue with Traka Key Cabinet . 4. Procedure : 4.1 Accessing the Traka Key Management System with Biometric Authentication : Log in to the Traka system using biometric authentication procedure, such as fingerprint scan, to open the door and pick the keys that are in green light indicator. The red light indicator is not accessible until the access provided by the SSC .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 96 4.2 New Key Request Using Biometric Authentication : 4.2.1 To request access to a key, Please fill Key Access Form and send it to ( SS - SupportControlCenter@hamad.qa ) with details of mentioning the reason for request and Hub details . 4.2.2 The Team will decide and give the access to the required Key in the online Traka System . 4.2.3 After the system is Synced, you can a ccess the Key Cabinet and get the required key . 4.2.4 For Emergency please call SCC +974 4032 8300 . 4.3 Key Retrieval Using Biometric Authentication : 4.3.1 Once the request is approved by the system or authorized personnel, go to the designated Traka cabinet or location . 4.3.2 Authenticate yourself using biometric access to open the cabinet and access the requested key indicated by Green light . 4.3.3 Close the Cabinet door and it will get locked automatically. If there is alarm sound it means the cabinet is not closed properly, close the door till the alarm sound is off . 4.4 Using the Key : 4.4.1 Ensure that the key is used responsibly and only for the approved purposes . 4.4.2 Do not do unauthorized handover of the key. Only Ambulance Vehicle

Keys can be handed over to the next crew dur ing the shift change . 4.4.3 Do not share access or leave keys unattended . 4.5 Returning the Key : 4.5.1 Log in to the Traka system using biometric authentication procedure, such as fingerprint scan, to open the door . 4.5.2 Return the key to its designated slot in the Traka cabin et promptly where the red light is blinking . 4.5.3 Confirm the return within the Traka touch system . 4.5.4 Close the Cabinet door and it will get locked automatically. If there is alarm sound it means the cabinet is not closed properly, close the door and hold it till the alarm sound is off . 4.6 Reporting Issues : 4.6.1 Report any issues, malfunctions, or damage to keys or the biometric access system immediately to the immediate Supervisor/Support Control Center (SCC) by calling +974 4032 8300 or by emailing ( SS - SupportControlCenter@hamad.qa ). 4.6.2 Do not attempt to repair or modify the Traka key Cabinet System . 4.6.3 Location Supervisor need to communicate with Support Control Center for any issues, malfunctions, or d amage to keys or the biometric access system with the Key Cabinet . 4.7 User Access Management : 4.7.1 New User Creation and Access i
s provided by Support Control Center. The Location Supervisor must send the details of the new employee via email to Support Control Ce nter ( SS - SupportControlCenter@hamad.qa ).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 97 4.7.2 Location Supervisor to assist in registration of biometric access to the new staff at the Key Cabinet . 4.7.3 The Support Control Center is responsible for managing u ser access rights, including adding, modifying, or removing users as necessary in coordination with communication from Location Supervisor . 4.7.4 Regularly review, update and remove resigned/terminated staff user access permissions as needed . 4.8 Auditing and Monito ring : 4.8.1 The Traka system logs all key transactions and biometric access events . 4.8.2 System Logs and transactions should be reviewed monthly to ensure compliance and security . 4.8.3 User Access to be reviewed quarterly . 4.8.4 Conduct internal audits to verify that the system is being used correctly . 4.9 Emergency Procedures: In case of emergencies or system failures, contact Location Supervisor and in case not available, contact Support Control Center (SCC) by calling +974 4032 8300 and follow the instructions as below : 4.9.1 Call Loca tion Supervisor/SCC and report the emergency for accessing the Emergency Key for the Cabinet and Keys . 4.9.2 Answer all the questions and fo

llow instructions from SCC . 4.9.3 Get the pin access from SCC to open the MASTER LOCK and get the Key A (Cabinet Emergency Key) and Key B (iFob Release Emergency Key ). 4.9.4 With Key A – Open the Key Cabinet

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 98 4.9.5 With Key B - Turn on the Emergency Key Release 4.9.6 Remove the designated iFob Key for use 4.9.7 Turn off the Emergency Key Release with Key B, close and lock the Key Cabinet Door with Key A . 4.9.8 Return the key to MASTER LOCK 4.9.9 Call back location Supervisor or SCC and mention key used . 4.9.10 Support Control Center should change the pin of the MASTER LOCK . 4.10 Documentation : 4.10.1 SCC to Maintain records of all key transactions, user access changes, biome tric access events and system maintenance activities . 4.11 Compliance : 4.11.1 All Staff to adhere to SOP and any variance will lead to disciplinary actions . 4.12 Review and Update : 4.12.1 Periodically review and update this SOP to ensure it remains accurate and aligned with organizational needs and changes in the Traka system .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 99 SOP 2.8 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.8 USE OF TOBACCO P RODUCTS Implementation Date 1 October 2016 Purpose To maintain a healthy, clean, and safe environment for all personnel, patients, and visitors . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Smoking of tobacco products (inclusive of but not limited to cigarettes and electronic/smokeless cigarettes): 1.1 Do not smoke anywhere inside or outside all HMC premises. 1.2 Do not smoke in any HMCAS vehicle, in both driver and patient/passenger compartments. 1.3 Do not smoke within 15 meters of any HMCAS vehicle. 1.4 Do not smoke/chew/use toba cco products during any patient care activities. 1.5 Always present yourself in a professional manner, free of any tobacco odour during all patient care activities. 2. PLEASE NOTE: 2.1 ‘ No Smoking’ Policy SA 1052 - “this policy is formulated for all Hamad Medical Cor poration (HMC) staff in conformance with the Qatar Law No. (20) of the year 2002 on the Control of Tobacco and its derivatives, and to provide a safe environment to protect pat

ients, visitors and staff from Active and Passive Smoking Exposure.” 2.2 Anyone foun d to be smoking on - site will be subject to a full and thorough investigation, potentially leading to formal disciplinary action.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 100 SOP 2.9 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.9 FITNESS TO OPERA TE Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide guidance on fitness to operate within the HMC Ambulance Service . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Commitment : 1.1 HMCAS committed to providing a safe and healthy working environment for staff and patients by actively working to optimize staff health and fitness. 1.2 HMCAS personnel have a professional responsibility to actively engage with health and fitness guidance. 1.3 HMCAS is committed to ensuring staff recover fully following a period of sick leave by ensuring that staff return to work within their required working hours (for a defined period) without completing additional over - time. 2. Medical Fitness: 2.1 All staff must undergo a medical assessment at the commencement of their employment with HMCAS, including: 2.1.1 HIV test. 2.1.2 Hepatitis B test. 2.1.3 Tuberculosis test. 2.1.4 Chest X Ray. 2.1.5 Vital signs (including blood pressure). 2.2 In the absence of the provision of a list of no

tifiable diseases by the Qatar Ministry of Public Health, staff are required to report the following occurrences to Staff Clinic: 2.2.1 Exposure/Potential Exposure to: Diseases notifiable under UK Law . 2.2.2 Exposure/Potential Exposure to: Infectious diseases listed as notifiable by the US Centre for Disease Control . 2.2.3 Any illness or injury that may compromise their fitness to operate on a temporary or permanent basis. 2.3 Pregnant staff may continue their frontline operational duties until unable to safely continue their patient facing role at which time they will continue their duties in a non - frontlin e role unless booked on light duty or booked off work by a doctor earlier. 3. Diet and Physical Fitness: 3.1 Whilst there is no mandatory fitness requirement for service HMCAS, all personnel are strongly encouraged to maintain a level of personal fitness that wil l allow them to fulfil their work obligations without unnecessary fatigue or injury and permit optimum longevity. This should incorporate a combination of cardiovascular training (e.g. jogging, walking, cycling), resistance training (e.g. weight training a nd bodyweight exercises) and flexibility (e.g. yoga/Pilates

). Swimming offers a good combination of resistance and Cardio Vascular training with the benefit of reduced impact on bones and joints. 3.2 Personnel are strongly encouraged to maintain a healthy weig ht through making appropriate dietary choices in combination with exercise.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 101 3.3 As healthcare professionals, staff should avoid smoking tobacco. (See Policy SA 1052). 4. Aeromedical Considerations: 4.1 Aeromedical personnel should not donate blood within 72 hours of commencing flying duties. 4.2 Aeromedical personnel must not participate in scuba diving within 24 hours of commencing flying duties. 4.3 No member of staff may undertake aeromedical duties if, for any reason, their physical or mental state is such that it could e ndanger the safety of the aircraft, crew, or patient(s). 4.4 Aeromedical personnel are duty bound to report concerns about the mental or physical fitness of themselves or others to the appropriate line manager. 4.5 Any aeromedical personnel deemed unfit to perform flying duties should report the same to the appropriate line manager. 4.6 Individuals prone to airsickness should discuss the matter with the appropriate line manager before undertaking any flight duties. 5. Sickness: 5.1 Staff who are suffering from any illness or injury that is contagious or potentially debilitating are not fit for patient - facing duties and should report sick to the Staff Clinic or an alter

native medical facility. A sick note/medical report is to be obtai ned and submitted to the appropriate line manager in accordance with HMC Sick Leave Procedure . 6. Returning to duty post a period of sick leave: 6.1 Following a period of sick leave HMCAS staff are not permitted to work overtime or provide event cover (unless the event is part of their scheduled hours) for a minimum of 10 days unless approved by the appropriate seni or operations manager. 6.2 Senior Operational Managers (and above) may extend this period of ‘no overtime and/or no events’ based on staff sickness record. 6.3 Following a period of sick leave of 3 months or more SOP 7.7 ‘returnin g to service following a break in service’ is to be followed .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 102 SOP 2.10 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.10 AMBULANCE SERVI CE FATIGUE MANAGEMEN T Implementation Date 1 October 2016 Purpose To provide guidance on management of fatigue for Ambulance Service personnel . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Commitment and Responsibility : 1.1 HMCAS is committed to maintaining a safe and healthy working environment for staff and patients through risk management activities, including active management of fatigue. 1.2 To comply with the HMC Health and Safety Mission: ‘Protect Life and Asset from Occupational Health and Safety hazards – professio nally and with minimum business interruption.’ 1.3 This SOP applies to all elements of Ambulance Service. 1.4 All personnel, including staff from partner organizations have a professional responsibility to actively engage with Fatigue Management activities. 1.5 Staff is duty bound to report when they are fatigued for any reason. 1.6 Self - declaration of being ‘Not Fit for Duty’ due to fatigue demonstrates professional maturity, integrity, strengt

h of character and indicates active participation in risk man agement and promotion of patient safety. 1.7 Operational line managers are responsible for monitoring staff compliance and the provision of mitigating options. 1.8 On long distance Transfers and Aeromedical Mission Team leaders are responsible for monitoring team members for signs of fatigue and for the implementation of fatigue management activities, including allocation of rest time for napping, eating, and drinking. 2. Aim: 2.1 To establish and maintain a proactive system that minimizes fatigue - related risks to the hea lth, safety, and welfare of HMCAS staff and patients. 3. Fatigue and Work: 3.1 Line managers will take active measures to ensure that personnel arrive fit for duty at all times. This includes roster planning that provides adequate opportunity for recovery sleep b etween shifts and during missions (in the case of aeromedical operations). The following mandatory post - flight rest periods will apply: 3.1.1 24 hours and 2 local nights following flight between UK/Europe and Qatar. 3.1.2 24 hours and 2 local nights following flight b etween Far East/Thailand and Qatar. 3.1.3 48 hours and 2 local nights fol

lowing flight between Qatar and United States of America. 3.1.4 Where flights consist of more than one sector, a period of 24 hours rest will apply for each sector to a maximum of 72 hours. 3.1.5 An ad ditional 24 hours in the country might be approved for certain cases. 3.1.6 Deviation from the matrix will be on a case - by - case basis and requires approval from the Operations Manager - Critical Care Services.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 103 3.2 The following behaviour is indicative of fatigue and should prompt the initiation of countermeasures: 3.2.1 Nodding off/dozing (microsleep) 3.2.2 Hunger and/or thirst 3.2.3 Moodiness, irritability, or anger 3.2.4 Lack of concentration 3.2.5 Eye rubbing 3.2.6 Boredom 3.2.7 Yawning, ‘heavy’ eyelids 3.2.8 Fidgeting 3.2.9 Lack of coordination 3.2.10 Unusually quiet and withdrawn 4. Countermeasures: 4.1 The only cure for fatigue is sleep. The following countermeasures can be implemented: 4.1.1 ‘Power Nap’/’Combat Nap’. A short period of sleep less than 30 minutes duration. This provides sufficient time for restorative sleep withou t entering REM (deep) sleep. This sleep phase has the highest awakening threshold. Power naps are usually followed by 5 - 20 minutes of sleep inertia, characterized by confusion and sluggishness. This factor must be considered by team leaders/managers. 4.1.2 Quick fix countermeasures including: 4.1.2.1 Keeping active and mobile, avoiding sitting for long periods. 4.1.2.2 Completion of manual tasks 4.1.2.3 Switching between tasks to avoid monotony 4.1.2.4 Consumption of 150 - 200mg caffeine (2

- 3 cups coffee) 4.1.2.5 Static exercise (e.g. jogging on the spot ) 4.1.2.6 Avoidance of large/carbohydrate - rich meals 4.1.3 Quick fix countermeasures are not a substitute for sleep. 5. Active Management: 5.1 The following principles apply to ground operations: 5.1.1 Shifts may not exceed 12 hours (plus travel period to rural dispatch locations) w ithout authorization from line manager. 5.1.2 Rest periods between shifts shall not be less than 10 hours. 5.1.3 No other duties (meetings, training etc.) should be undertaken during the 10 hours preceding duty. 5.1.4 Any fatigue break taken during rostered hours will be co nsidered duty hours. 5.1.5 Where operational activity precludes the scope for a full 10 - hour rest period, line managers must take action to mitigate the risk, including use of replacement staff, negotiating later start times etc. Where this is not possible, the risk must be escalated to the appropriate Senior Operations Manager.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 104 5.1.6 Individual staff members are responsible for ensuring that they obtain optimum rest during the minimum 10 hours rest period prior to duty. 5.2 The following principles apply to aeromedical operations: 5.2.1 Team composition will be configured to permit in - mission rest periods as per Crewing Matrix. Deviation from Crewing Matrix may only be authorized by SOM or his designee. 5.2.2 Shifts may not exceed 14 hours without authorization from an O perations Manager - Critical Care Service. 5.2.3 The rest period prior to commencement of aeromedical duties shall not be less than 10 hours. 5.2.4 No other duties shall be undertaken (meetings, training etc.) in the 10 hours preceding duty. 5.2.5 Any fatigue break taken du ring rostered hours will be considered duty hours. 5.2.6 Where operational activity precludes the scope for a full 10 - hour rest period, Team Leaders, in conjunction with Operations Manager - Critical Care Service must take action to mitigate the risk, including the use of replacement staff, negotiating later start times etc. Where this is not possible, the risk must be escalated to Senior Operations M

anager - Critical Care Service or his designee. 5.2.7 Individual staff members are responsible for ensuring that they ob tain optimum rest during the 10 hours rest period prior to duty. 5.2.8 Aeromedical personnel may not work for more than 6 consecutive shifts without at least one full day off, or work for more than 60 hours per week. 5.2.9 Aeromedical personnel are duty bound to repor t perceived fatigue affecting other team members. 5.2.10 Aeromedical personnel reserve the right to refuse a mission if they believe that crew, patient, or aircraft safety is compromised as a result of fatigue, following discussion with the Operations Manager - C ritical Care Service. 5.2.11 Circumstances likely to break the rules highlighted at 5.2.1 and 5.2.7 must be actively managed by Operations Manager - Critical Care Service, options include crew swaps and pre - positioning of replacement crews. 5.2.12 Roster scheduling must take into account on - call, actual and potential hours worked to anticipate fatigue management issues. 5.2.13 On call personnel activated for a mission will commence a period of 10 hours rest from the point at which they return home after mission completion. 6. Emer gent
Situations: 6.1 Fatigue management guidelines may only be broken in exceptional circumstances. Authority to breach fatigue management may only be granted by an Assistant Executive Director .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 105 SOP 2.11 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 2.11 WORKPLACE VIOLE NCE Implementation Date 31 January 2017 Purpose To maintain a healthy and safe violence - free environment for all personnel, patients, and visitors. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Definitions : 1.1 Workplace Violence: Behaviour in which an employee, former employee, patients or visitor to a workplace inflicts or threatens to inflict damage to property, serious harm, injury, or death to others at the workplace. Behaviours may include any act or threat of physical violen ce, harassment, intimidation, or other threatening disruptive behaviour, as well as threats and verbal abuse . 1.2 Threat : The implication or expression of intent to inflict physical harm or actions that a reasonable person would interpret as a threat to physic al safety or property. 1.3 Intimidation : Making others afraid or fearful through threatening behaviour . 2. Procedure following a violent incident at the workplace : 2.1 HMCAS does not tolerate ANY behaviour , implied or actual, t

hat constitutes workplace violence. 2.2 In t he event of a violent act/altercation, immediately inform your direct supervisor. 2.3 If the offender is the supervisor him/herself, inform the appropriate section manager. 2.4 Before beginning any inquiry, Senior Management and the Human Resources Department shal l be consulted. 2.5 The Senior Operations Manager (SOM) or the Department Assistant Executive Director (AED) shall be responsible for coordinating a response to the incident. 2.6 In instances that involve emergency situations or criminal activity, update your supe rvisor, and proceed to the hospital for medical attention, as appropriate. These incidents shall be referred to the Police Department for assessment and, if necessary, an inquiry. 2.7 In instances when it is not appropriate to refer an incident to the Police Department, the SOM/AED/ED and the Human Resources Director shall attend to the case and make a recommendation regarding the need for an inquiry. 2.8 The HMCAS Quality section shall coordinate all inquiry processes. 2.9 All parties involved in the reported incident shall be interviewed by the relevant supervisor/manager/AED or ED as appropriate, in order to develo

p a true and accurate account of the incident. 2.10 All inquiries shall be conducted in a manner that demonstrates objectivity, fairness, and a concern for confidentiality. 2.11 Inquiries may lead to further actions, depending on liabilities and legal implications. 2.12 Disciplinary actions may involve a warning (verbal or written), workplace transfer, or termination of employment, depending on the seriousness of the case.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 106 SOP 2.12 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operatio ns Procedure 2.12 HAND HYGIENE Implementation Date 23 June 2024 Purpose To ensure that Ambulance staff practicing hand hygiene to prevent and control the risk of transmission of infections . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL STAF F 1. DEFINITION: 1.1 Hand H ygiene : 1.1.1 A general term that applies to any action of hand cleansing which includes routine handwashing, antiseptic hand washing, antiseptic hand rubbing and surgical hand antisepsis. 1.1.2 Hand Hygiene is considered as the single most important procedure to prevent the spread of pathogens in the healthcare settings. 1.2 Hand Hygiene Practices: 1.2.1 Hand washing 1.2.1.1 Routine/Social hand wash: Washing hands with plain soap and water. 1.2.1.2 Antiseptic/Hygienic hand wash: Washing hands with antimicrobial soap and water . 1.2.1.3 Antiseptic Hand Rubbing (or Hand rubbing) – Cleaning hands with the use of a waterless antiseptic hand rub. 1.3 Hand Hygiene Products : 1.3.1 Liquid Soap – Soap that do not contain antimicrobial agents, or that contain

very low concentrations of antimicrobial agents effective solely as preservatives. 1.3.2 Alcohol - based (hand) rub (Waterless antiseptic agent) – An antiseptic agent that does not require the use of water. It comes in different formulations e.g. liquids, gels, or foam. 1.3.3 Antimicrobial soap (Antiseptic agent) – An antimicrobial substance which reduces and/or inhibits the growth of microorganisms on living tissues. 2. PROCEDURE : 2.1 Hand Hygiene Practices in the Scene and Ambulances : 2.1.1 Readily accessible alcohol - based hand rubs shoul d be available in all ambulance vehicles access to all facilities to perform hand washing if needed. 2.1.2 The staff shall perform hand rubbing If hands are not visibly soiled but are contaminated. 2.1.3 When hands are visibly dirty or contaminated with visible materi al or visibly soiled with blood or other body fluids use the disinfect wipes to remove the visible fluids and use hand rubbing and hand washing is required after hand over the patient in the hospital. 2.2 Hand Hygiene Technique with Alcohol - Based Formulations : (Duration of the entire procedure: 20 - 30 seconds).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 107 2.2.1 Apply a palm full of the product in a cupped hand, covering all surfaces (or follow the manufacturer's specification). 2.2.2 Rub hands palm to palm, right palm over left dorsum with interlaced fingers and vice versa. 2.2.3 Palm to palm with fingers interlaced 2.2.4 Backs of fingers to opposing palms with fingers interlocked 2.2.5 Rotational rubbing of left thumb clasped in right palm and vice versa 2.2.6 Rotational rubbing, backwards and forwards with clasped fingers of right hand in l eft palm and vice versa 2.2.7 Once dry, your hands are safe 2.3 Hand Hygiene Practices in Healthcare Facilities, Hubs, and Spokes : 2.3.1 The staff shall perform hand rubbing if hands are not visibly soiled but are contaminated. 2.3.2 The staff shall perform hand washing: 2.3.2.1 When h ands are visibly dirty or contaminated with visible material, or visibly soiled with blood or other body fluids, or if exposure to potential spore - forming organisms (e.g. C. difficile) is strongly suspected or proven. 2.3.2.2 After using the toilet, after changin g diapers, or cleaning up a child who has used the toilet. 2.3.2.3 Before eating 2.3

.2.4 After blowing your nose, coughing, or sneezing 2.3.2.5 When caring for patient with diarrhea or suspected exposure to Clostridium difficile; Bacillus anthracis (Anthrax). 2.3.2.6 After touching garbage 2.4 Handwashing Procedure (Duration of the entire procedure: 40 - 60 seconds). 2.4.1 Select a comfortable water temperature 2.4.2 Wet hands with running water 2.4.3 Apply plain soap or antimicrobial soap to cover all surfaces of the hands 2.4.4 Rub hands palm to palm 2.4.5 R ight palm over left dorsum with interlaced fingers and vice versa 2.4.6 Palm to palm with fingers interlaced 2.4.7 Backs of fingers to opposing palms with fingers interlaced 2.4.8 Rotational rubbing of thumbs clasped in right palm and vice versa 2.4.9 Rotational rubbing backwards and forwards with clasped fingers of right hand in left palm and vice versa. 2.4.10 Rinse the hands under running water to remove all soap residues keeping the hands lower than the forearms. 2.4.11 Dry (pat dry) hands with paper towel 2.4.12 Use paper towel to turn off the fa ucet or use your elbow/foot to turn off the faucet to prevent recontamination whichever is applicable.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 108 2.4.13 Dispose paper towels into a foot operated bin 3. General Indications for Handwashing and Hand Antisepsis : 3.1 All staff members are required to adhere to the WHO’s ‘Your 5 Moments of Hand Hygiene guidelines when providing patient care, demonstrating proficiency in hand hygiene techniques. Before having direct contact with patients. 3.1.1 Before donning gloves for an invasive procedure (i.e. starting an IV). 3.1.2 After con tact with body fluids or excretions, mucous. 3.1.3 After contact with a patient's (i.e. taking a blood pressure, lifting patient) 3.1.4 After teaching patient sounding; (including medical equipment) 3.1.5 After removing gloves 4. Monitoring of Hand Hygiene Practices : 4.1 The Romeo - on - duty shall be responsible for monitoring hand hygiene practices among staff and shall report staff compliance to the Infection Control Head on a monthly basis. 4.2 The monitoring process will focus on the 4th Moment of Hand Hygiene, which occurs after patient contact and the removal of gloves. 4.3 Staff monitoring shall take place in specific facilities, namely the Emergency Departments and Dialysis units.

4.4 Staff compliance to hand hygiene protocols shall be communicated to the IC Committee and displayed on television screens for staff awareness. 5. References : 5.1 WHO Save Lives Clean Your Hands: Guide to Implementation of the WHO Multimodal Hand Hygiene Improvement Strategy Revised August 2009 5.2 CDC, Hand Hygiene Guidelines http://www.cdc.gov/handhygiene/ 5.3 HMC Hand Hygiene Policy 2024

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 114 SOP 3.1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.1 NCC CALL ROUTING Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To direct the routing of emergency and non - emergency calls . • To direct the routing of calls for Patient Transport Services units requesting for Emergency Assistance. Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF 1. HMC Ambulance Service (HMCAS) utilizes the NCC Emergency Services “999” network and acts as a secondary Public Safety Answering Point (PSAP) for Medical Emergency calls. 2. Medical Emergency Calls are answered first by 999 Operators then routed to HMCAS Call Takers : 2.1 For direct ‘999’ ambulance requests, the HMCAS Call Taker should : 2.1.1 Immediately identify the address of the emergency. 2.1.2 Route the Call for Service (CFS) to the HMCAS Dispatcher without dela y. 2.1.3 Process the call through ProQA and update the dispatcher. 2.2 For calls where the ‘999’ caller does not directly request an ambulance: 2.2.1 The 999 operator will route the CFS to the AS dispatcher (and log the HMCAS as a participant to the

primary agency being d ispatched) 2.2.2 The HMCAS dispatcher should : 2.2.2.1 Dispatch an Alpha Unit (priority one/ two based on participant classification). 2.2.2.2 Notify the supervisor 2.2.2.3 Task a call - taker to call - back the caller 2.2.2.4 Override the dispatch priority based on the new ProQA update. 2.2.3 The HMCAS Call Taker should : 2.2.3.1 Process the case in the original CFS or link with new CFS, if the voice call has been received and is still online. 2.2.3.2 Make all attempts to call - back the caller if the caller voice not received. 2.2.3.3 Process the case through ProQA and upda te the dispatcher. 2.2.3.4 Log a note in the free text area of the original CFS about the call - back attempts if the caller does not answer the call - back. 3. Dedicated Ambulance Service Hotline (44797955) : 3.1 HMC Call Takers shall directly answer the dedicated hotline, u sually accessed by HMC facilities and other parties requiring the ambulance service. In these situations, the 999 operators are bypassed. 3.2 The call taker should open a CFS manually only if it is not automatically created in the NJM CAD system. 3.3 The number of hotlines is limited to the number of call takers ava
ilable to answer the calls. Other incoming calls will be kept on hold until a call taker becomes available.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 115 4. Misrouted Emergency calls : 4.1 Identify the location of the caller and verify the information. 4.2 Quic kly identify non - ambulance calls inadvertently received. 4.3 Ask the caller not to hang up and inform him/her about the call transfer. 4.4 Transfer the identified calls to the appropriate agency, using the most expedient method. 4.5 Verify that the call was transferr ed successfully. 4.6 Notify the shift supervisor when misrouted calls are received . Call Routing 1 A 999 call is placed by an end user within network. 2 Ooredoo delivers the emergency call to the Voice Communications System (VCS) switch at the Primary Public Safe t y Answering Point (PSAP) at the National Command Centre (NCC). 3 The NCC VCS delivers the call with its Automatic Number Identification (ANI) and Automatic Location Identification (ALI) to the 999 operator. The caller’s number and address are displayed on the NCC operator’s Life - X monitor and in NJM CAD. 4 999 operator triage the call and transfer it to the appropriate agency. 5 The Call is then rerouted back to VCS to be routed to HMC Ambulance Service Call Taker. 6 • The HMC AS cal

l taker receives the call and obtains & confirms caller’s number and the incident’s location as well the call details: • The HMC AS call taker may stay online with the caller or can terminate the call as determined in the Medical Priority Disp atch System. • After termination of the call, the Ambulance Service Medical Dispatcher or case controller may call back the caller at any time to gather more information about the address/patient/situation. • If the line disconnects or the caller hangs up bef ore the termination of the interrogation, the Ambulance Service operator must call back the caller • When multi agency response is required HMC AS call taker or case controller will notify the required agency via NJM and Life - X console. • When appropriate and possible, the HMC AS Call taker shall transfer the call to the operator of the concerned agency.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 117 SOP 3.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.2 NCC CALL TAKING Implementation Date 09 March 2020 Purpose To ensure effective and efficient receipt and handling of all emergency calls within the HMCAS Communications Centre within the NCC . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF (SPECIFICALLY C ALL TAKERS) 1. GENERAL CALL TAKING STANDARDS : 1.1 Prompt Answering: Answer all emergency calls arriving at the Ambulance Service consoles within 5 to 10 seconds. 1.2 Order of Answering Priority: Call takers must prioritize answering of calls; first ‘999’ line, then Hotlines, then administrative lines. 1.3 Answer all ‘999’ & Hotlines calls with the phrase, “Ambulance Service, this is (ASMD's name) What is the address of the emergency?” " خدمة ا إلسعاف, معاك (فالن/فالنة) ما هو عنوان حالة ا لطوارئ ؟ " 1.4 Answer non - emergency line calls with the phrase “Ambulance Ser vice, this is (ASMD's name) how may I help you?” “ خدمة اإلسعاف , معاك فالن / فالنة , كيف أقدر أساعدك ؟ ” 1.5 For non - emergency calls received on emergency lines, Refer to SO

P 3.14 and do not transfer the call to an administrative line. 1.6 Consider calls reported by HMCAS clinical or administrative staff via the phone to be routine emergency calls and be processed in same way as other 999 calls. 1.7 Interrogation and information gathering: Obtain the following minimum basic information from the caller: 1.7.1 The address or exact location of the incident/patient. 1.7.2 The call back number. 1.7.3 The type of emergency. 1.7.4 Medical Priority Scripted Ques tions and Dispatch Life Support (DLS) Instructions when appropriate using ProQA ®. 1.7.5 All required information for the Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD ). 2. STEP - BY - STEP SUMMARY OF TAKING A 999 CALL : Once the call prompts in Life - X, immediately answer the call within 5 seconds The Call taker will open a CFS manually if NJM does not open it automatically. Identify and verify the exact location (zone, street, building number), telephone number and document it accurately in NJM. Route the CFS for “ initial dispatch” as “Priority1” Lights and siren. Open ProQA to process the questioning and identify the Chief Complaint and the Dispatch Code. Make sure NJM is updated with ProQA code . Coordinate wit
h the dispatcher to upgrade or downgrade the responding unit as per ProQA code.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 118 Continue with the ProQA process to complete the questioning and provide Post - Dispatch/Pre - arrival Instructions when appropriate . Terminate the call as per the MPDS protocol. Fill in the administrative fields in NJM. Follow up the case with the dispatcher until the arrival of the crew and call back the caller if needed. 3. ADDRESS IDENTIFICATION & VERIFICATION : To provide all Emergency Medical Dispatchers with approved procedures and practices for obtaining and verifying accurate and complete address and phone number. 3.1 Address Identification & Verification : 3.1.1 A ddress identification and verification is a crucial ste p to ensure that the emergency responders are dispatched to the correct location. 3.1.2 All emergency phone lines will always be answered in the following manner: “Ambulance Service, this is (ASMD's name) What is the address of the emergency?” This may be repeat ed in Arabic. 3.1.3 The call - taker shall consider the address method volunteered initially by the caller as the preferred method for the address identification, especially when it is clear and accurate. Call - takers will not prompt

the caller to leave the patient 's side to look for the Qatar Area Referencing System (QARS) number unless the address provided initially doesn’t include enough information to enable the initial dispatch. 3.1.4 The call - taker will enter the address or location provided by the caller into the N JM CAD system using the most accurate information available from the caller (This could be QARS number, a numeric address, intersection, business, landmark, etc.) 3.1.5 Once the call - taker has entered the address/location into the NJM CAD system, he/she will geo - verify the entered address/location by ensuring that the system returns a valid address or location and it matches the initial information entered and obtained from the caller. 3.1.6 After documenting the address in the CAD, the call taker will verify the addre ss (or location) by stating the following: “Please repeat the address/location for verification.” The call - taker will then verify the address details entered in the CAD against the second statement of the caller and shall correct mistakes if any. 3.1.7 The locat ion of the event may be available from the Automatic Identification Location (ALI) feature in the NJM; the ca

ll taker will check the obtained address from the caller against the displayed location in the NJM. 3.1.8 If the address appearing automatically in the N JM CAD matches the initial address provided by the caller and after asking the address identification question “What’s the address of the emergency?” the call taker will not ask the verification question “Please repeat the address/location for verification ” instead he/she will state to the caller “Your address appears correctly in the map.” However, other details of the address shall be identified and documented properly in the CAD as stated above. 3.2 Unknown/Unclear Address : If the caller is unable to provide a numeric address. 3.2.1 The call - taker will say “Please give me the best location/ nearest landmark you have for the incident.” 3.2.2 Call - taker will notify the Team leader to follow up with him closely.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 119 3.2.3 For all residential (or suspected residential) locations the call - taker will ask “Is this a villa, compound or an apartment?” and correctly enter this information into the CAD system. The call - taker will also ask if the QARS (Qatar Address Referencing System) number is known. 3.2.4 If the QARS number is not available and/or for non - residential locations, the call - taker will obtain all necessary access information, which may include: building name, business name, floor number, apartment, office or suite number, specific entrance instructions and intersection o r street segment (for roadway incidents). 3.2.5 If the caller cannot obtain any of the information above: the call - taker shall advise the caller to give an alternative telephone number of someone who can provide clear address information. 3.2.5.1 When possible and appli cable, advise the caller to find and provide the Blue Plate - Qatar Address Reference System (QARS) in the entrance of the building and provide at least 3 consecutive plate numbers (zone, street, building, & Unit number “if available”). 3.2.5.2 When the QARS numbe r is not availabl

e, advise the caller to describe the directions to get the ambulance to his location. 3.2.5.3 If all attempts fail, advise the caller to send someone to rendezvous with the ambulance at a mutually known location. 3.2.5.4 If necessary, request an alternati ve contact number of someone able to guide the ambulance to the incident location. Keep the first caller on the line while the second caller is called. The call taker may choose one of the two option: 3.2.5.4.1 The initial caller shall be kept online for the call in terrogation and the second caller shall be contacted by another call - taker or the team leader. 3.2.5.4.2 The initial call taker can put the caller on hold and contact the second telephone number given. If no answer, he should go back to the caller and look for other alternative. 3.2.6 The call - taker will seek the assistance of the team leader or the OSCAR any time he has difficulties to identify the exact address. 3.2.7 The official HMCAS WhatsApp number might be used as an alternative to find location in challenging situation. 3.2.8 When address cannot be identified or patients does not know his location, DTL/Oscar NCC should be notified, they should add police in

vestigation and it is required to notify the corresponding agency by phone to endorse the case. 4. PHONE NUMBER VERIFICATION: 4.1 For each call, a call back number should be verified twice by asking the caller “ What is the phone number you are calling from? ” 4.2 If the stated number given by the caller matches the Automatic Number Identification (ANI) information in the NJM, this serves as double verification . 4.3 If the ANI information does not match the number given by the caller, the call taker will not repeat the number back, but instead, verify the number by asking the caller again to repeat the phone number.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 120 4.4 The call taker shall make a cross check verification that the telephone number is typed correctly in the NJM. 5. TRANSFERRING EMERGENCY CALLS TO ANOTHER AGENCY (NON - AMBULANCE REQUESTS) : 5.1 The call taker shall follow the below steps: 5.1.1 Advise the caller to call 999 again and request the appropriate agency. 5.1.2 Notify the DTL/OSCAR. 5.1.3 Add comments in the CFS that the caller is not requesting Ambulance services and he/she was advised to call back 999 and close the CFS. 6. TAKING NON - STANDARD CALLS : 6.1 Abandoned Calls : When the caller discon nects before the can be answered by call taker, call taker should attempt a call back twice. Make additional attempts to contact the caller if there are no other emergency calls in the queue. 6.2 Disconnected Calls : When the caller disconnects after being answ ered by a call taker and before it can be determined if assistance is needed, call taker should attempt a call back twice. Make additional attempts to contact the caller if there are no other emergency calls in the queue. 6.3 Silent calls : When the caller is n ot responding to the call taker and remains

silent, call taker will escalate the case to the DTL/OSCAR who will notify NCC officer of all silent calls for any possible transfer to “992” console, to determine if the caller is attempting to report an emergen cy using a special communications device for hearing impaired individuals. If the call taker hears background noises indicating an emergency is occurring, such as domestic violence or other medical emergencies, the call taker should initiate the appropriat e response and add the relevant agencies to the case. 6.4 Misdialed/ Unintentional Calls : when the caller stays on the 999 line and admits a ‘misdial’ or unintentional call and there is no indication of an emergency situation, no further action required. 6.5 Prank calls : when someone calls 999 for a joke, or calls and deliberately hangs up for fun, call taker will Treat as a real emergency until proven otherwise. Call back the called party and query to determine if further action or an emergency response is require d. Call taker will escalate confirmed Prank calls to the DTL/OSCAR 7. NEW CALLS REPORTED VIA RADIO BY ON - DUTY HMC AMBULANCE PERSONNEL : 7.1 The dispatcher will instruct a call taker to open a new CFS. 7.2

The dispatcher should notify DTL immediately. 7.3 The DTL shall en sure that one call taker is in charge of opening the CFS. 7.4 The call taker will omit ProQA and document in NJM the appropriate classification (Code): 7.4.1 AS10X for Priority 2 7.4.2 AS10Y for Priority 1 7.5 The dispatcher will send extra resources as per the request of the clinical crew on - scene. 8. FOREIGN LANGUAGE CALLS : 8.1 When available, the foreign language translation support service shall be used. 8.2 General Guidelines :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 121 8.2.1 MPDS card sets/ProQA™ Paramount, containing English/Arabic language protocols for Emergency Medical Disp atching, shall be made accessible to all call - taking positions. 8.2.2 The English card sets/Pro QA™ paramount shall be used by ASMDs skilled in the English language for the interrogation of English - speaking callers to the 999 system, and for the provision of PDI ’s and PAI’s. 8.2.3 It is recognized that the standard Arabic language version of the MPDS protocols may not always contain language understood by all persons that speak Arabic, given the cultural and geographic diversity of the Arabic speaking population. Subse quently, ASMD’s with the necessary language skills may rearticulate those words and phrases necessary to facilitate the caller’s understanding of the question or instructions in their own words after the scripted protocol question or instruction has been a ttempted. 8.2.4 Regardless of the language used by the caller, call receiving and dispatch for medical assistance shall be provided in a standardized manner following approved MPDS protocol scripts for 999 caller interrogation and provis

ion of post - dispatch and pre - arrival instructions. 8.2.5 ASMDs with the appropriate language skills must therefore ensure that any translation of the protocol scripts, whether using a foreign - language ProQA or spontaneous translation, reproduces the same meaning, syntax and phrasing as contained in the Arabic/English languages MPDS. 8.2.6 No modification or omission of questions is allowed when providing spontaneous translation. 8.3 Managing calls with language barrier: 8.3.1 Notify the DTL immediately that a language barrier call is in progress. 8.3.2 Attemp t to identify the language of the caller by asking what country they are from or interpretation of their response to various language options. 8.3.3 Attempt to obtain the address by using landmarks, shops, or schools in the area. 8.3.4 The Case Controller or DTL will determine if any call taker on duty is able to speak the caller’s language. If someone is available: 8.3.4.1 The initial call taker shall inform the caller about the transfer and shall use courtesy even with language barrier cases. 8.3.4.2 The call taker shall transfer th e call to the appropriate line extension and shall endorse the case in a reasonable tim

e. 8.3.4.3 The second call taker shall process the case using ProQA and all questions should be translated with an equivalent language. No changes to the questions meaning or or der are tolerated. 8.3.5 If no information can be obtained, no call taker with language skills is available. process ProQA and allocate 32 - B - 4 code (language barrier, no interpreter). 8.3.6 The DTL should contact MOI to determine if their call takers are able to talk in the caller’s language and then transfer the call to the MOI call taker to obtain the correct address and incident details. 8.3.7 The DTL or Case Controller shall obtain an update from the MOI call taker and notify the dispatcher. 8.3.8 Managing Abusive callers : (Refer to SOP 3.20 )

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 122 9. DUPLICATE CALLS: (When several calls have been received on the same incident). 9.1 When more than one call is received for the same incident, the call - taker must complete the Case Entry interrogat ion, and then determine if a complete ProQA interrogation is required, based on the caller’s answer to Case Entry questions. 9.2 The call - taker shall answer every emergency call with the expectation that the caller will be reporting new information not yet rec eived from previous callers, including those cases where an incident record may already exist in the CAD for the address/location provided by the caller and will complete Case Entry according to standard MPDS procedures. 9.3 Before determining a reported event to be a duplicate of an active incident, the ASMD must confirm that three information are strictly similar in the below order: 9.3.1 Address/location is the same as an existing incident already entered in the CAD. 9.3.2 Incident description (Chief Complaint, Mechanis m of Injury). 9.3.3 The caller statement that he called before (within the same time span) for the same incident or another person called ear

lier from a different number. 9.4 When the caller is a third - party caller (not in the immediate vicinity of the event/victim) , and the call - taker determines the event to be a duplicate incident, and no new information is offered by the current caller during the Case Entry interrogation, the ASMD call - taker may terminate the phone call once Case Entry interrogation is completed o nly after ensuring that another call - taker is processing the call with a second party caller. 9.5 If new information is provided by the caller during Case Entry, the call - taker must complete the Key Question interrogation and assign a final coding according to standard procedures. 9.6 If the new coding is a lesser priority than the previous code assigned, the response priority will not be changed. 9.7 If the new coding is a greater priority than the previous code assigned, consideration shall be given to upgrading the response priority. 9.8 When the caller is a first or second - party caller (either a victim/patient or someone with the victim/patient), the ASMD must complete the Key Question interrogation, assign a final coding, and provide DLS instructions according to stand ard procedures. 9.9

If the call taker ascertained that the call is duplicate, he shouldn’t dispatch a new CFS. But he should find the original CFS and update the details (location and ProQA) if provided. 9.10 If the call taker discovers while processing ProQA that the call is duplicate, he should inform the dispatcher and provide him with any update. The dispatcher should reject the duplicated CFS and link it together. 9.11 Confirm that location and incident is the same as an existing incident by different means in NJM. 9.12 Open ProQA and complete Case entry according to standard EMD procedures. 9.13 Update the NJM with any changes and additional information and inform the dispatcher of any required upgrade or downgrading. 9.14 Accurately document contact details of 2nd or 3rd caller in the “COMMENT” section of NJM. 9.15 Should the caller be 2nd party, complete the ProQA process including scripted PDI’s and PAI’s. 9.16 Should the caller be a 3rd party and no new information is given, then the call taker must reassure him/her that an ambulance has already been dispatched to the incident.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 123 9.17 Once the call taker is sure that the caller is reporting an incident that has already been received and any additional pertinent information is obtained, the caller will be advised that the call inform ation has already been reported. These calls should be disconnected as soon as possible in order not to tie up any emergency lines unnecessarily. 10. POST - DISPATCH INSTRUCTIONS (PDIS): 10.1 The call - taker giving PDIs will follow the protocol, giving instructions ap propriate to each individual call, and avoiding free - lance information. 10.2 PDI’s shall be provided to the caller whenever possible and appropriate to do so. 11. PRE - ARRIVAL INSTRUCTIONS (PAIS): 11.1 Pre - arrival instructions should be given verbatim when appropriate an d possible as per the MPDS (i.e. Cardiac Arrest, choking, unconscious, Childbirth…) 11.2 In the event that the caller hangs up before PAIs can be given, the call - taker must make all effort to re - contact the caller. 11.3 Call Management when providing Pre - Arrival Ins tructions: 11.3.1 The ASMD will never ask permission to give instructions. The best way to handle reluctant

callers is to simply start giving the instructions. Most callers expect to take direction from the trained ASMD on the other end of the phone line. 11.3.2 If the caller refuses to follow PAIs, the ASMD will make reasonable efforts to keep the caller online. 11.3.3 If the caller refuses to administer aid, the ASMD will ask if there is someone else there to speak to; if so, the ASMD will attempt instructions again with the new caller. 12. EMERGENCY INTER - FACILITY PATIENT TRANSFERS: 12.1 Treat as a routine 999 call 12.2 If unable to understand medical terminology, then the call must be transferred to another Call Taker capable of handling the call (NOTIFY the CC Emergency/Oscar - NCC). 12.3 Follo w Protocol 33 (PRIORITY - 1) or 37 (PRIORITY - 2) via ProQA. 12.4 Update the dispatcher accordingly with regards to Zulu/Yankee/X - ray status. 12.5 Transfer the call to the CC Emergency via Life - X only if at least one of the below conditions apply: 12.5.1 Special equipment requ ired 12.5.2 CCP requested by the healthcare facility 12.5.3 LifeFlight requested by the healthcare facility 12.5.4 Medication or blood transfusion to be monitored or given during ambulance transport. 12.6 Where ap

plicable, verify successful transfer of the call to CC Emergency, otherwise notify CC Emergency/Oscar to call back the healthcare facility. 12.7 The CC Emergency should update the dispatcher accordingly .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 124 SOP 3.3 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.3 NCC CALL DISPATCHING Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To standardize the HMCAS Communication Centre Dispatch Procedure for emergency cases . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF, SPECIFICALLY CALL DI SPATCHERS 1. General rules for Dispatch: 1.1 The dispatcher receiving the request for assistance shall ascertain the nature of the call, the address and other pertinent information as quickly as possible to permit rapid dispatch. 1.2 Obtain additional information and relay to responding units after initial dispatch. 1.3 Upon receipt, immediately air calls for service in the pending queue. 1.4 During extreme circumstances, always dispatch the highest priority first when multiple calls are in th e pending queue. If calls are of the same priority, dispatch with the principle of first - come first - served. 1.5 One dispatcher is assigned to monitor each operational channel . 2. Dispatch Priority: Units to be dispatched in accordance with the Dispatch Matrix. D ISPATCH PRIORITY

DISPATCH CODE VEHICLES TO DISPATCH ONE ZULU 1 Alpha Unit, 1 Charlie unit, and 1 Delta unit if available, moving with lights and siren. Life Flight to be dispatched if the call is outside Doha. ONE YANKEE 1 Alpha Unit & 1 Delta unit moving with lights and siren. TWO X - RAY 1 Alpha Unit & Delta unit if available moving without lights and siren. THREE Routine Interfacility Transfers 1 Alpha Unit or patient Transport Service Unit (Foxtrot) moving without lights and siren for non - emergency transport reasons 3. Role of the Dispatcher: 3.1 At the beginning of the shift to coordinate with the supervisor to make sure that calls belonging to the assigned Hub are highlighted in LIGHT GREY. Verify own assigned geographical area in the CAD (NJM ). 3.2 Continuously monitor and observe the screen for pending list (screen containing emergency calls in queue for dispatch . 3.3 ACTIVATE emergency calls within five seconds (5) of being routed from the call taker . 3.4 Once a CFS is activated, immediately visualize the CFS in the map. 3.5 Open the unit status screen and scroll to visualize ALL available and relevant HMCAS uni ts close to the incident . 3.6 Initiate the dispatch via the MDT by assig

ning the CLOSEST and most relevant unit to the incident within ten (10) to fifteen (15) seconds and try not to exceed thirty (30) seconds during peaks periods. If the closest unit is on a different radio channel, coordinate with the relevant

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 125 dispatcher to instruct the unit to switch radio channels. The field crew shall respond to the call as per the MDT and will not wait for the confirmation message through the Radio . 3.7 Confirm the dispatch b y calling the unit c all sign twice e.g. "Calling A1.1 - A1.1 , this is Control, Over " . On reply, provide the initial response priority and the location e.g. " A1.1 Priority one in Gharafa: from Shamal RA towards Sheikh Faisal RA, Over . " 3.8 If the ProQA response matrix ( X - ray/Yankee/Zulu code) is available, replace the ‘response priority’ with the ‘response matrix code’ on initial dispatch . 3.9 Inform the crew that “ Refer to the MDT for more information ” . 3.10 Simultaneously monitor the Responding unit’s AVL and u nit status updates in NJM. If the MDT is not available, the dispatcher shall coordinate with the crew vi a the radio and will enter all unit status from his/her NJM console . 4. Dispatcher Information Sharing : 4.1 The communication between to the dispatcher and the field crew shall be kept to the minimum and the unit shall read all CFS updates in the MDT. Necessary information to be given to

the responding units may include but not be limited to the following : 4.1.1 A ddress details not documented in the CAD or if guidance to access the patient is necessary. 4.1.2 Additional address information specific to special situation and explicitly said by the caller should be documented in the Call record and relayed to the responding crew e.g. ‘patient is in the annexure behind the villa…’ 4.1.3 Infection control concerns and risk of Communicable Diseases. 4.1.4 Upgrade or downgrade of the CFS priority 4.1.5 Cancellation of the CFS 4.1.6 All available safety information, including details about a violent situatio n, presence of weapons, hazardous materials, crime scene or dangerous animals, etc..... 4.1.7 For RTAs information are available in the MDT but if possible the dispatcher can repeat them via the radio for general broadcast to alert supervisors units: type of RTA and Mechanism of Injury, number of vehicles involved, number of patients, the presence of any hazard, fire, or explosion. 4.1.8 Update the responding unit if there are drastic changes in the situation, including the patient’s condition or changes in the scene or in the route. 5. Resource allocation : 5.1 Whe

n ProQA® code is available the Dispatcher shall update the response priority and coordinate resources as required i.e. either, upgrade, downgrade, or override ProQA code. 5.2 Assign and deploy extra resources as necessary (in consultation with the control room supervisor) in cases of : 5.2.1 C ases or any situation with one patient who requires more than two paramedics such as Cardiac arrest or poly - trauma patient. 5.2.2 More than one critical victim 5.2.3 Multiple casualties 5.2.4 Safety concerns/high risk situations 5.3 If the first assigned unit is unavailable or not answering the radio call within 30 sec .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 126 5.3.1 Immediately dispatch the next closest available unit. Do not cancel the initial unit from the CFS. 5.3.2 Notify the Distribution Team Leader 5.3.3 Co ntact the initial unit using other alternative methods; call by phone or mobile. If they reply and they accept the CFS then the disaster shall decide, in coordination the DTL, which unit is the closest to continue to the call. 5.3.4 The communications supervisor shall notify the Hub supervisor of the unit with delayed deployment and make necessary escalations and follow - up . 6. Follow up with responding units : 6.1 Follow up the location and status of the responding unit until arrival on scene. 6.2 Document in the “comments” free text area of the CFS record to document all important information and events during response to the scene. 6.3 Monitor the accuracy of MDT user unit status capture by the responding crew or accurately capture the reported “On Scene” time if the MDT is not available. 6.4 Inquire scene/patient information report from the responding crew. 6.5 Confirm the Qatar Early Warning Score of the patient (QEWS) when applicable and accurately document

it in the call record. 6.6 Follow up the unit’s on - scene duration and escalate if needed. 6.7 When units are leaving the scene , and moving to the receiving facility: 6.7.1 Monitor the time once the unit reports (10/20) they are leaving the scene. 6.7.2 Verify and confirm the destination, the priority of transport and the number of patients to be transported. 6.8 Upon arrival at the receiving hospital/facility : 6.8.1 Monitor the reported time in the Call record 6.8.2 Follow up the status of the unit at the facility 6.8.3 Notify the DTL and the HMCAS supervisor located at the receiving facility for units staying more tha n 15 minutes. 6.8.4 Document any applicable information in the call record e.g. the reason for delay. 6.8.5 Review the documentation in the call record, update it, and complete it accurately. 6.9 When the unit reports availability : 6.9.1 Log the reported time in the call record 6.9.2 Log all administrative data, including patient details 6.9.3 Close the call record of the incident 6.9.4 Assess the current standby coverage and immediately dispatch the available unit to the standby point . 7. Requests for HELP/ASSISTANCE from Primary Crew while on scen e : 7.1 Se
e table below for allocation of units when additional crew assistance is requested .

Inform DTL and Clinical Desk. ONE YANKEE Closest Charlie unit (LF if case outside Doha) and Delta unit. Other Alpha unit if required. Inform DTL and Clinical Desk if required . TWO X - RAY Closest Charlie unit (LF if case outside Doha) and Delta unit. Other Alpha unit if required. Inform DTL and Clinical Desk if required . THREE Routine Interfacility Transfers Closest Alpha unit, Delta unit; Charlie unit (LF if case outside Doha). Inform DTL and Clinical Desk if required 7.2 R equests for Scene Safety assistance : 7.2.1 Ac tivate police and/or Civil Defense and inform the Case Controller & DTL about the request as applicab le. 7.2.2 Notify the on - scene unit that the requested assistance is responding when appropriate . 7.3 When Special Scene or patient management assistance is requested . 7.3.1 Inform the DTL/PTL the need to activate the requested units e.g. 2nd unit for

cardiac arrest cases, Bariatric unit, Green Bus, 4X4 Ambulance . 7.3.2 Dispatch the required resources to the incident. 7.4 Unit moving priority 2 or 1 to hospital needing help : 7.4.1 Dispatch the closest available Charlie/Delta/Alpha unit 7.4.2 Update the dispatched unit with the latest patie nt’s status and conditions. 7.4.3 Arrange for a rendezvous point and guide the dispatched unit. 7.4.4 Re - establish transport priority towards hospital as needed. 7.4.5 Inform DTL and Clinical Desk 7.4.6 Record all information in the comments area of the CFS. Information to be doc umented includes but not restricted to: 7.4.6.1 The time when the first unit requested help 7.4.6.2 Reason for requesting help 7.4.6.3 Safety concerns 7.4.6.4 Rendezvous time of both units

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 128 SOP 3.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.4 OBVIOUS DEATH, E XPECTED DEATH & PRE - ARRIVAL CPR INSTRUCTIONS Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To handle obvious death and expected death cases by following the Medical Priority Dispatch System protocol and local procedures approved by the HMCAS Medical Director . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF (SPECIFICALLY C ALL TAKERS AND DISPATCHERS) 1. Protocol 9 (Cardiac or Respiratory Arrest/Death) Obvious Death Criteria : 1.1 Obvious Death: A patient’s condition that can be identified as incompatible with life, after all information has been obtained on the Case Entry Protocol, and Protocol 9 (Cardiac or Respiratory Arrest/Death). 1.1.1 The call taker shall: 1.1.1.1 Determine the patient to be not conscious and not breathing, through proper application of the MPDS, ensuring specific answers to Protocol 9. 1.1.1.2 Ensure that the presence of at least one of the above ‘obvious death’ conditions is unquestionable: 1.1.1.2.1 Cold and stiff in a warm environment 1.1.1.2.2 De

cap itation 1.1.1.2.3 Decomposition 1.1.1.2.4 Incineration 1.1.1.2.5 Non - recent death (confirmed as being greater than six hours). 1.1.1.2.6 Severe injuries obviously incompatible with life. 1.1.1.2.7 Brain matter expelled 1.1.1.2.8 Evisceration of chest or abdominal organs 1.1.1.2.9 Submersion (confirmed as being greater than 6 hours) 1.1.1.3 Not provide resuscitative measures i.e. PAIs for breaths and chest compression in any ‘Obvious Death’ situation. 1.1.1.4 Code all “obvious death” cases as 9 - B - 1 and inform responders of specific obvious death condition. 1.1.1.5 Keep the caller on the line and prov ide emotional support, where applicable and possible. 1.1.2 All other criteria not mentioned under section 1.2.2 that appears in the Obvious Death, will not be approved for use under the HMCAS Medical Director’s authority. 1.2 Expected Death: 1.2.1 Do Not Resuscitate (DNR ) case reported by HMC doctors within HMC facilities where caller reporting terminal illness with DNR order. 1.2.2 Resuscitative measures including PAIs for breaths and chest compressions will not be provided in any ‘Expected Death’ situation.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 129 1.2.3 The call taker mus t get specific answers to all applicable questions on protocol card 9 to arrive at an ‘expected death’ determination. 1.2.4 Code all “expected death” cases as 9 - Omega - 1 1.2.5 If applicable and possible, keep the caller on the line and provide emotional support. 1.3 Cardiac Arrest Pathway Box: 1.3.1 (C Only) Continuous compressions is NOT APPROVED . 1.4 Pre - Arrival Instructions conveyed by Call Takers: 1.4.1 Attempt pre - arrival instructions on all cardiac/respiratory arrest cases where the obvious death and expected death determinants (9 - B - 1 and 9 - Omega - 1) are not applied. 1.4.2 Do not say “Would you like me to tell you how to do CPR?”; simply proceed with PAIs . 1.4.3 If the caller refuses to follow PAIs, make reasonable efforts to convince the caller e.g. say, “The ambulance is on its way, but th is is important to give the patient the best possible chance until it arrives.” Repeat as necessary. 1.4.4 If the caller is unwilling to administer aid, ask if there is someone else you can speak to. 1.4.5 If no one else is available, keep the caller on the line and p rovide emotional

support. 1.4.6 Make it clear that if they change their mind about providing patient care you will tell them exactly what to do. 1.4.7 If the caller mention that a healthcare provider is on scene. 1.4.7.1 If CPR is in progress, the call taker shall reminder the caller to use the AED and provide AED instructions when possible. 1.4.7.2 If not sure that CPR is in progress the call taker shall start the PAIs until he/she confirms that a healthcare provider is doing CPR to the patient. Otherwise continue the PAIs . 1.4.7.3 Add a note to NJM and escalate to the supervisor and remain online for further assistance until ambulance is on scene . 1.4.8 Remain polite and courteous at all times . 2. Protocol 14 (DROWNING/DIVING/SCUBA ACCIDENT) Obvious Death Criteria: 2.1 Obvious Death: The follo wing situation is considered Obvious Death : 2.1.1 Submersion for 6 or greater hours

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 130 SOP 3.5 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.5 FIRST PARTY CALL ER (PATIENT) “NOT FOUND ON ARRIVAL” Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure appropriate intervention in the case of a first - party caller (patient) appearing to be “not found” or “gone” upon arrival at the scene . Responsibility ALL HMCAS COMMUNICATION STAFF 1. Never assume that a first - party caller (patient) “has left” until that has been reasonably established based on the procedure below. 2. If the arriving crews report back to dispatch that nobody appears to be at the address initially given by the caller, the doors and windows are locked, and a call back from dispatch goes unanswered, the following advice should be given to the crew on the scene: 2.1 Make sure the crew are at the correct address 2.2 Knock at the door several times, wh en applicable 2.3 Search around the scene/house/property to make sure there is no other entrance. 2.4 Wait on scene until the police patrol arrives 3. The dispatcher should immediately notify the Shift supervisor of

the situation and inform the relevant call taker to call back the caller (if not available any other call taker shall call back). 4. The call taker shall call back several times on the verified call - back number. For a minimum of 6 times of call - backs (during low call volume period the call taker can call back up to 15 times). 5. The call taker shall verify that any call - back number that is not answered is indeed a correct number . 6. Simultaneously the call taker or the shift supervisor (whoever is available) should notify police by calling the police NCC console and brief them with incident details with clear request of dispatching a police patrol to respond to the scene and search for the patient. 7. DO NOT advise responders to break - in to the scene/house. 8. FIRST PARTY callers classified in the below listed critical det erminant codes, are considered to be vulnerable patients and an extra level of regard is required because they appear to be initially stable, but could later deteriorate, therefore they might warrant a legal break - in by police whenever patient is not found on arrival :
