Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 171 SOP 3.18 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications - Healthcare Coordination Centre Procedure 3.18 CAREMONX CAD SHUTDOWN Implementation Date 09 March 2020 Purpose To ensure appropriate use of backup systems available to manage calls for service and resources in the event CareMonX CAD is not available. Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF 1. Staff are responsible for maintaining familiarization with these resources in the event of an unplanned CareMonX CAD crash or scheduled system down time. 1.1 Scheduled downtime requires coordination with affected stakeholders and pre - notification no less than 48 hours i n advance by the ASIS/VTL. 2. Each employee must routinely review CareMonX backup procedures and resources. 3. Each employee is responsible for soliciting assistance from the case controller or team leader if additional directions are needed. 4. Each employee must ensure they are adequately prepared to follow backup procedures should they become necessary. 5. In the event of a CareMonX failure or scheduled maintenance: 5.1 User must log of

f and restart the system to check if failure is user based or individua l system shutdown. 5.2 The Operation Officer for Communications or the Utilization Team Leader should notify immediately the OUNAK at 44391444 or via email to get a ticket opened immediately. 5.3 The Operations Officer for Communications or the Utilization Team Le ader must immediately notify the On - Call Communications Operations Manager (Silver Communications). 5.3.1 The On - Call Communications Operations Manager (Silver Communications) will be responsible for activating the full Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Plan when: 5.3.1.1 The primary facility providing the service is inaccessible; as a result, technology processing can no longer continue. 5.3.1.2 A major technology component failure has occurred and is preventing technology capabilities. 5.3.1.3 A power failure has occurred, and technology processing cannot continue. 5.3.1.4 Corruption of data has occurred, and data needs to be recompiled, restored, and synchronized. 5.3.1.5 There is a need to migrate or change IT systems or applications for an extended period. 5.3.2 This Disaster Recovery and Busines s Continuity Plan when, plus all the o

ther Disaster Recovery documents listed in Table 1 of the plan, should be stored on - site at Ambulance Service Healthcare Coordination Centre (HCC), Corporate HICT – DR/BC section, and at the primary backup site, the Na tional Command Centre, a secure remote location. 5.4 HCC shift supervisor or most senior officer on duty must notify ASIS team on (5509 3574/5518 4726, 55135126) in accordance with SOP 3.26 once they have notified OUN AK and received a ticket number.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 172 5.4.1 Any major outage or downtime, affecting the Ambulance Service operations will be considered as disaster event. An outage event will be broadly classified depending upon the downtime foreseen. 5.4.2 The downtime (or non - availabili ty of System or physical site) which will be either known (planned) or unknown (unplanned) can be broadly classified as following: 5.4.2.1 Level 1: downtime less than 1 hour; 5.4.2.2 Level 2: downtime greater than 1 hour, but less than 4 hours; 5.4.2.3 Level 3: downtime greater t han 4 hours, this is a major incident and will result into disaster declaration. 5.5 Communications officer shall utilize the Manual Communications Record (available from the UTL desk) to process calls for service and make dispatch assignments. 5.5.1 Any new inciden t received during the shutdown will be numbered as follows: 5.5.1.1 1st incident will take the number YYYYMMDD - 9000 thru to 9399 for PTS cases (Example: The 1st case opened during the shutdown will be: 20180125 - 9000). 5.5.1.1.1 2nd incident will take the number YYYYMMDD - 9001 etc... for PTS cases. Then the numbers will keep adding for each ne

w CFS (opened during the shutdown) until CareMonX is back to operation. 5.5.1.2 1st incident will take the number YYYYMMDD - 9400 thru to 9699 for IFT cases (Example: The 1st case opene d during the shutdown will be: 20180125 - 9400). 5.5.1.3 1st incident will take the number YYYYMMDD - 9700 thru to 9899 for RET cases (Example: The 1st case opened during the shutdown will be: 20180125 - 9700). 5.5.1.4 These numbers during the shutdown will be coordinated by th e on - duty UTL and will be written on the incident number manual - sheet before providing it to the dispatcher. 5.5.1.5 The dispatcher will then verbalize the Shutdown’s incident number to the Field Crew(s) via radio. 5.5.1.6 The running (active) incidents during the shutdow n will maintain their original CareMonX incident number and NO Shutdown incident Number will be created for them. 5.5.1.7 The last 4 digits of the Shutdown’s incident Number shall not be used twice during the same day. 5.6 Manual entry will require the same informatio n as the CareMonX CAD screen to be handwritten in the appropriate space on the Manual Communications Record. 5.7 Call taker shall enter the necessary information in accordance with SOP 3.22

HCC Call Taking before forwarding the sheet to the dispatcher. 5.8 The Dispatcher shall accurately capture all remaining information including time stamps. 5.9 Once the case is completed/cleared, the Dispatcher shall endorse the manual call record to the UTL for reviewing, completion and signatur e.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 173 6. Once CareMonX CAD has been restored: 6.1 Calls received during the shutdown will be logged in CareMonX CAD. 6.2 All running cases during the return of CAD should be processed and closed in CareMonX. 6.3 Notify CDO via email the duration & number of calls logged ma nually if still not entered to CAD. 6.4 The Case controller will collect all manual call records endorse it to the UTL for documentation and filing. 6.5 All reported equipment failures must be documented by the UTL or Operations Officer on duty via the “OVA” repor ting system as per HMC policy and complete a summary report, check list for CAD shut down. 7. Testing: 7.1 If there have been no failures of the system, the failover system will be tested and documented bi - annually .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 174 SOP 3.19.A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.19 .A NCC EVACUATION PLAN Implementation Date 01 October 20 22 Purpose This procedure identifies the disaster recovery site communications center to be used when the National Command Center (NCC) is to be evacuated and describes the levels of evacuation . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS (HCS) STAFF 1. PROCEDURE : 1.1 An evacuation will be considered for any situation that puts HMC - AS staff at the NCC and TCC out of service or in danger that it cannot safely and effectively operate at its co - located TCC/MOI facility in Duhail. 1.2 When it is deemed necessary to evacuate the TCC some staff will relocate to a disaster recovery sit e identified by the management team. 1.2.1 If the problem is limited to only one section of the control room, the operations in that area may relocate to another control room. In such situation HMCAS consoles in the TCC will relocate to the old building of NCC a t the Joint Operation Center (JOC) which includes dedicated consoles for HMCAS. 1.2.2 If the

problem impacts the entire buildings - complex, partial operations will move to the disaster recovery site located within the designated TCC/MOI primary disaster recovery site at Bin Omran Area while the same portion of the operations will move to the HMC - AS headquarter and some will remain standby for back to service. 1.2.3 TCC/MOI primary disaster recovery site is located at Civil Defense Headquarter and featured with 2 console s for Team leaders (1 DTL & 1 PTL). All HMCAS communications staff will be allowed to enter the building by presenting both HMC - ID and NCC access card. 1.2.4 HMC - AS headquarter backup center will be at HCC call center and will be featured as follows: 1.2.4.1 8 Consoles at HCC call center room number AS - 1 - 0120, 4 dispatching, 3 call taking and 1 Team Leader. The hotline 403 - 28201 for call takers will be provided to access CISCO DIDs 403 28201, 2 8202, 28203, the dispatchers will be provided to access to 403 - 28204, 28205, 28206, 28207. 55181032 mobile number for NCC supervisor. 1.2.4.2 3 consoles for the Clinical Desk at HCS Quality office room number AS - 1 - 0113; 1 Clinical Coordinator, 1 LifeFlight Coordinator and 1 EMD: Retrieval Coordinat

or . The contact number 4439 - 8833 f or the C linical Desk will be unchanged through IVR access when staff login to CISCO Finesse for DIDs 403 - 28222 - 28223. The hotline number 403 - 28499 is retained if the IVR experiences any issues while the Clinical Desk is at HMCAS HQ. 1.3 Once all disaster recovery site positions are staffed and operational, dispatchers shall notify all on duty supervisors and Managers that the relocation is complete and broadcast over all primary channels that service is now operational from the disaster recovery site. 1.4 Staff may transpo rt themselves to the designated disaster recovery via personal vehicles with no less than two people per vehicle. If staff vehicles are unable to be used, Oscar - NCC will

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 175 arrange to transport them to the designated disaster recovery site using HMC AS resour ces or HMC Transport. 1.5 If the evacuation is extended beyond shift change, the coming shift will report to the designated disaster recovery site directly via personal vehicle (One team leader from group1 shall notify the incoming shift staff to which locatio n they should respond). 1.6 Anytime an alarm or emergency condition affects the TCC building, Oscar - NCC or their designee must establish close contact with Communications Operations Manager to ensure all available facts regarding the incident are known to aid in the decision regarding evacuation. 2. EVACUATION PLAN: 2.1 To allow for uninterrupted emergency services; during an evacuation below steps will take place simultaneously by OSCAR NCC or his delegate at TCC . 2.1.1 Immediately notify the on - call management team and SME: Clinical Desk. The HMCAS emergency services manager will be notified first to start their intradepartmental notifications in a cascade manner. 2.1.2 Notify by mobile or radio the HCC on duty supervisor to p repare all the 11 backup consoles at HCC an

d HCS Quality office. 2.1.3 Coordinate with the 999 - officer to activate a radio channel between TCC 999 supervisor and one HMCAS ASMD call taker for endorsing emergency requests. (Radio folder and channel names will be arranged as per availability). 2.1.4 The call taker monitoring the 999 - radio channel will log the received cases in NJM - Shutdown paper forms or in a list including the exact address, the nature of incident and the caller telephone number. A coordinator or a tea m leader will assist to endorse these cases promptly to the group of dispatchers (refer to NJM shutdown SOP). 2.1.5 ProQA will be omitted and all cases will be dispatched as priority 1. 2.1.6 Instruct all dispatchers to keep monitoring their radio channels through TET RA while evacuating the building and document the current CFSs and unit status in a list in A4 paper for each dispatcher. 2.1.7 The operational channel will be merged as follows: 2.1.7.1 Dispatcher1: Doha 1 and South channel 2.1.7.2 Dispatcher2: Doha 2 and North channel 2.1.7.3 Dispatc her3: South 1 channels 2.1.7.4 Dispatcher4: South 2 and South2 channels 2.1.8 If the address is not clear and not complete the dispatcher will ask the case c

ontroller to call back the caller for clarifications. 2.1.9 Inform 999 officers not to forward 999 calls yet to HMCAS headquarter - HCC dedicated CISCO branch until a group of call taking consoles are ready. 2.1.10 All staff will move to the TCC/MOI assembly point while monitoring the channels and processing calls. 2.1.11 To ensure accountability for staff Oscar - NCC or the most senior pe rson in charge will do a head count prior to releasing staff to go to the designated disaster recovery locations or to remain in standby at TCC. 2.2 OSCAR NCC to start the move to the backup centers and will divide the staff to three groups.

NOTIFICATION : 3.1 Not ify Silver C

ommunications and NCC Operations Manager. The manager or designee will notify additional administrative personnel as needed but shall include the EAS, Clinical Desk and LifeFlight on - call managers. 3.2 NCC Operations Manager will announce and decla re the evacuation of the TCC/MOI building in collaboration with Oscar - NCC and MOI officers. 3.3 Notify HCC 402 8210/8209/8219 3.4 HMC - AS on call manager will be kept updated with all details and steps of the activation. 3.5 Notify the NHICC 402 6555 4. Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 178 SOP 3.19 .B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.19 .B HCC EVACUATION PLAN Implementation Date 0 1 October 20 16 Purpose This procedure identifies the backup communications center to be used when the Healthcare Coordination Center (HCC) is to be evacuated and describes the levels of evacuation . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS (HCS) STAFF 1. An evacuation will be considered for any situation that puts HMC - AS Healthcare Coordination Center (HCC) out of service or in danger that it cannot safely and effectively operate at Ambulance Service HQ. 2. When it is deemed necessary to evacuate the Healthcare Coordination Center all personnel will relocate to a backup center identified by HCS management. 2.1 If the problem is limited to only one section of the control room, the operations in that control room m ay relocate to another control room. 2.2 If the problem impacts the entire building, all operations will move to the backup center located within the National Command Center (NCC), which is the primary disaster recovery site. 3. A

ll telephone lines, CAD, and radi o channels of the backup communications center should be tested prior to being used. 3.1 Testing will be done weekly by HCC staff inside the NCC staff using a standardized checklist. 3.2 The checklists will be saved under the evacuation documents at HCC. 3.3 \ \ hmcfs05 \ EMS \ Communication Staff files \ PTS COMMUNICATIONS \ HCC REPORTS \ HCC WORKSTATION CHECKLIST. 3.4 All reported equipment failures must be documented by the Supervisor or Senior Officer on duty via the “OVA” reporting system as per HMC policy. 4. Anytime an alarm or em ergency condition affects the building, the Communications Operation Officer (Oscar - HCC) or their designee must stay in close contact with Communications Operations Manager to ensure all available facts regarding the incident are known to aid in the decisi on regarding evacuation. 5. STAGED EVACUATION: 5.1 To allow for uninterrupted emergency services; during a staged evacuation staff will divide into two groups. 5.1.1 The first group (Group “A”) will relocate to the designated backup center and activate the equipment and confirm phone lines have been redirected. 5.1.1.1 “Group A” should consist of the UTL, Case

controller ,1 dispatcher and 2 call - takers (those staff who can get access to NCC). 5.1.2 Once this has occurred, the remaining staff (Group “B”) will then relocate to the backup center. 5.2 During a staged evacuation, the HCC Operation Officer will remain with Group “B” at HCC. 5.3 The determination of which supervisor remains with which group may be made by the Communication Operation Manager or senior person in charge on a case by case basis. 5.4 During a staged evacuation, staff may arrange transport or transport themselves to the backup center via personal vehicle.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 179 5.4.1 If the evacuation is extended beyond shift change, the oncoming shift will report to the backup center directly via personal vehicle (UTL shall notify the incoming shift where to report). 5.5 Once all backup center positions are staffed and operational, dispatchers shall notify all on duty supervisors and Managers that the relocation is complete and broadcast over all primary channels that service is now operational from the backup center. 5.5.1 This will also occur during reversal of the plan from NCC to normal operations at HCC. 6. IMMEDIATE EVACUATION: 6.1 Immediate evacuations of all staff will occur without delay due to an immi nent threat. 6.1.1 An example would be the building filled with smoke. 6.1.2 If evacuation is due to a bomb threat, DO NOT transmit on mobile/portable radio equipment during the evacuation or within 300 feet of the building. 6.2 All staff will report to the HCC assembly p oint then to designated transportation center. 6.3 To ensure accountability for staff the OSCAR - HCC or the most senior person in charge will do a head count prior to releasing staff to go on to the backup center.

6.4 Staff may transport themselves to the backup ce nter via personal vehicles with no less than two people per vehicle. 6.5 If staff vehicles are unable to be used OSCAR - HCC will arrange to transport them to the backup center by HMC AS green bus or coordinate with fleet section to provide transport car. 7. ACTIVA TING THE PLAN: 7.1 HCC Operations Manager will announce and declare the evacuation of the HCC building in collaboration with OSCAR - HCC. 7.1.1 Together they will define the method and level of the evacuation (Staged or IMMEDIATE). 7.2 HCC Operations Officer/UTL will noti fy Oscar NCC/DTL. 7.3 NCC Operations Officer/DTL, PTL will take in charge the activation of the HCC workstations and coordinate staff access to NCC. 7.4 Retrieval desk will undertake all HCC incoming calls and ongoing cases at CAD. 7.5 HCC Operations Manager will be updated with all details and steps of the activation. 8. NOTIFICATION OF EVACUATION SHALL BE MADE TO: 8.1 Notify Silver Communications and HCC Operations Manager. The manager or designee will notify additional administrative personnel as needed but shall include the SAS on - call managers (or any other users like Diabetic) . 8.2 Notify the

hospital/Bleep station 40260334. 8.3 Notify HMC communications center 443 92210/6626 2083. 9. DEACTIVATING THE PLAN: 9.1 HCC Operation Manager Officer/OSCAR - HCC will follow the same process as fo r activating the plan and relay the need for reversal of the plan back to HCC. Test calls will follow to ensure a successful reroute back to HCC. 9.2 Notify HCC Silver Communication 9.3 Notify the hospital/Bleep Station 44260334

Documentation: 10.1 Immediate evacuations of all staff will occur without delay. 10.2 All staff will report to the HQ assembly point. 10.3 HCC Operations Officer/UTL will notify Oscar NCC/DTL. 10.4 NCC Oscar will notify Retrieval desk to undertake al l HCC incoming calls and ongoing cases at CAD. 10.5 HCC Operation Officer/UTL will notify Communication Manager and SAS and silver communication. 11. TESTING: 11.1 The Evacuation Plan should be tested annually with documentation of adherence to the plan and lessons lear ned for improvement purposes. 11.2 To ensure update implementation into the computers the Oscar/UTL will ensure the restart of the computers twice a week, Tuesday and Friday at low call volume periods. 11.3 To ensure the functionality of the HCC Phone number 16099 a regular checking on hourly basis is done by the operation officer or supervisor by dialing the number and getting connected to the agent .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 183 SOP 3.20 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.20 CUSTOMER SERVIC E COMMUNICATIONS FOR HEALTHCARE COORDINATION SERVICE CALL CENTERS Implementation Date 13 March 2018 Purpose • To standardize the approach of NCC and HCC staff in interacting with external and internal users of HCS Communication Centers. • To provide all ASMD with a well - defined procedure for managing callers and providing those callers with sufficient reassurance, emotional comfort, and customer service. • To ensure the delivery of hig hest customer service at HCS . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS ( NCC & HCC) STAFF 1. SOP STATEMENT : 1.1 Ambulance Service Medical Dispatchers (ASMDs) shall handle all calls for ambulance assistance, whether emergency or non - emergency, using established caller management and customer service practices. It is understood that not all callers will be calm and/or cooperative. ASMDs will anticipate those situations and respond as trained professionals following the MPDS. 1.2 HMCAS expects all HCC & NCC staff to exhibit the highest lev

el of customer service at HCS. This applies to all internal and external communications via telephone. 2. DEFINITIONS : 2.1 ASMD: 2.1.1 Ambulance Service Medical Dispatcher: Title given to a Calltaker/dispatcher that has successfully completed the internationally accredited dispatch training program. 2.2 MPDS: 2.2.1 Medical Priority Dispatch System: (Flip chart and Software) is a medically approved system used to dispatch the most appropriate medical resource to the person calling f or service. 2.3 PAIs: 2.3.1 Pre - Arrival Instructions: Life - sustaining Information and instructions given to the caller to follow while awaiting the arrival of the ambulance. 2.4 PDIs: 2.4.1 Post - Dispatch Instructions: General instructions given to all callers to follow while waiting for the ambulance to arrive. 3. CASE OPERATION PROCEDURE: 3.1 Opening the call : 3.1.1 For HCC, the greeting script will include: 3.1.1.1 "Ambulance Service - This is _____ (staff name) speaking. How may I help you?” 3.1.1.2 خدمة اإلسعاف, فالنة/فالن معاك, كيف أقدر أساعدك؟ 3.1.2 For NCC emergency and hotline calls, the ASMD will use:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 184 3.1.2.1 "Ambulance Service, This is _____ (staff name) speaking, What is the address of the emergency?” 3.1.2.2 خدمة اإلسعاف, فالنة/فالن معاك, ما هو عنوان حا ل ة الطوارئ ؟ 3.1.3 For NCC routine administrative calls, the below script will be applicable: 3.1.3.1 “Ambulance Service, This is _____ (staff name) speaking. How may I help you?” 3.2 Obtaining and confirming information : 3.2.1 The below information will only be required for HCC cases (Refer to the Calltaking SOP 3.22 ). 3.2.2 Ask politely for the caller’s information and properly document in the CAD. 3.2.2.1 “May I have your name, corporation number, and telephone number, please?” 3.2.3 Ask about the patient’s information: 3.2.3.1 “May I have the patient ’s HC number, name, exact location, destination, and the reason for transport?” 3.3 Using the Hold Guidelines : 3.3.1 Put the caller on hold only when absolutely necessary for a few seconds or no more than 1 minute and use the Hold/Mute button properly, making sure t he caller will not hear you when you put him/her on hold. 3.3.2 Never interrupt the caller just to put them on hold. 3.3.3 When necessary, do wai

t for a pause in the conversation before you put them on hold. 3.3.4 Tell them you are going to put them on hold and explain the reason before placing the caller on hold. “Mr. Ben, can you please hold on the line while I retrieve the information? {Pause for a response}. 3.3.5 Be sure to listen to the response. 3.3.6 Inform the caller that you will return to their call in as soon as possible. “Thank you. I will be back with you shortly. {Caller on hold}. 3.3.7 Thank the caller for waiting immediately when you return to the call and start the conversation “Thank you for holding Mr. Ben. I can now help you?...” 3.3.8 After placing your caller on hold, check back periodically (between 30 - 45 seconds). Give them the option to continue to hold if it takes longer than 60 seconds to find information or offer to call them back. 3.3.8.1 “Ms. Maryam, I will check to see if your appointment is confirmed. Can you please hold fo r a minute?” {Pause for a response}. 3.3.8.2 “Thank you. I will be right back.” {Caller on hold}. 3.3.8.3 “I’m sorry for the inconvenience. Your request is missing some information, May I have your complete name please?” 3.3.9 When placing multiple calls on hold, rememb
er to return to the first caller you placed on hold first. 3.3.10 Always apologize for any inconvenience or long hold and thank them for their patience and understanding.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 185 3.3.11 Don’t put the caller on hold multiple times in one call. 3.4 Transferring Calls : 3.4.1 Ask valid questions and ensure that the call will be transferred to the appropriate party. 3.4.2 Inform the caller to whom he/she will be transferred and state the reason for the transfer. 3.4.2.1 " I need to transfer your call to _____ so that he/she can answer your question ” . 3.4.3 Before transferring a call, confirm that the person to whom the call is being transferred is available. 3.4.4 As applicable, call taker may have to endorse pertinent information and provide a brief description/summary of what was discussed to the person to whom the caller is being transferred. 3.4.5 When receiving a transferred call, the call taker should always mention his/her facility name. This is to ensure correct transfer to a department/section. 3.5 Politeness, courtesy and listening behavior : 3.5.1 Always sound courteous , calm, and respectful throughout the call, no matter how mad a customer may be or behave. Avoid sounding unprofessional. (Avoid saying: “Hang on.” “Hold on.” “Who’s calling?” “I can’t help you. You’ll have to speak to

someone else .”) 3.5.1.1 Instead of saying “I can’t hear you, speak up!” use “I am having a little difficulty hearing you. Can you please speak up ?” 3.5.2 Utilize the words “ Would you please?”, “Thank you”, “Sorry for the inconvenience’ , or other relevant statements as appropriate. Use the words “ your quest ion,” or “your concern,” or “this situation,” instead of “your problem,” or “your complaint .” 3.5.3 Show empathy by saying “ I understand your concern and would be glad to assist you to resolve it ” or similar statements when needed. 3.5.4 After helping them with their request(s), ask if there is anything else you can help them with. “ Is there anything else I can help you with ?” 3.5.5 Do use the caller’s first name or title to establish a personal connection with the caller. ( Jassim, Bill, Mr. Jones, Mrs. Stevens ...) 3.5.6 When a ca ller speaks, listen to what he or she has to say without interruptions. 3.5.7 Speak with the normal speed to avoid the impression of “rushing” the call. 3.6 Show interest while handling the call : 3.6.1 Maintain a caring voice tone and pitch using a clear voice that is nei ther too loud nor too low. Words should be pronounced and said slow

enough that a caller is able to understand what is being said to him/her. 3.6.2 Positively answer all queries with confidence. 3.6.3 Paraphrase or summarize long details to confirm understanding. 3.7 Conversation skills : 3.7.1 Refrain from using jargon, abbreviations, technical or medical terms such as HCC, NCC, AP, and CCPs, etc.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 186 3.7.2 Take control of the conversation. Keep the focus. Politely but firmly direct the caller on answering all questions as you ask them “Sir/Madam, it’s important I get this information so I can help you.” 3.7.3 Use correct language (English or Arabic) in words, phrases, and greetings. 3.7.4 Refrain from personal conversations. Focus on the concern(s) of the caller. 3.7.5 Process the call i n an appropriate manner and close it within a suitable time frame. 3.8 Escalation Procedure : 3.8.1 Try to manage the caller’s issue once or twice depending on the caller’s tolerance. “Manage up” as appropriate when customers insist to talk with a supervisor. 3.8.2 For abu sive, threatening, or vulgar language, let the caller know that you may need to escalate the call to your supervisor if he/she will continue with this unacceptable behavior. 3.9 Closing the call : 3.9.1 Call taker is not allowed to close the line abruptly during the conversation. 3.9.2 End the phone call professionally after completing all required questions/Instructions as per the protocols. 3.9.3 The call taker will make it clear to the caller that the convers

ation is finished, and the call will be terminated. 3.9.4 In routine disconnects the call - taker will wait for the caller acknowledgement and will let him/her hangup first. 3.9.5 In urgent disconnects call - taker will wait for the caller acknowledgement and will close the line immediately to take another call. 3.9.6 Always st ate the following ‘send off’ script: 3.9.6.1 For HCC: 3.9.6.1.1 “ Thank you for calling Ambulance Service .” 3.9.6.1.2 "شكرا على اتصالك بخدمة اإلسعاف" 3.9.6.1.3 “ Please call us back for updates or if anything changes. Goodbye !” 3.9.6.2 For NCC: Please refer to the MPDS protocols (routine/urgent disconnect instructions ) . 4. CALLER MANAGEMENT : The ASMD will use the below procedures in managing callers with ProQA as described in SOP 3.2 for NCC or SOP 3.22 for HCC. 4.1 Ca ller reassurance and explanations : 4.1.1 Once Case Entry is complete, or whenever necessary to maintain caller focus, the ASMD will use reassuring statements and brief explanations. For example; 4.1.1.1 “ The ambulance is on the way to help you. Please stay on the line. I need to gather some important information so that I can tell you what to do to help the patient.” OR “My partner is d
ispatching the ambulance right now; I’m going to get more information and give you some instructions before help arrives.”

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 187 4.1.1.2 For HCC cases with initial dispatch: Call taker may reassure the caller by stating “the ambulance has been arranged and I need to ask some additional questions, stay reassured this will not delay the response . ” 4.1.2 Politely but firmly focus the caller on answering all questions as you ask them. If callers lose their focus, get agitated or uncooperative, say: 4.1.2.1 “Sir/Madam it’s important I get this information so I can tell you exactly what to do .” Repeat this as often as necessary using the same phrasing . 4.2 Coping with Distr essed, Hysterical, Rude, Angry and/or uncooperative Callers : 4.2.1 It is recognized that some callers will be highly distressed, uncooperative and, at times, abusive. Callers behave this way because they are frightened and feel helpless. When faced with these ca llers, the ASMD will maintain a professional demeanor and caring approach. 4.2.2 The ASMD will always remain calm and courteous. 4.2.3 The ASMD will maintain normal speaking volume and a professional, caring voice tone, avoiding yelling and any display of anger or con tempt. 4.2.4 Whenever possible, the ASMD will give

clear, brief explanations as to what he/she is doing and why. For example, explain to the caller that they will need to ask some questions (so that you can get the proper priority). 4.2.5 The ASMD will, when necessary , obtain and use a caller’s first name or title (Jassim, Bill, Mr. Jones, Mrs. Stevens, etc.) 4.2.6 The ASMD will use ‘REPETITIVE PERSISTENCE: 4.2.6.1 Give the caller an action, followed by a reason for complying with the action. 4.2.6.2 Repeat this, using the same phrasing an d in a calm, level voice, as often as necessary until the caller listens and cooperates. 4.2.7 The ASMD will continually reassure callers that he/she is there to help. It may be necessary to repeat this. The ASMD will explain that trained help is on the way and repeat it when necessary. 4.2.8 The ASMD will, when necessary, use ‘POSITIVE AMBIGUITY.’ Do not ‘lie’ to the caller, even if motivated by kindness. Do not make promises or create unrealistic expectations for the caller. Examples follow: 4.2.8.1 The caller asks, “How long will the ambulance take to arrive?” The ASMD will say, “The ambulance is on the way. It will be with you as soon as possible.” DO NOT GIVE AN EXACT TIME FOR ARRIVAL OF R

ESPONDING CREW(S). The ambulance may break down or get stuck in traffic. 4.2.8.2 The caller asks, “The patient is going to be all right, isn’t s/he?” The ASMD will say, “Everyone will do the best they can to help.” 4.2.8.3 The caller asks, “Will this save him/her?” The ASMD will say “This will help give him/her the best possible chance.” 4.2.9 During pre - arrival instructions, the ASMD will give the caller firm but gentle encouragement. For example, if the caller says, “Nothing’s working!” The ASMD will say, “Don’t give up. You’ve got to keep doing it. This will keep him/her going until the ambulance crew arrives.”

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 188 4.2.10 When the caller is unable to answer questions or perform rescue techniques after the ASMD makes multiple attempts to employ caller management techniques, the ASMD will calmly ask the caller to let him speak to someone else. 4.2.11 The ASMD will never make any statements that foster or create feelings of helplessness, guilt, or panic in a caller. 4.2.12 The ASMD will never threaten a caller in any way, or engage in any discriminatory, derogatory, or demeaning behavior toward the callers, patie nts, family members or bystanders, explicitly or implicitly, through language, attitude, or voice intonation. 4.3 Managing Abusive callers : The ASMD should distinguish between manageable behaviors such as hysteria, distress, anger, etc. and unmanageable abuse. 4.3.1 Abuse is when an ASMD is subjected to a personal attack, especially when it is based on, race, age, gender, or religion, it is considered hateful and it is not accepted nor tolerated by HMCAS. ASMD should: 4.3.2 Attempt to de - escalate the situation. It is impor tant not to respond to the behavior in the same way. ASMDs must not abuse callers, despite the cal

ler abusing the ASMD. 4.3.2.1 Allow the caller to speak. Try not to talk over the caller. 4.3.2.2 Display empathy and compassion. Use words and phrases consistent with profe ssional, compassionate communication to acknowledge the caller’s feelings. e.g. “I’m here to help you”, “we’re doing this together”, “I understand that” (explain what they have said, so they know you are listening). 4.3.2.3 Use appropriate speech. Speak clearly, c oncisely, pace your words allowing the caller to understand, use a pitch that is not too high or low and use a firm and even tone – ensuring microphone placement is not distorting the volume. 4.3.2.4 Use the caller’s name or title. 4.3.3 If the caller fails to stop and the ASMD finds the abuse intolerable, ask to speak to someone else at the address. If there is no one else available, the ASMD should seek assistance from their supervisor for immediate intervention. 4.3.4 (For NCC) If no triage has been completed, code the inci dent 32B01 UNKNOWN. 4.3.5 Document “ABUSE” into the incident comments, so the call will be flagged for review by a supervisor or Manager. 4.3.6 In situations where a caller becomes abusive after the triage process is complete, A

SMD may utilize the Urgent Disconnect s cript and must also notify their supervisor. Use of Urgent Disconnect will be in line with the “Urgent Disconnect SOP” and must include the reading of the protocol - specific PDI’s. ASMD may escalate to his/her supervisor if required at any time. 4.3.7 When an ASM D refers an abusive caller to their supervisor, they should take over the call, allowing the ASMD to disconnect. They will explain that they are the ASMD’s Supervisor and that they will arrange help for them. The remainder of the call process should then b e followed. (If the call cannot be transferred, ASMD will inform the caller that he/she will be called back, the supervisor should not delay calling back the caller and should be prepared to deal with the situation) . 4.3.8 Call Termination Process (Supervisor): 4.3.8.1 In situations where the caller remains abusive after speaking with a supervisor (and the Supervisor finds this intolerable) the caller is to be

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 189 advised that his/her behavior is not acceptable and provide 3 warnings that the call may be discon nected if the abuse will continue. 4.3.8.2 If the abuse continues and it is intolerable the Supervisor is to advise “Help is being arranged, I am hanging up now, if anything changes call us back. 4.3.8.3 The Supervisor who terminates the call should enter a comment in CAD saying that the caller was abusive towards the ASMD. 4.3.9 Follow up and Reporting Process: 4.3.9.1 Supervisors/Oscar should immediately debrief abusive caller incidents with the concerned ASMD as soon as practicable, check that no further support is required. 4.3.9.2 Sup ervisors are required to escalate to the Quality unit any call with Abuse to review the incident and provide any further support/coaching that may be identified. 4.3.9.3 In cases where the supervisor believes further action is required, they should escalate this to the management and document the incident through the OVA process. 4.4 Providing Pre - Arrival Instructions : 4.4.1 The ASMD will never ask permission to give instructions (i.e. Do not say “ Would you like me to tell you how

to do CPR ?” or “ I can give you CPR instruc tions if you want to try .”). The best way to handle reluctant callers is to simply start giving the instructions. Most callers expect to take direction from the trained ASMD on the other end of the phone line. 4.4.2 If the caller refuses to follow PAIs, and only if there is no safety concerns will say, the ASMD: 4.4.2.1 “ The ambulance is on its way, but this is important in order to give the patient the best possible chance/care until professional help arrives.” Repeat as necessary. 4.4.2.2 If the caller still refuses to adminis ter aid, the ASMD will ask if there is someone else there to speak to; if so, the ASMD will attempt instructions again with the new caller . 4.5 Third Party Calls : 4.5.1 The ASMD will not assume that third party callers know nothing, even if they say they know nothin g. 3rd party caller can provide some valuable information about the call. 4.5.2 The ASMD will always attempt to ask all Case Entry and Key Questions of third - party callers. 4.5.3 Whenever possible, the ASMD will try to convert a 3rd party caller to a 2nd party caller by asking them if he/she can go to the patient to render aid. If they agree, th

e ASMD will give PDIs and PAIs. 4.5.4 Once the ASMD has made several attempts to gather information without success, and only after finishing ProQA interrogation and identifying the f inal coding he/she may choose to terminate the call, once it has been determined that the caller has no further information and cannot or will not get close to the patient or the incident.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 190 5. QUALITY ASSURANCE AND CALL MONITORING : 5.1 To ensure compliance with the established Customer Service procedures within NCC & HCC, a sample size from the total calls (emergency and non - emergency) will be subjected to random quality reviews. 5.2 The below ten (10) key HCC specific customer service metrics will be measured for evaluation : Key Metrics Competencies 1. Opening the call Call taker used the standard greeting script Call taker identified her/himself to the caller 2. Obtaining and confirming information Asked for the caller's name Asked for Caller’s corporation number Asked for Caller’s telephone number Asked for patient Health Card number Asked for patient's name Asked for patient's exact location Asked for patient's destination Asked about the reason for transport/transfer Asked about the requested priority/acuity level Captured the caller's information 3. Using the Hold guidelines Calltaker used the Hold/Mute button when necessary Calltaker informed the caller before placing him/her on hold and explained the reason. The hold duration was less than or equal to 1 minute (when possible and applicable) C

alltaker kept in touch during long hold Calltaker thanked the customer for holding 4. Transferring Calls Calltaker asked valid questions to ensure the call is transferred to the correct party when appropriate Calltaker informed the caller where & why they were transferred to Calltaker transferred the call to the right department/section when appropriate Calltaker mentioned his/her facility name when receiving a transferred call 5. Politeness, courtesy and listening Calltaker sounded courteous & respectful throughout the call Calltaker used "please" when appropriate Calltaker used "thank you" when appropriate Calltaker apologized using "Sorry for the inconvenience" or other statements when appropriate Calltaker showed empathy using "I understand your concern and would be glad to assist you to resolve it" or similar statements when appropriate Calltaker asked if caller wanted help with anything else, when applicable Calltaker used the caller’s name throughout the call & used the right prefix Dr./Mr./Ms./Mrs. or Sir/Madam (when applicable) Did not interrupt the caller Did not rush the call 6. Interest shown whi le handling the call The Calltaker maintained proper tone, pitch, volume,
and pace throughout the call. Positively answering all queries with confidence when appropriate

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 191 Used paraphrasing/summarizing to confirm understanding 7. Conversation skills Avoided usage of “jargon” (technical/medical terms), abbreviations, or acronyms. Calltaker was in control of the conversation. Calltaker was professional with correct use of language either in English or Arabic. (in wordings, in phrases, or greetings) Refrained from turning the conversations personal with the caller The way of handling the call — processing and closing — is withi n a suitable time frame. 8. Escalation Procedure Tried once or twice (based on the caller's tolerance) to address the caller's issue. Escalated the call on caller's insistence without upsetting him. 9. Closing the call Calltaker used the “send - off” script 10. Prohibited Behaviors Calltaker employed a statement that fostered/created feelings of anger, guilt, or panic in the caller. Calltaker yelled at the caller. Calltaker used derogatory, offensive, demeaning, or discriminatory language or tone of voice. Calltaker showed disrespect for the caller. Calltaker used threatening statements Calltaker made any sound that may be interpreted as offensive or derogatory such
as laughing, chuckling, sighs of frustration, etc. Calltaker blamed the calle r for any of his/her actions Calltaker disconnected call without sufficient reason

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 192 SOP 3.21 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.21 HCC CALL ROUTING Implementation Date 09 March 2020 Purpose To direct the routing of calls for the Transfer and Retrieval Services Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF 1. HMC Ambulance Service (HMCAS) national Healthcare Coordination Centre (HCC), which provides call center and control room operations for the Scheduled Ambulance Service (SAS) and Ambulance Service (AS) Critical Care Services (CCS). 1.1 SAS is accessed by patients, family members, care givers and providers through 16099 or 974 443 - 98555. 1.2 CCS is access by practitioners at 974 443 - 98833 1.3 All phone lines as well as the chat/broadcast functions are recorded for quality assurance and improvement purposes. 1.4 Extensions numb ers should not be given to anyone EXCEPT; 1.4.1 MIR ‘on call staff’ SILVER or GOLD category, 1.4.2 HCC staff, 1.4.3 NCC DTLs/PTLs to reach HCC UTLs, 1.5 If an NCC EMD staff need to transfer a caller to HCC, they must use 16099 or 4439 8555 and navigate through the phone system. 1.5.1 NCC

is NOT allowed to transfer these types of calls to direct extensions. 2. Phone System Essentials: 2.1 The designated staff should be at the right desk/room at all times according to the desk/room assignments and the pre - assigned role of each individual staff. 2.2 Staff are expected to log in to the CISCO agent (or supervisor if applicable) at the beginning of the shift and log out at the end of the shift. 2.2.1 If going on break or a temporary absence, staff will use the NOT READY statuses and select th e most relevant reason with notification to the shift UTL. 2.2.2 Staff should not have to log out to switch users (change desks for dispatcher rotation). 2.2.3 Staff are expected to regularly monitor the dashboard to monitor their readiness and see potential calls waiting on the queue they are assigned to. 2.2.3.1 Supervisors (Oscar, UTL, CC) will have the ability to monitor ALL queues to see potential calls waiting on the queues. 2.2.3.1.1 Staff working in Retrievals Desk, will need to monitor their queues as well. 2.2.3.2 Supervisor s (Oscar, UTL, CC) will have the ability to monitor their readiness. 2.2.3.2.1 This is important to ensure that someone who is NOT READY has not le

ft themselves in the status by mistake so they can answer calls presented in the ACD. 2.2.4 Supervisors are authorized to mak e queries about readiness as well as update the NOT READY status on behalf of the staff so they can receive calls.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 193 2.2.4.1 The status NOT READY “Call No answer” means that the agents phone logged out after the phone was not answered within 15 seconds of a call rin ging to it, UTL have to follow up and take necessary action as needed. 2.2.4.2 The status NOT READY SUPERVISOR means the ‘supervisor’ initiated the status for the agent from the supervisor desktop. 2.3 Staff are expected to answer all calls within 15 seconds (1 - 2 rin gs), 90% of the time. 3. Agent queues: 3.1 Calls will be routed via the Automatic Call Distributor (ACD) to the agent which is not busy (on rotation) in accordance to what queues it is assigned to (attached). 3.2 Staff will be presented in the dashboard with the lang uage and option the caller selected in the IVR (for example PTS _ARABIC or RET English). 3.2.1 It is important to pay attention to the caller’s selection when answering the phone. 3.2.2 The standard call opening should be in the same language selected by the caller wh ich is displayed on Cisco Agent Desktop. (Refer to SOP 3.22 for further details). 4. Misrouted calls: 4.1 If any staff receives a call that is not for them, for example the calle

r selected PTS when they meant to select nationa l diabetic center hotline: 4.1.1 The staff will advise the caller they have reached the incorrect section and that they will assist them in reaching the correct section and to please hold the line. 4.1.2 The staff will then transfer the call back into the IVR and nav igate the options to get the caller where they need to go, and then release the line. 4.1.3 Incorrect pathway calls should not be transferred to the agent extensions directly. 4.2 Notify the shift supervisor when misrouted calls are received. 4.2.1 Callers, who are misusing the system by not accurately navigating it, for exampling pressing 1 thereby entering the Arabic queue when they do not speak Arabic, should be reported. 4.2.2 The high priority calls (IFT/PHCC) might be routed to any available agent ( CT)regardless the selected language whenever the selected language /agents are busy and here the CT should process the call without any delay. 4.3 On occasion, the Retrievals Desk may receive IFT and CCS calls. 4.3.1 If someone on the Retrievals Desk received an IFT call, it should be processed according to SOP 3.22 (HCC Call Taking). 4.3.2 If someone on the Retrievals

Desk received an CCS call, unless otherwise described in a call handling process, the staff will consult the CC Retriev al to see who can best handle the call and then transfer it to their extension .
