Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 197 Call Routing for Ambulance Service Healthcare Coordination Centre 1 A 16099/974 4439 8555 call is placed by an end user. 2 The caller reaches an Interactive Voice Response (IVR) which greets the caller in Arabic and English advising them to select number 1 for Arabic language and number 2 for English language. 3 The caller will choose one of the services by pressing respected number as follow: • For Primary Healthcare Cooperation patient transfer press 9. • For Ambulance service transportation booking press 1. • For National diabetic and hotline press 4. • To repeat this information press 0. 4 When the caller presses 1 for Ambulance service transportation bookings he will have the following options : • For patient transport to/from home press 1. • For interfacility patient transfer press 2. • For specialized team retrieval services and international patient transfer press 3. • For healthcare practitioners arranging communicable disease patient transfer press 4. • To repeat this information press 0. • To go to main menu press*. 5 The HMC AS call taker receives the call and obtains & confirms the patients demographics i

ncluding HC number as well as caller name and number and the location and destination of the patient to process a booking query or request: a. Calls will be processed in the Computer Aided Dispatch system (CAD) by section ( PTS, IFT RET) and use ProQA whenever applicable. b. After termination of the call, the Ambulance Service Medical Dispatcher or case controller may call back the caller at any time to gather more information about the patient if seemed to be necessary. c. Anytime the line disconnects or the caller hung - up before the termination of the call, the Ambulance Service operator must call back the caller.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 198 SOP 3.22 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.22 HCC CALL TAKING Implementation Date 13 April 2021 Purpose To ensure effective and efficient receipt and handling of all the calls within the HMCAS Communications Center - Healthcare Coordination Center ( HCC) . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF (SPECIFICALLY C ALL TAKERS) 1. General Call Taking Standards : 1.1 Answer all the calls arriving at the Ambulance Service consoles within 1 to 2 rings. 1.2 Answer all the calls coming to HCC with the phrase “Ambulance Service, this is _____ (staff name) speaking. How may I help you?” 1.3 For emergency calls received by HCC lines, advise the caller to call back on 999. Do not transfer the call to the NCC hotline. 1.4 Obtain the following minimum basic information from the caller: 1.4.1 The address or exact location of the incident 1.4.2 The caller name and call back number 1.4.3 The type of call (Emergency, PTS, IFT, RET) 1.4.4 Chief complaint/reason for transfer 1.4.4.1 As needed for IFT or RET, as k if the case has been coordinated wi

th the receiving facility. 1.4.5 Complete all necessary information required for the Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) for regular/ routine calls which may include: 1.4.5.1 Caller name and contact number 1.4.5.2 Name, age, gender, nationality, a nd HC number of the patient. 1.4.5.2.1 By entering the HC number in the CAD, the rest of the patient details appear automatically. Therefore, the call taker shall verify the name and the age additional to the HC number. 1.4.5.3 Weight Actual or estimated (inclusive of body mass). 1.4.5.4 The exact location/facility where the patient will be picked up includes floor/unit, room, and bed. 1.4.5.4.1 If the transport is from a residence, determine/document if there are any stairs or other obstacles present that prevent access to the residence that may require special equipment or additional manpower. 1.4.5.5 Destination where the patient is going exactly, for example floor/unit/ward, room, and bed. 1.4.5.5.1 If the transport is to a residence, determine/document if there are any stairs or other obstacles present to gain access to the residence that may require special equipment or additional manpower.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 199 1.4.5.6 As needed for IFT or RET, obtain receiving facility contact number and contact person. 1.4.5.6.1 This can be used to verify transfer details and confirm acceptance of the patient . 1.4.5.7 What is the patient diagnosis and pertinent medical history? 1.4.5.7.1 Although the HCC CAD utilizes the ‘chief complaint’ field to capture the reason for transfer, the patient diagnosis and clinical condition should be documented in ‘special considerations’ as to the reason the patient has to go by ambulance. 1.4.5.8 Medical needs and transport requirements (may include O2/suction/IV therapy, or ambulatory requirements based on patient request consistent with 1.4.5.4.1 and/or 1.4.5.3, etc.) 1.4.5.9 Call taker shall ask mobility q uestionnaire whenever applicable. 1.4.5.10 Will additional personnel be accompanying the patient? 1.4.5.11 Does the patient have any communicable disease? 1.4.6 Call in take is not complete unless all fields in CAD are filled with responses to questions from (points 1.4.5.1 thru 1.4.5.10). 1.4.6.1 If the answer to the question is not applicable, the call taker will select NONE in the applicable CAD

field. 1.4.7 For emergency calls, as to not delay Ambulance dispatch, limited key information will be gathered (points 1.4.1 thru 1.4.5 .2 refer to point 9 detailed steps). 1.4.8 For booked cases; 1.4.8.1 COLLECTION TIME - means the time the ambulance crew needs to "Collect" the patient from Home/Facility. 1.4.8.2 APPOINTMENT TIME - means the time the patient is expected to see the Doctor, or rather begin thei r procedure/treatment/assessment. 1.5 Consider calls reported by HMCAS clinical or administrative staff via the HCC line to be routine calls and be process the same way as other calls. 1.6 Report any unusual call/demand to the shift supervisor immediately . 2. HCC Cal l taking Process (Step by Step ) : Once the call comes into the screen, immediately answer the call within 1 - 2 rings. The call taker will be opening CAD call taker board which related to the section assigned (PTS, IFT, RET). Identify and verify the exact location and destination of call (zone, street, building number), telephone number and document it accurately in CAD. Check the availability of booking by reviewing the hourly cases for PTS/IFT scheduled calls. Use ProQA to process the questionin g and identi

fy the ProQA Chief Complaint and the Dispatch priority for IFT cases. Also obtain patient diagnosis and clinical condition. For Scheduled Ambulance Requests advice the caller to fill and send patient transportation request form Via Email or T hrough online booking request form. If the nurse requesting immediate transfer for a Scheduled ambulance request (patient going to same or low level of care facility) use

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 200 ProQA to triage the case and escalate the request to shift supervisor/Oscar or clinic al desk for further decision. Terminate the call once all the information obtained and the booking time confirmed. Fill in all fields in CAD. 3. Address identification & verification : 3.1 Verify all addresses reported by asking the caller to repeat the address once again. 3.2 If exact location is unknown/unclear, notify the UTL/shift supervisor immediately and do the following: 3.2.1 Advise the caller to find the Blue Plate - Qatar Address Reference System (QARS) in the entrance of the building and provide the 3 c onsecutive plate numbers (zone, street and building number). 3.2.2 When the QARS number is not available, advice the caller to describe the directions to get the ambulance to his location. 3.2.3 If necessary, request an alternative contact number of someone able to gu ide the ambulance to the location. 3.2.4 If all attempts fail, advise the caller to send someone to rendezvous with the ambulance at a mutually known location. 4. Taking non - standard calls: 4.1 If a call disconnects/is dropped before it can be determined if

assistance is needed, attempt a call back twice. Make additional attempts to contact the caller if there are no other calls in the queue, escalate the Issue to UTL/Oscar on duty. 4.2 Prank calls: Treat as a real emergency until proven otherwise. Call back the called part y and query to determine if further action or an emergency response is required. 5. New calls reported via radio by on - duty HMC Ambulance Personnel: 5.1 The dispatcher of the channel will need to verify if this is an emergency call request or a transport request. 5.2 For Non - Emergency Transport request, dispatcher will need to verify if the call is not booked in the CAD system. 5.3 Ask the call taker to open a new request at CAD. 5.4 If it’s an emergency case, take basic information and notify the UTL/shift supervisor. 5.5 Instru ct the unit to change to respective emergency radio channels (Channels) and report the incident. 5.6 Shift supervisor/ O peration officer will need to call NCC operation officer desk and report the incident. (Refer to SOP 3.14 for more details). 5.7 Extra resources will be sent as per the request of the clinical crew on - scene. 6. Foreign Language calls: 6.1 Process the call as per n
ormal 6.2 Alert the Case Controller and UTL on duty

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 201 6.3 Attempt to identify the language of the c aller by asking what country they are from or interpretation of their response to various language options. 6.4 Attempt to obtain the address by use of landmarks, shops, or schools in the area. 6.5 The Case Controller will determine if any HMCAS staff on duty is able to speak the caller’s language and transfer the call to the appropriate line extension accordingly. 6.6 UTL or Case Controller can contact NCC (National Command Center) to obtain more information and get assistance. 7. Redundant Calls : (When several calls ha ve been received on the same call); 7.1 Confirm that patient HC number, location and incident is the same as an existing CAD case record. 7.2 Update the CAD with any changes and additional information and inform the dispatcher of any changes. 7.3 The call taker must r eassure the caller that an ambulance has already been dispatched to the call or the case already booked and updated and disconnect the call. 8. Inter - Facility Patient Transfers: 8.1 Diagnostic, procedures, assessments, and consultations as well as Airport cases and other prescheduled appoi

ntments shall be treated as routine HCC calls. 8.2 For IFT calls that require ProQA in accordance with Appendix 3.22 , call taker should use ProQA after taking basic information. 8.3 Basic Information includes the caller details, patient details, patient pick up and patient destination. 8.4 If unable to understand medical terminology, then the call must be transferred to another Call Taker capable of handling the call notify the UTL or Ca se Controller. 8.5 Once information obtained from the caller and the case confirmed is not a routine IFT call, and requires emergency intervention, escalate as necessary to the Clinical Desk for clinical review and triage. 8.5.1 If the case is referred to by another hospital (not where the patient is located), the call taker will contact the sending facility to verify the transport details. 8.5.2 For example, Sidra Medicine calls HCC and needs a patient moved from PEC Al Sadd to Sidra. After completing the interrogation, t he call taker will contact PEC Al Sadd to confirm. 8.6 Notify the UTL/shift supervisor 8.7 If the nurse requesting immediate transfer for a Routine or scheduled ambulance call (patient going to same or low level of care facility

) use ProQA to triage the case and e scalate the request to shift supervisor/Oscar or clinical desk for further decision. 8.8 Call taker must book the case in the CAD and not refer the caller to do online booking. 9. HMC Immediate transfer/PHCC calls: 9.1 For PHCC calls once call taker obtains exact loc ation immediately shall notify case controller/UTL to start initial unit dispatch. This is a mandatory step for both P1 and P2 calls. 9.1.1 The call taker shall ensure the followings : 9.1.1.1 The call taker shall continue gathering patient information (HC Number) confir ming patient name and age then do Partial registration .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 202 9.1.1.2 If a call appears to be an extreme emergency, or the caller stated that the patient is not yet admitted or particularly PHCC cannot handle this type of emergency the call taker shall immediately notif y the case controller or UTL. 9.1.1.3 Process the interrogation without delay. If the patient’s HC number is unknown by the caller or not available for any valid reason call taker can type “00000000” and add patient info manually and if the caller is not able to answer any question, then document as unknown and do Partial Registration. 9.1.1.4 After obtaining the minimum information the call taker should reassure the caller that resources are coordinated for dispatch immediately. 9.1.1.5 Continue the interrogation through CAD and ProQA and update the Dispatch controller and UTL. 9.1.2 Once notified the Dispatcher shall ensure the followings : 9.1.2.1 If SAS unit can be deployed, the dispatcher will immediately dispatch the crew via the radio towards the PHCC and documentation in CAD can be ad ded when the CAD is available to the dispatcher. 9.1.2.2 If a SAS unit is not available, the dispatcher shall immediately

notify the ULT who shall start coordinating a resource from EAS by calling NCC DTL. UTL shall request for the closest unit to PHCC to change t o the tango channel for a P1 or P2 call. Refer to SOP 3.14 for more details. 9.1.2.3 Dispatch controller and UTL shall closely follow up all PHCC calls/any immediate transfer from the receipt time to on scene time and shall mitigate any response time delay. 9.2 HMC Immediate Transfer 9.3 The call taker shall do partial registration at any time of interrogation if the case is going to ICU or has a critical complaint. (P1 obvious cases, code blue) otherwise the case shou ld be processed as normal IFT and process the call in ProQA until final code. 10. Online Booking Requests : 10.1 The dedicated call taker for online booking must frequently check the online booking dashboard for any pending requests either by monitoring the pop - up messages or refreshing the search option continuously. 10.2 Upon receiving a new request, the call taker must process it immediately by either acceptance or rejection. 10.3 The call taker shall accept the booking request whenever the information is complete, and a slot is available and there is no need to call the

facility (unless required for the exceptional situation only) also the call taker should not expect a confirmation call from the facility. 10.4 The Call taker shall reject any request with incomplete/uncl ear information or whenever the requested slot is not available. It’s mandatory to add a comment about the rejection reason to justify the rejection. Example: mention what exact info is missing or propose a new slot based on the availability. 10.5 Case Controll er should double check the dashboard frequently for requests that need to be actioned and to inquire/coordinate with the call taker accordingly. 10.6 The Call taker/case controller may have to consult the Clinical desk for some special cases for further screeni ng if needed.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 203 10.7 If the nurse requesting immediate transfer for a Routine or scheduled ambulance call (patient going to same or low level of care facility) escalate the request to shift supervisor/Oscar or clinical desk for further decision or if advised call the facility and triage the case by using ProQA. 10.8 Call taker must book the case in the CAD and not refer the caller to do online booking. 11. Email Booking Requests : 11.1 Call taker must check the shared mailbox ( Amb ulanceBooking@hamad.qa ) frequently throughout the shift for any new email request for ambulance booking. 11.2 Upon receiving a new email - booking request call taker shall validate the request and process booking the request in CAD with No delay based on slot ava ilability. 11.3 If the request is accepted, the call taker shall book the case accordingly and attach the booking request to the relevant CAD number and doesn’t need to call back unless required. 11.4 Once the call taker processes the email request, he/she should re ply back to the sender within 15 mins as acknowledgement or acceptance of the request or otherwise to reply back as rejection or inq

uiring further about any missing/unclear info and if requested time slot is full, reject the booking and propose a new time. 11.5 When the booking is completed, ‘mark /flag the email as complete so that staff will know that the booking is complete and avoid any duplication. 11.6 In case another Call taker received a follow up call and processed the request he/she must immediately inform the dedicated email - booking person as well as to notify the case controller or UTL to avoid any duplication. 11.7 The call taker may refer/consult the case to clinical desk whenever needed. Case Controller should double check the shared email inbox frequently during the shift for requests that need to be actioned and to inquire/coordinate with the call taker accordingly. 11.8 If a request seemed to be an emergency transfer, the call taker must immediately notify the UTL or the Oscar or clinical des k or if advised call the facility and triage the case by using ProQA. 11.9 Call taker must book the case in the CAD 12. MTPS Protocols 45 - 46 - 47 : 12.1 All calls for ambulance requests must be processed in ProQA based on the below definition for each protocol: 12.1.1 Protocol 45 : Specialized Unsched

uled Up - Care Transports; Used when a patient is transported to a higher, more acute level of care. 12.1.2 Protocol 46 : Scheduled Interfacility Transfers (Routine): Used when a patient is routinely transported to another facility with a simila r level of care. 12.1.3 Protocol 47 : Mental Health Transfer: Used when a patient with diagnosis or symptoms of mental illness is transported to a medical or support facility. 12.2 Case Exempted from ProQA : Ambulance request received via booking, emails and return trip s (unless there is an upgrade in care or request by nurse for immediate transport which call taker must process the call in ProQA and notify immediate supervisor or clinical desk ) .
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 204 Guide for Protocol Use

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 205 SOP 3.23 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.23 HCC CALL DISPAT CHING Implementation Date 02 June 2019 Purpose To standardize the HMCAS Communication Center Dispatch Procedure for cases received by HCC . Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF, SPECIFICALLY C ALL DISPATCHERS 1. General rules for Dispatch: 1.1 The dispatcher receiving shall use the CAD dispatcher board for the assigned section (PTS, IFT, RET). 1.2 PTS dispatcher needs to review all the cases and bookings to plan the unit calls based on the locations and special requirements. 1.3 Obtain additional information and relay to responding units whenever needed. 1.4 Acknowledge the total number of units and the type of ambulances at the beginning of dispatching. 1.5 One dispatcher is assigned to monitor each operational channel. 1.6 All units responding to cases shall be tracked in CAD, assigned to an actual incident: 2. Dispatch Priority: 2.1 Units to be dispatched in accordance with the Dispatch Matrix. 2.2 In particular for all PHCC or whenever applicable for IFT imme

diate transfer obvious P1 cases the dispatcher shall initially dispatch the response unit as P1 based on the initial information (Partial resignation). 2.3 Dispatcher to follow up and should advise the response unit to downgrade the response or continue as P1 based on the final ProQA response code. 3. Role of the Dispatcher: 3.1 Monitor and continuously observe the screen for pending list. 3.2 Coordinate with the Case Controller or shift su pervisor to make sure that calls are dispatched based on the criteria. 3.3 Review CAD maps to visualize the case’s location. 3.4 Open the unit status screen to visualize ALL available HMCAS units close to the cases location. 3.5 Assign the CLOSEST unit to the case and monitor the timing and the collection time. If a special unit is needed and is on a different radio channel, coordinate with the relevant Dispatcher to instruct the unit to switch radio channels. 3.6 Initiate the dispatch by calling the unit Call sign twice e .g. "Calling Tango Alpha One, One – Tango Alpha One, One". On reply, advise the unit that they have a call and to check the MDT for more details. If MDT not available, the unit to be guided by Radio with the case details. 3.7

Simultaneously update the unit sta tus in CAD accurately and monitor the accuracy of MDT user updates (when the MDT is available). 3.8 Reply on all the requests popping up on dispatching board and update the status accordingly (stood down, breaks or unit unavailability).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 206 3.9 Must be fully aware wit h the unit assigned in fixed location as starting point and should assigned to the nearest patient in their area. 3.10 Should closely monitor the location and movements of the units. 3.11 Must coordinate with duty UTL to account the different type of ambulance avail able to be dispatch accordingly to needs of the patient. 3.12 Document important information, incident and event that transcribe during the call and logged in the CAD Call Notes. 4. Dispatcher Information Sharing : 4.1 Necessary information to be given to the respondin g units should include but not be limited to the following: 4.1.1 All details of the address (Zone name/number, Street name/number, building name/number including the floor and flat number if applicable. 4.1.2 Landmarks near the address e.g. names of Roundabouts and b uildings. 4.1.3 Additional address information specific to special situation and explicitly said by the caller should be documented in the Call record and relayed to the responding crew e.g. ‘patient is in the annexure behind the villa…’ 4.2 Update the unit with further information about the location

and the patient condition as soon as it is received and updated in CAD case record. This should include: 4.2.1 Additional details of the location if required 4.2.2 Patient status change 4.2.3 Patient information or details 4.2.4 All availabl e safety information, including the presence/risk of infection (e.g. communicable diseases). 4.2.5 Guide the responding unit to the identified location through the shortest and quickest route or by setting a rendezvous point with the caller or with other respond ing agencies. 5. Resource allocation : 5.1 Assign and deploy extra resources as necessary (in consultation with shift supervisor) in cases of: 5.1.1 More than one patient found on scene 5.1.2 Special requirement or consideration 5.1.3 Safety concerns/high risk situations 5.2 If the fir st assigned unit is unavailable or didn’t answer the radio call within 30sec: 5.2.1 Call the unit again by radio 5.2.2 Notify the UTL/shift supervisor 5.2.3 Contact the unit using other alternative methods; call by phone or mobile. 5.2.4 The communications supervisor shall notify the operations supervisor of the delayed unit and make necessary notification and follow up. 6. Follow up with responding unit
s : 6.1 Follow up the location and status of the responding unit until arrival on scene.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 207 6.2 Using the “comments” free text area of the call record, document all information and events during response to the scene. 6.3 Accurately capture the reported “On Scene” time (or monitor accuracy of MDT user unit status capture when applicable). 6.4 Follow up the status of the unit on scene 6.5 Document all reported information or events in the CAD. 6.6 Upon arrival at the receiving hospital/facility or the residence : 6.6.1 Log the reported time in the CAD Call record 6.6.2 Follow up the status of the unit at the facility or residence. 6.6.3 Notify the UTL and the operations supervisor for units staying more than 15 minutes and/or not answering radio. 6.6.4 Document any applicable information in the call record e.g. the reason for delay. 6.6.5 Review the documentation in the call record, update and complete it accurately. 6.6.6 For units return ing same patient back, dispatcher informs call taker to make the return case to be assigned for the same responding unit. 6.7 When the unit reports availability : 6.7.1 Log the reported time in the call record 6.7.2 Log all administrative data 6.7.3 Close the call record of

the incident 6.7.4 Assess the current standby coverage and immediately dispatch the available unit to standby point. 7. Requests for HELP/ASSISTANCE from Primary Crew while on scene : 7.1 When requests for Scene Safety assistance is made: 7.1.1 Notify the Case Controller & UTL ab out the request as applicable, dispatch operation supervisor if needed. 7.1.2 Notify the on - scene unit that the requested assistance is responding when appropriate, for example like MIR1 or other TRS unit. 7.1.2.1 If the unit requesting assistance is at a facility and n eeds a CCP (Charlie), consider utilization of the Retrievals Charlie by coordinating with the TRS Clinical Coordinator. 7.1.3 Have any secondary units switch to the channel of the primary unit needing assistance. 7.1.4 Monitor in system any other unit near the drop lo cation of patient for help. 7.2 In cases where help is not available, should coordinate with supervisor and unit inform unit requesting for help. 7.3 When Special Scene or patient management assistance is requested: 7.3.1 Inform the UTL the need to activate the requeste d units e.g. ICU unit, MIR unit, Green Bus, 4X4 Ambulance or wheelchair unit. 7.3.2 Dispatch the required

resources to the incident. 7.3.3 Have any secondary units switch to the channel of the primary unit needing assistance. 8. Unit moving to hospital and/or residence n eeding help (patient condition change) :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 208 8.1 Instruct the unit to change to Clinical 2 channel and consult if Charlie unit needed. 8.2 The closest available Charlie unit will be dispatched by NCC. 8.3 The unit will update with the latest patient’s status and conditions. 8.4 Arrange for a rendezvous point and guide the dispatched unit. 8.5 Re - establish transport priority towards hospital after Charlie Unit rendezvous if HCC dispatcher handled the case not NCC dispatcher. 8.6 Inform UTL/shift supervisor. 8.7 Record all informa tion in comments area of the call records. Information to be documented includes but not restricted to: 8.7.1 The time when the first unit requested help 8.7.2 Reason for requesting help 8.7.3 Safety concerns 8.7.4 Rendezvous time of both units 9. Delayed Cases : 9.1 All cases with a pot ential delayed response must be escalated to UTL/Dispatch controller. 9.2 UTL/Dispatch controller or delegee must call the caller (patient/family/nurse) to inform them of the delay. 9.2.1 A ‘DELAYED RESPONSE’ is defined as an expected arrival greater than the collec tion time. 9.3 This call should be made between 15 - 30 minutes at minimum prior to the

collection time and documented in the case notes. 9.4 Advanced calling allows those with scheduled appointments the opportunity to cancel the ambulance BEFORE it arrives at the p ick - up location. 9.5 When necessary and possible, HCC staff may provide the best ‘Estimated Time of Arrival’ to the caller (patient/family/nurse) in consultation/coordination with the Dispatch Controller or assigned crew . 9.5.1 Understanding that this is an estimate , it is appropriate that HMC staff just simply advise that the ambulance will be there as soon as possible. 9.5.2 Understanding that situations change, if the response is extended beyond the time initially described, additional calls will be made as needed to th e caller (patient/family/nurse) by the UTL to keep them informed. 9.6 HCC staff will not make 'excuses' as to the delay. For example, citing staffing issues, mechanical issues, 'too busy', etc. UNLESS approved by the UTL. 9.7 As an example, at 09:30 as a part of t he calling process the HCC staff can simply state, 9.7.1 "Regarding the ambulance booking for 10:00… I apologize, but it looks like we are running behind schedule and the ambulance will arrive between 10:15 - 10:20, is tha

t okay?" 9.8 If the booking has an appointment time, HCC staff can simply state, 9.8.1 "Regarding the ambulance booking for 10:00… I apologize, but it looks like we are running behind schedule and the ambulance will arrive between 10:15 - 10:20. I

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 209 understand you have an appointment at 11:00. Do you know if th e doctor/facility will see you if you arrive late? Based on travel time, we won't get there until 11:30." 9.9 For other non - urgent cases like discharges and returns, it may also be appropriate to state, 9.9.1 "Regarding the ambulance booking for 10:00… I apologize, but it looks like we are running behind schedule. The ambulance is coming from “X” area and based on traffic conditions could arrive within the next 30 - 45 minutes, is that okay?" 9.10 HCC staff will always leave it in the hands of the caller (patient /family/nurse) to decide whether the ambulance will continue AS WELL AS coordinate the delay and acceptance with the doctor/facility. 9.10.1 If IFT, the sending facility needs to communicate with the receiving facility to avoid any unnecessary travel. 9.11 In addition to documenting the delay, HCC staff will document the result: 9.11.1 CALLER INFORMED OF DELAY, EXPECTED PICK UP NOW 10:20 AND THEY ARE OKAY. 9.11.2 CALLER INFORMED OF DELAY, EXPECTED PICK UP NOW 10:20 AND THEY STATED THE DOCTOR WILL STILL SEE THEM IF THEY ARRIVE LATE, THEY ARE OKAY
. 9.11.3 CALLER INFORMED OF DELAY, EXPECTED PICK UP NOW 10:20 AND THEY STATED THEY WILL MISS THEIR APPOINTMENT, CANCEL .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 210 SOP 3.24 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3.24 MANAGING OFFSHO RE CALLS A T NCC Implementation Date 13 March 2018 Purpose To standardize response procedure for Offshore cases requiring a helicopter evacuation of a medical/trauma patient to the Emergency Department. Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS ( NCC) STAFF 1. Background and Purpose : 1.1 HMCAS regularly receive requests to transport medical patients from offshore areas including oil rigs, ships/boats and islands to hospital. Due to the limited access, these cases are serviced by LifeFlight; however, offshore operations impose additional safety risks on HMCAS staff and patients requiring unique dispatch procedures. 1.2 This SOP should be read together with SOP 4.11.A.14 which sets out the procedures relating to the specific LifeFlight aircraft dispatch, coordination, and offshore operations. 2. Definitions: 2.1 A7S stands for “Alpha 7 Sierra” which is the disaster management control center operated by Qatar E nergy and, among other responsibilities, is responsible for coordi

nating offshore medical evacuations. 2.2 DTL means Distribution Team Leader 2.3 LFC means LifeFlight Coordinator 3. Call taking and Dispatching – Calls originating from A7S : 3.1 A7S will call the HMCAS NCC hotline (40442555/40002055) to request helicopter medivac support. 3.2 The HMCAS call taker will receive the call and immediately notify the DTL of the request. 3.2.1 DTL must immediately notify the LifeFlight Coordinator of the incoming A7S offshore request and ad d a note in the CFS of the notification. 3.2.2 DTL should assist the call - taker as necessary and monitor the case progress. 3.2.3 DTL must ensure that the appropriate “A7S Offshore Retrieval Checklist” is used as a guide on actions to be taken and it is completed appr opriately for all A7S offshore cases. ( Appendix 3.24 NCC - LF Offshore Checklist ). 3.3 The HMCAS call taker will take the caller’s name and the address of the patient ( designated helideck name and helideck code ) from A7S and document this properly and accurately in NJM. 3.4 The HMCAS call taker will reconfirm the designated helideck name and code by repeating this back to the A7S caller. 3.5 The HMCAS call taker will identify and verify the telepho

ne number of the caller. 3.6 The HMCA S call taker will advise the caller to stay online for more questions and route the NJM CFS for dispatch. 3.6.1 While the call - taker continues with ProQA interrogation, the HMCAS dispatcher (responsible of that geographical area) will receive the case and assign LF to the CFS and update the LFC.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 211 3.7 The HMCAS call taker will continue ProQA interrogation using Protocol 33 and confirm if there is any communicable disease or contamination with a hazardous substance by asking “Is the patient suspected with a communicable disease or contamination by a hazardous substance?” . 3.7.1 If the answer is YES , the HMCAS call taker must immediately inform the DTL and the LFC, then complete ProQA and provide PDI/PAI as applicable. 3.7.2 If the answer is NO , the HMCAS call taker will remain onlin e with the caller and complete the ProQA and provide PDI and PAI as applicable. 3.8 Once the HMCAS call taker has completed call taking, and before closing the call, he/she should advise the A7S caller that the LFC will contact them shortly. 3.9 Once the HMCAS cal l - taker has closed the call with A7S they must immediately update the LFC and DTL, advising that they have finished with A7S and the case is handed over to the LFC. The call - taker must add a note in the CFS of the completion of call - taking and handover to LifeFlight Coordinator. 4. Call taking and Dispatching – Calls NOT originating from A7S : 4.1 Patients or bysta

nders might call 999 requesting ambulance assistance for calls located offshore. 4.2 The HMCAS call taker will receive the call and handle the case following SOP 3.2 and SOP 3.3 . 4.2.1 If the patient is in a moving vessel, and an appropriate meeting point at a mainland port where an ambulance can be dispatched can be arranged, the call - taker shall assign the address pinpoint at this meeting point and should continue with ProQA interrogation. 4.2.1.1 If necessary and appropriate the call - taker may stay online with the caller for logistic co ordination until an ambulance is with the caller. 4.2.1.2 The dispatcher must assign the nearest appropriate Alpha and, if applicable, Delta unit according to the ETA of the patient’s arrival at the meeting point. If the patient’s ETA exceeds 15 - minutes, the Ambul ance Service response should be priority 2. 4.2.2 If the patient is in a moving vessel, and an appropriate meeting point at a mainland port where an ambulance can be dispatched cannot be arranged, the call - taker must inform the DTL as in 4.3 below, add the coast guard to the CFS, and continue with ProQA interrogation providing all necessary PAIs. 4.2.2.1 Closure of the case can

only be done after full endorsement of the case to the coastguard or the search and rescue team. 4.3 The HMCAS call taker must immediately update the DTL as soon as they become aware of the case being an offshore case. 4.3.1 The DTL must update the LFC of the case as soon as they are notified and advise them the case is not from A7S. 4.3.2 The LFC should immediately call A7S advising them of the incoming call and c heck whether they are aware of the case or not. 4.3.2.1 If the case is being coordinated by A7S, the LFC should advise A7S that we will be waiting on their confirmation and the Offshore Retrieval Request Form before activating LifeFlight. This must be clearly docu mented in NJM at the time of the call by the LFC/DTL.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 212 4.3.2.2 If the case is unknown to A7S, the case should be handled following 4.2 above. 5. Notification Procedure : 5.1 In accordance with SOP 4.11.A.14 : 5.1.1 The LFC will co ntact A7S when call taking is complete to verify further details and update them of LifeFlight dispatch and ETA as soon as possible. 5.1.2 The LFC will notify the Consultant Critical Care Paramedic: Effectiveness and SME: LifeFlight as soon as possible. 5.2 LFC must update the DTL of LifeFlight status, ETA and keep them informed of mission progress. 5.3 DTL will keep Oscar NCC updated on case progress as necessary and appropriate. 5.4 Oscar NCC will ensure regular notification of the Management On - call if needed. 5.5 Oscar NCC w ill oversee the management of the case and, where necessary and in collaboration of LifeFlight and Clinical Desk management, provide feedback to the staff. 5.5.1 If necessary the Oscar NCC, SME: LifeFlight or SME: Clinical Desk may conduct or request further review from QIU team .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 213 SOP 3.26 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Procedure 3.26 HMCAS INFORMATION SYSTEMS ON CALL Implementation Date 13 March 2018 Purpose To deliver 24/7 support for operations on the key operational Ambulance Service IT Systems. Responsibility ALL AMBULANCE SERVIC E STAFF 1. Definitions : 1.1 ePCR : Electronic Patient Care Record 1.2 CAD : CareM onX computer Aided Dispatch System 1.3 MDT : CareM onX Mobile Data Terminal 1.4 SMS : Scheduling Management Systems 1.5 FMS : Fleet Management Systems 1.6 Biometrics : Time and Attendance 1.7 OOH : Out - of core hours 07:00 - 15:00 Sunday – Thursday, excluding HMC public holidays) 2. Procedure : 2.1 Any failure of an IT Device (ePCR/MDT/iPhone) or Ambulance Service software application (incident) identified by staff of Ambulance Operations, Fleet, Scheduling or HCC users must be reported to the on - duty hub/production/supervisor. 2.2 For both Hardware and Software, the hub/production/supervisor will raise an incident with HICT helpdesk either by phone or by email to HICT@hamad.qa , also copied to ASIS_Support@hamad

.qa 2.2.1 Email wil l auto generate a HICT helpdesk incident number . 2.3 Details to be provided are: 2.3.1 Category – this describes the application with the fault 2.3.1.1 ePCR Category is ‘Ambulance Service – ePCR Application’ 2.3.1.2 MDT Category is ‘Ambulance Service – MDT Application’ 2.3.1.3 Fleet Management Category is ‘Ambulance Service – FMS Application’ 2.3.1.4 Scheduling System Category is ‘Ambulance Service – SMS Application’ 2.3.1.5 Control Room CAD Category is ‘Ambulance Service – CAD Application’ 2.3.2 Priority : 2.3.2.1 Pr iority 1: Total failure for all users for the application . 2.3.2.2 Priority 2: Failure of application key functionality affects more than 50 % of users . 2.3.2.3 Priority 3: Single User affected, unable to work . 2.3.3 Incident Description : 2.3.3.1 Provide in as much detail as possible t he details of the incident . 2.4 The staff member must complete an OVA form if there is a damaged device detailing the nature of the damage and the method by which the damage occurred .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 214 2.4.1 The OVA number will be given to the Hub/Production supervisor . 2.5 For hardware issues: 2.5.1 Staff will exchange the faulty device with the Hub/Production supervisor . 2.5.2 Hub/Production supervisor will arrange to bring the defective unit to the IT delivery team ( Ambulance HQ) with Helpdesk reference and OVA form during normal office hours (Sun day – Thursday 07:00 - 15:00) . 2.5.3 The IT Delivery team log the incoming device and replace the defective unit with another device . 2.5.4 This will be returned to the hub/production supervisor . 3. Notification : 3.1 The Staff member will then endorse the incident to the Hub/Production supervisor and the staff member will continue their duty. 3.2 HICT Helpdesk will verify the incident with assistance from HICT User Support and IT delivery team . 3.3 HICT User Support, HICT Helpdesk or the IT Delivery Team will call /email the Hub/P roduction supervisor to provide support for the resolution of the incident . 3.4 Hub/Production supervisor will inform Production Manager and Oscar on duty. Production manager will inform the IT delivery support number with the HICT ti

cket number for further as sistance. Oscar will inform Silver Communications. 3.5 For escalation of the incident, the on - call Silver Communications will then escalate to the IT Delivery Team and HCS Executives. 3.6 For software issues that cannot be resolved with the IT Delivery team and/or the Local Service Engineer, HICT User Support will then raise the incident to Valentia who will be in contact with the Hub/Production supervisor until resolved.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 215 SOP 3.27 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 3.27 EMD CERTIFICATI ON Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To inform all Ambulance Service Medical Dispatchers (ASMD’s) of the requirements for EMD certification and re - certification. Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS S TAFF (SPECIFICALLY C ALL TAKERS AND DISPATCHERS) 1. Ambulance Service Medical Dispatchers Training PROCEDURE : 1.1 EMERGENCY MEDICAL DISPATCHER CERTIFICATION : 1.1.1 All current and future personnel employed in the position of Ambulance Service Medical Dispatchers (ASMD) are required to obtain Emergency Medical Dispatcher (EMD) and the Emergency Telecommunication (ETC) Certifications with the International Academy of Emergency Dispatch (IAED). 1.1.2 Hamad M edical Corporation Emergency Services will provide the necessary training and re - training opportunities to facilitate obtaining and maintaining this certification. 1.1.3 In the event that an employee does not pass the certification examination on the first attem pt, he/she will be provided with supportive tra

ining based on feedback received from the IAED. Student who does not pass the certification exams will then be invited to take the re - test. 1.1.4 Should the ASMD still be unsuccessful in passing the re - test, they wi ll be invited to participate in a complete EMD course in the future. They may then take the certification examination and, if necessary, the re - test. 1.2 RECERTIFICATION : 1.2.1 ASMDs are required to maintain current EMD and ETC certifications. This requires completi on of a minimum of 24 hours Continuing Dispatch Education (CDE) per two - year period, achieving a passing score in an open book EMD/ETC examinations at two - year intervals, and maintaining current CPR certification. 1.2.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Health Coordinat ion Services Quality Improvement & Training section will provide all necessary opportunities for completion of the Continuing Dispatch Education requirement and CPR recertification. It will also maintain CDE records and a record of EMD/ETC certification st atus. 1.2.3 CDE Program Management: 1.2.3.1 The Medical Dispatch Quality Review team shall be responsible for defining the topics that the CDE program will address based on the QI report

s of previous month. 1.2.3.2 Appropriate CDE topics may be identified in a number of ways: 1.2.3.2.1 As a result of the Quality reviewers’ recommendations 1.2.3.2.2 Via requests from EMDs 1.2.3.3 The Quality Improvement and training section for Communications shall be responsible for the following: 1.2.3.3.1 Scheduling educational opportunities as necessary to address the nee ds identified above.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 216 1.2.3.3.2 Ensuring that necessary educational opportunities are: 1.2.3.3.2.1 Delivered by qualified personnel 1.2.3.3.2.2 Adequate in their content/format to address the identified learning need/objective. 1.2.3.3.2.3 Relevant to EMDs and their associated work 1.2.3.3.2.4 Attended by all EMDs unless it is designed for specific role in the call centre like the case controller or the Supervisor. 1.2.3.3.3 Ensuring that appropriate records are maintained regarding the CDE program in the QIU filing system and for each EMD individually. 1.2.3.3.4 Ensuring that a CDE l esson plans are completed to an adequate standard for all classroom - based education. 1.2.4 Types Of CDE: 1.2.4.1 The minimum CDE requirement in any given year shall be 12 hours of completed CDE per EMD, at least eight hours of which shall be didactic in nature. 1.2.4.2 In addit ion to the CDE hours, types, and topics discussed above, each EMD must maintain current CPR certification. 1.2.4.3 The bulk of the subject matter accepted as fulfilling IAED requirements will be directly related to the science of Emergency Medical Dispatch and the use of the MPDS and

ProQA software. However, other Ambulance Service - related material will be considered by the NAED for its educational relevance. 1.2.4.4 The following are acceptable formats and their associated maximum hours for CDE: 1.2.4.4.1 Official Academy - Created E ducational Products and Programs (no Maximum) (Journals, Advancement series, navigator…) 1.2.4.4.2 Workshops and seminars (16 hours maximum) 1.2.4.4.3 Attendance at planning and management meetings (8 hours maximum) 1.2.4.4.4 Quality assurance and case review (8 hours maximum) 1.2.4.4.5 Review of EMS related audio, video, and written materials (4 hours maximum) 1.2.4.4.6 Teaching (4 hours maximum) 1.2.4.4.7 MPDS protocol review (4 hours maximum) 1.2.4.4.8 Miscellaneous, such as ride - along (4 hours maximum) 1.2.5 CDE Program Objectives: 1.2.5.1 Development of a better understanding of telecommunications and of the EMD’s specific roles and responsibilities. 1.2.5.2 Improving skill in the use or application of all component parts of the MPDS, including interrogation and prioritization.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 217 1.2.5.3 Providing opportunities for discussion practice of skills, and for constructive feedback of performance. 1.3 CERTIFICATION EXPIRATION, REVOCATION OR SUSPENSION : 1.3.1 Should an ASMD’s EMD certification expires or become void due to suspension or revocation, the employee will be removed from EMD call - taking until such time as the EMD certification is reinstated or renewed. 1.4 ADDITIONAL TRAINING REQUIREMENTS : 1.4.1 All personnel employed in the position of ASMD (Call takers/Dispatcher) are required to obtain the technical training that allow them to operate efficiently in their area of assignment. 1.4.2 The following are the mandatory orientations that both call takers and dispatcher should complete prior to be signed off to process emergency calls: 1.4.2.1 ProQA training 1.4.2.2 Communication’s Standards Operating P rocedures and Policies 1.4.2.3 Mapping System i ncluding 1.4.2.4 NJM application: The Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) application for resources management and deployment of units. 1.4.2.5 Life - X system ( T elephone & Radio systems operation Consoles). 1.4.2.6 Telephone techniques 1.4.2.7 Radio
system operation and voice procedures 1.4.2.8 Mobile Data terminals (MDT) 1.4.2.9 Mass Casualty Incident orientation

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 223 SOP 3.29 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Health Coordination and S upport Services Training and Quality Improvement D epartment Procedure 3.29 D HP AND TRAINING REQU IREMENTS FOR AMBULAN CE SERVICE MEDICAL DISPATCHERS Implementation Date 08 December 2020 Purpose To ensure that all Health Coordination Services (HCS) ASMD Staff are on - boarded appropriately and receive consistent orientation and training required by HMC and JCI . Responsibility ALL HCS AMBULANCE MEDICAL DISPATCHERS 1. ONBOARDING OF AMBULANCE SERVICES MEDICAL DISPATCHERS (ASMD) : 1.1 Minimum Required Orientation: This Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) describes the minimum required orientation and training for all Ambulance Services Medical Dispatchers (ASMD) to be completed during the initial induction to meet HMC, International Academy of Emergency Dispatch (IAED) and Joint Commissions International (JCI) requirements. 1.2 HMC Human Resources (HR) Orientation : Upon arrival, all new staff will i nitially report to HR to complete orientation procedures and documentation. Once this process is completed,

he/she will then report to the Health Coordination Services (HCS) Training Department for the on - boarding training. 1.3 HMC - HCS On - Boarding : 1.3.1 HCS Trainin g Unit will assist the new ASMD staff with completion of the training based on the below checklists: 1.3.1.1 HMC Corporate Orientation Checklist 1.3.1.2 HMC - AS Orientation Checklist 1.3.1.3 Departmental/Job Specific (ASMD) Orientation Checklist 1.3.2 The new staff member will attend the following lectures required by the corporate the HMCAS group: 1.3.2.1 Dress Code 1.3.2.2 Training Unit House Rules 1.3.2.3 HMCAS Infection Control (or HMC e - learning module equivalent). 1.3.2.4 HMCAS Fire Safety (or HMC e - learning module equivalent). 1.3.2.5 HMCAS Environmental Safety (or HMC e - learning module equivalent). 1.3.2.6 HMCAS Quality Improvement and Patient Safety (HMC e - learning module equivalent). 1.3.2.7 HMC Major Incidents (e - learning module) 1.3.2.8 Introduction to the OVA reporting platform 1.3.3 The new staff member will be iss ued the following documents and attend the relevant training as preparation to the ASMD job: 1.3.3.1 HMCAS group Orientation Book (Hard Copy) 1.3.3.2 HMCAS group Standard Operating P
rocedures Books (Soft Copy) 1.3.3.3 HMCAS group policies and operation plans (9032, 9012 etc.) (So ft Copy)

Staff members need to ensure that they have an effective facial fit in ord er to ensure proper protection. 1.3.4.3 Should the staff member “fail” the fit test he/she will be re - tested with a different size/mask until the correct fit is found. 1.3.4.4 Should a “failed” test be the result of the staff member refusing to remove facial hair, the sta ff member will be required to sign a

waiver. A copy of the waiver will be saved in the staff member’s file and a copy will be sent to Operations. 1.3.5 HCS Training Unit will assist the staff with the application for his/her HMC username creation (this can only be done once the staff member has received his/her Corporate ID card which is the responsibility of the HMC HR department to issue during the HR Orientation). 1.3.6 HCS Training Unit will start a hard file for the new staff member to house all records and docume ntation pertaining to the above orientation/training. Once the staff member training data is loaded onto the Scheduling Management System (SMS), the relevant documentation will then be uploaded onto their electronic profile. 1.3.7 All the above training represen ts the minimum requirements before staff members attend patient - facing call taking/dispatching or any ride along. 1.4 Ambulance Service Medical Dispatch Course and Initial Training : 1.4.1 HCS Training Unit will provide an orientation for all newly hired Ambulance Se rvice Medical Dispatchers (ASMD) for both call taking and dispatching skills. This course is required for staff before they can apply licensure with the Department of Healthcare

Professions. The Course will include but not limited to: 1.4.1.1 The didactic course generic for both National Command Center (NCC) and Health Coordination Center (HCC) and will include all materials relevant for both centers (Customer Service, EMD/ETC courses) some specific training will be given to the staff according to their cen ter of assignment (the Computer Aided Dispatch, Telephone system); 1.4.1.1.1 Basic Life Support Course (BLS) 1.4.1.1.2 Emergency Medical Dispatch Course (EMD)

ANNUAL TRAINING REQUIREMENTS AND MINIMUM MEDICAL DISPATCH HOURS FOR ASMDs : 2.1 Introduction : 2.1.1 Licensure with DHP is dictated for all HMC Health Coordination Service ASMD staff members. 2.1.2 In order to be able to recertify and maintain medical dispatch currency the EMD/ETC courses and re - license with DHP, ASMD staff will be required to meet minimum annual training requiremen ts. 2.1.3 The minimum standards are a combination of those requirements set forth by the Department of Healthcare Professions (DHP), Joint Commission International (JCI), and the HCS executive management members. 2.1.4 All minimum requirements as described in this sec tion must be met by all ASMD licensed staff. 2.2 Definitions : 2.2.1 Continuous Professional Development (CPD) is any training, course, or learning activities, which meet the standards of DHP and is accredited or accepted by the DHP for CPD hours. 2.2.2 The three primary H CS Core CPD activities run by the HCS Training and QI Depart

ment that run continuously and cover the following topics: 2.2.2.1 Dispatch Life Support (DLS) Protocols Course 2.2.2.2 Trauma Protocols Course 2.2.2.3 Medical Protocols Course 2.3 Minimum Requirements for Ambulance Service Medical Dispatchers : 2.3.1 Continuous EMD related Professional Development hours – minimum of 24 hours per 2 years as outlined by IAED. 2.3.2 In - date HMC Basic Life Support (BLS) Course must be completed every 2 years. 2.3.3 DLS protocols Course must be completed once every year. 2.3.4 Trauma protocols Course must be completed once every year. 2.3.5 Medical protocols Course must be completed once every year. 2.3.6 To maintain the privilege of ASMD, staff must maintain shift hours as ASMD or ASMD related supervisory jobs – minimum of 2 to 4 shifts per month. HCS deem the following areas of ASMD practice as being patient care for maintenance of clinical hours: 2.3.6.1 Any hours allocated shift at HCC and/or NCC for call taking, dispatching, Quality review, Dispatch training or Supervisory roles. 2.3.6.2 Any hours allocated shift within MCU/MCC unit and/or deployed special operation role where the ASMD is required to respond to Major Incidents.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 227 2.3.6.3 Any hours allocated shift at Hamad International Airport watch - room. 2.3.6.4 Covering the on - call rota where the nature of th e period of on - call is to respond following a Major Incident activation and/or to provide Medical Dispatch command - based decisions or Medical Dispatch advice outside of a major incident. 2.3.6.5 HCC/NCC Event coverage 3. REPORTING OF COMPLIANCE TO MANDATORY CLINICAL TRAINING : 3.1 Reporting Frequency : A compliance report will be provided to the HCS Head of Professions each end of semester. 3.2 Report Content : 3.2.1 Percentage of staff who that are compliant with each of the HCSS Core Continuing Professional Development training modules: 3.2.1.1 Dispatch Life Support (DLS) Protocol Course 3.2.1.2 Trauma Protocol Course 3.2.1.3 Medical Protocol Course 3.2.1.4 CDE Courses 3.2.2 Percentage of staff that are compliant with the HMCAS group offered safety courses will be tracked and provided by HMCAS training unit: 3.2.2.1 Fire Safety 3.2.2.2 Infection Control 3.2.2.3 QPS Training 3.2.2.4 Environmental Safety 3.3 DHP Validation of Clinical Training Compliance Reports : 3.3.1 Continui

ng Professional Development courses is highly recommended to be accredited by the DHP. Signed attendance records and course co ntent shall be maintained, as required by DHP, for review and verification. 4. SCHEDULING OF CORE HCS CPD COURSES FOR ASMDs : 4.1 Scheduling of CPD sessions : 4.1.1 It is the responsibility of the ASMDs to coordination with HCS training unit to book their core CPD courses. 4.1.2 Each department within HCS should maintain a member within the department to act as a liaison between his/her department, HCSS Training Unit and the Scheduling supervisor regarding the scheduling of their staff to CPD activities. 4.1.3 Staff should moni tor their schedule and ensure that they access the online content when applicable and complete all pre - course activities at least 2 weeks prior to attending the course. 4.1.4 The Training Unit will release a CPD schedule to all departments each 3 months. 4.1.5 All sch eduling of CPD events should be done by the Scheduling supervisor and an updated schedule of events should be sent to the Training Unit. 4.1.6 Any queries regarding the scheduling of Training sessions should be taken up with the staff member’s department liaison or th
e Scheduling supervisor directly.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 228 4.1.7 All staff should be scheduled for the CPD activity a minimum of 2 weeks prior to the scheduled activity. 4.1.8 Staff are NOT to be scheduled to attend CPD training the day AFTER a night shift. 4.2 Cancellation of Attendance on a Core CPD activity : 4.2.1 The HCS Core CPD activities are considered as any other normally assigned duty, failure to attend is the same as failing to attend a regular duty shift. All relevant procedures for attendance or cancellation are to be followed. 4.2.2 If a s taff member becomes sick resulting in him/her being unable to attend the CPD activity (If schedules in mandatory shift), normal HMC sick leave procedure should be followed. 4.2.3 Staff are not permitted to cancel their attendance on a HCS Core CPD activity unles s the cancelation has been approved by their Operations Manager – staff may not cancel training to: 4.2.3.1 Undertake paid over - time 4.2.3.2 Attend a paid event 4.2.3.3 For casual leave (unless agreed by Operations Manager) 4.2.4 Failure to attend an allocated CPD training activity is classed as being ‘absent’ and is a disciplinary event. 4.2.5 Staff who frequently

cancel training sessions will be subject to review by the Head of Training and their line manager. 4.2.6 The Operations Manager or their designate is responsible for contacting the Sched uling supervisor to notify them of the approval of a staff member’s cancelation of attendance. 5. EMERGENCY DRIVER TRAINING : 5.1 Only staff approved by the Operation Manager can attend the Emergency driving course. 5.2 Selected HCS staff will attend the driving training facilitated by HMC AS training unit .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 229 SOP 3.30 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Communications Procedure 3 . 30 HANDLING PARTICIPANT CASES Implementation Date 1 6 August 20 22 Purpose To ensure that EMDs effectively process ambulance request from other emergency agencies at National Command Center (NCC/TCC). Responsibility ALL COMMUNICATIONS STAFF 1. Criteria of responding to assistance requests from other emergency agencies at TCC : These guidelines should be adhered to when government emergency agencies request that Ambulance service respond to call for assistance under the following conditions, HMCAS Medical emergency Dispatch center will deploy the closes t resources as requested, whenever possible. 1.1 Any kind of emergency where a patient(s)/victim(s) is reported to be on scene. 1.2 Any kind of emergency where a patient(s)/victim(s) is entrapped, and medical care maybe required at extrication. 1.3 High index of suspicion for requiring medical assistance for public individuals or emergency services personnel such as search and rescue, active fire with no patients, ( assault and Domesti

c issue ). 1.4 Major incident confirmed or potential even if no victims reported. ( Such as strike cases ). 2. Required information for assistance requests from other emergency agencies at TCC: The following information are required to be added in NJM prior to any call for service requests. 2.1 Exact location with all egress details. D ocumentation in NJM must be verified and completed preferably with QARS details or additional comments to describe the best access to the patient/scene. 2.2 All safety details (Aggressive patients, use of weapon, dangerous animals, and other hazards). 2.3 Number o f patients/victims 2.4 Brief description of the Nature of illness or mechanism of injury. 2.5 METHANE report if major incident 3. Process of requesting assistance by other emergency agencies at TCC : All governmental agencies can request HMC AS assistance as following : 3.1 Add HMCAS (EMS) to the Call for Service (CFS) as participant agency and route the CFS to HMCAS dispatcher with all details in point2 accurately added to CFS. 3.2 Highly preferred that 999 operators to transfer the caller to HMCAS call taking consoles with th e same CFS that was routed to the HMCAS dispatcher. 3.3 999 ope

rators or any other agency requesting the assistance should avoid transferring or routing duplicated calls with the same CFS not to cause confusion and overload at HMCAS consoles. 3.4 In case of high a cuity incident, major incident or any other outstanding situation the officer/agent of the agency requiring HMCAS assistance should call HMCAS on - duty officer (OSCAR) to endorse the cases. 4. Process of handling requests of assistance from other emergency age ncies at TCC : When receiving a CFS as participant the ASMD dispatcher should activate the CFS and read the details carefully:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 230 4.1 If the case does not require an ambulance dispatch such as car stuck, event, GIS balloon footage etc., the ASMD shall reject the C FS and close it. ( After consult with DTL ). 4.2 If the case seems duplicated with another existing CFS, the ASMD must follow all the steps of duplicated calls SOP 3.2 point 8. 4.3 If the call is not rejected and not a duplicate, ensure initial dispatch as follows: 4.3.1 If the case is a Road Traffic Accidents and/or a death cases the ASMD dispatcher will deploy the closest unit Priority 1 (a solo unit with Lights and Siren). Deployment can include the closest Alpha or Delta u nit unless more information is available requiring additional resources. 4.3.2 For all other participant calls the ASMD dispatcher will deploy the closest unit Priority 2 (a solo unit no Lights and Siren) unless more information is available requiring additional resources. 4.4 For all processed participant calls ASMD dispatcher will endorse the CFS to a call - taker to start the call back attempts immediately. 4.5 If call back attempts are successful (within 2 minutes), the ASMD complete the ProQA interrogation,

update the code, and notify the dispatcher. The dispatcher will send resources according to the ProQA code and must notify the DTL. ( Call back attempt should not more than 2 times ) . 4.6 If the second call back attempts is not successful (within 2 minutes), continue the initial dispatch and DTL will coordinate with the owner agency when required, upgrade or downgrade the response priority/resources if needed. 4.7 OSCAR NCC and OSCAR Command may add more resources (Delta, Charlie, Additional Alpha units, GB , MIR…) to the call based on additional information provided by other agencies through NJM or verbal conversation .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 234 SOP 4.1 .A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.1 .A PATIENT & FAMILY RI GHTS – CONSENT, PRIV ACY & CONFIDENTIALITY Implementation Date 01 January 2017 Purpose To ensure that due diligence is given to patient and family rights with regard to consent, privacy, and confidentiality . Responsibility ALL HMCAS STAFF 1. HMCAS has a legal and ethical obligation to ensure privacy and confidentiality of all patients. 2. Patient & Family Rights: 2.1 The patient and/or patient’s family has the right to expect and receive the following from HMC Ambulance Service personnel: 2.1.1 To access HMC Ambulance Service regardless of race, religion, national origin, beliefs, values, l anguage, age, or disability. 2.1.2 To receive care and services without unnecessary delay. 2.1.3 To receive respectful and considerate care at all times in a safe, secure, and non - threatening environment and to maintain the patient’s personal dignity. 2.1.4 To have physical privacy during assessment and care and to avoid unreasonable, unwanted interference into the private

affairs. 2.1.5 To have all records and medical information trea ted as confidential. 2.1.6 To accept or refuse medications, treatments, or procedures and to be informed of medical consequences of such refusal. 2.1.7 To make the decision to withhold or withdraw treatment and ceasing of resuscitative effort. 2.1.8 To be protected from phy sical assault. 2.1.9 To protect the valuables and possessions from loss or theft. 2.1.10 To express a concern or a complaint regarding the quality of care and/or service received. 3. Consent: 3.1 Refers to the provision of approval or agreement from the patient or legal guardian for treatment and interventions following thoughtful consideration. 3.2 HMCAS personnel shall: 3.2.1 Obtain general consent from patients for assessment, treatment, and transportation at the point of first contact. 3.2.2 Consider verbal or Implied consent is an a cceptable means of receiving the patient’s or his/her representative’s [e.g. parent or legal guardian] general consent before performing procedures and this should be documented on the electronic Patient Care Report (ePCR). The Ambulance Service deems impl ied consent exists through the 999 call for service and/or t

hrough booked request for transportation vi a Retrieval Service UNLESS the patient verbally with - holds consent . 3.2.3 Perform necessary high - risk procedures and interventions without obtaining any consen t only in a life - threatening emergency situation or where the patient is

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 235 incapacitated with a reduced level of consciousness . Patient’s inability to consent or the non - availability of a representative should be documented on the ePCR. 3.2.4 Exercise implied cons ent regarding the rights of minors and incompetent adults, through their representatives if available. 3.3 A competent patient has the right to refuse any treatment, procedures and/or transportation. Medical consequences of refusal should be explained to the p atient. The patient’s refusal to consent should be documented in the ePCR before leaving the scene ( SOP 4.8.D ). 3.4 The ambulance service does not operate a system of ‘treat and release’ but in cases where patients are treated and discharged on scene (following refusal of transportation to hospital), or in cases where patients are not treated because of refusal of treatment or where patients refuse care the ePCR must be signed by the patient, family member or guardian ( SOP 4.8.D ). 3.5 Where a patient refuses treatment and/or treatment and subsequently refuses to sign the ePCR the ambulance crew should . 3.5.1 Inform the National Command Centre 3.5.2 Request the attenda

nce at the patient’s location of a duty supervisor . 3.5.3 Docu ment the refusal to sign ePCR within the comment section of the ePCR . 3.6 The ambulance service does not obtain consent nor offer advice on organ donation. Where a patient or a family member wishes to discuss organ donation then this will be handed over to the receiving hospital. 4. Patient Privacy : 4.1 Privacy in healthcare is an individual’s desire to limit the disclosure of personal health information and avoidance of notice or display . Patients trust us with private information, so out of respect for our patients we must ensure that it stays that way. 4.2 HMCAS employees shall NOT: 4.2.1 Release any Protected Health Information 4.2.2 Take pictures or videos of patients or a patient’s injuries (regardless of the inability to identify the patients face), unless given written permiss ion by the patient. 4.2.3 Take pictures or videos of any accident scene or vehicle damage even if in public view, unless an HMCAS vehicle is involved in the accident. 5. Patient Confidentiality : 5.1 Confidentiality carries the responsibility for limiting disclosure of private matter, including the responsibility to use, disclose, or r

elease such information only with the knowledge and consent of the individual concerned. 5.2 HMCAS employees shall NOT: 5.2.1 Discuss Patient information with any person that is not directly involved in the treatment of the patient. 5.2.2 Discuss patient information when it can be overheard by people not directly involved in the care of the patient. 5.2.3 Discuss patient information in a public place. 5.3 Discussion of patient information should be restricted to HMCAS employees and staff from receiving facilities that are involved in the patient’s treatment. 5.4 Store all recorded information in a manner which only authorized personnel have access to it.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 236 5.5 DO NOT copy e PCR’s or NJM detailed reports in any way unless permitted by an HMCAS Officer. 5.6 Any request for information from an individual that is not an HMC employee should be referred to the HMCAS Quality Improvement Manager. 6. Under no circumstance should an HMCAS employee divulge patient information to the media.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 237 SOP 4.1.B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.1.B ABUSE AND NEGLECT OF CHILDREN AND ADULTS Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To support the identification of patients who are suffering abuse. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Purpose : 1.1 This SOP is designed to support staff in understanding what actions to take in a case of suspected neglect of abuse. This SOP is based on HMC policy for ‘abuse and neglect of children and adults’ (CL7217). 2. Definition : 2.1 Abuse: a harmful or injurious treatment of another human being that may include physical, sexual, verbal, psychological, emotional, intellectual, or spiritual maltreat ment . 2.2 Neglect: failure to meet a dependent person’s basic, physical, medical, educational, and social needs and/or emotional deprivation, and/or desertion. 2.3 For the purpose of this policy HMC defines children as being under the age of 14. 3. Actions on Transportation to Hospital : 3.1 The primary purpose of the Ambulance Service is to report episodes of suspected abuse and/or negl

ect to the receiving hospital so that the hospital can comply with CL7217. 3.2 Confirming a case of suspected neglect o r abuse is a com plex process which is outside of the scope of an ambulance service and may result in a criminal investigation. Therefore : 3.2.1 Avoid making judgements or being overly emotive. 3.2.2 Remain professional at all times. 3.2.3 Contact a duty supervisor to attend the scene . 3.2.4 Clearly record your observations and findings on your ePCR . 3.2.5 DO NOT record your opinions . 3.3 On arrival at hospital handover your concerns/findings to the receiving doctor . 4. Actions on Patient/Family Refusal for Transfer : 4.1 Request supervisor to attend the scene and up - date the NCC . 4.2 Encourage patient/family to attend hospital . 4.3 Where patient continues to refuse AND no family member is present offer to contact police. 4.4 Document the patient’s response to this offer on your ePCR. 4.5 Where family are present and the famil y refuse to allow the patient to go to hospital. Inform NCC (regardless of call sign) and request NCC OSCAR updates NCC senior police officer .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 238 SOP 4.1.C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.1.C CONFIRMATION OF DHP QUALIFICATIONS OF C LINICAL ESCORTS AND/OR FIRST RESPOND ERS ON SCENE Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To confirm that private nursing staff and/or physicians are qualified with Department of Healthcare Professions ( D HP) should they form part of a patient’s clinical escort. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Escorting clinicians provided by Hamad Medical Corporation (HMC) : 1.1 HMCAS has a service level agreement with HMC hospitals covering roles and responsibilities during inter facility transfers and therefore confirmation of qualifications is not required from HMC supplied clinical escorts . 1.2 The referring hospital/doctor is responsible for ensuring that any escorting clinician provided by HMC is qualified and privileged to provide ongoing care . 2. Escorting clinicians provided by SIDRA hospital : 2.1 HMCAS has a service level agreement with SIDRA covering roles and responsibilities during inter facility transfers and therefore conf

irmation of qualifications is not required from SIDRA supplied clinical escorts . 2.2 The referring hospital/doctor is responsible for ensuring that any e scorting clinician provided by SIDRA is qualified and privileged to provide ongoing care . 3. Private home care nurses and/or private physicians : 3.1 HMCAS paramedics will request to see the private home care nurse (and/or physician) D HP license . 3.2 Private home care nurses/physicians m a y continue to provide . 3.2.1 Ongoing nursing care and care of activities of daily living . 3.2.2 Care of patient’s personal medical equipment if required during transportation . 3.3 In the event of a clinical emergency private healthcare nurses and/or p hysicians may assist (but not direct) emergency care . 4. Healthcare provider providing immediate medical care as a member of the public : 4.1 On arrival immediate deal with any threat to life . 4.2 Obtain a history from the bystanders and associated healthcare provider whilst assessing the patient in accordance with CPG1 . 4.3 Request to see a valid Department of Healthcare Professions license from any bystander reporting to be a healthcare provider . 4.4 Members of the public (to include H
ealthcare Providers) may assist the ambu lance service with basic life support but they may not: 4.4.1 Direct patient care 4.4.2 Operate HMCAS equipment
