Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 242 SOP 4.2.A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.2.A ROUTINE OPERAT IONAL SHIFT HANDOVER VEHICLE CHECKING Implementation Date 01 September 2020 Purpose To ensure that routine operational vehicles are checked appropriately prior to use . Responsibility CREW OF ALPHA, CHARL IE, DELTA, OSCAR, TA NGO, BIKE, EVENT, AN D SPECIAL OPERATIONS U NITS 1. All staff working on the vehicle is responsible for keeping it clean and tidy. 2. The oncoming and outgoing crew must carry out a detailed handover and check of the vehicle and equipment TOGETHER . 3. Check and test all vehicle equipment at the commencement of the shift. 4. Commencement of Shift: 4.1 All operational staff are required to report for duty fifteen (15) minutes prior to the commencement of their shift. 4.2 Collect allocated vehicle keys and hand - held radio from the PTL or from staff handing over and ensure that the radio identification number is documented by the PTL. 4.3 A 10 - minute time limit is allocated to check a vehicle and have it operational, except in unusual circumstance

s. 4.4 Ensure batteries of all equipment have at least 50% battery capacity prior to becoming available for calls. At least one back - up battery should always be f ully charged. 4.5 Ensure large oxygen cylinders are replaced or have a level of at least 50% capacity. At least one large cylinder should always be full. 4.5.1 KEEP the main oxygen tap in the "ON" position - DO NOT CLOSE this tap. 4.5.2 Ensure that the flow dial is kept a t "0" (ZERO) to prevent leakage of oxygen. 4.5.3 Remove plastic wrap from ALL portable cylinders - MUST be ready for immediate use. Ensure all portable cylinders are full, with no more than one cylinder at min. 50% full. 4.6 Report any vehicle/equipment problems to the PTL. 5. Completion of Shift: 5.1 Complete all relevant HMCAS documentation accurately and comprehensively prior to leaving the base. 5.2 Replenish/replace any used stock/consumables/equipment in the vehicle. 5.3 Ensure all equipment is present and accounted for and i n working order. 5.4 Ensure that the vehicle fuel level is above 75%. 5.5 The unit must remain ready to respond at all times. 5.5.1 Staff may not leave until their relief has arrived and a complete handover is done . 5.5.2 W

here there is no relief that will take over the unit, the unit must remain fully prepared to respond until completion of the shift time . 5.6 Document and report any unusual incidents during the shift. 5.7 Record any equipment that was left at a hospital or handed over to a Life Flight crew and notify the PTL if it w as not possible to retrieve this equipment during the remainder of the shift.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 243 5.8 If the vehicle is not going to be used upon completion of the shift, ensure that the vehicle is adequately stocked, cleaned, and locked and the keys handed in to the HMCAS stores or a HMCAS Supervisor for safe keeping. 6. The appropriate mechanical checklist for the vehicle type must be completed fully: 6.1 Start the vehicle and allow to it to idle during the checking process. 6.2 Briefly test all lights, indicators, warning devices an d sirens, taking into consideration the surrounding environment and population. 6.3 If there is a failure or deficiency of an essential element (as per check list), notify the production supervisor or PTL for unit change or repair. 6.4 Note any other failures or d eficiencies on the checklist. 6.5 Provide details for failures or deficiencies on a Mechanical Defect Report and attach to the checklist. 6.6 The vehicle should be clean without visible soiling or dust. Where cleaning is required, report to the production supervis or to determine availability of cleaning staff. 6.7 Where fuel levels are below half, report to the production supervisor to determine availability for refuellin

g before refuelling. 7. The appropriate clinical equipment and supplies checklist must be completed fully: 7.1 Check electronic clinical equipment for currency of service date and specific tests as follows: 7.1.1 Monitor/Defibrillator: user test, attach printout to checklist. 7.1.2 Suction unit: suction test 7.1.3 Glucometer: as per Ambulance Paramedic duty checklis t 7.1.4 Ventilator: as per Critical Care Paramedic duty checklist 7.2 Note the HBE number of all electronic clinical equipment on the checklists. 7.3 If there are failures or deficiencies of essential equipment or supplies (as per check list), which are unavailable at t he Hub, report to a supervisor or PTL for further instructions. 7.4 Note any other failures or deficiencies on the checklist. 7.5 Equipment failures should be managed according to SOP 4.3.A . 8. Radio test and booking on: 8.1 Staff shoul d book available with the PTL, within 10 minutes of shift start, after completing the essential mechanical and clinical checks. Do not book available until oxygen system is ready. 8.2 Staff must report: 8.2.1 Unit, vehicle, radio, and monitor/defibrillator numbers; 8.2.2 Vehicle fuel level and 8.2.3 Names of the staff an

d any observers or students present. 8.3 Once staff have booked on with the PTL they should notify the appropriate Dispatcher of their location. 8.4 Where a unit has two radios (two portable, or one portable and one vehi cle), use one radio for the initial communication with the PTL and the other for communication with the Dispatcher to ensure that both radios are working correctly. 8.5 If a radio fails, the test it should be reported to a supervisor or PTL for replacement .

B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.2 . B NON - ROUTINE OPERAT IONAL AND NON - OPERAT IONAL SERVICE VEHICLE CHECKING Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To ensure that HMCAS vehicles are checked regularly with respect to: • Non - routine operational vehicles viz. MCI vehicles, “Green Busses”, the Bariatric ambulanc e . • Non - operational vehicles viz. travel cars, vans and decommissioned ambulances used for other purposes . Responsibility HUB PRODUCTION SUPER VISORS, MANAGERS WIT H ASSIGNED VEHICLES AND STORES STAFF & A NY OTHER APPLICABLE STAFF 1. Following the use of any vehicle: 1.1 Ensure that fuel level is above 75%; 1.2 Replace used stock and equipment an d 1.3 Clean the vehicle and perform surface decontamination. 2. The above responsibilities of staff that used the vehicle do not take away from the responsibilities of the staff checking the vehicle, as described below. 3. Utilize and complete the ap propriate mechanical and clinical equipment checklists for the vehicle type. 3.1 Start the vehicle and a

llow it to idle during the checking process . 3.2 Test all lights, indicators, warning devices and sirens. 3.3 If there is a failure or deficiency of an essential element, report the vehicle to the PTL as out of service; deficiencies should be corrected with urgency. 3.4 Note other failures or deficiencies on the checklist and rectify as soon as practically possible. 3.5 Provide additional details for any failure or deficiency on an OVA and document the OVA number on the checklist . 3.6 The vehicle should be clean without visible soiling or dust. 4. Utilize and complete the appropriate clinical equipment and supplies checklist : 4.1 Check electronic clinical equipment for full battery levels, currency of service date and specific tests as follows: 4.1.1 Monitor/Defibrillator: user test, attach printout to checklist. 4.1.2 Suction unit: suction test 4.1.3 Glucometer: as per Ambulance Paramedic duty checklist 4.1.4 Ventilator: as per Critical Care Paramedic duty checklist 4.2 Note the HBE number of all electronic clinical equipment on the checklists. 4.3 If there are failures or deficiencies of essential equipment or supplies, which are unavailable at the Hub, notify the supervisor o

r PTL. 4.4 Inspect generator s and battery charging systems. 5. Perform a Radio test with the PTL, indicating that the vehicle has been checked . 6. Considerations for MCI vehicles, “Green busses” not in active duty .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 245 6.1 During the checking process, drive the vehicle for 5 to 10 minutes instead of just idling the vehicle. 7. All operational vehicles must be checked at the start of the shift .

A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3 . A ISSUING & MAINTENA NCE OF MEDICAL EQUIP MENT & SUPPLIES Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure that all operational personnel manage HMCAS medical equipment appropriately . Responsibility OPERATIONAL CLINICAL PERSONNEL (E.G. ALP HA, CHARLIE, DELTA, BIKE AND EVENT STAFF) 1. On every shift, complete the approved equipment checklists and note the following on the checklist in the allocated space: 1.1 Biomedical Engineering (HBE) Number (found on the piece of equipment concerned) 1.2 Date of next service 1.3 Presence of any defects/malfunction of the equipment. 2. If a piece of equipment (e.g. LifePak monitor, Suction Unit, LUCAS device or Glucometer) has reached its due date for service, return that piece of equipment to the HMCAS stores and request a replacement. 3. If a piece of equipment is found to be defective or malfunctioning: 3.1 Return the identified equipment to the HMCAS stores or Hub supervisor. 3.2 Complete an O VA report 3.3 Request a replacement for the

defective equipment from the HMCAS stores. 4. Carefully manage all medical equipment to avoid damage or malfunction : 4.1 Do not leave equipment in direct line of traffic when on the scene of an RTA. 4.2 Ensure that equipment is sent in for service at least 2 weeks before the service date. 4.3 Handle all equipment with care when setting up and packing away. 5. Issue/Re - stocking of Supplies/Consumables : 5.1 When supplies (e.g. IV fluids, oxygen masks, etc.) have been used on a ca ll, re - stock supplies either at the main HMCAS storeroom OR in any HMC ED’s or Hubs . 5.2 The Production Supervisor or Production Team Leader (PTL) may authorize the HMCAS unit to restock at HMCAS Base or arrange for stock to be transported to the unit’s location. 5.3 Ensure that a “STOCK REQUISITION” is completed appropriately and legibly.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 250 SOP 4.3.B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3.B MANAGEMENT OF NON - CONTROLLED MEDIC ATIONS BY AMBULANCE PARAMEDICS & CRITICAL CARE PAR AMEDICS Implementation Date 06 July 2021 Purpose To ensure that all clinical operational personnel manage HMCAS approved non - controlled medications appropriately . Responsibility OPERATIONAL CLINICAL PERSONNEL 1. All medications shall be dispensed from any of M edication D ispensing L ocations (MDLs) which include; relevant HMC hospital ED (Emergency Department)/AS (Ambulance Service) interface, and the respective AS hub. 2. All medicat ions shall be stored and handled with respect to AS 9002 policy and in accordance with the manufacturer’s instructions to maintain the quality, efficacy, and security of the medications. U nless otherwise directed, all stored or transported medications shal l be kept out of direct sunlight and within an air - conditioned environment. 3. A HMCAS authorized personnel will be available 24/7 to assist with dispensing of non - controlled medications. 4. Do not leave me

dications unattended (e.g. in an unlocked vehicle or in an unlocked office). 5. If the HMCAS vehicle is not being handed over to oncoming HMCAS healthcare professional, then return and sign in the medication bags to the medication dispensing location. 6. Medication dispensing locations’ staff shall check expiry dates of medications inside medication bags at least once monthly. 7. Any inappropriate medication labeling shall be communicated to supervisor and AS pharmacy department through an OVA. 8. Medications are considered acceptable for use up to: 8.1 The end of the expi ry month where a MONTH - YEAR expiry date is stated OR 8.2 The end of the stated day date where a DATE - MONTH - YEAR is stated. 9. Re - stocking of Non - Controlled Medications: 9.1 When medications need to be restocked it can be obtained from the closest medication dispensing location 9.2 Complete the “MEDICATION REQUISITION” form legibly. 9.3 If there is no relevant incident/trip number (as in cases of expired medications or general restocking of medication bag) , then write the word “Restock” in the “Trip No.” block. 9.4 Place all received medications immediately into your assigned medication bag. 10. Broken

ampoules : 10.1 In cases of broken medication ampoule/vial/bottle or spilled medications: 10.1.1 Safely discard the broken pieces of the medication ampoule/vial/bottle. 10.1.2 Replace the medication at the appropriate medication dispensing location following the usual re - stocking procedure. 10.1.3 On duty Emergency Operations Officer 10.1.4 In case Penthrox® vial is broken, raise an OVA .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 251 SOP 4.3.C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3.C CONTROLLED MED ICINES MANAGEMENT BY CRITICAL CARE PARAMEDICS Implementation Date 06 July 2021 Purpose To ensure Critical Care Paramedics manage HMCAS approved controlled medications appropriately . Responsibility CRITICAL CARE PARAME DICS 1. All controlled medications shall be dispensed as pre - packed Controlled Medication Cases ( CMCs ) with predefined standard content, from approved M edication D ispensing L ocations (MDLs) (such as H GH - ED pharmacy, Helipad pharmacy and AS hubs). Within these locations, the pre - packed narcotic cases are stored inside prepositioned secured control medica tion cases cabinets. 2. Only a licensed Critical Care Paramedic (CCP) shall be eligible to be issued controlled medications. 3. A designated pharmacy staff is 24/7 available to assist with dispensing & receiving of CMCs . 4. At the beginning of the shift: 4.1 Pharmacy staff are responsible for preparing the ready - for - dispense, pre - packed CMCs in accordance with the agreed predefined stand

ard quantities of controlled medications. 4.2 Request a pre - packed CMCs from the pharmacy staff at the medication dispensing loca tion. 4.3 Complete the relevant controlled medications register confirming the receipt of CMCs . 4.4 Open the CMCs inside the medication dispensing location and ensure that it contains the correct and intact standard quantities of the controlled medications. 5. At the end of the shift (returning the CMCs ): 5.1 Ensure the CMCs are clean before returning to the medication dispensing location. 5.2 Both pharmacy staff and the CCP will sign in the relative register to confirm the return of the CMCs . 5.2.1 Pharmacy staff is responsible fo r confirming stock count inside CMCs. 6. Re - stocking of controlled medications: 6.1 If controlled medication is used during patient contact and requires re - stocking : 6.1.1 Attend the appropriate MDL and request a new ready - for - dispense CMC from the pharmacy staff, retu rn the used CMC together with the residual of used controlled medications (as per point 7). These 3 processes (returning used CMCs , returning residuals, receiving new CMCs ) must be documented in the relative registers and signed by both pharmacy

staff and the CCP or doctor . 6.1.2 Due to large volume of medication High Acuity Transfer Services may exchange medication on an ampoule for ampoule bases . 6.1.3 Pharmacy staff is responsible for reviewing and confirming both the contents of the returned, partially used CMCs an d the residuals of used narcotics (in accordance with the filled - in controlled drug administration form) as per point 7 . 6.1.4 Pharmacy staff is responsible for recording the count of remaining ready - for - dispense and returned CMCs in the CMC cabinets.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 252 7. Returning of residual controlled medications: 7.1 If a CCP has used any controlled medications, they will need to return residual medication and the empty ampoule to the appropriate MDL. 7.2 Used empty syringes (with the needles removed) and the glass head/neck of broken a mpoules (once all residual medication has been drawn out) should be discarded. 7.3 The empty ampoules/vials together with the syringe containing residuals of withdrawn controlled medication, closed with a stopper (without sharps) are to be placed in a Plastic Zipper Storage Bag (PZSB) and returned to the appropriate MDL, together with a completed “Controlled Drug Administration Form”. 7.4 High Acuity Transfer Services to adhered to agreed process for discarding controlled medication whilst on overseas mission. 7.5 Any controlled medication prepared, but not administered to a patient, must be annotated in the free text section of the ePCR (e.g “Midazolam prepared and drawn up, but not administered due to seizure self - termination ”) . 8. Issues Requiring Escalation a nd Reporting: 8.1 In the context of HMCAS controlled medications, generally esca

lation and reporting will be between the Pharmacy staff and Oscar Effectiveness Officer and/or Clinical Manager on call by phone and then by email. All correspondence needs to be documented accordingly. 8.2 In the event of an accidently broken ampoule or spilled controlled medication: 8.2.1 Whenever possible, attempt to retrieve as much of the spilled medication as possible (by drawing the fluid into a syringe). 8.2.2 A ttempt to salvage all the pieces of the broken ampoule . 8.2.3 Place the syringe containing the salvaged medication together with salvaged pieces of ampoule in a PZSB. 8.2.4 Complete a “Controlled Drug Administration Form” (with brief details in the “Remarks” section) and place the completed form in the PZSB. 8.2.5 Replace the CMC as per point s 6 and 7. 8.2.6 Notify the pharmacy staff and Effectiveness Officer. If Effectiveness Officer is unavailable, then contact the on - call Consultant Paramedic. 8.2.7 Submit an OVA describing the break age of the ampoule. 8.3 Any discrepancy in narcotic drug count will be escalated by the Pharmacy Supervisor as per AS 9004. 9. Staff and situations where controlled mediations may be held for protracted periods : 9.1 CCPs who ful

fil l an on - call capacity either as duty officers and/or on the Major Incident Response roster and/or provide surge capability may hold a CMC for the duration of their on - call period. 9.1.1 A record must be held within the appropriate MDL that the CMC has been signed out as per point 4. 9.1.2 The CMC must b e returned and signed in as per point 5. 9.1.3 The maximum duration that a CMC drug box can be held by CCPs who fulfil an on - call capacity either as duty officers and/or on the Major Incident Response roster is one calendar month.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 253 9.1.3.1 All CMCs must be returned by th e last day of the month to ensure contents remain in - date. 9.1.4 CCPs who fulfil an on - call capacity either as duty officers and/or on the Major Incident Response roster must comply with points 7 and 8. 9.2 CCPs on overseas missions and/or when providing protracted care at a location within Qatar and/or overseas may retain a CMC: 9.2.1 A record must be held within the appropriate MDL that the CMC has been signed out as per point 4. 9.2.2 The CMC must be returned and sign ed in as per point 5 . 9.2.3 There is NO maximum time period that CCP s on overseas missions and/or when providing protracted care at a location within Qatar and/or overseas may retain a CMC for. This is to reflect the nature of the duration of these events. However, an estimated date of return must be provided to the pharmac y during receiving the CMC. 9.2.4 CCPs collecting a CMC that will be used for a protracted time period should request a CMC with a long expiratory date. 10. Storage of CMC : 10.1 CMCs must not be left unattended in a response unit or car and should remain with the respons ible clinician

. 10.2 For CCPs holding CMCs for protracted periods: 10.2.1 When on duty, CMC must not be left unattended in a response unit or car and should remain with the responsible clinician. 10.2.2 When off duty BUT STILL ON CALL, the CMC must either be with the respons ible clinician or locked within a static location . 10.3 CCPs on overseas missions and/or when providing protracted care at a location within Qatar are to comply with point 10.2.2 . 11. Major incident medication : 11.1 Major Incident supplies of controlled medication are a vailable in HMCAS main pharmacy. These may be transported to an incident site as part of the major incident response. This is primarily the responsibility of either a CCP or a Pharmacist and/or a Pharmacy T echnician. In extra ordinary circumstances, Silver Commander may request that a LICENSED Ambulance Paramedic transport - controlled medication .

D NEAR PATIENT BLOOD TESTING Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To ensure supply, functioning and training of near patient blood testing equipment for operational units. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Blood Glucose Meter : 1.1 General considerations for point of care glucose testing : 1.1.1 Initial equipment issued via Support Services will be checked and calibrated by Support Services Equipment section. 1.1.2 Glucose testing strips will be issued, via the Ambulance Service Group operational stores within HUB locations via Support Services which are in date, ready for use with test strips which are recommended by the manufacturer. 1.1.3 Test strips will be discarded 6 months after the first opening date of the test strip container as rec ommended by the manufacturer, when expired, or whichever comes first. All containers must be labelled with the first opening date. Labels will be supplied to all Hub Stores by Support Services. 1.1.4 The glucometer machine wi

ll be issued by the Ambulance Service (AS) Sub - Stores located in HUBs as part of the Ambulance Paramedic or Critical Care Paramedic response bag and will be returned to HUB Stores as part of standard rotation of equipment as required. 1.1.5 Calibration of the glucometer machine will be done accordi ng to the AS process guided by the manufacturer’s recommendations by the Store - man, Production Supervisor, or trained Paramedic. Recommendations as follows: 1.1.5.1 When a new vial of strips is ordered and opened for that specific glucometer. 1.1.5.2 When the glucometer o r test strips is suspected not to be working correctly. 1.1.5.3 When unexpected blood glucose results were repeatedly recorded with discrepancies. 1.1.5.4 When a blood glucose machine was dropped or accidently damaged. 1.1.5.5 When a new battery change is required. 1.1.6 In the event of equipment failure, discrepancies with blood glucose test results or accidental breakage, an OVA and equipment defect report should be completed and submitted followed by replacing the defective unit with a working unit. 1.1.7 All calibration tests must be doc umented by the person calibrating the glucometer on a glucometer test log s

heet/log book located at respective Hub and Store locations. 1.1.8 All staff required to perform glucose testing and glucometer calibration will receive training during their induction pe riod and/or during their Ambulance Paramedic course by the AS Training section in accordance with the manufacturer’s recommendations. 1.1.9 Subsequent training will occur as and when a new blood glucose machine is introduced into operational use.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 255 2. iStat : 2.1 The iSt at is exclusively used by Transfer and Retrieval Ambulance and Critical Care Paramedics . 2.2 Transfer and Retrieval Ambulance and Critical Care Paramedics are trained on the correct use of the iStat during their induction period. 2.3 Refresher skills drills are co nducted once yearly in Service or as part of a Refresher training. 2.4 iStat machines and test cartridges are stored, maintained, and issued at request by Production Transfer and Retrieval . 3. Critical Values : 3.1 The Ambulance Service does not define critical values because near patient blood testing is carried out by the person requesting the test. 3.2 Values above or below a treatment threshold are defined where appropriate in CPG. 3.3 The decision to treat is based on clinical judgement taking clinical data and patient condition into account . 3.4 Results of near patient blood tests are recorded within the paper or electronic Patient Care Record . 3.5 High and low levels of blood glucose that may require treatment are outlined in C PG 7 . 3.6 The lower level of haemoglobin that may require treatment is outlined in SOP 5.4.F.4 Bl

ood Transfusion for Transfer and Retrieval Service. 3.7 The decision to act on results in respect pO2, pCO2 or pH are based on clinical judgement by the transfer ring physician or Critical Care Paramedic.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 256 Do a Control Solution test: When a new vial of strips is ordered and opened for that specific glucometer. When the glucometer or test strips is suspected not to be working correctly. When unexpected blood glucose results were repeatedly recorded with discrepancies. When a blood glucose machine was dropped or accidently damaged. When a new battery change is required . Note: Use only the approved Control Solution provided to your Glucometer strip device . Control solution test must be done at room temperature (20 - 25˚C). Make sure your meter, test strips and control solution are at room temperature before testing. Write the discard date on the control solution bottle 1. Glucometer test strip must be requested from AS Sub - stores, AS Hub Production Supervisor locations and Romeo locations. 2. Stores staff, Production S upervisors and clinical staff must perform a calibration on the glucometer using the approved control solution. 3. Calibration test should be: • Done according to the manufacturer’s process using the new test strip with the test solution (high and low). Note: solution ranges should be within the expected

parameters printed on the test solution vial. • Record the two (2) readings (High and Low) on log sheet provided at the respective locations. • Label the expiry date of the test strip vial from the dat e opened (expiry date is or whichever comes first) . 4. If the two (2) results are outside the parameters specified on the test solution vial then: • Complete an equipment defect report form and OVA. • Replace the defective device with a new glucometer and new strips followed by the above pr ocess . • The defective device should be returned to the Equipment section for maintenance.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 258 SOP 4.3.E Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3.E HIGH ALERT AND LOOK ALIKE SOUND AL IKE MEDICATIONS Implementation Date 04 March 2020 Purpose To maximize patient safety when using High Alert Medications Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Definition: 1.1 High Alert Medications (HAM): Medications that are associated with a high percentage of errors or have greater risk for adverse outcomes or sentinel events if error occurs, as well as Look - Alike - Sound - Alike (LASA) medications (refer to AS 9010 Policy). 1.2 These drugs include, but are not limited to; 1.2.1 Intravenous Inotropic and/or chronotropic drugs and/or vasoconstrictors . 1.2.2 Intravenous vasodilators 1.2.3 Intravenous concentrated electrolytes 1.2.4 Intravenous irritant agents 1.2.5 Intravenous antiarrhythmic agents 1.2.6 Intravenous sedation and/or anti - epileptic 1.2.7 Intravenous opiates 2. Look - alike Sound - alike medication are deemed to exist where : 2.1 SOUND - alike is where the drug name is similar to a diff erent drug . 2.2 LOOK - alike where t

he packaging is similar to a different drug . 2.3 A complete list of medications deemed to be High Alert medications and/or LOOK - alike/SOUND - alike medications is appended with this SOP. The list of High Alert medications and/or LOO K - alike/SOUND - alike medication also indicates in which division of the Ambulance Service (999/ Emergency or Transfer and Retrieval Services ) that these medications are available for use. 3. Governance : 3.1 The Ambulance Service Clinical Guidelines and Standards Co mmittee will make recommendations to the Ambulance Service Executive Governance Committee for all medications carried within the Ambulance Servic e including high alert medications. The Ambulance Service Executive Governance Committee, in conjunction wi th t he HMCAS Group Medical Director, will agree on all newly recommended medications. Whenever any new medication is a dded, it is examined whether it is a HAM or LASA or not. 4. Generic procedures for High Alert Medication and LOOK - alike/SOUND - alike : 4.1 All Ambulance Service Drug bags are held in the Ambulance Service Pharmacy unless issued to a member of the ambulance service including; 4.1.1 Critical Care Paramedic 999/Emerge

ncy , LIFEFLIGHT or Retrieval . 4.1.2 Ambulance Paramedic 999/Emergen cy or Scheduled Ambulance Service (SAS) (this includes production supervisor) . 4.1.3 Ambulance Service doctor 4.1.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Doctor

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 259 4.2 Ambulance service drug bags are only allocated to specific grades of clinician either a Critical Care Paramedic (CCP), Ambulance Paramedic (AP), medical doctor (member of the ambulance service or member of transfer team from Hamad Medical Corporation) or nursing staff employed by the ambulance service. The type of drugs contained within these drug bags reflect the TRAININ G of the individual as well as the scope of practice outlined by Department of Healthcare Professions . 4.3 An independent check of all medication of all High Alert or LOOK - alike/SOUND - alike will be instigated prior to drug administration . 5. Procedure/Processes for High Alert Medication : 5.1 A list of High Alert medication will be contained within this SOP. 5.2 All High Alert medi c ations will be identified by RED label stating HIGH ALERT. 6. Procedure/processes for LOOK - alike/SOUND - alike : 6.1 A list of Sound - alike are to be contained within this SOP. 6.2 All SOUND - alike medi c ations names will be typed by using the Tallman lettering (emphasize parts of dissimilarities by using upper case letters). 6.3 Photos of updated Look - A

like pairs are sent by pharmacy through an alerting email to CCP and AP supervisors to be disseminated to all CCPs and A P s. 6.4 All LOOK - alike/SOUND - alike medi c ations will be kept separate from their LASA pair(s). 6.5 The potential error regarding LOOK - alike/SOUND - alike medi c ations will be considered when introducing a new medication. 7. Attachment : 7.1 List of High Alert medications ( Figure 4.3.E ) 7.2 List of LOOK - alike and SOUND - alike medications. ( Figure 4.3.E )

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 262 SOP 4.3.F Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3.F ADMINISTRATION OF HIGH ALERT AND L OOK - ALIKE AND SOUND ALIKE MEDICATION Implementation Date 06 July 2021 Purpose To maximize patient safety when administering Look - Alike/Sound - Alike and High Alert Medications . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. The aim of this Standard Operating Procedure is to outline the procedures to be undertaken when administering either a High Alert Medication or a Look - Alike and/or a Sound - Alike medication. 2. Identification of high risk, look alike and sound alike medication: 2.1 Look - Alike or Sound - Alike (LASA) medications shall be identified by: 2.1.1 Utilizing TALL MAN lettering for labeling 2.1.2 Physic ally separated from their LASA pair(s) 2.1.3 Look - Alike/Sound - Alike label (Figure 1) 2.2 Each High Alert Medication will be labelled with a red label stating, “ HIGH ALERT ” (Figure 2) . 2.3 Prior to administration of medication , the following procedures are required: 2.3.1 Admi nister medication as per Clinical Practice Guid

elines . 2.3.2 Confirm any allergies (whenever possible) and check what medication has recently been administered and record on e PCR . 2.3.3 In an emergency act in the patient’s best interest s according to all applicable pr ofessional, ethical and medicolegal frameworks and Hamad Medical Corporation Patient and Family Rights. 3. Packing and Storage of medication : 3.1 Pharmacy is responsible for packing all medication into predefined medication bag/container compartments. 3.2 CCPs and Ambulance Paramedics are not permitted to move medication between these predefined compartments.

G Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3 . G LP15 TRANSMISSION GU IDELINES Implementation Date 08 January 2019 Purpose To provide guidance and troubleshooting steps when LP15 transmission errors/failures occur . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Transmission of ECG data to the Toughpad ePCR: 1.1 Select and transmit “ALL” when transmitting ECG and vital signs data to the Tough pad ePCR on completion of a case. 1.2 Once ECG and vital signs data has been transmitted to the Toughpad ePCR, open and view the ECG data file in the ePCR to ensure that all data has been successfully transmitted and is complete prior to closing the case. 1.3 Should an ECG data transmission to the ePCR fail, restart the ePCR Tough p ad (using the Microsoft Windows Start Menu Restart option) and the LP15, enter the archives and attempt to re - transmit the ECG data again. Ensure you select the correct case from the archives. 1.4 Should this fail, request assistance from the Clinical Documentation Officer (CDO). Should the CDO be unable to su

ccessfully transmit the ECG data to the ePCR, print out two complete ‘Code Summary’ and hand one copy to the CDO on duty, together w ith the relevant case identifying details (CFS and LP15 HBE number). Hand the second ‘Code summary’ to the receiving hospital/facility. 1.5 The CDO shall complete an OVA for any transmission failures and include the CFS and LP15 HBE number in the OVA. 1.6 Document in the ePCR that the ECG data failed to transmit. 1.7 Notify the CDO if it is a Critical Case/Death in Care and hand the LP15 to the CDO. The CDO is to notify the Quality Team by sending an email to ASGQPSRM@hamad.qa w ith the CFS number and relevant case details. The CDO will retrieve the ECG data from the LP15. 1.8 DO NOT open a new case until you have completed and closed the previous case. This is to prevent the wrong ECG and vital signs data being transmitted to the wro ng case. 1.9 It is mandatory to transmit LP15 ECG data to the Toughpad for all cases, regardless of severity. 2. Conducting the daily “User Test”: 2.1 Turn on the LP15. 2.2 Press the “Options” button and select “User Test”. 2.3 The User Test will then be conducted and once c omplete will automatically print out the resul

ts of the test – either “User Test Passed” or “User Test Failed”. 2.4 Should the User Test fail, return the LP15 immediately to the production supervisor and replace it with a functioning LP15 which has passed the user test. 2.5 The Production Supervisor shall complete an OVA reporting the equipment failure. The OVA must include the LP15 HBE number and where the LP15 was sent for inspection. 3. If 12 - Lead ECG fails to transmit to NCC:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 265 3.1 Check the modem cable is securely plug ged in. Do not attempt to remove and plug the modem cable back in. 3.2 If indoors, place the LP15 near a window. Press “Transmit” and ensure the selected “SITE” is “NCC”. Press “SEND” to re - attempt the transmission of the 12 - Lead ECG to the NCC. 3.3 If you are in a remote/rural area with poor network signal, attempt to re - transmit the 12 - Lead ECG once you are mobile to hospital and in an area with a stronger network signal. 3.4 Should these steps fail, notify the Clinical Desk that you are unable to transmit a 12 - Lead ECG to them. Notify your Production Supervisor and arrange to have the LP15 replaced. 3.5 The Production Supervisor shall complete an OVA which will include the CFS number, LP15 HBE number and where the LP15 was sent for inspection. 4. Keep the 4 - lead ECG cable plugged in the LP15 at all times, regardless of whether you are using the 4 - lead ECG or not . 5. Pair the ePCR Toughpad with your assigned LP15 at the start of every shift, before leaving the Hub in order to change the Toughpad if any pairing issues are identified. 6. The ePCR Toughpad is re

quired to be restarted at the start of your shift, or earliest convenience following the correct Microsoft Windows Restart procedure. Do not do a hard shut down by holding down the Toughpad power button.

n, by using the arrow on the ZOLL. 1.7.1.2 Then press the circle bottom (beside the arrow). 1.7.1.3 Check the “Hostname” that appears on the scre en and double - check the number with the one that appears on the Tough Pad. 1.8 Should an ECG data transmission to the ePCR fail, restart the ePCR Toughpad (using the Microsoft Windows Start Menu Restart option) and the LP15, enter the archives and attempt to r e - transmit the ECG data again. Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 268 SOP 4.3.H Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3.H DISCARDING PARACETAM OL AND IBUPROFEN SYRUP Implementation Date 28 January 2020 Purpose To outline the process of disposing of oral paracetamol and ibuprofen syrup once opened . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Definition : 1.1 HMCAS supplies paracetamol and ibuprofen syrup for the treatment of pain and mild pyrexia (as per HMCAS Clinical Practice Guidelines). Paracetamol and ibuprofen syrup may be administered to adults as well as children. 1.2 Once opened and the initial dose is administered , the ‘opened’ bottle should be discarded . 2. Process : 2.1 Administer the correct dose as indicated by the Clinical Practice Guidelines . 2.2 When measuring paediatric treatment dose , use the provided measuring ‘cap’ (you may use the age defined treatment doses indicated on the bottle) . 3. D isposal : 3.1 Once the bottle has been opened it must be discarded at the end of the patient treatment episode . 3.2 Where multiple paediatric patients are anticipated (such as during event

cover) a single bottle of paracetamol and ibuprofen syrup maybe used for mul tiple patients . 3.2.1 The paracetamol and ibuprofen syrup bottle should then be discarded at the end of the event . 3.3 Discard by placing the bottle in to the yellow bag within the ambulance .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 269 SOP 4.3.I Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3.I SELECTING AMBULANCE SERVICE MEDICATIONS A ND EQUIPMENT Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To outline internal HMCAS processes for selecting medication and equipment . Responsibility ALL LICENSED HMCAS PARAMEDICS A ND CRITICAL CARE PAR AMEDICS 1. Background and Purpose : 1.1 This SOP outli nes the process of selecting medications and equipment for the Ambulance Service . 1.2 HMCAS Ambulance Paramedics and Critical Care Paramedics are authorized to administer classes of medication (by D HP). However, the type, dose, and indication fo r the use of any medication is agreed upon by the Medical Director in associati on with the Head o f Professions. 2. Choice of Medication : 2.1 P roposed medications will be identified by the Head of Professions through the following processes: 2.1.1 F ollowing clinical audit 2.1.2 F ollowing international changes in clinical practice 2.1.3 I n response to a request from outside agencies where this is deemed to be clinically appropriate (e.g. Hear

t Hospital for STEMI patients) . 2.1.4 F ollowing case review 2.1.5 U pon HMCAS staff request where this is deemed to be clinically appropriate . 2.2 New medication will be agreed with t he Medical Director and where deemed appropriate the new medications will be discussed at the HMCAS Clinical Forum which is chaired by the Medical Director . 2.2.1 Additional external expert advice will be sort ed as deemed appropriate by the Medical Director . 2.3 New ly recommended medications will be signed off into clinical practice by the Ambulance Service Executive Governance Committee. 3. Clinical Practice Guidelines : 3.1 Clinical Practice Guidelines will be reviewed at least annually . 3.2 Clinical Practice Guidelines will b e published on the HMCAS Learning Management System and on the ePCR tablet . 4. Ambulance Service equipment : 4.1 T he purchase and evaluation of ambulance equipment will be overseen by the HMCAS equipment and uniform committee . 4.2 All new equipment will be evaluated, and appropriate feedback provided to the equipment committee and/or HMC Department of Material Management.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 270 SOP 4.3.J Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.3.J AIR LIFTING AND MOVING D EVICES Implementation Date 07 September 2021 Purpose To provide guidance on lifting and moving bariatric patients considering safety measures for both staff and patient . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. HoverJack : 1.1 The HoverJack is considered as an air moving and lifting device used to transport patient whose weight or girth poses a potential health risk for the paramedics responsible for the lifting or evacuating patients. The key function of the HoverJac k are: 1.1.1 The HoverJack device is used to lift a patient in supine position from the floor to bed or stretcher height. 1.1.2 The HoverJack device can also be used to evacuate patient’s downstairs in the event of an emergency. 1.1.3 For patient whose weight or girth poses a potential health risk for the paramedics responsible for the lifting or evacuating said patients . 2. HoverMatt : 2.1 The HoverMatt is used to assist paramedics with patient transfers, positioning, turning and proning

of patients whose weight represents a patient safety risk. During use air simultaneously escapes from the holes on the underside, reducing the force needed to move the patient by 80 - 90%. 2.2 For patient whose weight or girth poses a potential health risk for the paramedics responsible for repositioning or laterally transferring said patients .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 271 3. Air Supply: 3.1 T he Air Supply is an electric device used to inflate, with different levels, the HoverJack and the HoverMatt . 4. Safety Precautions: 4.1 HoverJack : 4.1.1 Make sure patient safety straps are secured before moving . 4.1.2 Move the HoverJack using the transport straps and/ or the transport handles located along the top perimeter. 4.1.3 Never use patient safety straps to pull the HoverJack , as they may tear. 4.1.4 When moving a patient on the inflated HoverJack , use caution and move slowly . 4.1.5 Additional paramedics are recommended when moving or evacuating a patient over 113kg. 4.1.6 Never attempt to move a patient on an uninflated HoverJack . 4.1.7 Never leave a patient unattended on an inflated HoverJack . 4.1.8 Use only attachments and/or accessories that are authorized by HMCAS. 4.2 HoverMatt : 4.2.1 For safety always use minimum two people opposite sides during patient transfer . 4.2.2 Additional paramedics are recommended when moving/ transferring a p atient over 340kg. 4.2.3 Never leave a patient unattended on an inflated device. 4.2.4 If possible, transfer from higher surface to a lower

surface. 4.2.5 Ensure patient is centered on receiving equipment prior to deflation. 4.2.6 Ensure that transfer surfaces are as close as possible and lock all wheels. 4.2.7 Never attempt to move a patient on an uninflated HoverMatt . 4.2.8 Use only attachments and/or accessories that are authorized by HMCAS. 4.3 Air Supply: 4.3.1 Ensure that the power cord is deployed so that it is free from hazard. 4.3.2 Avoid blocking the air intakes of the Air Supply. 4.3.3 Avoid electric shock. Do not open Air Supply. 4.3.4 The Air Supply must only be connected to a supply main with protective earth .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 272 4.3.5 The Air Supply is not compatible with DC power supplies. 4.3.6 Use only attachments and/or acc essories that are authorized by HMCAS. 5. Disinfection and Maintenance: 5.1 THE HoverJack : 5.1.1 The HoverJack should be periodically inspected to ensure the following: 5.1.1.1 All deflation valves are equipped with a red cap 5.1.1.2 The red caps are intact 5.1.1.3 All patient safety straps a re attached 5.1.1.4 Transport handles and straps are attached 5.1.1.5 Inflation valves are all self - sealing with no evident leakage 5.1.1.6 There are no punctures and tears 5.1.1.7 Do not launder the HoverJack 5.1.1.8 In between patient use, the HoverJack should be wiped down with a cleaning solution as directed by HMCAS infection control recommendations for surface cleaning. 5.1.1.9 A gentle scrub brush can also be used on the affected area if necessary, then clean the area according to HMCAS cleaning protocols . 5.2 The HoverMatt : 5.2.1 Is a single use device - Do not launder or reuse. 5.2.2 Prior to use, a visual inspection should be performed on the HoverMatt to ensure that there is no visible damage. 5.2.3 The HoverMatt s

hould have all of its patient safety straps and handles. 5.2.4 If any damage is found th at would impact safe use, then DO NOT USE, and request a replacement HoverMatt . 5.3 Air Supply: 5.3.1 After use, the surface of the Air Supply should be wiped down with a cleaning solution as recommended by HMCAS. 5.3.1.1 Do not spray cleaners/liquids directly on the Air Su pply 5.3.1.2 S pray the cleaning solution onto on cleaning clothe and then apply to Air Supply unit. 5.3.2 Prior to use visually inspect the Air Supply : 5.3.2.1 T o ensure the power cord is not damaged. 5.3.2.2 If damage remove from service and report to production. 5.3.3 The air filter must be assessed quarterly, and filter cleaned. 5.3.4 Between uses with an airborne isolation patient, the air supply filters can be removed and disinfected or replaced if HMCAS requires . 6. Usage: 6.1 The airlifting and moving set ( HoverJack / HoverMatt /A ir Supply) are available on the MIR first response units and can be dispatched upon request .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 273 7. Manpower : 7.1 A minimum of 8 - 10 staff should be deployed to operate the HoverJack Device. 7.2 If any occasion the above number of the staff not available due to manpower or space limitation safe to do, it’s not safe to do the stairwell evacuation in term of patient safety first, and paramedic safety . 7.3 Activating and deployment of the device it will the responsibility of Senior supervisor on scene and coordination w ith bronze clinical . 7.4 Crew on scene (Delta & paramedics) must assess the scene (stairwell wide and sharp point) for the proper movement of the device . 7.5 The MIR unit are overall IN CHARGE of the extrication . 8. Key safety points : 8.1 Maximum weight for stairwell evacuation - 300kg . ( 700lbs ) . 8.2 Maximum weight for vertical lift - 545 kg . ( 1200lbs ) . 8.3 Activation and deployment should be for the patient weight 200 kg above. 8.4 When moving a patient on inflated evacuation HoverJack, use caution and move slowly. 8.5 Never use patient safety strap to pull evacuation Hover J ack . 8.6 Make certain that patient safety strap is secured before moving . 8.7 Ensure patient is attached and

stabilized while on evacuation Hover J ack . 8.8 Do not release multiple chambers at once. 8.9 Ensure that pa tient remains centered on evacuation HoverJack and that his/ h er head does not slump forward and restrict breathing during evacuation. 8.10 For stairwell evacuation, deflate chambers 3 & 4 . 8.11 Do not launder

Implementation Date 17 January 20 23 Purpose To govern the process of transporting HMCAS medications by pharmacy staff and the travelling of staff from one location to another during duty. Responsibility HMCAS PHARMACY STAFF 1. Definitions: 1.1 Authorized vehicles for medication transportation : V ehicles under the direct management of HMCAS or HMC Transport Departmen t. 2. Procedure/Process: 2.1 HMCAS pharmacy staff may occasionally need to accompany HMCAS medications and transport them from one location to another . 2.2 HMCAS pharmacy staff are not permitted to use their private vehic les for neither the purpose of transporting HMCAS medications nor for the purpose of individual traveling from one location to another during duty . 2.3 There are cases when a staff may travel one journey without medications but may be asked to carry medication s with him in his return. Therefore, private cars are not allowed fo

r transportation during duty (unless an approval is granted from the staff superior in advance ). 2.4 HMCAS pharmacy staff must use an authorized vehicle for medication transportation and their travelling across locations during duty .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 277 SOP 4.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.4 EMERGENCY UNIT D ISTRIBUTION - HUB AN D SPOKE MODEL Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To direct and coordinate HMCAS resources for public access, including Hub and Spoke distribution, standby procedure, and allocation of escalated resources . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. The HUB and SPOKE model are designed to ensure that the public have rapid access to delivery of emergency care. See Appendix 4.4 . A : 2. The HUB represents the locations where ambulance personnel start and end their shif ts: 3. The SPOKES represent the various satellite/standby locations where HMCAS operational vehicles will be deployed: 4. Procedure : 4.1 Spoke standby allocation is restricted to available hub resources. 4.2 Vehicles must not exceed a maximum of three [3] hours of stand by at any spoke point where there is no physical structure provided to accommodate staff during standby. 4.3 When the call volume for a particular hub increases, the escalation to cover the spoke will be initi

ated by the Distribution Team Leader/Oscar NCC. 5. Res ource Escalation : 5.1 When the call volume for any particular hub increases, the escalation to cover Spoke 1 of that particular hub is as follows: 5.1.1 Vehicles from Spoke 2 – 4 of that particular hub will be redeployed to cover Spoke 1. 5.1.2 If these units are called a way - Charlie Unit assigned to the hub will be deployed to spoke 1. 5.1.3 If the Charlie unit is called away - Delta Unit assigned to this hub will be deployed to spoke 1 and On - Call Mike Units/Oscar Units will be notified. 5.1.4 If Delta unit is called away On - Call M ike Units/Oscar Units will be deployed to Spoke 1, and all On - Call Executive Team will be notified. 5.1.5 On - Call Executive Team will be deployed to Spoke 1, and Mike 1 notified. 5.2 When less than 30% of units are available over the entire system and are not expected to be available within the next 30 minutes the following procedure should be initiated by the DTL/Oscar NCC. 5.2.1 Contact the On - Call Silver Command 5.2.2 Call in extra staff if approved by Silver Command On duty. 5.3 When less than 10% of units are available ov er the entire system the following procedure will be initia

ted by the DTL: 5.3.1 Announce low coverage situation as general broadcast on the radio. 5.3.2 Hold all Priority 2 and inter - facility transfers 5.4 References : 5.4.1 Appendi x 4.4.A Emergency Unit Distribution - Current Hub and Spoke locations

Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONAL STAFF 1. HMCAS units primarily operate on Operational (Distribution) channels for the dispatch of ground units and LifeFlight. The most used channels are detailed in the table below : ASSIGNMENT Folder CHANNEL HUB - 1 MAIN - OPS AS - Doha 1 HUB - 2 MAIN - OPS AS - Doha 2 HUB - 4 MAIN - OPS AS - South 1 HUB - 5 MAIN - OPS AS - South 2/AS - South 3 HUB - 6 MAIN - OPS AS - North HUB - 7 MAIN - OPS AS - South HUB - 8 MAIN - OPS AS - Airport CHARLIE UNITS (CCP) MAIN - OPS AS - North 3 LIFEFLIGHT MAIN - OPS Air - Med 1/Air - Med 2 CLINICAL DESK MAIN - OPS Clinical 1 Coordination between Charlie and Alpha Crew MAIN - OPS Clinical 2 PRODUCTION TEAM LEADER (PTL) - NCC MAIN - OPS Production 1 PRODUCTION TEAM LEADER (UTL) - HCC MAIN - OPS Production 2 SAS - PTS MAIN - OPS AS - Tan

Other channels will be activated as needed for events, special events, MCI, and other. 2. Response Priority Definitions: 2.1 Priority Codes and examples : PRIORITY CODE EXAMPLES OF UNITS DISPATCHED Priority 1/Zulu code Alpha Unit & CCP unit (Charlie) Priority One, moving with lights and siren. Priority 1/Yankee code Alpha Unit Priority One, moving with lights and siren. Priority 2/X - ray code Alpha Unit Priority Two, moving without lights and siren. 2.2 Responding Unit/s need to follow the instructions of the Dispatcher . 3. Responsibilities of the dispatcher: 3.1 Assign the CLOSEST unit to the incident via NJM/MDT .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 283 3.2 Initiate the dispatch by calling the unit Call sign via the radio twice e.g. " Calling A1.1 - A1.1 " to Confirm the CFS reception . 3.3 Request the unit to change their radio channel to the appropriate one, If the closest unit does not belong to the same radio channel . 3.4 Notify the responding unit of any additional safety and/or infection control concerns. 4. Responsibilities of the responding crew: 4.1 Accept the CFS via the MDT immediately 4.2 Answer radio within 10 seconds of the radio dispatcher’s transmission . 4.3 Carefully read details in the MDT and listen to the details of the radio call and response priority as conveyed by dispatcher . 4.4 Confirm location details with dispatcher and if necessary, request step by step guidance . 4.5 Update the unit status in the MDT acc ordingly and use radio when necessary .

B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.5 . B NON - CONTROL CENTRE ACTIVATION Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To ensure appropriate activation of emergency services when it is requested directly by the public or external agencies. • To ensure efficient resource management via the National Command Centre (NCC). Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. HMCAS emergency vehicles may be notified of an incident/patient directly by the public or other agencies including but not limited to the police, civil defence , military, etc. 2. ALWAYS notify the NCC when a HMCAS vehicle is responding to an incident . 3. When s taff receives an emergency call directly from any source other than the NCC, the following procedure applies: 3.1 Immediately notify the dispatcher of the approximate location of the call while proceeding to the incident. 3.2 Notify the NCC if there is a need to r espond out of the allocated spoke area. 3.3 When applicable, allow the member of public to accompany the crew in the HMCAS vehicle. 3.4 If

it is necessary to follow a guide (member of public or police vehicle) to the incident, give clear and concise directions to the dispatcher to ensure rapid assistance from other units responding to the incident. 3.5 On arrival on scene, follow routine procedures as if you were activated by the NCC.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 285 SOP 4.5 . C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.5 . C RADIO COMMUNICATIO N & REPORTING Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure a uniform radio procedure for the communication and reporting of basic information between field crew and the Control . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. INTRODUCTION : 1.1 HMCAS Staff should be able to operate the radio equipment in accordance with the HMC Ambulance Service SOP 1.2 HMCAS staff are required to use approved radio voice procedures and strive to become proficient communicators. 1.3 Comprehensive and continuing training is critical to the successful use of communications equipment. All HMCAS staff (new personnel and existing staff) must be trained to use the radio equipment pro perly in conjunction with all the accessories. 1.4 It is the responsibility of the staff to report to his immediate supervisor to solicit assistance if additional training or direction is needed for the use of radio equipment. 2. BASIC OPERATING RULES: 2.1 Official L anguage : The Ambulance Servi

ce radio communication's official language is English. In an emergency if a language barrier exists, the operator may use his/her native language. 2.2 Control of Communications : The Communication Center has control of communications , and all units shall comply with all instructions in matters relating to the order and time of transmission. In some occasions (events/Clinical Coordination…) an operator from outside the communications can be designated as a channel controller. 2.3 R ecording s : All radio communications are automatically recorded. Recordings are confidential and will be monitored/released for authorized purposes only. 2.4 Care of Equipment: HMCAS personnel assigned with a TETRA radio is solely responsible for its security and will be held accountable for any loss or damage, all operators should ensure that their radio device is: 2.4.1 Kept with them and constantly monitored. 2.4.2 Secured either with a belt holster or place in the trousers radio pocket or vest. 2.4.3 Operational and battery charged. 2.4.4 Set to the correct talk group and to a suitable volume so it can be heard at all times. 2.4.5 All TETRA radios shall be accounted for at shift change by the HMCAS

stores representative and be recorded in the TETRA Radio Duty Logbook in the HMCAS stores or hub. 2.5 TETRA Radio Channel Allocation (see SOP 4.5.A ) Radio Reporting : 2.5.1 Arrival report : Upon assessment of the patient, transmit the following information to the NCC Dispatcher being as concise as possible: 2.5.1.1 Qatar Early Warning Score [Q.E.W.S] – Within 2 minutes if possible. 2.5.1.2 Delta or Charlie assistance required or not.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 286 2.5.1.3 Requirement for more Ambulances and number, if applicable. 2.5.1.4 Requirement for Civil Defense, Traffic Police, Life Flight etc., if applicable. 2.5.1.5 Before tra nsporting the patient to hospital, update the NCC Dispatcher as follows: 2.5.1.6 Unit number, destination, and ETA 2.5.1.7 Patient age, sex, and nationality 2.5.1.8 Level of consciousness and orientation 2.5.1.9 Chief complaint and paramedic impression, including: 2.5.1.9.1 Severity 2.5.1.9.2 Symptoms 2.5.1.9.3 Pain score on a scale of 0 - 10 2.5.1.9.4 Mechanism of trauma/pertinent scene information 2.5.2 Other reports : 2.5.2.1 IMIST - AMBO report ( SOP 4.8 . B & 4.8 . L ) 2.5.2.2 METHANE report ( SOP 4.10 . A ) 2.5.2.3 Reporting unit collision ( SOP 4.6 ) 2.6 Urgency & Distress Communications Procedure: A unit having a message of higher precedence than the routine transmission in progress may b reak in and suspend the transmission in the following manner: 2.6.1 Urgency Communications: The proword ‘ BREAK’ spoken three times means cease transmission and listen for an incoming URGENT message. Silence will be maintained until the unit breaking - in

has passed its message. In most cases this ‘break’ will be requested for Urgency Communications with important information of concern such as Ambulance RTA, patient condition has deteriorated with CPR in progress, Major incident ,… Example: Unit BREAK - BREAK - BREAK, This is Alpha one one I have important message 2.6.2 Distress Communications: Scenarios involving life - threatening situations to personnel or people and requiring immediate assistance will have the highest priority; the chan nel is to be cleared of any other transmissions not pertaining to the emergency in progress. The proword to be used is “Emergency, Emergency, Emergency” The Distress call format will be as follows: 2.6.2.1 The distress signal “EMERGENCY” spoken three times; 2.6.2.2 The prowords “THIS IS”; 2.6.2.3 The call sign of the unit in distress spoken three times. 2.6.2.4 The nature of the distress and the kind of assistance required.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 287 2.6.2.5 Any other important information (Exact Location, Number) . 2.6.2.6 Radio Silence can be imposed by the Control using the pr oword “ SILENCE ” three time . 2.6.2.7 If Radio Silence was imposed, the control will lift it using the proword “ SILENCE LIFTED ” . Example A: Distress Call : Unit “ EMERGENCY, EMERGENCY, EMERGENCY, Control, this Alpha one three, Alpha one three, Alpha one three, We are facing an attacker armed with knife, we need police immediately. Our current location is Al Watan Petrol Station . ” Example B: Radio Silence Control “ All units except Alpha one three this is control, SILENCE, SILENCE, SILENCE out . ” Example C: Lifting Radio Silence Control “ All units this is control, SILENCE LIFTED out . ” 2.7 Closing Down / Terminating the use of an established talk - group or channel: In some situations, such as special events, Major Incidents, etc. When an established talk - group is no longer required and is to be closed down, notice must be given to all units on that specific talk - group/channel for closing down or terminating the use of the established talk - group. No unit should close down wi

thout prior permission from the Control. The Proword CLOSE DOWN will be used Example A: Motor GP event Control Unit “All Units, All Units, All Units. THIS IS Control. CLOSE DOWN OUT.” (No reply required.) Example B: End of Major Incident Control Unit “All Units, All Units, All Units. THIS IS Control. We are terminating use of CONTROL MAJOR CHANNEL (No reply required.)

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 288 All units shall return to their normal operational channels OUT.” 2.8 Changing a Radio/Talk Group/Channel: In some circumstances, it may be necessary for the unit to change channels or talk groups for a certain task or assignment. 2.9 The proword SWITCH TO is used to direct unit(s) to change to another talk - group/channel. 2.9.1 When your unit has been instructed to change to another channel for dispatch, remain on the new channel until the call is completed . 2.9.2 On completion of the call, update the dispatchers that you will switch back to original channel and update the other dispatcher once you are in the channel. Example: Step A. Control directing a unit to change to a new channel Control Unit “Charlie One, SWITCH TO major incident channel OVER.” “ Control, Charlie One, switching to Major Incident Channel, OVER.” 3. SHIFT CHANGE PROCEDURE FOR OPERATIONAL CREW . 3.1 At the start of the shift, the Production Supervisor/Staff announce availability on the Standby Channel with the PTL and relay the following information: 3.1.1 Unit Call Sign 3.1.2 Vehicle registration number 3.1.3 LP15 HBE number, MDT number 3.2 Ch

ange to the duty channel and announce availability and standby location to the NCC Dispatcher. 3.3 Notify the NCC Dispatcher and the Production Supervisor/PTL that you are “Out of service” or “Handing over” the vehicle to the next shift, or any emergency for vehicle or equipment failure . 4. APPENDIX A - Authorized Procedure Words (Prowords) in HMC Ambulance Service : APPENDIX A Authorized Procedure Words ( Prowords ) in HMC Ambulance Service Proword or Phrase Meaning/Definition/Instructions ACKNOWLEDGE Confirm that you have received and understood the message. AFFIRMATIVE This means YES or Correct. Normally used when a question is asked, and the reply is YES. ALL AFTER Used with “SAY AGAIN” Meaning to repeat parts of message after a specific word. (Example: SAY AGAIN ALL AFTER “Street” .) ALL BEFORE Used with “SAY AGAIN” To repeat parts of a message before a specific word (Example: SAY AGAIN ALL BEFORE “Street” .) ALL BETWEEN Used with “Say again” to define the part of the message to be repeated do not use unless it is related to a written message .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 289 Proword or Phrase Meaning/Definition/Instructions ALL UNITS Call signs for all units in the channel BE ADVISED For impartin g/conveying information BREAK, BREAK, BREAK I have an URGENT Message, All units will immediately cease Transmission (Used only for URGENT Communications) . CLOSE DOWN A n order over the radio to close a specific channel . CONFIRM Have I received the following ... or Did you receive the message? COPY Message is heard and understood. CORRECTION An error has been made in last transmission. Correction transmission follows. DISREGARD Consider this transmission as not sent/ignore what has just been said. EMERGENCY, EMERGENCY, EMERGENCY I have a message of Life - threatening Importance – All other transmissions must cease . ETA Estimated Time of Arrival EXERCISE, EXERCISE, EXERCISE Used to indicate that the following message is practice/exercise and not a real situation. FIGURES Group of one or more numerals. The numbers are said by each numeral . GO AHEAD Proceed with your message GRID A Grid reference to follow HOW DO YOU READ? What is the readability of my transmission? I SAY AGAIN I repeat all or sp

ecified part of a message to clarify or emphasize. (Remember we don’t use the word “repeat”.) I SPELL I will spell the next word or group of words phonetically. (See Phonetic Alphabet). I READ BACK The following is my reply to your request to read back. READY FOR SERVICE A unit is available to respond . LONG MESSAGE What follows is a long message. This is to make the receiver ready to receive and/or write the message. MINIMISE Reduce transmission – to essential messages only. RESPONDING En route/ Responding to the call. LOUD & CLEAR ( U sed with RADIO CHECK Signal Strength). Can receive and understand transmissions MONITOR Listen on (Channel/talkgroup). MORE TO FOLLOW More information to follow. NEGATIVE No, or that is not correct . NO FURTHER ASSISTANCE NEEDED No assistance required . NOTHING HEARD ( U sed with RADIO CHECK Signal Strength). No transmission received . ON SCENE Arrived at the scene. OUT I have finished my transmission and no response is expected.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 290 Proword or Phrase Meaning/Definition/Instructions OUT OF SERVICE A unit is unavailable to respond . OVER My transmission ended, and I expect a response from you. PRIORITY 1 Responding to a call with lights and siren. PRIORITY 2 Responding to a call without lig hts and siren. PROCEED To go or to continue. RADIO CHECK What is my signal strength and readability? READ BACK Repeat all or the specified part of this message back to me exactly as received . READABLE (used with RADIO CHECK Signal Strength). Can receive and understand transmissions (good readability 90 – 100%) . RECEIVED Message/Transmission is completed/received. RENDEZVOUS Meeting place of two or more responding units. Also a message to say “Go to the place or meet.” SAY AGAIN Meaning repeat you r last transmission. Alternative way to say “COME AGAIN”. (Do not use the word “REPEAT”) SPEAK SLOWER Reduce the speed of transmission. SPEAK FASTER Increase the speed of transmission. SILENCE, SILENCE, SILENCE An international expression to advise that a distress situation is in progress. SILENCE LIFTED An international expression to indicate that the distress situat

ion has ended. STANDBY Stay alert, further information to follow /Do not transmit again until called upon . SWITCH TO Change to a new radio channel . THIS IS The originator call sign of the transmitting UNITS follows. TO The addressee(s) immediately following is (are) addressed for action. UNABLE TO LOCATE Unable to find the location UNREADABLE Can receive but cannot understand transmissions (readability of less than 10%) . D Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.5 . D USE OF MOBILE DATA T ERMINALS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure efficient workflow during dispatch via Mobile Data Terminals . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Make sure the MDT is fully operational by testing the MDT is “ON” and the Username is active . Remember that MDT should be endorsed LOGGED ON , not LOGGED OFF . 2. Do not turn off or shut down the MDT especially when changing shift; simply just switch user (by log off). 3. The MDT could be restarted once a day (preferably on the evening shift) after dispatcher notification. 4. If the MDT is malfunctioning, report to the Production Supervisor to replace the MDT. 5. Login with username (use of a training username or other colleagues’ username is strictly prohibited). 6. Once the MDT is fully operational, report to the PTL via the standby channel and confirm whether the MDT is linked to the assigned vehicle. 7. Once the PTL gives the “ go ahead ” reply, report to the control room via operation

al radio channel to do the radio check and repor t “ MDT active ” . 8. Responders shall be advised via radio about the incoming P1 or P2 call on the MDT . If information is not received on the MDT, revert to the standard procedure. 9. If the unit has an active MDT, receive the call via the radio and simultaneously accept it through the MDT . All other updates of the unit status will be done via MDT ONLY, EXCEPT when: 9.1 Calling for additional help 9.2 Reporting a safety issue 9.3 MDT is offline or when there is MDT failure 9.4 In emergency (Requesting for specific urgent assistanc e, reporting the number of victims, MCI, and any status not available in the MDT). 10. At the end of the call and when becoming available, update the unit status through the MDT as “ CLEARED ”. 11. Responsibilities of the PTL: 11.1 Keep an updated record of all units with Active MDT. 11.2 Update any MDT change as required. 11.3 Notify the DTL and Oscar on duty with the list of units with Active MDT. 12. Responsibilities of Production/Vehicle - Control Supervisor: 12.1 Ticket MDTs appropriately 12.2 When necessary, change MDT from one vehicle to another vehicle. 12.3 Immediately notify the PTL if any MDT
is changed from one vehicle to another.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 294 SOP 4.5.E Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.5.E DELTA DISPATCH PROCE SS Implementation Date 25 January 2020 Purpose To provide guidance on the dispatch criteria for the Delta units. Responsibility ALL HMCAS DELTA STAFF 1. The Ambulance Service Supervisor commonly named DELTA oversees delivery of Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service (HMCAS) activity within his Service Delivery Area. This oversight should be reviewed against the appropriate Service Delivery Plans that match resource to demand and are consistent with the HMC corporate service delivery framework. The DELTA is required to work with colleagues from all Directorates to ensure sufficient service delivery is achieved. The DELTA is required to monitor and ensure quality of the service in his area as measured through existing and futur e performance indicators. The DELTA is required to take on appropriate supervisory roles during Special Events, Multiple Casualty Incidents (MCI) and other major incidents as required. 2. The DELTA is dispatched by NCC to the following c

ase types based on the Dispatch Matrix: 2.1 All cases where other emergency and safety departments are deployed, such as Civil Defense, ISF, Traffic, Police, Customs, Coast Guard . 2.2 All cases within governmental and official instances, like Ministries, Embassies, Police Stations . 2.3 All cases with safety concern, such as behavioral and psychiatric disorder, cases within jails, strikes, suicide attempts, and maritime accidents . 2.4 All cases with confirmed High Mechanism of Injury, long fall, building collapse, blast injuries, trapped casualt ies, Human Run Over, Crushing Injuries . 2.5 All cases of undetermined status of life, Cardiac Arrest, Agonal Breathing, Peri - Arrest status, undeniable death, entrapment . 2.6 All cases where the DELTA has been notified by TETRA radio that the DELTA will be the initial responding unit. 3. The DELTA should monitor the following: 3.1 All Yankee cases with predefined medical complaint and/or traumatic injury . 3.2 All Yankee cases within Health Care Facilities: Hospitals, Health Centers, Private Clinics . 3.3 All Yankee cases where his response time would exceed the scene time allocated to the dispatched unit. 4. The DELTA should self - di

spatch to the following incidents: 4.1 Whenever DELTA backup is requested . 4.2 Whenever the DELTA witnesses any emergency status . 5. The DELTA should DIVERT from t heir initial dispatch, as follows: 5.1 Whenever their presence on scene would be beneficial for HMCAS service delivery to the patient, to the patient’s family, or to any incident involving interference from other emergency agencies.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 295 SOP 4.5.F Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.5.F CANCELLATION OF CHAR LIE O R LIFE F LIGHT Implementation Date 07 September 2021 Purpose Procedure to cancel a Critical Care Paramedic Unit or LifeFlight . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Background and Purpose: 1.1 This SOP provides guidance for the cancellation of the responding Charlie unit or LifeFlight. The following procedure must be followed when an on - scene unit needs to cancel a Charlie unit or LifeFlight. 2. Acronyms : 2.1 CCP: Critical Care Paramedic 2.2 AP: Ambulance Paramedic 2.3 ECG: Electrocardiogram 2.4 CC Emergency: Clinical Coordinator: Emergency 2.5 AirMed 1: Primary TETRA Radio frequency for LifeFlight 2.6 Clinical 1: Primary TETRA Radio frequency for CC: Emergency 2.7 Clinical 2: TETRA Radio frequency used for coordinating a rendezvous with CCP 3. Cancellation of a responding Charlie unit : 3.1 When the transporting unit requires cancellation of the responding CCP unit , the unit must notify the dispatcher of their intention change to Clinical 2. T

he dispatcher must acknowledge the request and ask the responding CCP unit to switch to Clinical 2 in accordance with SOP 4.8.L . 3.2 The tr ansporting unit must ensure that a full set of vital signs has been completed and provide an IMIST - AMBO report to the responding CCP unit on Clinical 2. The responding CCP will decide to continue to the scene, set a rendezvous point, or stand down from the case if the CCP determines that CCP assistance is not required. 3.2.1 Where a CCP is required, the transporting unit and Charlie unit must remain on Clinical 2 to coordinate a rendezvous point. 3.2.2 Where the on - scene unit have completed a clinical assessment and de termines that a CCP is not required, the on - scene unit must contact their dispatcher and advise the dispatcher that Charlie is not required. 3.3 The dispatcher must acknowledge the request and cancel and assign the responding CCP unit as available . 3.4 The on - scen e unit should return to their original dispatch channel and transport the patient to receiving facility avoiding unnecessary delays in transport. 4. Cancellation of LifeFlight: 4.1 Any on - scene CCP or Delta may directly cancel LifeFlight via the regular disp

atch channel. However, all other unit must consult with CC: Emergency for the cancellation of LifeFlight. 4.2 LifeFlight must be cancelled as soon as possible when it is identified that LifeFlight is not required either for LifeFlight capabilities or for the Critic al Care resources.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 296 4.2.1 The on - scene unit must notify the dispatcher of their intent to change to Clinical 1, and the dispatcher must acknowledge the request to switch channels. 4.2.2 The on - scene unit must endorse the patient to CC: Emergency, using the IMIST - AMBO handover and include information on the patient’s location. 4.3 The CC: Emergency must account for the following criteria before confirming the request to cancel LifeFlight: 4.3.1 Geographic location of the patient and need for retaining resources in the area. 4.3.2 Wheth er transport by air would provide clinical benefit to the patient. 4.3.3 The possible deterioration of the patient and the patient requiring a CCP transport. 4.4 If it is agreed that LifeFlight should be cancelled: 4.4.1 The on - scene unit must return to their regular dispatch channel after the consultation and advise the dispatcher that LifeFlight is cancelled. 4.4.2 The CC: Emergency must immediately update the LifeFlight Coordinator who will cancel LifeFlight over AirMed 1 and update the CFS notes. 4.5 Where LifeFlight will continue to the case: 4.5.1 The on - scene unit must switch to AirMed 1 an

d maintain radio communication with the Flight Coordinator and LifeFlight crew. 4.5.2 The CC: Emergency must immediately advise the LifeFlight Coordinator of the updated incident and clinica l details. 4.5.3 The LifeFlight Coordinator must update the CFS notes and update the LifeFlight crew on the rendezvous location and clinical update.
