A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.6 . A DRIVING OF EMERGEN CY VEHICLES Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To ensure safe and lawful driving behaviour when responding to emergencies, as well during routine driving and parking . • To ensure safe and lawful driving behaviour during transportation of patients . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Before moving the emergency vehicle, ensure that ALL occupants of the emergency vehicle are buckled - in safely, including strapping/buckling - in of the patient on the stretcher. 2. Fasten vehicle doors properly to ensure they do not open whilst the vehicle is on the move. NEVER move an ambulance vehicle whilst any doors are open. 3. Secure equipment properly to avoid injuries to passengers. 4. Ensure that the rear step of the ambulance i s in the ‘up’ position before moving off. 5. Proceed with due caution to other road users and adhere to safe driving practices at all times especially whilst responding to emergencies. 6. Never drive PRIORITY - 1 unless trai

ned in PRIORITY - 1 driving techniques. 7. Us e emergency warning devices [lights and sirens] only for calls that fit criteria classified as a medical emergency [Priority - 1], as determined by the dispatch matrix. 8. In extreme low - resource situations, the on - duty Executive Team member may authorize vehic les to function with lights and sirens in non - Priority - 1 situations. 9. As applicable you may use two types of Priority 1 driving; Priority 1 Response and Priority 1 Transport. 10. Priority - 2 driving implies normal driving behaviour, following all road rules and excludes the use of emergency lights and sirens. 11. When possible, the driver should not operate the handheld radio whilst driving. 12. Priority - 1 Response Driving Guideline: 12.1 These guidelines should be followed for all response driving to a priority one case. 12.2 An emergency vehicle DOES NOT have the automatic right to proceed through a red traffic light across traffic. 12.3 By operating emergency lights and sirens, the HMCAS vehicle requests the permission of other motorists for the right of way. 12.4 Report to the N CC any significant delays in response due to congested traffic. 12.5 Ambulances MUST

come to a complete stop precisely in the following two positions when approaching a red traffic signal : 12.5.1 At the pedestrian lane at any intersection or crossing. 12.5.2 At the yellow bo x . 12.5.2.1 When directed by a law enforcement officer. 12.5.2.2 Red traffic lights and STOP signs. If in a quiet suburb with high visibility, the driver may yield at STOP signs ONLY IF SAFE TO DO SO.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 301 12.5.2.3 When the driver cannot account for all lanes of traffic when crossing an intersection. 12.5.2.4 When other intersection hazards are present. 12.5.2.5 When encountering a stopped bus, due to the risk of alighting passengers. 12.5.2.6 When there are any other potential hazards or adverse conditions. 12.6 Ambulances are not permitted to use the contraflow lane u nless police forces are actively controlling traffic during a Major Incident . 12.7 Whilst responding Priority 1, the emergency response vehicle may exceed the legal posted speed limit by a maximum of 20 km/h e.g. driving a maximum of 100km/h in an 80km/h zone. 12.8 Be alert for other responding emergency vehicles. The right of way for two [2] responding emergency vehicles shall be as follows: 12.8.1 When approaching red traffic signals or intersections , priority shall be given to Civil Defence vehicles . Ambulance drivers should allow Civil Defence vehicles to proceed first as they are the first who secure the scene. 12.8.2 Vehicle having the right of way by traffic control devices. 12.8.3 Vehicles that will be travelling through an intersection withou

t negotiating any turns will have preference over vehicles having to turn. 12.8.4 The first vehicle in the intersection shall have the right of way. 12.9 When following another responding vehicle, allow a following distance of 1.5 vehicle lengths per 10 km/h of speed in order to stop safely shou ld the lead vehicle stop abruptly. 12.10 Be aware of the possibility that motorists may pull into your path after yielding to the lead vehicle[s]. 13. Vehicle safety on - scene : 13.1 Secure a safe ambulance parking within a designated or appropriate parking space. 13.2 Ensure parking selected is able to provide safety for yourself, your patient and other road users. 13.3 Vehicles should have their engines turned off or the Run Lock system should be activated, secure by locking with key/remote and keys to remain with AP crew a nd the vehicle key should only be shared with HMC AS Staff. 13.4 If vehicles on - scene need to be left idling for AC and emergency lights, ensure that all vehicles doors are locked. 13.5 Prior to leaving the scene, ensure all equipment is onboard and secured and ensu re all doors are appropriately locked. 13.6 Ensure all patient, all patient’s escorts (HMC Staff/Fami

ly/Friends/Police, etc.) and AS staff are well secured prior to leaving the scene. 14. Priority - 1 Transportation to Hospital using Warning Devices: 14.1 These guidelines should be followed for all driving while transporting a patient to hospital where based on the patient’s condition rapid transport to hospital is the recommended action in the interest of best patient care. 14.2 NEVER use HMCAS vehicles to provide an escort fo r an ambulance conveying a critical patient to hospital. 14.3 Adhere to all speed limits during Priority - 1 driving to hospital with a patient on board.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 302 14.4 Avoid sudden harsh braking and jerking of the vehicle. 14.5 Drive in a way that allows for effective patient manag ement without undue patient care disruption. 14.6 If there is a need to stop the ambulance to undertake any clinical procedure/intervention, do so in a safe and responsible manner (as directed by SOP 4.8.I ). 14.7 An emergency vehicle DOES NOT have the automatic right to proceed through a red traffic light across traffic. 14.8 By operating emergency lights and sirens, the HMCAS vehicle requests the permission of other motorists for the right of way. 14.9 Bring the emergency vehicle to a complete stop under the following circumstances. 14.9.1 When directed by a law enforcement officer. 14.9.2 Red traffic lights and STOP signs. If in a quiet suburb with high visibility, the driver may yield at STOP signs ONLY IF SAFE TO DO SO. 14.9.3 When the driver cannot account for all la nes of traffic when crossing an intersection. 14.9.4 When other intersection hazards are present. 14.9.5 When encountering a stopped bus, due to the risk of alighting passengers. 14.9.6 When there are any other potential hazards or

adverse conditions. 14.10 Contra - flow driving is extremely dangerous and should NEVER be used. 14.11 Be alert for other responding emergency vehicles. The right of way for two [2] responding emergency vehicles shall be as follows: 14.11.1 Vehicle having the right of way by traffic control devices. 14.11.2 Vehicles that will be travelling through an intersection without negotiating any turns will have preference over vehicles having to turn. 14.11.3 The first vehicle in the intersection shall have the right of way. 14.11.4 Be aware of the possibility that motorists may pull into your path after yielding to the lead vehicle[s]. 15. Reversing/backing - up of vehicles: 15.1 Prior to reversing, the driver and attendant/passenger must agree a safe path for the vehicle to take. 15.2 The attendant/passenger shall act as guide where ever p ossible. 15.3 Do not reverse the vehicle until the guide has checked that the area into which the vehicle is to travel is clear. 15.4 The guide shall stand in the position from which they can best be seen and heard by the driver in their nearside mirror. 15.5 Verbal inst ructions alone are inadequate as they may be drowned by the vehicle engine or othe

r noise. Use agreed hand signals. 15.6 When the guide’s view is restricted from the front or side of the vehicle, he/she shall take up a position towards the rear of the vehicle a nd eye contact must be maintained between the driver and the guide. 15.7 The guide must not position themselves between the reversing vehicle and a static object (wall, post, etc.).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 303 15.8 Reversing cameras MUST NOT be relied upon in isolation but are to be used as an aid to the driver. 16. In case of an RTA with an HMCAS vehicle refer to SOP 4.6.D . 17. In the case of a family/accompanying party request to upgrade the Alpha unit priority from P2 to P1 while transporting patient to hospital: 17.1 The patie nt, family/relatives on board, family escorting unit, or any other non - HMCAS person is NOT allowed to demand an upgrade to P1 driving. 17.2 Explain to the requesting person/s that it is unnecessary to upgrade the unit to P1 since: 17.2.1 The patient is clinically stab le; 17.2.2 There is no clinical benefit to rush to hospital; 17.2.3 There is an increased risk to safety of the patient, other passengers, the crew on board and public road users. 17.3 If the situation is hostile and you have no choice, immediately notify NCC dispatcher and upgrade to P1 driving with caution (as per bullet - point 13 above). 17.4 Immediately request for a Qatari liaison officer to contact the patient attendant to speak with the crew. 17.5 Request MOI assistance to arrange a police officer to meet the ambulance at

the rec eiving facility. 17.6 As soon as possible, escalate the incident to immediate supervisor, AS supervisor at receiving facility and Operations Officer on shift. 17.7 After handover of the patient to receiving facility document the incident on the OVA system. 17.8 A Qatari liaison officer shall contact the family within 24 hours to reaffirm the inappropriateness/risk/dangers of the family request to upgrade to P1 driving .

B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.6 . B PRAYER DURING HMCA S WORKING HOURS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To ensure that emergency calls are not delayed during Islamic Prayer times, whilst HMCAS staff are on active duty . • To ensure that there is ample opportunity for HMCAS Islamic staff to pray during working hours . Responsibility ALL HMCAS STAFF 1. HMCAS shall endeavour to provide a place of Islamic worship with adequate ablution facilities and religious paraphernalia in each of its locations. 2. General Rules : 3. Emergency calls have precedence over the right to pray. 4. Standby at a spoke point always takes precedence over the right to pray. 5. Priority - 1 and Priority - 2 calls cannot wait until an employee finishes his or her prayer. 6. A Priority 3 Call is not an emergency and can wait until an employee finishes his or her prayer. 7. A Spoke Point without Facilities is a geographical ly optimized response point where no physical structure is provided for HMCAS staff, thus requiring employees to

remain in their vehicles whilst waiting for calls; e.g. roundabouts and petrol stations. 8. When employees are on standby at spoke points without facilities, the HMCAS Communications Centre shall attempt to allow the unit to return to a location with prayer facilities at prayer time; however operational priorities and the needs of all employees to have equal opportunity for refreshment and rest (as well as prayer) will be taken into account. 9. If an employee is interrupted while praying, every attempt will be made to allow him or her to return to a location with prayer facilities as soon as possible, taking into account operational priorities. 10. Priority - 1 and Priority - 2 Calls : 10.1 If praying and your unit is dispatched, respond immediately to the call. 10.2 Ensure that while praying, a member of HMCAS staff is monitoring the radio. 10.3 If all employees at a location are praying at the same time, notify the NCC Dispa tcher and monitor both the radio and mobile phones. 10.4 If a call comes in during prayers the employee monitoring the radio shall immediately notify the praying crews and respond to the call without delay. 11. Attendance of Prayers at a Mosque : 11.1 First

request permi ssion from the HMCAS Communications Centre before attending prayers at a mosque. 11.2 Permission shall only be granted for employees to attend a mosque at an operationally suitable location and operational priorities will need to be considered. 11.3 If monitoring of the radio is impossible, notify the HMCAS Communications Centre to contact the unit by mobile phone. 11.4 Be in a position for a quick exit if praying in a mosque.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 305 SOP 4.6 . C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.6 . C EMERGENCY RESPONSE AND NON - EMERGENCY D RIVING DURING ADVERSE WEATHER COND ITIONS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure safe and defensive driving principles are used when driving in adverse weather conditions . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L CLINICAL PERSONNEL (E.G. ALPHA, BRAVO, CHARLIE, DELTA, OSCA R, MIKE, AND EVENT STAFF) 1. During general difficult driving conditions , where traction, vision and handling implications are likely to occur or has occurred: 1.1 Inform NCC of adverse weather condition/s. 1.2 Reduce speed and move over to a slow lane that will allow for safer driving conditions, keeping both hands firmly on the steering wheel. 1.3 Be courteous to other road users and be mindful of road conditions, signage and posted speed limits . 1.4 Increase the distance between your vehicle and the vehicle ahead, to allow for safe stopping. 1.5 Obtain maximum visibility by turning on low - beam headlights . Request permission from NC C to turn on emergen

cy lights. 1.6 Keep the windows and windshield clear. 1.7 Stow loose items securely and keep the dashboard free of clutter. 1.8 Frequently check mirrors for approaching vehicles. 1.9 Accelerate and brake as smoothly and gently as possible, avoiding sha rp turns and sudden stops (improves ride quality and promotes patient safety and comfort). 2. Driving in rain or fog conditions: 2.1 Follow all procedures for general difficult driving conditions and; 2.2 Be aware of slow - moving vehicles. 2.3 Be aware that vehicles usin g hazard lights are likely to be stationary. 2.4 Avoid flooded roads and large puddles; seek an alternative route. 2.5 If visibility is poor, pull over safely and completely off the road; turn on the hazard lights; wait until visibility improves and it is safe to resume driving. 2.6 Avoid harsh acceleration and braking . If roads are slippery, consider using cadence braking. 3. Driving in windy/sand storm conditions: 3.1 Follow all procedures for general difficult driving conditions and; 3.2 Prepare to make adjustments in speed and steering to compensate for wind conditions. 3.3 Safely pull over if necessary, to allow strong gusty winds to subside. 3.4 Anticipate gu

sts of wind in rural areas and when overtaking, crossing culverts or bridges, or being passed by large vehicles. 3.5 Be aware of slow - moving vehicles. 3.6 Be aware that vehicles using hazard lights are likely to be stationary.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 306 3.7 If visibility is poor, p ull over safely and completely off the road; turn on the hazard lights; wait until visibility improves and it is safe to resume driving . 4. Driving in sun - glare conditions: 4.1 Follow all procedures for general difficult driving conditions and; 4.2 Use the sun visor appropriately and wear sunglasses if appropriate. 4.3 Use lane markers to help maintain lane discipline. 4.4 If visibility is poor pull over safely and completely off the road; turn on the hazard lights; wait until visibility improves and it is safe to resume driv ing.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 307 SOP 4.6 . D Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.6 . D RTA WHILE DRIVING EMERGENCY VEHICLES Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure the safety of HMCAS Staff, Patients and Bystanders . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L CLINICAL PERSONNEL (E.G. ALPHA, BRAVO, CHARLIE, DELTA, OSCA R, MIKE, AND EVENT STAFF) 1. If a HMCAS vehicle is involved in an RTA/collision the crew shall: 1.1 Stop the vehicle, ensure scene safety, and park the vehicle in a safe place (if possible). 1.2 Ensure access to the victims, treat, and facilitate transport to hospital if possible. 1.3 Notify NCC/HCC via current channel 1.4 Inform PTL, then immediate supervisor 2. Injur y to staff /patient and safe transportation : 2.1 Injury to the staff or patient being transported. 2.1.1 Assess the scene for safety issues and call NCC for help and other unit(s). 2.1.2 Communicate with the opposite driver/passengers to reach agreement to allow transfer of patients to the hospital before proceeding to the police station. 2.1.3 If the opposite driver is not in

agreement , call for another unit(s) and stay ON SCENE until arrival of the police. 2.2 Provide medical treatment to the victim if possible, until another unit arrives on the scene. 2.3 Park the vehicle in a safe place and maintain communication with public and police officers. 3. Collision with another vehicle: 3.1 If there are any patients, follow Section 2 above. 3.2 If there are no patients, communicate with the opposite d river to reach agreement to proceed to the police station for police report; if you do not reach agreement, wait on scene for the police after parking the vehicle in a safe place (if possible). 3.3 If possible, take photographs of the damage to the vehicle/s f or documentation reasons . 3.4 Before the end of the shift, the involved staff shall ; 3.4.1 Complete an OVA report 3.4.2 Obtain the police report 3.4.3 Obtain the staff RTA investigation report 3.4.4 ( Note: Police report and Staff Investigation Report should be completed within 48 hours ) . 4. Collision with a fixed object: 4.1 Follow Section 3 above 4.2 Provide a copy of your Driving License and QID to Fleet, and report to immediate supervisor with the mentioned documents. 4.3 ( Note: Police Report s
hould be completed within 10 days )

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 308 5. Post RTA procedure: 5.1 Staff shall be invited to the local RTA panel meeting in the event of any RTA related damage (vehicle, TETRA radio, MDT, touch screen struck, water or other unexplained damages). 5.2 The local RTA panel shall include representation from Operations, Production, Quality, Driver Training and Fleet Supervision. 5.3 The Executive RTA panel shall include representation from Operations, Production, Quality, Driver Training and Fleet Management and shall: 5.3.1 Be chaired by an Assistant Executive Director . 5.3.2 Confirm or adjust the Local RTA Panel Decision to meet the criteria for suspending staff from driving immediately. 5.3.3 Decide internal sanctions and if necessary, recommend overall sanctions to the Executive team. 6. Situations that may result in disciplinary a ction: 6.1 Blatant disregard for SOP 4.6.A 6.2 Repeated “at fault” RTAs or collisions . 6.3 Staff may be suspended from driving in the case of serious incidents as detailed in the table below. SERIOUS INCIDENT (Stop from driving) NOT SERIOUS INCIDENT Accident occurs while crossing the Red - light Minor Damage Failure to follow SOP
Not a mistake of staff Accident results in injured victims Accidents puts vehicle out of service Negligent behaviour and actions Repeated RTA > 2 in 1 month Executive Panel Decision

A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.7 . A EMERGENCY SCENE ARRIVAL AND SI ZE - UP Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure a safe general response of HMCAS staff to emergency calls and emergency scenes . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L CLINICAL PERSONNEL (E.G. ALPHA, BRAVO, CHARLIE, DELTA, O SCAR, BIKE AND EVENT STAFF) 1. HMCAS staff shall base their actions and decisions on the HMCAS Code of Conduct. 2. Arrival on an Emergency Scene: 2.1 Notify the NCC Dispatcher of arrival on scene and conduct a hazards assessment. 2.2 Park the responding vehicle in a safe place, as close as possible to the point of patient contact. 2.3 Identify obvious and potential hazards, and where possible, identify access and egress routes, notifying the NCC where required. 2.4 If hazards exist or if there is uncertainty rega rding the safety of personnel or patients, request assistance from the NCC . Use reasonable judgement based on available when electing to delay patient care. 2.5 If no hazards exist that will prevent safe pa

tient access, approach the patient ensuring appropriat e Personal Protective Equipment [PPE] is worn. 2.6 Carry the minimum primary response equipment which includes the Primary Response Bag, Oxygen cylinder, Suction Unit and ECG Monitor/Defibrillator and Medication Bag . 3. Arrival on the Scene of a Road Traffic Accident (RTA) : 3.1 Ensure that the vehicle’s location and position provides safety while working on scene. 3.2 If other agencies have secured the scene already, position the emergency vehicle past the RTA scene (or uphill/upwind in the presence of hazardo us materials). This provides a safe area to load the patient/s at an already secured scene. 3.3 If no other agencies have secured the scene yet, position the emergency vehicle well behind the RTA [minimum 30 meters] in the fend - off position as illustrated belo w. 3.4 The fend - off position ensures that the emergency vehicle becomes a protective barrier to personnel and patients and deflects other vehicles that may collide into the active RTA scene.

B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.7 . B PREPARING A LANDIN G ZONE (LZ) Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To outline critical safety aspects for selecting and preparing a safe landing zone for helicopter emergency operations . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF, SPECIFICALL Y DISTRIBUTION SUPERVISORS 1. LifeFlight helicopter crews need assistance selecting suitable landing zones, warning pilots of potential hazards and securing landing zones during flight operations. 2. Selecting a Daytime Landing Zone: 2.1 Determine if the area is large enough to land a helicopter safely, no smaller than 70m x 70m [Fig. 1]. 2.2 The surface should be flat and firm (ideally, a road). Avoid loose, powdery san d or dirt. 2.3 The area should be free of any loose materials that might be blown into the rotors or engines [e.g. laundry, cardboard boxes, plywood etc.]. 2.4 The surface should be as flat and level as possible having a less than 10 - degree slope. 2.5 Landing on major highways may only take place if the road has b

een closed and access is controlled by the police. 3. The Touchdown Area: 3.1 The touchdown area should be at least a 70m x 70m during daylight hours. [Fig. 1]. 3.2 A non - emergency personnel/bystander clearance of 100m from the edge of the LZ. [Fig. 2]. 3.3 The landing site must be clear of obstructions (vehicles, trees, poles, and wires). [Fig. 3]. 3.4 The landing site must be free of building rubble, posts, and large rocks. 3.5 Electric and telephone wires are a particular hazard to helicopters, as they are much easier to see from the ground looking up than from a helicopter looking down into the ground clutter . Figure 1 Figure 2 Figure 3

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 314 4. Helicopter Landing Zone Preparation: 4.1 Preparation of a landing site is an essential function of ground personnel. Proper preparation of a safe LifeFlight landing site should include the following: 4.1.1 Clear the site of all loose objects (such as hats, paper, linen, etc.) that could be swept up in the rotor wash and sucked into the engine intakes or the rotors. 4.1.2 Secure the landing area with enough ground personnel to keep all unauthorized bystanders and vehicles away. 5. Landing Zones at Night: 5.1 LifeFlight helicopters will only land at designated Helicop ter Operating Sites [HOS] in the desert or ‘no road’ environments and in compliance with point 2.5 above for major highways at night. 5.2 Turn off unnecessary lights during the landing operation to help preserve the pilot’s night vision. 5.3 Pilots will utilize Ni ght Vision Goggles (NVG) for night flying operations. 5.4 During night flight operations, all spotlights, floodlights, and handheld lights should be pointed at the ground - NEVER at the helicopter. 5.5 All non - essential lights should be turned off, and any vehicle lights used to illuminate t
he landing zone should be on low beam. 5.6 LifeFlight helicopters are equipped with landing lights for night operations and will use them for landing. [Fig.4 ] . Figure 4

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 315 6. Landing Zone Coordinator Responsibilities: 6.1 Command and secure the LZ. 6.2 Establish radio contact with LifeFlight on AirMed 1 Channel. 6.3 Assist flight crew in locating the LZ. 6.4 Information required by LifeFlight Crew: 6.4.1 Landmarks (Malls, Water/cell phone towers, Schools, Sport or football fields, Major Road intersectio ns/round about, etc.). 6.4.2 Type of LZ [i.e. field or road] 6.4.3 LZ surface [i.e. sand, grass, concrete, gravel, dirt] 6.4.4 Boundaries of the LZ [i.e. trees, houses, wires, emergency vehicles] 6.4.5 Approach and departure pathways. 6.4.6 Landing Zone Hazards (power lines, camel fenc es, vehicles, oblivious bystanders, or any obstacles that might interfere with landing). 6.4.7 Notify the Flight Crew when you hear the helicopter and when you see the aircraft. 6.4.8 Direct the aircraft to your location using the clock method, remembering the pilot i s facing the 12 o’clock position [Fig. 5]. 6.4.9 Contact the flight crew after landing to determine any safety issues . Figure 5 7. Helicopter Safe on the Ground: 7.1 Once the helicopter has landed, wait for the rotor blades to stop and for t

he pilot to signal before anyone approaches the helicopter. [Fig. 6] 7.2 Always approach the helicopter in full view of the pilot - between 10 and 2 o'clock of the cockpit and ensure the pilo t sees you and waves you forward before approaching the helicopter. [Fig. 7]. 7.3 Keep a crouching, low body profile to show others that you are aware of the rotor hazard. 7.4 If you have a helmet, secure the chin strap. Do not wear hats, caps or headscarves; avoi d anything loose, such as a stethoscope around your neck. 7.5 Never carry anything overhead, including IV fluids. 7.6 Carry all long objects such as LBB’s parallel to the ground. 7.7 Collapse stretcher IV poles before approaching the helicopter.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 316 7.8 Always walk; never run , to and from a helicopter. 7.9 Never approach or depart from the uphill side if the landing zone is uneven . Always use the downhill side where you are in the pilot's view. [Fig. 6]. 7.10 NEVER approach the helicopter from the rear, the LZ Coordinator is to ensure that NO - ONE , including flight crew, will approach the rear of the helicopter. 7.11 No Vehicles should approach within 50 metres of the aircraft unless directed by the flight crew . 7.12 Under no circumstances will any vehicle drive under the helicopter rotor blades . [Fig.9] 7.13 Follow Flight Crew instructions at all times . Figure 6 Figure 7 Figure 9

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 317 8. Preparing the Patient for Helicopter Transport: 8.1 Secure anything that may be disturbed by rotor wash. 8.2 Protect the patient and yourself against debris/dirt blown. 8.3 Use eye protection and protect exposed skin. 8.4 Assist the helicopter crew in securing patient to stretcher. 8.5 Patient should be inside the ambulance in preparation for transport to helicopter. 9. Preparing for Departure : 9.1 Clear all groun d personnel away from the helicopter before the engine starts. 9.2 No one may approach the helicopter after engines have started. 9.3 Re - establish radio contact with the Flight Crew on AirMed 1 and confirm the LZ is secure. 9.4 Notify the Flight Crew immediately if an unsafe situation develops. 9.5 Always give the highest priority to the safety of the helicopter and its crew. 10. Designated Helicopter Operating Sites : 10.1 Helicopter Operating Sites (HOS) at Truck Weigh Stations will facilitate the landing of LifeFlight helicopters closer to incidents along freeways. 11. Designated HOS GPS coordinates are detailed in Appendix 4.7 . B - 1 .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 318 SOP 4.7.C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.7.C SCENE SAFETY Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide guidance on maintaining the highest level of safety while conducting on - scene operations . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. The goal of this SOP is to ensure safety of Ambulance Service staff, patients, and members of the public while on scene of each call. 2. Approaching the scene : 2.1 When approaching the scene, the Ambulance Service crew should scan the area for hazards such as ele ctrical power lines, spilled fluids, etc. 2.2 Any identified hazards should be reported to NCC and assistance requested from Delta and/or police. 3. Parking on scene : 3.1 All Ambulance Service vehicles will be parked in a safe area. 3.2 Prior to parking on the scene, precautions for hazardous situations will be observed as follows: 3.2.1 Park clear of any overhead hazards, such as electrical power lines. 3.2.2 Park uphill from any spilled liquids. 3.2.3 In case of fire, the crew must park a safe distance a way from the

scene. 3.3 Do not park in a manner that will obstruct exit from the scene. 3.4 The driver should attempt to park in an area where reversing is not required when parking or leaving the scene. 3.5 The ambulance should be parked in a manner to allow for imme diate and safe exit from the scene. 4. On Scene Operations : 4.1 Ambulance Service crews will enter a private residence after receiving appropriate permission. 4.2 While approaching the patient, the ambulance crew should be alert for any potential hazards, always maki ng sure the scene is safe to provide effective care. 4.3 When on scene with RTA: 4.3.1 The ambulance must be parked after the site of the accident in a safe place as far from the flow of traffic as possible. 4.3.2 All crew must wear a reflective safety jacket/vest if the RTA is on a public road. 4.3.3 If the ambulance is parked on a public road, the emergency lights should remain on in order to warn other drivers. 4.4 If the crew encounters any violent patient or family member, notify NCC immediately and retreat to a safe location u ntil additional resources arrive. 4.5 If Ambulance Service staff are given orders to perform an unsafe action or action outside of thei
r scope of practice, the crew shall not perform such action.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 319 4.5.1 If the individual continues to insist that staff take unsafe act ions, notify Oscar Command at NCC, and escalate to Senior Management. 4.5.2 While waiting for a Delta to arrive, every effort should be made to avoid confrontation with members of other agencies. 5. All Ambulance Service staff shall maintain a professional manner a t all times when dealing with bystanders and family members. If there is any resistance or conflict, immediately ask for assistance and stop all unnecessary conversation.

A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . A SELECTING APPROPRI ATE RECEIVING FACILI TIES FOR SPECIAL CLINICAL CASES Implementation Date 15 October 2019 Purpose To ensure that patients are transported to the appropriate destination facility and unit in terms of presenting clinical condition . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Receiving Facility Abbreviations : 1.1 HGH - ED: Hamad General Hospital: Emergency Department 1.2 HGH - TRU: Hamad General Hospital: Trauma Resuscitation Unit 1.3 SIDRA: Sidra Medicine 1.4 PEC: Paediatric Emergency Centre 1.5 NCCCR: National Centre for Cancer Care and Research 1.6 AWH : Al Wakra Hospital 1.7 CH: Cuban Hospital 1.8 HH: Heart Hospital 1.9 AKH: Al Khor Hospital 1.10 AAH: Aisha Bint Hamad Al - Attiyah Hospital 1.11 HMGH: Hazm Mebaireek General Hospital 1.12 MGH: Mesaieed General Hospital 2. The Ambulance Service aims to bring the ‘the right patient, to the right hospital at the right time.’ 3. Unless there is an agreed patient specific pathway then all patients will be delivere

d to the nearest appropriate Emergency Department. 4. Agreed pathway patients will be taken to the appropriate receiving fa cilities as detailed in the table below. 5. For all trauma bypass cases, contact the Clinical Desk to confirm HGH - TRU acceptance/bypass criteria ( Appendix 4.8.A ) . 6. All paediatric medical cases up to the age of 18 years will be transported to SIDRA. 7. All paediatric trauma cases less than 14 years , including those who meet Trauma Bypass Criteria can be taken to SIDRA. The following exceptions apply: 7.1 If the patient does not meet Trauma Bypass Criteria, they are to be treated in a facility appropriate for that geographical area. 7.2 In cases when an adult family member is also injured in the same incident, both patients are to be transported to HGH - TRU if they meet Trauma Bypass Criteria. In these cases, the objective is not to split the family unit (i.e. all family members should be treated at the same facility). 8. In cases of uncertainty, contact the Clinical Desk .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 324 CLINICAL CONDITION ADULTS PAEDS Near drowning and drowning WITHOUT associated/suspected trauma HGH - ED SIDRA Near drowning and drowning WITH associated/suspected trauma HGH - TRU SIDRA Electrocution WITHOUT associated/suspected trauma HGH - ED SIDRA Electrocution WITH associated/suspected trauma HGH - TRU SIDRA Envenomation HGH - ED SIDRA Hanging HGH - TRU SIDRA Adult patients with burns > 20% body surface area burns HGH - TRU SIDRA Facial and neck burns regardless of %age of body surface area affected HGH - TRU SIDRA Burns associated WITH trauma (e.g. explosion), regardless of %age of body surface area affected HGH - TRU SIDRA Paediatric patients with > 10% body surface area burns - SIDRA Cancer patients already attending NCCR and require urgent care NCCR - ED - 8.1 If patient or family request NCCR when the patient is stable, then the Clinical desk can contact NCCR for admission. Urgent care patients should be taken to the respective ED . 9. If the patient or patient’s family member refuses to go to SIDRA, the following actions are to be completed: 9.1 Politely and respectfully encourage them to use SIDRA as this i

s a specialist pediatric facility. 9.2 If they continue to refuse: 9.2.1 Inform NCC of the refusal 9.2.2 Document within your ePCR 9.2.3 Hand this information over to the receiving facility 10. Inclusion Criteria for Direct Transfer to the HMC H eart Hospital : 10.1 The criteria below MUST be determined directly by a CCP or by Clinical Desk (via ECG telemetry). All CCPs/Clinical Desk are to note that only 12 - lead ECGs meeting Heart Hospital admission criteria are to be transmitted to Heart Hospital. Hea rt Hospital and its staff are not in a position to provide online ECG advice. 10.1.1 Adult patients with ST elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) identified on 12 - lead ECG with supporting history with or without chest pain (refer to CPGs). 10.1.2 Adult patients with S T segment depression in leads V1 – V3 identified on 12 - lead ECG indicative of a posterior STEMI (refer to CPGs). 10.1.3 Left bundle branch block (LBBB) identified on 12 - lead ECG WHERE THE CCP believes the patients SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS are suggestive of a STEMI. Iso lated LBBB without symptoms suggestive of a STEMI should be transported to the closest Emergency Department. 10.1.4 Cardiac arrest WITNESSED by HMCA

S staff with return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) where the pre - arrest 12 - lead ECG was indicative of a STEMI. 10.1.5 W here patients insist on transportation to Heart Hospital, but their 12 - lead ECG does not meet admission criteria, the attending clinician should advise the patient and/or

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 325 patient’s family that this is not feasible and should attempt to persuade them to acc ept transportation to the appropriate facility. If they continue to insist, the NCC should be updated. 10.1.6 Where other healthcare service providers (e.g.: health centers, clinics etc.) insist that a patient is transported to Heart Hospital, but the patient’s E CG does not meet admission criteria, the Clinical Desk is to contact Heart Hospital and confirm acceptance. If the patient has not been accepted, the discretion lies with the attending CCP regarding the final destination for the patient. 10.1.7 All other presenti ng arrhythmia are to be transported to the closest Emergency Department. 11. Where a receiving specialist hospital cannot receive the patient (to include pathway patients) then the patient should be taken to the nearest emergency department .

B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . B PATIENT HANDOVER A ND ENDORSEMENT Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To facilitate accurate transfer of information and safe and effective transition of care within HMCAS and to healthcare facilities . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL STAF F (INCLUDING TRS DOC TORS) 1. Handover occurs every time the care for a patient is transferred from one healthcare provider to another, either within the Ambulance Service or to another healthcare facility (HCF). Every patient must be formally handed over and received through verbal and/or written communication. 2. As the treating clinician, decide which HCF (a nd specifically which section at the HCF) is most appropriate for the patient and communicate this information to the HMCAS staff member driving the ambulance. If in doubt, seek advice from the Dispatcher/ Clinical Desk . 3. As the treating clinician/clinical team, conduct the handover (in the case of the treating clinician being mentored then the senior HMCAS clinician will monito

r the handover and correct any omissions). 4. The receiving health care facilities include, but are not limited to: 4.1 Hamad General Hospital (HGH) 4.2 Heart Hospital 4.3 Paediatric Emergency Centres (PEC) [Al Sadd & Al Rayyan] 4.4 Al Wakra Hospital 4.5 Al Khor Hospital 4.6 Women’s’ Hospital 4.7 Al Amal Hospital (National Centre for Cancer Care and Research) 4.8 Cuban Hospital 5. Use the following acron ym to guide verbal handover ( See Figure 4.8 . B - 1 ): I Identify the patients age, gender, and time of onset of complaint . M Describe the Mechanism of Injury or current Medical Complaint. I List the Injuries sustained (trauma patients) or information ab. S List the Signs and Symptoms. T List the Treatment given and response thereto. A List any Allergies the patient has. M List any Medications the patient is taking. B Summarize the patient’s Background , i.e. Past Medical History. O Describe any Other issues , e.g. social situation, organ donation. 6. On completion of handover, ensure that the receiving healthcare provider signs the Patient Care Report. 7. Pre - arrival notification for seriously ill or injured patients :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 327 7.1 While enroute to hospital, as the treating clinician, update the Clinical Desk with an IMIST - AMBO report, via the CLINICAL - 1 radio channel, in order to facilitate early pre - notification of the hospital. This is not necessary for planned high acuity patient transfers (TRS) . 7.2 Update the dispatcher to notify Romeo 1 (in the case of patients being transported to HGH) or the Clinical Desk (for all other healthcare facilities) for the following situat ions: 7.2.1 If a representative from the HCF cannot be reached . 7.2.2 If assistance (e.g. police, security, or extra personnel to help carry the patient or equipment) is required at the receiving HCF . 7.2.3 If the patient carries a highly infectious disease . 8. Handover to an alternate HMCAS clinician 8.1 This step is only applicable when the treating HMCAS clinician is handing over a patient to another HMCAS clinician . 8.2 Responsibilities of the clinician that is receiving the patient: 8.2.1 Identify self, greet, and acknowledge the primary HMCAS treating clinicians as well as the family members/friends/colleagues/bystanders. 8.2.2 Indicate willingness to r

eceive the patient handover and accept responsibility thereof. 8.2.3 Facilitate the physical transfer of the patient and direct colleagues (for example, when handing a patient to LF, the instructions given by the LF personnel should be adhered to). 8.2.4 Continue to be transport the patient to the nearest most appropriate HCF. 8.3 Responsibilities of the clinician that is handing over : 8.3.1 Clearly identify self as the treating clinician . 8.3.2 Introduce the patient’s family/friends/colleagues to the receiving HMCAS Clinician . 8.3.3 Commence with the verbal handover of the patient whilst preparing for the physical movement of the patient if necessary ( for example, to the LifeFlight (LF) helicopter stretcher). 8.3.4 Use the acronym “IMIST - AMBO” to guide the patient handover. 9. Ambulance Paramedic requesting assistance or advice from a Critical Care Paramedic : 9.1 Notify the dispatcher of your intention to switch to CLINICAL - 2 radio channel, in order to speak to the CCP on the relevant Charlie unit. 9.2 Provide a brief IMIST - AMBO report to the CCP, clearly indicating your patient’s condition . 9.3 The CCP shall give clinical advice to the AP as necessary. 9.4 The CCP may

decide to continue responding to the scene/rendezvous point or may instruct the Alpha unit to proceed directly to hospital, if appropriate. 9.5 Both the Alpha unit and the Charlie unit shall return to their regular dispatch channel after the consultation has been concl uded. 10. Upon arrival at the HCF, the HMCAS treating clinician shall do the following: 10.1 Avoid using abbreviated words during the handover. 10.2 Identify and verbally confirm which receiving HCF doctor or nurse will accept responsibility for the continued care of the patient. 10.3 Clearly identify self as the treating clinician.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 328 10.4 Introduce the patient’s family/friends/colleagues and commence with the verbal handover. 10.5 Use the acronym IMIST - AMBO to guide the patient handover . 10.6 If appropriate highlight any key social issues and/or indicate if the patient and/or patients relative has enquired about organ donation . 10.7 Assist the HCF doctor/nurse receiving the patient within clinical scope of practice. 10.8 Ensure efficient transfer of the patient to alt ernate monitoring and treatment devices, minimizing interruption of the clinical treatment being administered to the patient. 10.9 The HMCAS treating clinician should briefly re - evaluate the patient’s clinical presentation during the handover. If the patient’s clinical presentation or parameters differ significantly from the initial handover then this could indicate a pre - morbid state and should be highlighted to the receiving doctor/nurse and documented accordingly in the “Treatment” section of the e PCR. 10.10 Once t he receiving HCF doctor or nurse has indicated that they are satisfied with the handover, ensure that they sign the e PCR to acknowledge receipt and responsibi

lity for the patient. If the HCF doctor or nurse’s name and HMC Staff number is illegible then req uest clarification. 10.11 Remove HMCAS equipment that is not needed and notify the NCC of unit availability accordingly.

C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . C COMMUNICATIONS FOR VERBAL ORDERS GIVEN VIA RADIO/TELEPHONE Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure correct, consistent, and smooth communication during verbal orders via radio or telephone. Responsibility OPERATIONAL CLINICAL PERSONNEL (E.G. ALP HA, CHARLIE, DELTA, BIKE AND EVENT STAFF) 1. The requirement for verbal orders, within the ambulance service, is limited by the existence of the Clinical Practice Guidelines. Where a verbal order is required then a written record, within the ePCR, is required so s to provide a formal record. 2. Sender of the verbal order: 2.1 Speak slowly and clearly to send an order to the receiver of the verbal order. 2.2 Request ‘read - back of verbal order’ to confirm receipt of exact order by the receiver and thereby preventing communication errors. 3. Receiver: 3.1 Write down sender’s orders 3.2 Repeat order back to sender to confirm that order was clearly received. 3.3 Document clearly on the ePCR 3.3.1 The verbal order drug name, dose

r oute of administration . 3.3.2 The name of person providing the order 4. Keep all radio communications clear, short, and concise. 5. At handover to receiving hospital staff, provide documentation confirming all treatment (including verbal orders) rendered to the patien t. 6. Hand over the patient in accordance with SOP 4.8.B .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 331 SOP 4.8 . D Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . D NON - TRANSPORT AND RE LEASE OF PATIENTS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure that appropriate steps are taken when treatment and/or transportation of a patient is refused . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. A key principle of the Ambulance Service is to provide treatment and transportation to all persons who request it, therefore: 1.1 Avoid suggesting or implying that transport to a HCF might not be necessary for a patient. Other role players (e.g. police) may also fee l that further medical care is not necessary. 1.2 If it is suspected that a person is abusing the Ambulance Service, discuss with a supervisor after the treatment and transportation is completed. 1.3 If a patient is violent or otherwise a danger to the HMCAS perso nnel, then seek the assistance of the police and escalate the matter to a supervisor. 1.4 Treating and transporting patients in order of priority following triage is NOT considered refusal of treatment or transport. 2. Refusa

l of treatment and/or transport by a p atient : 2.1 Soon after arriving at the patient’s side and while simultaneously performing the functions stipulated in the HMCAS clinical practice guidelines and directives, communicate to the patient (and other role players) that transport will be offered in a n ambulance or helicopter (if appropriate) to the most appropriate Health Care Facility (HCF). 2.2 As the HMCAS treating clinician, when possible: 2.2.1 Encourage the patient to have a clinical assessment. 2.2.2 Attempt to acquire a history and a baseline set of vital sig ns in line with CPG 1. 2.2.3 Attempt to get all the role players (especially the patient) to agree that further clinical follow - up at the HCF is the best course of action and to attempt to allay any fears or concerns that the patient (and others) may have. 2.2.4 Make at least three attempts to convince the various role players that the patient should ideally be transported to an HCF. Each attempt should use a different approach (not merely repeating oneself). 2.2.5 Gain the trust of the various role players by being gentle and empathetic (avoid threatening approaches or body language). 2.2.6 Empower the patient by as

king why they are relucta nt to go to a HCF and attempt to dispel any concerns. 2.2.7 Paraphrase questions and words to ensure that the patient understands what is being said. 2.2.8 Remind the role players that the HMCAS staff members are merely concerned about the patient’s well - being and want what is best for the patient. 2.2.9 Remind the role players that the HMCAS is free of charge. 2.2.10 Use an interpreter for patient communication if necessary.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 332 2.2.11 Ensure that only one person at a time is talking to the patient or primary role player, t o avoid anxiety and discomfort. 2.2.12 Crouch or kneel next to the patient to be at their eye - level (as opposed to looking down overbearingly at them). 2.2.13 Explain to the role players that the HMCAS staff members are also concerned about the role player ’ s safety and liability if the patient deteriorates. 2.2.14 Encourage the patient and/or role players to call for an ambulance again at a later stage if the need arises if it is ultimately decided that the patient will not be transported to an HCF. 2.2.15 Escalate the matter to a Sen ior HMCAS staff member if the patient’s clinical presentation suggests that there is an imminent clinical risk. 2.3 If the patient refuses transportation but accepts treatment, then provide the patient with that treatment, if it is clinically safe to do so, an d leave the patient on scene. 2.4 If the patient refuses treatment but accepts transportation, then transport the patient to the appropriate facility. Clearly communicate to the receiving health facility the patient’s refusal of tre

atment. 2.5 Request the patient to read the “Patient Refusal of Ambulance Service” section on the electronic Patient Care Record ( e PCR) if he/she can be reasonably determined to be competent to make an informed decision to refuse further care should be . Alternatively, request a parent or primary caregiver of the patient to complete and sign the “Patient Refusal of Ambulance Service” section. 2.6 If a translator assisted with communicating details to the patient then that person’s details (name, surname, identification/passport number) should be included in the “Assessment” or “Treatment” section of the e PCR. Ensure that this translator/witness also signs the e PCR next to their details. 2.7 A witness (preferably a family member) should then complete and sign at the bottom of this section. 2.8 Assist in answering any questions the role players may have and all correspondence with role players (including any signatures that are written on documents) should preferably be witnessed by a fellow HMCAS staff member. 2.9 There are some rare occasions that all of th e role players will refuse to cooperate or agree to sign the e PCR. In these instances, notify the Clinical Desk . 2.9.1 E

scalate the situation to the consultant CCP effectiveness and Oscar access to assist . 2.9.2 If consultant CCP effectiveness or Oscar acces are not a vailable to assist, then inform Oscar command accordingly and complete the ePCR; indicating that the role players refused to cooperate with the procedure . 2.9.3 In the notes section of the ePCR, detail the circumstances and also the attempts that were made to tr y and convince the role players that the patient should go to hospital . 2.9.4 At least two HMCAS staff members involved in the scene should ensure that the details in the ePCR are accurate . 2.9.5 All HMCAS members involved should ensure that they are added as practiti oner in the ePCR . 3. Refusal of treatment and/or transportation by a patient not competent to make decisions : 3.1 Discuss with the patient’s representative the need for forced treatment and/or transportation.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 333 3.2 If forced treatment and/or transportation is considere d, then the assistance of the police should be sought, and the matter escalated urgently. 3.2.1 If the patient is assessed and found to be in a safe, non - urgent, stable clinical condition then leave patient in the care of their representative and request them to sign the e PCR refusal section on the patient’s behalf. 4. Refusal of treatment and/or transp ortation by the police on behalf of the patient : 4.1 If the patient is assessed and found to be in a safe, non - urgent, stable clinical condition then leave the patient in the care of the police and request them to sign the e PCR refusal section on the patient’s behalf. 4.2 If it is believed that a real and immediate threat to the patient’s life or health exists, then staff should escalate the case urgently. 5. Refusal of treatment and/or transportation by a parent, spouse, or other person on behalf of the patient : 5.1 If t he patient is assessed and found to be in a safe, non - urgent, stable clinical condition then leave the patient in the care of the responsible person and request them to sign the e PC

R refusal section on the patient’s behalf. 5.2 If it is believed that a real an d immediate threat to the patient’s safety, life or health exists then staff should seek the assistance of the police and escalate the case urgently. 6. Non - treatment where there are no patients; e.g. response to a minor road traffic accident : 6.1 Search the scen e to identify all persons involved in the incident. 6.2 Ask each person if they require any clinical assessment, assistance, or transportation to a health care facility . 6.3 When no patients are identified, report this to the Communications Centre (for recording i n the Call Record) and document it in the e PCR. 6.4 There is no need to have any person sign refusal of treatment and/or transport as there are no patients.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 334 SOP 4.8 . E Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . E SAFEGUARDING PATIE NT’S VALUABLES Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure responsible management of possessions and valuables of patients in the care of HMCAS. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONAL STAFF 1. To reduce the instances requiring the need to safeguard patient’s belongings, encourage patients to look after their own valuables, unless their condition is such that they are unable to take adequate care of their belongings. [e.g. unconscious, spinal motion restricted etc.] 2. Procedure: 2.1 Encourage the patient to leave non - essentials items at home or in the care of companion or family members. 2.2 Return all identification documents [QID, Health Card, Passport etc.] receiv ed from patients or their representative to the person who provided it after the necessary data has been captured. 2.3 Do not routinely hand over identification documents to the receiving facility staff. 2.4 In situations where the patient’s condition is such that they cannot safeguard the

ir property, it is the responsibility of HMCAS staff to ensure the safety of those valuables. 2.5 When possible, ensure valuables are handed to family and the e PCR signed as confirmation. 2.6 Where patient’s property [e.g. shoes or jewelr y ] has been removed for assessment, retain these items for safekeeping and endorse them to the receiving facility staff. 2.7 List all valuables taken over on the electronic Patient Care Report [ e PCR]. 2.8 Ensure that both ambulance crew members sign the e PCR. 2.9 Place valuables into a plastic bag e.g. jewelry, seal and ensure that the valuables are hand - delivered to the receiving hospital staff . 2.10 In the event that no bag is available, ensure that the valuables are hand - delivered to the receiving hospital staff. 2.11 On arrival at the hospital, all valuables are to be handed over to the receiving hospital staff. 2.12 Request the receiving responsible person to sign on the e PCR an acknowledgement of receipt. 2.13 Document the endorsement in the free text area of the ‘Assessment’/‘Tr eatment’ blocks or in the ‘Notes’ section on the ePCR .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 335 SOP 4.8 . F Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . F MANAGEMENT OF PATI ENTS IN POLICE CUSTODY Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To ensure appropriate management of patients who are in police custody • To ensure appropriate communications with police when dealing with patients in police custody . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONAL STAFF 1. If a patient that requires treatment is a suspect who needs to be taken into custody by the police, then a senior HMCAS staff member/DTL MUST be notified: 1.1 Approach the most senior police representative near the patient, with the intention of convincing them to allow the patient to receive treatment and transportation to a Healthcare Facility (HCF). 1.2 Remind the police representative that the police processes can continue enroute and at the HCF (police officials at the hospital could facilitate any additional police processes required). 1.3 Encourage a police representative to accompany the patient/suspect in the back of the ambulance or follow the ambulance (at a safe distance)

to the hospital. 1.4 Inform the DTL and Romeo 1 that a suspect i s enroute to the hospital and to please have security and/or police representatives available to assist should the need arise. 2. On some occasions the police representative might refuse to allow the HMCAS treating clinician access to the patient or do not al low the patient to be transported to hospital using HMCAS resources. 2.1 Avoid treating patients in police vehicles; patients should preferably be transported and managed by HMCAS operational staff in HMCAS designated vehicles. 2.2 Notify a senior HMCAS staff memb er and/or Clinical Desk /DTL accordingly. 2.3 Explain to the police representative that the police will be assuming responsibility for care of the patient once the HMCAS resources leave the scene. 2.4 Request the police representative assuming responsibility for th e care of the patient/suspect to read the “Patient Refusal of Ambulance Service” section on the back of the e PCR. 2.4.1 Cross out the word “Patient” in the section that reads “Name and Signature of Patient” and write “Police official” instead. 2.4.2 Request the police official assuming responsibility for the patient/suspect to write his nam

e and corporation number in the space provided and sign accordingly. 2.4.3 Request a second police official to sign as witness in the “Witness” section (and include his corporation number next to his signature). 3. The police representatives may not be aware of this procedure and may be reluctant to cooperate with these steps. 3.1 Approach police officials respectfully and explain that the approach is a “Standard Operating Procedure” laid out by t he Hamad Medical Corporation’s Ambulance Service. 3.2 Notify DTL and/or a senior HMCAS staff member. 3.3 Document details of the correspondence in the “Assessment”/“Treatment” section of the e PCR. 3.4 If possible, include in the e PCR the names of the police representatives and the police vehicle numbers (if applicable).
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 336 3.5 To avoid conflict details, record all details in a non - threatening manner .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 337 SOP 4.8 . G Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Oper ations Procedure 4.8 . G PATIENT ESCORTS/AC COMPANIMENT Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure that safe procedures are adhered to when family/associates of the patient ride - along in the ambulance, enroute to hospital . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. A maximum of 4 (four) persons are allowed in the back of an ambulance i.e. 1 (one) patient on the stretcher and 3 (three) other sitting persons . 2. All persons in the back of the ambulance MUST be wearing a seatbelt during transport . 3. Family member/s of the patient being transported may be allowed to accompany the patient unless: 3.1 There is insufficient space in the back of the ambulance to accommodate t he extra person/s. 3.2 The care of the patient will be compromised because of limited space. 3.3 The family member/s are hostile and is perceived to be an interference to patient care. 4. Do not allow accompanying persons in the ambulance to sit in the front compartm ent of the ambulance as this may interfere with the d

riving of the ambulance, especially in the case of intimidating persons. 5. Allow the mother of paediatric patients accompany the patient. 6. If a family member/patient associate is following behind the ambula nce in a private vehicle, warn them to keep a safe distance behind the ambulance. 7. If the vehicle following the ambulance begins to hoot and flash headlights in an attempt to force the ambulance to drive faster, continue to follow normal driving procedures safely and report this immediately to the NCC.

H Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector HMC Ambulance Service s Clinical Desk Procedure 4.8 . H RENDEZVOUS WITH CR ITICAL CARE PARAMEDI C Implementation Date 01 June 2022 Purpose To ensure appropriate and efficient re sources to provide effective patient care. Responsibility ALL STAFF ASSIGNED TO CLINICA L DESK 1. Background: 1.1 To provide the best and most appropriate level of care to all patients and prevent unnecessary transport delays, all calls dispatched as ZULU code, according to the dispatch matrix, will receive a Critical Care Paramedic response. 1.2 The on - scene unit should provide treatment to all patients according to the Clinical Pr actice Guidelines and, where appropriate, transport the patient as soon as possible to rendezvous with the responding CCP (Charlie Unit or Life Flight). 2. Principles of Rendezvous Point Selection: 2.1 In cases where the CCP Unit will not be able to rendezvous wi th the transporting unit before they reach the receiving hospital the responding CCP should be stood down . 2.2 Choose an appropriate rendezvous point so that th

e transporting Unit does not stop and wait for the responding CCP. The CCP should arrive at the rend ezvous point first and wait for the transporting Unit . 2.3 Where the transporting unit will rendezvous with a Charlie or Retrieval CCP unit : 2.3.1 The agreed rendezvous point should always be in the direction of the hospital from the scene . 2.3.2 The transporting unit sho uld NOT travel in a direction away from the receiving facility to meet with the CCP unit, this practice is inefficient and delays definitive care where the transporting unit could reach the receiving facility sooner. 2.3.3 Rendezvous units must not stop in a rou nd - about as it is unsafe and leads to traffic congestion. Rather stop on the side of the round - about exit road in the direction of the hospital (see Figure 4.8.H - 1 below). 2.3.4 Due to limited opportunities for vehicles to change directions between rendezvous po ints, the responding CCP unit should not pass a rendezvous point unless it is certain that they will reach the next rendezvous point before the transporting unit does . 2.4 Where the transporting unit will rendezvous with LifeFlight : 2.4.1 The agreed rendezvous point should be the nearest

most appropriate HOS or other safe alternative landing zone as determined by the LifeFlight Coordinator in collaboration with the Pilot in Command if necessary. 2.4.2 Wherever reasonably possible the selected HOS or landing zone should be in the direction of the receiving hospital; however, it is acknowledged that this is not always possible, and where necessary, the transporting unit may travel in a direction other than toward the receiving facility. 2.4.2.1 The decision to travel in a direction other than toward a receiving facility to rendezvous with LifeFlight should be discussed with a Clinical Coordinator and/or the Consultant CCP: Effectiveness .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 339 2.4.3 The LifeFlight Coordinator should advise the transporting unit on Airmed 1 of the selected rendezvous HOS or landing zone as soon as reasonably possible as well as the ETA of LifeFlight. 2.4.4 If rendezvous with LifeFlight is not possible : 2.4.4.1 The LifeFlight Coordinator must inform the DTL and Clinical Coordinator as soon as possible . 2.4.4.2 A Charl ie or Retrieval CCP should be dispatched to rendezvous with the transporting unit if likely to reach the unit before they reach the receiving hospital. 2.4.4.3 The transporting unit should proceed to the receiving facility and coordinate a rendezvous with a Charli e or Retrieval CCP . 3. Rendezvous Procedure – Charlie or Retrieval CCP: 3.1 The on - scene crew should request CCP assistance from their dispatcher as soon as the need for a CCP is identified. 3.2 The transporting unit and responding CCP unit should communicate directl y over Clinical 2 to identify an appropriate rendezvous point. 3.3 The transporting unit should maintain radio contact with the responding CCP unit over Clinical 2 until both unit’s rendezvous or the rendezvous is cancelled . 3.4 Mini

mize time delays at the rendezv ous point and resume transportation at the earliest possible opportunity to ensure reduced scene to hospital transport times. 3.5 Responding units should follow SOP 4.6.A for priority 1 driving to hospital. 3.6 The transporting unit should follow SOP 4.8.I if stopping for clinical procedures is necessary. 3.7 The transporting unit and CCP unit should update their MDT (per SOP 3.15 ) and dispatcher when at t he rendezvous point. 4. Rendezvous Procedure – LifeFlight: 4.1 The on - scene crew should request CCP assistance from their dispatcher as soon as the need for a CCP is identified . 4.2 On dispatch of LifeFlight, the LifeFlight Coordinator should request, through the DTL , for the transporting unit to change to Airmed 1. 4.2.1 If required, the LifeFlight Coordinator should request, through the DTL, for a Delta unit to be dispatched to the case. 4.3 The transporting unit should change over to Airmed 1 upon request of their dispatcher and remain on Airmed 1 until released by the LifeFlight Coordinator. 4.4 The LifeFlight Coordinator must advise the transporting unit of the HOS or landing zone location and the ETA of LifeFlight. 4.4.1 If necessary, th

e LifeFlight Coordinator should guide the transporting and responding units to the HOS or landing zone selected for rendezvous. 4.5 The transporting unit should update their MDT (per SOP 3.15 ) and LifeFlight Coordinator when at the rendezvous point. 4.6 The responding and transporting units should secure the landing zone and prepare the patient in accordance with SOP 4.7.B .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 340 4.7 Minimize time delays at the rendezvous point and resume transportation at the earliest possible opportunity to ensure reduced scene to hospital transport times . 4.8 The LifeFlight Coordinator should request the transporting unit to return to their dispatch channel as soon as LifeFlight and taken - off from the HOS or landing zone .

I Hamad Me dical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4. 8 . I STOPPING THE AMBUL ANCE FOR CLINICAL PR OCEDURES Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure that safe procedures are followed when an ambulance that is enroute to hospital is required to temporarily stop to perform a clinical procedure . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. It may be necessary to temporarily stop the ambulance to perform certain interventions in time - critical patients while enroute to hospital. This is referred to as a “ Procedure Stop ”. 2. General procedure : 2.1 Communicate to the driver, the need for a procedure stop. 2.2 Identify a safe position to stop the ambulance. 2.3 It may be necessary to obstruct traffic in order to complete a vital clinical procedure. 2.4 Stop the ambulance in a safe position, with emergency lights switched on. 2.5 Update the NCC Dispatcher about the need to stop the ambulance e.g. “ Alpha 5.2, Procedure stop ”. 2.6 Inform the driver when the procedure is completed. 2.7 Resume transport as quickly as possible,

with a maximum of 3 minutes stoppage time. 2.8 Update the NCC Dispatcher as soon as the ambulance is mobile again e.g. “Alpha 5.2, Continuing to hospital ”. 3. Examples of Proced ure Stops : 3.1 Ac quisition of a 12 - lead ECG tracing (after setting up equipment whilst mobile). 3.2 Vascular access (after setting up equipment whilst mobile). 3.3 Rapid Sequence Intubation (after setting up drugs and equipment whilst mobile). 3.4 Other critical interven tions that may be required based on the reasonable judgement of the attending clinician.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 342 SOP 4.8 . J Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . J EMERGENCY BLOOD TRANSFER PROTOCOL Implementation Date 16 January 2018 Purpose • To guide efficient communication between HMCAS Communications Centre and HGH Blood Bank . • To expedite delivery of urgent blood units/products to a receiving hospital . Responsibility EMDS, DTLS AND ALPHA AND DELTA UNIT STAFF 1. Hamad General Hospital Blood Bank will activate the Emergency Blood Transfer Protocol in the following manner: 1.1 Call the NCC and ask for the DTL. 1.2 Provide the DTL with the following information: 1.2.1 ‘Emergency blood transfer protocol has been activated and it is not a test’ 1.2.2 Receiving hospital name 1.2.3 Blood Bank contact name and number 2. The DTL shall ensure that the following occurs: 2.1 Open a new Call for Service 2.2 Enter the ProQA code – 33 - C - 7 2.3 Dispatch an appropriate Delta un it to the Blood Bank 2.3.1 Dispatch an Alpha unit for the first transportation if no Delta unit is available in reasonable proximity. 2.4 Provide the Delta unit with contact

person’s details at the Blood Bank and receiving hospital. 2.5 Update the Blood Bank and receiving hospital regarding estimated time of arrival of the HMCAS unit. 3. The dispatched unit should perform the following: 3.1 Respond Priority One to the Blood Bank. 3.2 Collect and sign for pa ckaged blood products. 3.3 Transport the package Priority One to the receiving hospital. 3.4 Deliver and sign over the packaged blood products. 3.5 Complete ePCR and attach any documentation. 4. This process may be repeated a number of times for the same patient. 4.1 Subsequ ent transport of blood may be performed by an Alpha unit. 5. If blood spillage occurs: 5.1 The unit should: 5.1.1 Manage spill in the same manner as blood from a haemorrhaging patient. 5.1.2 Report blood spill immediately to DTL. 5.1.3 Raise an OVA for the incident. 5.2 DTL should immediately ensure the following:
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 343 5.2.1 Inform the Blood Bank and receiving hospital. 5.2.2 Dispatch another Alpha or Delta unit to collect a new blood package.

K Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . K PREVENTION AND MAN AGEMENT OF PATIENT F ALLS Implementation Date 1 6 November 202 1 Purpose To reduce the risk of patient falls during the pre - hospital care phase . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL CLINICAL PERSONNEL 1. Identifying patients at risk of falls: 1.1 All patients treated/attended to by the Ambulance Service are at risk of falling and therefore be treated as such . 2. Patient Handling and Transfer Procedures for Fall Prevention : 2.1 Do not allow any patient to walk unnecessarily, patients clinically suitable for transport on stretcher should remain strapped and transferred into receiving facility on stretcher. 2.2 At least one clinician should walk with/next to any self - mobili zing patient. 2.3 The movement of a patient from scene (field of incident, hospital bed, and home care bed) to the ambulance should be done using an approved carrying device such as a stretcher, carry sheet, scoop stretcher, long spinal board (as an extricatio n device) or a stair chair. 2.4 Se

cure all patients to the ambulance stretcher using shoulder straps/chest straps, and leg straps. 2.4.1 For any patient refusing any safety restraint during transport (for example, chest straps), explain all risks, document the expla nation and note the patient’s refusal. 2.5 Request assistance with carrying patients via additional Ambulance Service personnel such as Delta, Bariatric unit or a second ambulance dispatched to scene. Civil Defense can be contacted, if required, via the Nation al Command Centre. 2.6 Transfer the patient from the transporting vehicle to the healthcare facility or patient residence, using HMCAS approved safe lifting and moving techniques and following all above listed points. 2.7 Move patients from ambulance stretcher or stair chair to hospital bed with at least three staff to ensure safe transition. 2.8 Clearly communicate the lifting/moving plan to all persons involved prior to moving the patient in any direction or disposition and communicate all steps to the patient, ensur ing their understanding and acknowledgment of the explanation. 2.9 Never use the side door to move patient from stretcher to wheelchair due to high trip hazards. 3. General Processe

s for management of a patient that has fallen : 3.1 Assist patient immediately. 3.2 Request appropriate assistance if needed. 3.3 Notify NCC and escalate to immediate supervisor. On - call manager shall be notified by the NCC Officer on duty. 3.4 Treat and transport patient to the appropriate hospital and notify hospital staff of fall episode. 3.5 Submit an online OVA report as soon as possible. 3.6 Report any defective equipment as necessary .

L Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . L UTILIZATION OF CLINI CAL - 2 RADIO CHANNEL Implementation Date 05 March 2017 Purpose To facilitate direct radio communication between the Ambulance Paramedics and Critical Care Paramedics . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. All Ambulance Paramedics (AP) and Critical Care Paramedics (CCP) are requested to use the CLINICAL - 2 radio channel for communicating directly with each other when required, whilst adhering to correct radio communication etiquette. 2. The National Command Centre ( NCC) shall continue to manage the dispatch process as normal. 3. Once an ALPHA or DELTA unit arrives on scene and the crew decides to either downgrade/cancel or request clinical advice from a CHARLIE unit, the following procedure shall be followed: 3.1 The on - sce ne treating clinician (AP) shall switch and request the responding CCP to switch to the CLINICAL - 2 radio channel in order to give a quick IMIST AMBO clinical report ( SOP 4.8.B ) . 3.2 The dispatcher will confirm and ackn ow

ledge receiving the request to switch channels. 3.3 If the dispatcher has already downgraded or cancelled the CHARLIE unit, the CCP may request the on - scene ALPHA crew to switch to the CLINICAL - 2 radio channel in order to receive a quick IMIST AMBO clinical report. 3.4 The NCC Clinical Desk may be requested to join the radio conversation on the CLINICAL - 2 channel for transmitted ECG interpretation. 4. After receiving the IMIST AMBO report and clarifying the condition of the patient, the CCP will: 4.1 Decide to continue to the scene/rendezvous point to assist the ALPHA crew or 4.2 Give clinical advice to the ALPHA crew and elect to make the CHARLIE unit available for the next Call For Service (CFS). 5. To ensure correct, consistent, and smooth communications over the CLINICAL - 2 radio channel, please adhere to SOP 4.8.C . 6. Utilization of Clinical 2 - Flow Pathway :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 346 Clinical 2 - Flow Pathway : Consultation Process between CCP and AP On Patient Management/Care NCC To manage the dispatch according to ProQA CHUTE & REPSONSE times as normal Once an Alpha/Delta unit arrives on scene and decides to downgrade a Charlie or cancel a Charlie based on pt presentation The following will occur AP/Delta or CCP can request for the said unit to change to CLINICAL 2 Dispatcher to confirm and acknowledge request VERBAL COMMUNICATION SOP 4.8 B + IMIST AMBO SOP 4.8 C To be shared between AP and CCP Decision Do I need to continue to call? YES Switch over to Dispatched Channel NO Switch over to dispatched Channel Continue to do so Report continuation to scene to dispatcher SOP 3.3 Make themselves available Update MDT SOP 3.8 Report to dispatcher SOP 3.3 CTL can be requested to join the conversation for ECG interpretation until tough pads are able to receive ECGs Any patient information required requested must be provided timeously IF NO CCP available DEFAULT to CLINICAL 1 NO CCP AVAILABLE CCP Dispatched Update CTL if NOT involved in consultation process
