M Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . M COHORTING O F PATIENTS AWAITING BEDS AT HAMAD GENERA L HOSPITAL Implementation Date 10 October 2018 Purpose Outline the process and procedure for cohorting patients at Hamad General Hospital when there are NO beds available to off load patients . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Introduction : 1.1 The function of this Standing Operational Procedure is to outline the process for cohorting patients within the four bedded holding bays at Hamad General Hospital when there are no immediate beds available to off load an ambulance trolley . 1.2 The premise is that 1) care will continue to be provided by HMCAS until a bed is available whilst 2) ensuring the maximum number of ambulances are available for deployment to support community calls. 2. The When : 2.1 Cohorting will be instigated by the HMCAS ROMEO officer (based at HGH) under the following circumstances 2.1.1 Once one or more ambulances are waiting for a bed space within RIAMS and there is no bed available 2.1.2 On

the understanding that the selection criteria (outlined in Section 4) are adhered too and the paramedics selected to cohort have been briefed. 3. The How : 3.1 There must be THREE licensed ambulance paramedics available to monitor cohorted patients w ith one paramedic being present at all times. TWO of the paramedics must be active Emergency Service Ambulance Crews or a Critical Care Paramedic . 3.1.1 The first ambulance crew that arrives will initially act as the supervisory cohorting team until they are rel eased by the ROMEO (overview of key functions are listed in Appendix 4. 8.M ). 3.1.2 The Clinical Documents Officer will act as the third supervisory clinician . 3.1.2.1 The clinical documents Officer will be responsible for ensuring ePCR and/or paper PCR are up - loaded on to CERNER . 3.1.2.1.1 The preferred method of record keeping will be the transfer of one ePCR record to second ePCR held by the CDO . 3.1.2.1.2 A paper IMIST - AMBO form will be completed by the handing over crew as part of the handover to the Cohorting Ambulance Paramedic team. 3.1.2.1.2.1 The IMIST - AMBO handover form must be completed in full . 3.1.2.1.2.2 The IMIST - AMBO form Must include ALL medicati
ons administered pri or to handover . 3.1.2.1.3 Paper PCRs will be used at the discretion of the ROMEO officer .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 348 3.2 Once handover to the Cohorting ambulance team the accompanying ambulance paramedics will: 3.2.1 Add themselves as ‘practitioner’ on the ePCR 3.2.1.1 They will document any changes in patien t condition and/or additional drug administration in the ePCR 3.2.1.2 Should a patient deteriorate then crew should be immediately escalated to bay 1 (or if appropriate Trauma) 3.2.1.2.1 ePCR should be completed 3.2.1.2.2 A paper code summary, from the LP15, should be printed and han dover to the admitting hospital team . 3.3 A maximum of FOUR patients are cohorted at any one time UNLESS instructed, via GOLD COMMAND, that more than four patients can be cohorted . 3.3.1 The maximum number of paramedic to patients is one two - person crew to FOUR pati ents plus a single Clinical Documentations Officer. Therefore, if more than four patients are to be cohorted then a second two - person ambulance crew is to be made available . 3.4 At all times ONE ambulance paramedic must be supervising the cohort area thereby allowing two ambulance paramedics to transport the waiting patient to an in - hospital bed space as identified by Emergency

Department staff . 3.5 That adequate supplies of oxygen and cardiac monitoring are available . 3.6 Patients requiring monitoring are to be attac hed to a new LP15, so that the current LP15 can be released back to the ambulance once all data is transferred to the ePCR, a code summary should be printed . 4. Exclusion Criteria : 4.1 Patients who meet an agreed pathway are NOT to be cohorted 4.1.1 Stroke 4.1.2 STEMI 4.1.3 Trauma 4.1.4 QEWS > 5 4.2 Patients who require admission to Bay 1 are NOT to be cohorted . 4.3 Pregnant patients are NOT to be cohorted 4.4 Patients with mental health issues are NOT to be cohorted . 4.5 Children are NOT to be cohorted 4.6 Patients who have experienced a SEIZURE are NOT to be cohorted . 4.7 Patients requiring more than 4lpm of nasal oxygen are NOT to be cohorted . 4.7.1 Sp02 for those patients receiving oxygen must be greater than 95% for non - CO PD patients and greater than 88% for patients with confirmed COPD (this does not include asthma which is not a COPD diagnosis) . 5. Key Roles a nd Functions o f Staff Cohorting : 5.1 Romeo 5.1.1 Ensure cohorting AP’s are briefed

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 349 5.1.2 Ensure observation report is recorded every 30 minutes 5.1.3 Maximum of four patients to be cohorted unless instructed otherwise by GOLD command . 5.1.4 Ensure ePCR has all patients’ details transferred 5.1.5 Ensure that IMIST - AMBO form completed 5.2 Clinical Documentation Officer 5.2.1 If not wearing HMCAS identifiable uniform put on Hi - Vis tabard ‘saying paramedic’ . 5.2.2 Take handover from ambulance crew . 5.2.2.1 Ensure ePCR is transferred to your tuff - pad/note book . 5.2.2.2 Ensure IMIST AMBO form is completed : 5.2.2.2.1 All medication 5.2.2.2.2 Complete set of vital signs 5.2.2.2.3 Crews call sign , date, and time 5.2.2.3 If monitoring is required attach a new LP15 allowing crew to be released . 5.2.3 Ensure you remain in the area, observing patients, at all times once cohorting is commenced UNLESS you are transporting patients to an Emergency Department bed . 5.2.4 Ensure you remain in the area, observing patients, at all times once cohorting is commenced . 5.2.5 Ensure ePCR has all patients’ details transferred . 5.2.6 Ensure that IMIST - AMBO form completed 5.2.7 If patients deteriorate act in a

ccordance with CPG’s and ensure events documented on ePCR . 5.3 Cohorti ng Ambulance Team 5.3.1 If not wearing HMCAS identifiable uniform put on Hi - Vis tabard ‘saying paramedic’ . 5.3.2 Take handover from ambulance crew . 5.3.2.1 Ensure ePCR is transferred to your tuff - pad/note book . 5.3.2.2 Ensure IMIST AMBO form is completed : 5.3.2.2.1 All medication 5.3.2.2.2 Complete set of vital signs 5.3.2.2.3 Crews call sign , date, and time 5.3.2.3 If monitoring is required attach a new LP15 allowing crew to be released . 5.3.3 Ensure you remain in the area, observing patients, at all times once cohorting is commenced UNLESS you are transporting patients to an Emergency Department bed . 5.3.4 Ensure ePCR has all patients’ details transferred . 5.3.5 Ensure that IMIST - AMBO form completed 5.3.6 If patients deteriorate act in accordance with CPG’s and ensure events documented on ePCR .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 350 SOP 4.8 . N Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8 . N BROUGHT IN DEAD (BID ) PROCESS PATHWAY Implementation Date 01 October 2019 Purpose To provide guidance on all deaths transported to hospital WHERE active resuscitation in NOT ongoing . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Definition : 1.1 All deceased patients where resuscitation efforts have been ceased (following consultation with a CCP) who are being transported to HMC facilities . 1.2 All deceased patients where HMCAS is assisting the police to remove a body from a public place to a HMC mortuary . 2. Process : 2.1 The deceased patient is to be transported in an Ambulance with the Air Conditioning turn on. Driving should comply normal Qatar road laws . 2.2 The deceased patient is to be treated with respect and dignity maintained by ensuring the body is covered at all times . 2.3 Where resuscitation was attempted it should be fully documented within the ePCR and subsequently the following should be recorded . 2.3.1 The resuscitation procedure 2.3.2 Time resuscitation c

eased 2.3.3 Remove all IV cannulae, LTA, and ETT 2.3.4 Cover all wounds with gauze 2.4 Where resuscitation was NOT attempted the following should be recorded : 2.4.1 History of all the events leading to the dis covery of the patient . 2.4.2 Physical examination of the patient to include signs of injury . 2.4.3 Any medications found 2.4.4 Measures taken in the management and care of the patient . 2.4.5 Any forced entry by the care giver to gain access to the patient and items moved to acces s the patient . 2.4.6 Name of Police Officer present 2.5 The deceased patient should be transported to the closest HMC mortuary (Hamad General Hospital, Al Wakra or Al Khor) adhering to the proce ss outlined in the ‘Brought in Dead (BID) Admission P athway’ ( Appendix 4.8 . N ) . 2.6 The ePCR should be completed and then signed by the mortuary clerk and/or technician once signed the ePCR should be printed and a copy left with the mortuary clerk .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 351 SOP 4.8.O Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8.O TRANSPORTING IMMUNOC OMPROMISED/CHEMOTHER APY PATIENTS Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide direction on the handling and transportation of i mmunocompromised/ chemotherapy patients. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Procedure : 1.1 Identify patient group : 1.1.1 Patients receiving chemotherapy 1.1.2 Patients who EITHER ‘ self - identify’ or are medically diagnosed as being immunocompromised . 1.2 Perform strict hand hygiene before and after handling patients . 1.3 Ambulance crew to follow hand hygiene ( SOP 2.12 Hand Hygiene ), apply gloves and wear a surgical face mask. 1.4 A surgica l mask should be worn by the patient during transportation unless the patient refuses. Any refusal should be documented on the ePCR. 1.5 Complete patient handover to receiving facility. 1.6 If the patient has an associated infectious disease, follow SOP 4.8.P Transporting Patients with Suspected or known of Infectious Disease. 1.7 Clean and disinfect equipment after trans

porting the patient and perform social cleaning on ambulance surfaces. 2. References: 2.1 Guidelines for Care of the Immunocompromised Patient - infection control.ucsfmedicalcenter.org/.../Sec%204.1%20Immunocompromised%20Pt. Jan 2015 2.2 HMC Policy CL 7233 Categories of Isolation

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 352 SOP 4.8.P Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8.P TRANSPORTING PATIENT S WITH SUSPECTED OR KNOWN INFECTIOUS DISEASE ( E MERGING AND R E - E MERGING D ISEASE S) Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide direction on the handling and transporting patients with suspected or known Infectious Disease to ensure the safety of healthcare workers and to prevent the potential risk of infection to other patients and the general public . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONAL ST AFF 1. Procedures: 1.1 National Command Centre (NCC) and Healthcare Coordination Centre (HCC) will triage calls to ascertain risk of infectious disease and will update the transporting ambulance crew accordingly. 1.2 Apply standard precautions including hand hygiene before and after contact of the patient and avoid touching your face. 1.2.1 Airborne Precaution : N95 mask should be worn by crew standard surgical mask to be worn by patient. 1.2.2 Contact Precaution : Gloves and gown should be worn by crew. 1.2.3 Droplet Precaution : Crew to wear surgical mask a

nd eye protection. Patient is to wear a surgical mask. 1.3 Family members and other contacts of infectious patients should not ride in the Ambulance. Where a family member is required to be transported with the patient (e.g. a child) then the family member should be evaluated for fever and if present the family member should be instructed to wear a surgical mask. 1.4 Wherever possible use vehicles that have a separate driver and patient compartment and close com municating windows between driver and patient compartments. 1.5 Patient compartment extraction fans should be turned on. 1.6 Aerosol - generating procedures (e.g. nebulizer treatment) should be minimized during transportation, unless clinical necessary. 1.7 NCC or HCC ( as appropriate) must notify the receiving facility about the arrival of the patient and their suspected or known infectious risk. 1.8 For confirmed infectious cases, contact production supervisor for deep cleaning of the transporting ambulance (as per SOP 2.4 Deep Cleaning of Patient Transporting Vehicles ). 1.9 For suspected infectious cases, contact production supervisor for deep cleaning of the transporting ambulance (as per SOP 2.3 Social Cleaning and Si

mple Decontamination of Vehicles and Non - Critical Equipment ). 1.9.1 This S OP maybe subject to change during periods of suspected outbreaks of infectious disease (e.g. MERS or COVID - 19) – specific advice will be provided as directed by the Ministry of Public Health. 2. References : 2.1 Ambulance Services Infection Control Policy AS 9041 2.2 CL 7279 Infection Prevention and Control Program for Emergency Preparedness and Control for Communicable Diseases .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 353 SOP 4.8.Q Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8.Q VULNERABLE AND HIGH - RISK PATIENT TRANSPO RTATION Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To ensure that all Operational Staff are aware of processes when managing Vulnerable and High - Risk Patients in line with HMCAS CPG, SOP’s, Staff Directives/Circulars. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. All patients encountered by HMCAS are vulnerable either due to acute illness/injury and/or chronic age - related condition. Therefore, HMCAS paramedics and critical care paramedics shall care for all patients in - line with corresponding HMCAS Clinical Practice Guideline (CPG). 2. Assessment and reas sessment of all patients shall be based on the clinical presentation of the patient in accordance to HMCAS CPG 1. 3. All Staff should plan care in collaboration with the patient, parent, and/or family to meet the specific needs of HMCAS patients. 4. Patient care shall be documented in the electronic Patient Care Report (ePCR) in accordance to SOP 4.9 . A . 5. Specific subgroups of p

atient represent heighten vulnerability due to their underlying medical needs and frequent contact w ith the ambulance service for routine transportation . 6. These patients include : 6.1 F rail elderly (refer to vulnerable patient section of CPG) 6.2 Paediatric (refer to paediatric section of CPG) 6.3 Patients with tracheostomy (refer to vulnerable patient section of CPG) 6.4 Patients receiving dialysis (refer to vulnerable patient section of CPG) 6.5 Patients receiving Chemotherapy (refer to SOP 4.8.O Transporting Immunocompromised Chemotherapy Patients )

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 354 SOP 4.8.R Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8.R TRANSPORTING P ATIENTS WITH SUSPECT ED OR KNOWN INFECTIOUS DISEASE I N THE GREEN BUS Implementation Date 12 March 2020 Purpose To give a clear direction in handling and transporting patients with a suspected or known infectious disease and to ensure the safety of healthcare workers and to prevent the potential risk of infection to other patients. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONAL STAFF 1. This SOP is to provide guidance for Ambulance Service staff who are assigned to the GREEN BUS. 2. The Ambulance Service supervisor is responsible for overseeing the transportation process. 3. Equipment : 3.1 Minimize the amount of equipment inside the green bus. 3.2 Do not place equipment on the patient seats . 3.3 Equipment must be placed in the rear of the vehicle. 3.4 Green Bus staff must follow green bus checklists and equipment setup. 4. Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) : 4.1 The following PPE m ust be available: 4.1.1 Gloves 4.1.2 Gown 4.1.3 Mask (surgical/N95) 4.1.4 Eye protection 4.1.5

Goggles 4.1.6 Portable hand sanitizer 4.1.7 Disinfectant wipes 5. Procedure : 5.1 Only stable cases can be transported via the green bus. 5.2 Suspected and confirmed cases must not be transported together on the same bus. 5.3 The maximum number of patients to be transported in the green bus is FIVE (5). 5.4 Patients must be seated 1 meter from each other. 5.5 Full PPE must be worn prior to approaching patients. 5.6 Provide a surgical mask to each patient before entering the bus. 5.7 Sanitize patients’ hands before entering the bus. 5.8 Instruct patients to keep their masks intact, and not to touch their eyes . 5.9 Keep air ventilation switched on non - recycle mode. 5.10 Use yellow bags to dispose of wipes and tissues used by patients. 5.11 After transpo rting patients, doff PPE in the designated area at each facility.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 355 5.12 Perform social cleaning on both the equipment and other frequently - touched surfaces after each patient transportation, using HMC - approved disinfectant wipes. 5.13 Send the vehicle for deep cleani ng at the end of each shift . 5.14 Any used equipment must be manually decontaminated with an HMC - approved disinfectant .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 356 SOP 4.8.S Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector HMC Ambulance Services Clinical Desk Procedure 4 . 8.S CANCELLATION OF RESP ONDING CCP UNITS Implementation Date 1 5 J une 20 22 Purpose To standardize and guide the procedure for cancelling responding CCP units . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Background : 1.1 To provide the best and most appropriate level of care to all patients and prevent unnecessary transport delays, all calls dispatched as ZULU code, according to the dispatch matrix, will receive a Critical Care Paramedic response . 1.2 The on - scene unit should provide treatment to all patients according to the Clinical Practice Guidelines and determine the need for a CCP based on their initial impression and clinical assessment . 1.3 On - scene Ambulance Paramedics should only cancel a CCP unit after performing an initial assessment . 1.4 Where it is determined that no CCP is required, the on - scene unit should cancel the responding CCP without delay . 2. Cancellation of Charlie or Retrieval: 2.1 The on - scene unit should provide a situation report to

the dispatcher in accordance with Clinical Practice Guidelines and inform the dispatcher that they do not require a CCP. 2.2 The dispatcher must acknowledge the situation report and confirm no need for a CCP on - scene . 2.3 The dispatcher should then stand down the responding CCP making them available in NJM . 2.4 The on - scene unit is not required to change to Clinical 1 to cancel a CCP response . 2.5 The on - scene unit may consult with their responding CCP unit over Clinical 2 if they wish to discuss the case before cancelling the CCP . 3. Cancellation of Lifeflight: 3.1 If LifeFlight has been dispatched, or put on standby, and is not required, the LifeFlight unit should be cancelled without delay . 3.2 If the cancelling on - scene unit is an Alpha un it, cancellation should be confirmed with the Clinical Desk on Clinical 1 . 3.2.1 The on - scene Alpha unit must advise their dispatcher that they wish to cancel LifeFlight and will change to Clinical 1 . 3.2.2 The dispatcher will acknowledge the message and update the DT L . 3.2.3 The Alpha Unit will change to Clinical 1 and consult the Clinical Coordinator providing a full endorsement of the patient following SOP 4.8.B and requ

est confirmation to cancel LifeFlight . 3.2.4 The Clinical Coordinator should consider the clinical risk for deterioration likely to require CCP support and if transport by LifeFlight will facilitate improved rural coverage . 3.2.5 If LifeFlight is not required, as confirmed on Clinical 1:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 357 3.2.5.1 The Clinical Coordinator will c onfirm with the unit that they will cancel LifeFlight . 3.2.5.2 The Clinical Coordinator will notify the LifeFlight Coordinator to cancel LifeFlight . 3.2.5.3 The LifeFlight Coordinator will update NJM and NCC DTL as soon as LifeFlight is cancelled. 3.2.5.4 The on - scene unit will c hange back to their dispatch channel and update the dispatcher that LifeFlight has been cancelled . 3.3 Where the cancelling on - scene unit is a CCP, Delta, Oscar Access, or Consultant CCP: Effectiveness, LifeFlight can be cancelled without Clinical 1 consultati on . 3.3.1 The on - scene cancelling unit (Delta, Oscar Access, or Consultant CCP: Effectiveness) will contact the dispatcher and request LifeFlight cancellation . 3.3.2 The dispatcher should immediately update the DTL and LifeFlight Coordinator to cancel LifeFlight . 3.3.3 The LifeFlight coordinator will update NJM and assign the LifeFlight unit available . 3.3.4 The LifeFlight Coordinator will update the Clinical Coordinator: Emergenc y of the cancellation .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 358 SOP 4.8.T Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8.T REQUEST & DISP ATCH OF SPECIALIZED UNIT Implementation Date 01 January 2024 Purpose To provide guidance on HMCAS Specialized Unit activation procedure, roles, and responsibilities . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Aim: 1.1 This document aims to ensure the correct dispatch of an appropriate specialized response unit when a request is made by the Ambulance Service (AS) crew on scene. 2. Definitions : 2.1 Box Type Ambulance (ICU): 2.1.1 AS Bariatric Ambulance: is an ICU ambulance designed to accommodate a bariatric patient on a stretcher regardless of their clinical condition. 2.1.2 A Bariatric Patient: can be a patient with body width of equal to or greater than 60cm (2x ruler length). 2.1.3 A Bariatric Patient: is a patient whose weight is equal to or greater than 150Kg or a patient with a large body physique with BMI of >40. 2.2 Wheelchair Ambulance (Long Vehicle): 2.2.1 A Wheelchair Ambulance is a wheelchair - accessible ambulance that provides non - emergency transpor

tation. 3. Equipment : 3.1 Standard Ambulance equipment shall be provided by HMCAS. 3.2 Specialized Ambulance Type and Equipment. Equipment Type of Ambulance Equipment Measurement Ford Ambulance Box Type ICU/Long Vehicle Wheelchair Ambulance Stretcher Weight Max Stretcher Load limit Stretcher Width Stretcher Length Stretcher Height(cm) 999 - Ferno Stretcher YES NO 36 kg 270 kg 55 cm 210 cm High 98.7 Low 35.6 Stryker Stretcher NO YES (limited access) 66 Kg 318 kg 58 cm 206 cm High 108 Low 63 Electronic Stair - Chair NO YES 25.67 Kg 227 kg 51.6 cm - 95.24 Wheelchair NO YES 36.74 Kg 317.5kg 76.2 cm - -

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 359 Sliding Sheet YES YES - 317.5kg 152.4 cm 203.2cm - Ramp NO YES - - - - - 4. Process of Activation : 4.1 The first unit on scene assesses the patient condition then confirms and verbalizes through the radio for assistance. 4.2 Oscar Command should contact the unit on scene to confirm the type of assistance required ensure the correct dispatch of the specialized unit (Flow Diagram Below). 4.3 Directly after gathering all necessary information related to the case the Oscar Command shall contact the Healthcare Coordin ation Center (HCC) Supervisor on duty and provide all the information. 4.4 The HCC will instruct the crew of the appropriate Specialized Unit to switch the relevant radio channel and report to the NCC dispatcher for further instructions. 4.5 The Oscar Command shou ld notify the DTL at NCC so that the dispatcher can dispatch and follow the appropriate resource accordingly. 4.6 SAS Supervisor should be dispatched to scene if no ES Supervisor or Oscar enroute/on scene already. 5. Criteria for Activation : 5.1 Box Type Ambulance: 5.1.1 The Common name used to request a specialized ambulance with bariatric stretc

her is Box type Ambulance (ICU) . 5.1.2 After assessing the patient and identifying the nature of help needed, the initial unit must follow the process of activation as provid ed in section 4. 5.1.3 After endorsing the patient and assisting in the movement of the patient to the Specialized Unit, the initial unit should book and the Specialized Unit will transport the patient to the hospital. 5.1.4 First Alpha unit on scene if not transporting the patient should complete EPCR and send to the unit that will be transporting the patient. 5.2 Wheelchair Ambulance: 5.2.1 If the patient has his own wheelchair and insists for a wheelchair ambulance , the initial unit can request for the Wheelchair Am bulance following the process of activation in section 4. The Wheelchair Unit assisting the patient shall transport the patient to the hospital and the first unit on scene shall book available. If no Wheelchair Ambulance available, the Alpha Unit will tran sport the patient on a stretcher and not delay on scene for a wheelchair ambulance. 5.2.2 If there are steps inside the patient house (10 steps) and no lift available , the initial unit can request for Wheelchair Ambulance with Electronic St

air Chair to help them to move the patient to the ambulance safely, following the process of activation as outlined in section 4. The requesting unit should continue with the patient and the Specialized Unit book available. If no Wheelchair Ambulance available, the NCC must dis patch alternate units to help move the patient. 5.2.3 If there are 3 - 5 steps at the main entrance of the patient’s house , NCC should provide additional units if required to carry the patient safely.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 362 SOP 4.8.U Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.8.U MENTAL HEALTH TRANSFERS Implementation Date 01 January 2024 Purpose To provide guidance on HMCAS Mental Health Transfers between Facilities procedures, roles, and responsibilities . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 Scheduled Ambulance Service will provide a triple paramedic crewed ambulance for all scheduled Mental Health inter - facility transfers from ED to the Receiving Mental Health Facility (Rawdat Al Khail or Mesaieed Hospital ). 2. Scope : 2.1 Standard Ambulance equipment shall be provided by HMCAS. Any additional equipment must be supplied by the transferring facility, whose staff are knowledgeable/privileged about operating the equipment. 2.2 All HMCAS crew should adhere to the management of a Mental Health patient as mandated within the Ambulance Service CPG 7.1 Acute Behavioral Disturbance . 3. Definitions : 3.1 What is Mental Health? It is ''a state of well - being in which the individual realizes his or her abilities, can cope with the normal st

resses of life, can work prod uctively and fruitfully, and is able to contribute to his or her community.” (WHO,2022). 4. Procedure: 4.1 Two fully stocked ambulances will be prepared and ready for deployment daily, one positioned in Hub 7 and one positioned at Scheduled Ambulance Service spok e point . 4.2 The HCC will utilize CAD to prioritize and dispatch the closest unit to transfer the patient safely and efficiently . 4.3 Patients originating from the community will be managed accordingly to AS CPG and transported to the nearest ED. 5. Operating Hours : 5.1 The unit located at Hub 7 will commence duty between the hours of 08:00 - 20:00 (12 - hour shifts on a 4 - day cycle). 5.2 The unit located at Scheduled Ambulance Service Spoke Point will commence duty between the hours of 12:00 - 00:00 (12 - hour shifts on a 4 - day cycle). 5.3 Outside of these operating hours, the facility is expected to provide a nurse escort to facilitate smooth and safe movement of the patient. If no escort is available, the booking time should be negotiated within operational timing in above points. 6. Completion of Transfer : 6.1 At completion of transfer HMCAS will not return the hospital transfer te
am or equipment. 7. Logistics: 7.1 HMCAS will be responsible for post - use cleaning of the ambulance and fault reporting.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 367 SOP 4.9 . A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.9 . A ELECTRONIC PATIENT CARE RECORDI NG Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose • To ensure that all patient care and findings are accurately recorded . • To ensure clinical documentation is appropriately managed . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. All patient contact s requires the completion of an electronic Patient Care Record (ePCR) : 1.1 Ensure that the treatment recorded accurately reflects the clinical/physical findings. 1.2 Clearly document refusal of treatment and attested thereof by the patient. 1.3 Carry out a periodic pain score assessment of patients presenting with pain during their care and accurately record this on the ePCR. 1.4 Record a provisional diagnosis on the ePCR. 1.5 Justify prolonged scene time on the ePCR. 1.6 Attach the vital signs record (‘Code Summary’) to the ePCR. 2. Documentation Responsibilities : 2.1 The first treating clinician documents the following: 2.1.1 Patient Complaint; 2.1.2 Initial Patient Medical History obtained [including

medications and allergies]; 2.1.3 Clinical assessment, signs, and symptoms; 2.1.4 Preliminary Vital Signs and 2.1.5 Treatment initiated and response to treatment. 2.2 If a CCP continues care of the patient to hospital, he/she should document any further assessment and treatment and note any changes in patient diagnosis or condition. 2.3 The completion of the ePCR is the respons ibility of the highest qualified transporting clinician. The CCP must document a stand down ePCR if they have assessed a patient but will not accompany the patient to hospital. 2.4 Should the electronic PCR platform fail or is not available, a paper PCR shoul d be used. 2.5 For paper hardcopy PCR, hand - over the “blue” copy of the PCR with the patient to the receiving facility. Ensure that receiving clinician signs the “white” copy (original) of the PCR. 2.6 Do not copy PCRs in any way unless permission is received from the immediate supervisor. 2.7 The Paper PCR should be handed over to the CDO once completed or kept secure until the end of shift where it can be handed over to the CDO or supervisor. 2.8 Handle all toughpads/clinical documents with clean hands/clean gloves. If a toughpad becomes contaminat

ed clean the toughpad with the approved wipes, if a document becomes contaminated with body fluids, make a duplicate and destroy/shred the original. 2.9 All printed ECG strips must include patient identifiers (in accordance with SOP 4.9.C and International Patient Safety Goals) when handed over to another treating clinician. This can be in the form of a hospital sticker or handwritten by the clinician.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 368 3. Filling in the data fields on t he PCR: 3.1 Ensure all applicable data fields in the ePCR are completed. If using a paper PCR, write in neat PRINT style and not “joined - up” (cursive) text, using a black pen and complete all applicable data fields. If using a paper PCR, delete written errors by placing a ruled line through the text to be deleted. Sign and date the deleted error. 3.2 Where the option in the ePCR exists to select “Yes”, “No” or “Unknown” from a drop - down list: 3.2.1 Where the patient/guardian is able to understand and answer the question, select Yes or No (For example: the patient does have an allergy – select yes and document the type of allergy, or the patient does not have an allergy or is not aware of any known allergies, select no). 3.2.2 Where the patient/guardian is unable to understand o r answer the question, select unknown (For example: select unknown if there is a language barrier, patient is unconscious or the patient/guardian is unable to verbalise for some other reason. Document the presence of language barrier or other explanation f or why options are unknown eg unconscious, neck tr

auma that would impair vocalisation). 3.3 Accurately document all medications used in the relevant medication section. 3.4 If clinical skills are performed, appropriately document the skill performed in the relevan t care management section .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 369 SOP 4.9 . B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.9 . B REPORTING OF OCCUR RENCES/VARIATIONS/AC CIDENTS (OVA) AND ADVERSE DRUG REACTIO NS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To establish a systematic reporting of events occurring during routine patient care and practice to aid the quality improvement process. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. An OVA Report should be filled in under the following general circumstances: 1.1 Any event or circumstance, which results in an unintended or unnecessary harm to any person or loss/damage to HMC or personal property . 1.2 An event or situation that could have resulted (near miss) in an ac cident, injury, or illness, but did not, either by chance or through timely intervention. 1.3 An occurrence that results in a loss to a third party due to bodily injury, or property damage or destruction. 1.4 An event that departs from expectations (variance); an act contrary to a usual rule i.e. a difference between what is expected and what actually occurs. 1.5 An unplanned, unexpected, and

undesirable event (accident), which occurs suddenly and results in damage, injury, or harm. 2. Reporting of Adverse Drug Reactions : 2.1 An adverse drug reaction is any undesirable effect of a drug (or combination of drugs) beyond its expected therapeutic effects occurring during clinical use. 2.2 Report the findings of a witnessed suspected adverse drug reaction through the HMCAS Communicatio ns Centre, via the Clinical Desk . 2.3 The minimum standard information required by the Clinical Desk are: 2.3.1 Age of patient 2.3.2 Chief complaint and medical history 2.3.3 Trade/generic name of suspected drug 2.3.4 Description of adverse reaction/s 2.4 In the PCR, document all the eve nts associated with the suspected drug, including the date, time, and notifications. 2.5 Submit an online OVA report through the HMC intranet (Click on HIS Applications Gate → Groups → Other Applications → OVA Incident Reporting System . Hand - written reports are not permitted. 3. Reporting Road Traffic Accidents involving HMCAS vehicles : 3.1 In the case of an RTA involving a HMCAS vehicle, the following procedure ( Figure 4.9B - 1 ) should be followed:

C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.9 . C ACCURATE IDENTIFIC ATION OF PATIENTS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure that all patients are accurately identified during transition from the pre - hospital to in - hospital phase of care . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L CLINICAL PERSONNEL (E.G. ALPHA, CHARLI E, DELTA, BIKE AND EVEN T STAFF) 1. The ambulance service will use two unique patient identifiers prior to transport and prior to discharge to a receiving individual or organization. The primary unique identifiers are patients NAME , Qatar Identification Documentation (QID) and Health Card (HC) . 1.1 On arrival at a patient’s side, introduce self and clarify patient’s name. 1.2 Politely request the patient (or family member/friend) to provide his/her QID to confirm identity. 1.3 If the patient is not in possession of a QID (visitor/new resident of Qatar) then request a copy of patient passport to verify i dentity. 1.4 Record patient’s full name and birth date on the ePCR. 1.5 For mass - casualty inc

idents, identify patients by patient triage card numbers initially. 1.6 Pediatric patients must be identified by their own identifiers and not those of their parents. If the patient is a newborn and has been registered with a name, they will be identified as a baby of _________. 2. If none of the above - mentioned patient identifiers are available or possible : 2.1 Document the patient’s name as “Unknown” on the e PCR. 2.2 Identify the p atient by the CFS and ePCR number until the correct relevant details can be updated by the MOI or relevant parties . DO NOT DELAY if the patient requires rapid transportation to a definitive care facility.

D Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Se rvice Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.9 . D CARE AND MANAGEMEN T OF THE ELECTRONIC PATIENT CARE RECORD PLATFORM Implementation Date 01 November 2016 Purpose To ensure that the HMCAS electronic patient care record (ePCR) platform is appropriately accessed, utilized, and managed. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Management of the Electronic Patient Care Toughpad® : 1.1 Complete a patient care record for all patients assessed and/or treated ( SOP 4.9.A ) on a Toughpad® (ePCR) . The ePCR data is automatically transmitted to an electronic server every 10 minutes. 1.2 Use the paper PCR if the ePCR is unavailable or malfunctionin g. Notify your production supervisor for advice/replacement as soon as possible. 1.3 Receive the Toughpad® in good working order as defined in SOP 3.17 . 1.4 At the start of shift, record on the vehicle checklist and report the following to the PTL: 1.4.1 ePCR serial number; 1.4.2 MDT serial number; 1.4.3 Toughpad® serial number; and 1.4.4 LifePak 15 device number 1.5 Do not use the Toughpa

d® for internet browsing or accessing any other software application except the ePCR application . Report any violations to the i mmediate supervisor. 1.6 Transmit all monitored vital signs and ECG data via Bluetooth from the multi - parameter monitor (LP15 or other) to the ePCR platform, before officially signing the patient over to the receiving hospital staff. 1.7 If a CCP assesses a patien t but does not ride along to the hospital, he/she shall note his/her credentials in the appropriate field on the ePCR. 1.7.1 If either the AP or CCP is using a paper PCR, the ePCR Trip_ID must be noted on the paper PCR and given to the facility at handover when a Trip_ID (barcode) is requested. 1.7.2 At handover, the AP/CCP shall ensure that the receiving facility staff endorses the paper PCR. 2. Handover Procedure : 2.1 On completion of the verbal handover at the receiving facility, complete the mandatory information on ‘Inc ident information’ tab to generate a bar code. 2.2 The receiving facility staff shall scan the ePCR generated bar code in order to transfer the record to the hospital. 2.3 Continue to finalize the outstanding ePCR details/information. 2.4 Where there is a problem with th
e device (low battery or malfunction) or Bluetooth, print and handover the code summary (LP15 or other) with the CFS number, for the facility staff to attach the patient sticker and file manually.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 373 2.5 At handover, the receiving clinician shall sign and not e his/her credentials in the appropriate ePCR screen using the Toughpad® stylus. 2.6 Save and close the case normally unless there is no server connectivity during handover of a critically ill/injured patient - print the ePCR at the receiving facility and hand over to the receiving facility staff with a code summary. 2.7 If there is a problem with the device e.g. low battery or malfunction, use the paper PCR . Notify the Romeo or Clinical Documentation Officer at the facility or Production Supervisor when available. 2.8 Manually advise the Trip_ID and the patient’s Health Card Number, if the receiving facility is unable to scan the bar code from the device. 2.9 For a non - HMC facility or an HMC Facility not using Cerner®, the receiving clinician shall sign and note his/her cr edentials in the appropriate screen using the Toughpad® stylus as a ‘non - HMC Staff’. Advise the receiving person to request a fax/email copy via email address: ambulanceCDO@hamad.qa . 3. Cleaning Procedure : 3.1 Handle the ePCR with clean hands or clean gloves in accordance with hand

hygiene instructions. 3.2 If drops of water or other stains get on the surface of the LCD panel, wipe them off immediately (failure to do so may result in staining). 3.3 The ePCR device shall be cleaned twice daily or when visibly soiled or between patient encounters or between each staff handling. 3.4 To clean the LCD panel: 3.4.1 wipe with a disposable soft damp cloth such as gauze. 3.4.2 clean with HMC approved non - alcoholic wipes (e.g. Medizar), if potent ially soiled with blood or other infectious material. 3.5 For all areas, other than the LCD panel, clean with HMC approved non - alcoholic solution (Medizar) . 4. CAUTION : 4.1 Do not use benzene, thinner, or rubbing alcohol since it may adversely affect the surface caus ing discoloration, etc. 4.2 Do not use commercially - available household cleaners and cosmetics, as they may contain components harmful to the surface. 4.3 Do not apply water or detergent directly to the computer, since liquid may enter inside of the computer and c ause malfunction or damage.

A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.10 . A MIR FIRST UNIT ON SCENE Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To standardize the role and responsibility of the first arriving unit on scene at a major incident. Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. FIRST AMBULANCE OR RESPONSE DRIVER arriving on scene (See Figure 4.10 . A - 1 ): 1.1 Book ‘On Scene’ with NCC on designated channel. 1.2 Park as near to the scene as safety permits; do not leave vehicle. 1.3 Don high visibility vest and safety helmet. 1.4 Assume role of Silver Control on the channel instructed by NCC. 1.5 LEAVE FLASHING Emergency lights and hazards warning lights ON. 1.6 Maintain communication link between ambulance paramedic attendant and NCC. 1.7 Provide initial METHANE report to NCC. 1.8 Request additional resources as requ ired. 1.9 Hold all staff at your vehicle until they are briefed by silver command. 1.10 Ensure all staffs wear appropriate PPE and have triage packs. 2. If the first arriving unit is single crewed, then he/she will be responsibl

e for carrying out the ATTENDANT duties described below. 2.1 Don high visibility vest and safety helmet 2.2 Assume role of Silver Commander 2.3 Carry out a scene size up of the incident 2.4 Report back to NCC using the METHANE: 2.4.1 M - MAJOR INCIDENT DECLARED at appropriate LEVEL 1/2/3 . 2.4.2 E – EXACT LOCATION with map reference if possible. 2.4.3 T - TYPE OF INCIDENT with details of structure, vehicles. 2.4.4 H – HAZARDS present and potential. 2.4.5 A – ACCESS ROUTES and suitable Rendezvous Point. 2.4.6 N – NUMB ER OF PATIENTS and severity. 2.4.7 E – EMERGENCY SERVICES present and required. 2.5 Declare a major incident based on criteria as set out by HMCAS policies. 2.6 Think command and control and designate roles. 2.7 Issue ACTION CARDS (available from Delta/Charlie/Oscar units) 2.8 Instruct the second arriving crew to assist with triage. 2.9 In liaison with other emergency services establish: 2.9.1 Access and egress routes to and from scene

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 378 2.9.2 Ambulance Casualty Clearing Points 2.9.3 Ambulance Control Point 2.9.4 Ambulance Parking Point 2.9.5 Ambulance Loading Poi nt(s) 2.10 Consider potential HAZARDS when designating the above. 3. The first crew on scene shall not attempt to rescue/treat casualties until relieved of their initial “First on Scene” roles by Ambulance Service supervisors or managers.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 379 Figure 4.10 . A - 1 APPROACH TO A SIGNIFICANT/MAJOR INCIDENT APPROACH EVERY SCENE AS A POTENTIAL HAZARDOUS INCIDENT If you see hazardous materials at the scene: • DO NOT APPROACH • Stay back a safe distance, up - wind from the incident • Inform NCC IF THERE ARE MORE THAN 3 RED/YELLOW PATIENTS FIRST UNIT ON SCENE • Put on high visibility vest and helmet • Carry out scene size - up • Count the number of patients and their severity status Red/Yellow/Green) • Report back to NCC using the METHANE Report M – Declare as potential Major Incident E – Exact Location of incident T – Type of Incident H – Hazards A – Access and Egress Route N – Number and Status of Patients E – Emergency Services Present and Required • Instruct SECOND ARRIVING UNIT: - DRIVER to set up a PARKING area - ATTENDANT to start TRIAGE SECOND UNIT ON SCENE • Put on high visibility vest and helmet • Prepare to take the BRONZE PARKING role - You will be responsible for setting up a parking area for Ambulances • Prepare to take the BRONZE PRIMARY TRIAGE OFFICER role - You will be responsible for Triaging Patients

B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.10 . B MAJOR INCIDENT SCE NE SETUP Implementation Date 01 June 2016 Purpose To establish an orderly flow of patients from scene through the transport area . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. On arrival of MIR Trucks at the scene: 1.1 Park the trucks in a safe area and report to silver command to designate the setup location. 1.2 Park the MIR truck on the right side and logistic truck on the left side. 1.3 Off - load the container and keep it attached to the truck if there is space to accommodate it. 1.4 Consider lighting if the incident is at night. 2. Setup: Divide the staff into two teams, one to set the blue and yellow tarpaul in, one to set the red then green tarpaulin . 2.1 Put down the blue tarpaulin first to start secondary triage early (see the diagram) . 2.2 Place the Spine boards, scoop stretchers and basket stretchers on the right side of the tarpaulin. 2.3 Put down the red tarpaulin then yellow and green.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 381 2.4 Place portable oxygen cylinder (small cylinders), LP15 and Pax treatment bags on tarpaulins, starting with the blue tarpaulin. 3. Erect the BLUE shelter (secondary triage tent) first; if possible, erect the red shelter simultaneously. 3.1 Assign a minimum of five staff to erect the tent, four (4) staff and one (1) MIR supervisor to guide and supervise the procedures as well as staff safety. 3.2 The location of the tent should be the safest area facing the incident. 3.3 The required equipment in the blue shelter are as follows: 3.3.1 Four NATO stretchers with ground stabilizer (if the ground surface is not stable). Place the stretchers in the middle of the shelter, with the head facing the shelter exit door. 3.3.2 Place the blue Zarges in the middle of the back to back NATO stretchers. 3.3.3 Four LP15 or (LP12) 3.3.4 Four small oxygen cylinders and four portable suctions. 3.3.5 Minimum of four tent lights maximum of six. 3.3.6 Fire extinguisher near the shelter exit in a safe place, fixed with the tent straps. 3.3.7 Two (2) air conditioner (A C) minimum per tent; keep the AC outside the tent using the AC hose from
the tent AC holes.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 382 4. Erect the RED shelter second if it is not possible to do at the same time with the blue shelter. 4.1 Assign a minimum of five (5) staff to erect the tent, four (4) staff and one (1) MIR supervisor to guide and supervise the procedures and safety of the staff . The tent should be located on the far - left side of the truck containers. 4.2 The required equipment in the red shelter are as follows: 4.2.1 Six (6) NATO stretchers with ground stabilizer (if the ground surface is not stable) . Locate three on each side, keeping the stretcher head facing the middle of the tents. 4.2.2 Six (6) red Zarges, one next to each stretcher 4.2.3 Six (6) LP15 or (LP12) 4.2.4 Six (6) Oxylog 1000 4.2.5 Six (6) portable suctio n units 4.2.6 Minimum of four (4) tent lights maximum of six (6) lights 4.2.7 Fire extinguisher near the shelter exit in a safe place, fixed with the tent straps 4.2.8 Two (2) Air conditioner (AC) minimum per tent; keep the AC outside the tent and use the AC hose from the tent AC Holes. 4.3 Keep the mass oxygen delivery system in the tent near the exit door without causing an obstruction. 4.4 NOTE : Connect all si
x outlets and the alarm system. Check the pressure and Oxylog connectors after attaching . Keep the oxygen delivery tu bing on the inner side of the tent.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 383 5. Erect the Yellow shelter: 5.1 Assign minimum of five (5) staff to erect the tent, four (4) staff and one (1) MIR supervisor to guide and supervise the procedures as well as staff safety. 5.2 The location of the tent should b e on the left side of the truck and on the right side to the red tent. 5.3 The required equipment in the yellow shelter are as follows: 5.3.1 Eight (8) NATO stretchers, with ground stabilizer (if the ground surface is not stable). 5.3.2 Four (4) stretchers on each side, t wo (2) near the tent entrance, two (2) near the tent exit and two (2) next to each other in the middle of the tent on each side. 5.3.3 Four (4) yellow Zarges container (see the diagram page 92) 5.3.4 Eight (8) LP15 or LP12 5.3.5 Four (4) portable suctions 5.3.6 Minimum of four (4 ) tent lights maximum of six (6) 5.3.7 Fire extinguisher near the shelter exit in a safe place, fixed with the tent straps. 5.3.8 Two (2) Air conditioner (AC) minimum per tent; keep the AC outside the tent and use the AC hose from the tent AC Holes. 5.3.9 Keep the mass oxyg en delivery system in the tent near the exit door without causing an obstructi
on. 5.3.10 NOTE: Connect all eight (8) outlets and the alarm system. Check the pressure and Oxylog Connectors after attaching . Keep the oxygen delivery tubing on the inner side of the t ent.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 384 6. Erect the Green tent (shelter) last: 6.1 Assign a minimum of five (5) staff to erect the tent, four (4) staff and one (1) MIR supervisor to guide and supervise the procedures and staff safety. 6.2 Place the green tent on the right side of the MIR and logistic containers. 6.3 The required equipment in the blue shelter are as follows: 6.3.1 Two (2) NATO stretchers, with ground stabilizer (if the ground surface is not stable). Place them on either side of the shelter next to the exit door, with the head facing the tent wall. 6.3.2 Twenty chairs, six (6) on each side of the tent and eight (8) in the middle . 6.3.3 Two (2) green Zarges in the tent 6.3.4 Two LP15 or LP12 6.3.5 Minimum of four (4) tent lights, maximum of six (6) 6.3.6 Fire extinguisher near the shelter exit in a safe place, fixed wit h the tent straps. 6.3.7 Two (2) Air conditioner (AC) minimum per tent, keep the AC outside the tent and use the AC hose from the tent AC Holes.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 385 7. Erect the Loading shelter (white coloured tent): 7.1 Assign minimum of five (5) staff to erect the tent, four (4) staff and one (1) MIR supervisor to guide and supervise procedures and staff safety. 7.2 The location of this tent is behind the red and yellow tent towards the middle area. 7.3 Equipment required in this shelter are as follows: 7.3.1 Two (2) air conditioner (AC) if ava ilable; keep the AC outside the tent and use the AC hose from the tent AC holes. 7.3.2 Draft table 8. Erect the incident Morgue shelter: 8.1 MIR production supervisor shall facilitate getting this shelter from logistic village warehouse. 8.2 Assign minimum of 6 staff to erect the tent . Four (4) staff and one (1) MIR supervisor to guide and supervise the procedures and staff safety. 8.3 Place this tent behind the red tent. 8.4 The required equipment in the blue shelter are as follows: 8.4.1 Two (2) air conditioners (AC) minimum per tent , the AC should keep outside the tent and use the AC hose from the tent AC holes. 8.4.2 Body bags 8.4.3 Blue sheets 8.4.4 Patient care reports

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 386 9. Erect the survivor Reception shelter: 9.1 Assign minimum of 8 staff to erect the tent. Four (4) staff and one (1) MIR supervisor to guide and supervise procedures and staff safety. 9.2 Place this tent behind the green tent. 9.3 The equipment required are as follows: 9.3.1 Two (2) ACs if available; keep the AC outside the tent and use the AC hose from the tent AC holes . 9.3.2 Chairs if available 10. Re - packin g the equipment : 10.1 The same team allocated to a tent shall re - pack the equipment and the tent used at the incident. 10.2 Check all generators, wires, equipment for any damage. 10.3 Report any damage immediately to your Oscar or manager . 10.4 Check the fuel level and refuel the truck and generators . 10.5 Re - stock all missing or used items 10.6 Write defect reports if required 11. General considerations : 11.1 Safety is a top priority for staff, patients, colleagues, survivors. 11.2 Don’t leave the generators unattended; one (1) MIR staff has to attend to the generators all the time. 11.3 Teamwork is the key to success. 11.4 Ask for help if you cannot do the task allocated to you, and if you are unfa
miliar with any equipment and/or procedure. 11.5 Don’t leave your area of responsibility without informing your te am leader.

C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.10 . C DECONTAMINATION (D ECON) SETUP (SCENE L AYOUT), RESPONSE AND MITIGATION Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose Response and Mitigation to Hazardous Materials . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L (PART 1) AND HAZMA T STAFF (PART 2) 1. PART 1 : The ‘1 - 2 - 3’ system (to be used when the cause of the incident is unknown) . 2. When attending emergency calls, adopt the ‘1 - 2 - 3’ system to ensure staff safety and to identify a possible chemical incident/attack . Follow up with the use of the METHANE report as soon as practical. 3. PART 2 : HAZMAT team considerations . 4. Protective Clothing: HMCAS uses LEVEL C or LEVEL D Protective Clothing. 4.1 Before use of protective clothing: 4.1.1 Determine that the clothing material is correct for the specified task at hand. 4.1.2 Visually inspect for imperfect seams, non - uniform coatings, any tears, and malfunctioning closures. 4.1.3 Hold up to light and check for pinholes 4.1.4 Flex the product and observe for cracks 4.1.5 Observe

for other signs or shelf deterioration 4.1.6 If the product has been used previously, inspect inside and out for signs of chemical attack (discoloration, swelling and stiffness) . 4.1.7 Pressurize glove to check for pinholes. Blow into glove, then roll gauntlet towards fingers or inflate glove and hold under water. In either case, no air should escape. 4.2 During the work task, periodically inspect for: 4.2.1 Closure failure 4.2.2 Tears 4.2.3 P unctures 4.2.4 Seam discontinuities [ 1 ] : ONE casualty Approach using normal procedures. [2] : TWO casualties Approach with caution, consider all options . Report on arrival and update control . [3] : THREE or more casualties DO NOT approach the scene: ✓ Withdraw ✓ Contain ✓ Isolate yourself ✓ REQUEST SPECIALIST HELP (HAZMAT TEAM)

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 388 4.2.5 Evidence of chemical attack such as discoloration, swelling, stiffening, and softening (keep in mind, however, that chemical permeation can occur without any visible effects). 5. LEVEL C protective clothing : 5.1 Not Acceptable for Chemical Emergency Response 5.2 Support Function Protective Garment (meets NFPA 1993) 5.3 Full - face piece, air - purifying, canister - equipped respirator 5.4 Chemical resistant gloves and safety boots 5.5 Two - way communications system, hard hat 5.6 OPTIONAL: Face shield, escape SCBA 5.7 Protection Provided: The same level of skin protection as Level B, but a lower level of respiratory protection. Liquid splash protection but no protection to chemical vapours or gases. 5.8 Used When : Contact with site chemical(s) will not a ffect the skin. Air contaminants have been identified and concentrations measured. The site and its hazards have been completely characterized. 5.9 Limitations: Protective clothing items must resist penetration by the chemical or mixtures present . Chemical air borne concentration must be less than IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life and Health) levels. The at

mosphere must contain at least 19.5% oxygen. 6. LEVEL D Protective Clothing : 6.1 Not Acceptable for Chemical Emergency Response 6.2 Coveralls, safety boots/shoes, safety glasses or chemical splash goggles 6.3 OPTIONAL: Gloves, escape SCBA, face - shield 6.4 Protection Provided: No respiratory protection, minimal skin protection 6.5 Used When : The atmosphere contains no known hazard. Work functions preclude splashes, immersion, potential for inhalation, or direct contact with hazard chemicals. 6.6 Limitations : This level should not be worn in the Hot Zone. The atmosphere must contain at least 19.5% oxygen. 6.7 The type of equipment used, and the overall level of protection sho uld be re - evaluated periodically as the amount of information about the HAZMAT incident situation or process increases. HMC AS HAZMAT should upgrade or downgrade their level of protection only with concurrence with the Team Leader/Bronze Decon/Bronze Tactic al. 7. Donning Procedure : 7.1 Establish and practice periodically for donning the various ensemble configurations that the HMCAS HAZMAT team may use. 7.2 The procedures assume the wearer has previous training in respirator use and decontamination proc

edures. 7.3 Once the equipment has been donned, its fit should be evaluated. If the clothing is too small, it will restrict movement, increase the likelihood of tearing the suit material, and accelerate wearer fatigue. If the clothing is too large, the pos sibility of snagging the material is increased, and the dexterity and coordination of the wearer may be compromised. 7.4 Inspect clothing and respiratory equipment before donning.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 389 7.5 Standing or sitting, step into the legs of the suit; ensure proper placement of the feet within the suit; then gather the suit around the waist. 7.6 Depending on type of suit: put on long - sleeved inner gloves (similar to surgical gloves). Secure gloves to sleeves, for suits with detachable gloves (if not done prior to entering the suit) . Additional over gloves, worn over attached suit gloves, may be donned later. 7.7 Put on hard hat, if needed 7.8 Raise hood overhead carefully so as not to disrupt face seal 7.9 Begin to secure the suit by closing all fasteners on opening 7.10 Secure all belts and/or adjus table leg, head, and waistbands 7.11 Have assistant first ensure that wearer is breathing properly and then make final closure of the suit. 7.12 Have assistant check all closures 7.13 Have assistant observe the wearer for a period of time to ensure that the wearer is com fortable, psychologically stable, and that the equipment is functioning properly. 8. Doffing procedures : 8.1 Remove any extraneous or disposable clothing, boot covers, outer gloves, and tape. 8.2 Have assistant loosen and remove the wearer's

safety shoes or boots. 8.3 Ha ve assistant open the suit completely and lift the hood over the head of the wearer. 8.4 Remove arms, one at a time, from suit. Avoid any contact between the outside surface of the suit and the wearer's body -- and lay the suit out flat behind the wearer. Leave internal gloves on, if any. 8.5 Sitting, if possible, remove both legs from the suit. 8.6 After suit is removed, remove internal gloves by rolling them off the hand, inside out. 8.7 Remove internal clothing and thoroughly cleanse the body. 9. User Monitoring : 9.1 The w earer must understand all aspects of clothing/equipment operation and their limitations; during protective clothing use, end users should be encouraged to report any perceived problems or difficulties to their team leader. These malfunctions include, but a re not limited to: 9.1.1 Degradation of the protection ensemble; 9.1.2 Perception of odours ; 9.1.3 Skin irritation; 9.1.4 Unusual residues on clothing material; 9.1.5 Discomfort; 9.1.6 Resistance to breathing; 9.1.7 Fatigue due to respirator use; 9.1.8 Interference with vision or communication; 9.1.9 Restrict ion of movement; and 9.1.10 Physiological responses such as rapid pu
lse, nausea, or chest pain.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 390 10. Before HMCAS HAZMAT team members undertake any activity in their protective ensembles, the anticipated duration of use should be established. Several factors limit the length of a mission, including: 10.1 Air supply consumption as affected by wearer work rate, fitness, body size, and breathing patterns; 10.2 Suit ensemble permeation, degradation, and penetration by chemical contaminants, including expected leakage through suit or respirator exhaust valves (ensemble protection factor); 10.3 Ambient temperature as it influences material chemical resistance and flexibility, suit and respirator exhaust valve performance, and wearer heat stress and 10.4 Coolant supply (if necessary). 11. Response an d Mitigation Plan: Hazardous Materials : 11.1 A response and mitigation plan shall be developed and set up before any HMCAS HAZMAT personnel or equipment is allowed to enter areas where the potential for exposure to hazardous substances exists. The response and mitigation plan should: 11.1.1 Determine the number and layout of decontamination stations; 11.1.2 Determine the decontamination equipment needed; 11.1.3 Determine a

ppropriate decontamination methods; 11.1.4 Establish procedures to prevent contamination of clean areas; 11.1.5 Establish methods and procedures to minimize wearer contact with contaminants during removal of personal protective clothing and 11.1.6 Establish methods for disposing of clothing and equipment that are not completely decontaminated. 11.2 The plan should be revised wh enever the type of personal protective clothing or equipment changes, the use conditions change, or the chemical hazards are reassessed based on new information. 11.3 The response and mitigation process for decontamination should consist of a series of procedur es performed in a specific sequence. Each procedure should be performed at a separate decontamination line in order to prevent cross contamination. 11.4 Decontamination lines should be separated physically to prevent cross contamination and should be arranged i n order of decreasing contamination, preferably in a straight line. Separate flow patterns and stations must be established to isolate workers from different contamination zones. Entry and exit points to exposed areas should be conspicuously marked. Dressi ng stations for entry to the decontami

nation area should be separate from redressing areas for exit from the decontamination area. 11.5 All equipment used for decontamination must be decontaminated and/or disposed of properly. Buckets, brushes, clothing, tools, and other contaminated equipment should be collected, placed in containers, and labelled . All spent solutions and wash water should be collected and disposed of properly. Clothing that is not completely decontaminated should be placed in plastic bags, pen ding further decontamination and/or disposal. 11.6 Decontamination of workers who initially come in contact with personnel and equipment leaving exposure or contamination areas will require more protection from contaminants than decontamination workers who are assigned in the decontamination line .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 391 12. Storage : 12.1 Clothing must be stored properly to prevent damage or malfunction from exposure to dust, moisture, sunlight, damaging chemicals, extreme temperatures, and impact. 12.2 Guidelines for general storage of protective clothing include: 12.3 Potentially contaminated clothing should be stored in an area separate from unused protective clothing. 12.4 Potentially contaminated clothing should be stored in a well - ventilated area, with good air flow around each item, if possible. 12.5 Diffe rent types and materials of clothing and gloves should be stored separately to prevent issuing the wrong material by mistake. 12.6 Protective clothing should be folded or hung in accordance with manufacturer instructions. 13. Maintenance : 13.1 In no case should you atte mpt to repair equipment. 13.2 All repairs made must be recorded for the specific clothing along with appropriate inspection results (if clothing has been sent for manufacturer repair) . 14. Actions for HAZMAT vehicles arriving at the Scene : 14.1 Park the vehicles in a sa fe area and the Team Leader report to Silver Command to coordinate the decontaminatio

n setup location in conjunction with the relevant external agency. 14.2 Park HAZMAT 0 vehicle on the warm - zone/cold - zone border. 14.3 Park the CBRNe truck on the warm - zone/cold - zone border. 14.4 Off load the container and keep it attached to the truck if there is enough space on scene and if it doesn’t negatively impact access or egress routes or hamper the safe movement of patients. 14.5 Consider lighting if the incident is at night. 14.6 Ensure that wind direction is observed (if the HAZMAT truck is on scene, make use of the windsock) . 15. Phase 1 (minimum 5 team members) : 15.1 Set up the individual decontamination with the entrance 2m back from the border of the hot - zone. Set up the bas in in the tent. 15.2 Don the appropriate level of PPE (Level C/D) to undertake decontamination operations. 15.3 Remove the water heater from the vehicle. 15.4 Set up the 100 - gallon waste bladder on the side of the tent that has the sprayer connection. 15.5 Set up the sump - pum p in the decontamination shelter basin and connect the grey hose to the bladder. 15.6 Ensure that both valves are in the open position. 15.7 Run appropriate voltage (blue connections) extension cable from ele

ctrical box to distribution point. 15.8 Secure the electrical b ox to the frame of the tent with zip - ties. 15.9 Setup the water heater on side of the waste bladder furthest away from the decontamination tent.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 392 15.10 Connect the high - pressure hose (fire hose) to the tent and run out towards the cold - zone border. 15.11 Run appropriate vol tage (blue connections) extension cable from water heater to distribution point. 15.12 Connect both the low - pressure hoses to the bottom fittings on the water heater - red hose to wash, blue hose to rinse. 15.13 Connect the low - pressure hoses to the tent with the red hose on the inlet closest to the bladder and the blue hose on the opposite side of the tent. 15.14 Setup two conveyors meeting at the exit of the decontamination tent so that they extend out either side of the tent. 15.15 Place the conveyors on the side of the sprayer connection. 15.16 Place one floor riser in the tent running parallel to the conveyor system and one floor riser at the exit of the tent running parallel to the conveyor system. 15.17 Place one foldable bin with bin bags in place at the entrance to the tent on the sid e opposite to the conveyor. 15.18 Place a second foldable bin with bin bag in place with patient belonging bags inside next to it. 15.19 Place a plastic bin at the exit to the tent on the oppos

ite side of the conveyor system. 15.20 Connect tent lights to the frame at both t he entrance and the exit of the decontamination tent. Light 1 at entrance with the long cable on the right and light two at the exit with the long cable on the right. 15.21 Lights are to be connected in series. 15.22 All excess cabling to be rolled and secured to the tent frame with zip - ties. 15.23 Run appropriate voltage (yellow connections) extension cable to lights from distribution point. 15.24 Don the appropriate level of PPE (Level C/D) to undertake decontamination operations as a safety person/relief. 15.25 Place disposable t owels and suits/foil blankets at the exit of the tent on the side opposite to the conveyor system. 15.26 Assume roles and responsibilities of Decontamination Team Leader . 16. Phase 2. (minimum 9 team members) : 16.1 Continue with decontamination operations as required. 16.2 Setup 2 - line decontamination approximately 5m laterally from the 100 - gallon waste bladder . 16.3 Setup the basin in the tent. 16.4 Setup two 500 - gallon waste bladders between the two lines decontamination and the 100 - gallon waste bladder. 16.5 Place the first bl adder inline next to the 100 -

gallon bladder. 16.6 Place the second bladder behind the first bladder. 16.7 Setup the sump - pump in the basin and connect the grey hose to the second waste bladder. 16.8 Run appropriate voltage (blue connections) extension cable from electric al box to distribution point. 16.9 Secure the electrical box to the frame of the tent with zip - ties.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 393 16.10 Ensure that both valves are in the open position on the second bladder. 16.11 Ensure that the valve is closed on the first bladder. 16.12 Hang the interior curtain that sep arates the wash basin from the de - robing area. 16.13 Hang the centre curtain between the conveyor section and the walking section. 16.14 Connect the hand sprayers. 16.15 Connect low pressure hoses to the water heater (top two connections) – red hose to wash, blue hose to ri nse. 16.16 Connect low pressure hoses to tent – red hose to inlet closest to hot - zone, blue hose to inlet closest to cold - zone. 16.17 Setup a conveyor from the curtain out towards the cold - zone. 16.18 Align the conveyor towards the side of the tent closest to the bladders leaving enough room for decontamination personnel to be able to work between the conveyor and the tent. 16.19 Place a foldable bin outside the re - robing area. 16.20 Place disposable towels inside the re - robing area. 16.21 Place suits/foil blankets in the re - robing area. 16.22 Place two floor rises inside the basin running longitudinally through the basin. 16.23 Place two floor rises in the r

e - robing area. 16.24 Place two floor rises just outside the re - robing area. 16.25 Setup tent lights in the 2 - line decontamination. 16.26 Connect tent lights to the frame at both the entrance and the exit of the decontamination tent. 16.27 Light 1 at entrance with the long cable on the right. 16.28 Light 2 in the decontamination wash - down area. Wiring run through the tent secured to the ceiling of the tent with zip - ties. 16.29 Light 3 at the exit with the long cable on the right. 16.30 Lights are to be connected in series. 16.31 All excess cabling to be rolled and secured to the tent frame with zip - ties. 16.32 Run appropriate voltage (yellow connections) extension cable to lights from distributio n point. 16.33 Switch water over from individual decontamination to 2 - line decontamination. 16.34 Ensure water is flowing on both the wash and rinse lines. 16.35 Move the two conveyors from the individual decontamination to the 2 - line decontamination. 16.36 Connect them to the si ngle conveyor in situ running out towards the hot - zone. 16.37 Commence decontamination operations in the 2 - line decontamination unit. 16.38 Ensure individual decontamination is maintained in ope

rational state to be used for emergency personnel decontamination. 17. General consideration for the use of the Face Piece : 17.1 The Promask face piece is designed to protect the eyes and face and respiratory system in an environment requiring air purifying protection . 17.2 The respirator must not be used where oxygen is deficit less than 19. 5 atmospheres .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 394 17.3 The face piece is intended to seal against the user’s face and allows the user to breath the air drawn directly through the filtration media (negative pressure) or through breathing tube assembly (positive pressure) . 17.4 Type : tight fitting face piece with adjustable five - point head harness . 17.5 Material : butyl rubber 17.6 Size : regular and small 17.7 Limitations of use of the face piece: 17.7.1 It may not be used by personnel with facial hair that obstructs the seal of the face piece and users face or have any other condition which would prevent a good face seal. 17.7.2 Do not used with eye glass 17.7.3 Do not use if the user fails to fit the respirator and face piece to his/her face. 17.7.4 Do not use if any components are missing, damaged or worn . 17.8 Use of the facemask : before use, verify that the face piece is complete and not damaged. 17.8.1 Inspect the following: 17.8.1.1 Face piece condition 17.8.1.2 Head harness condition, buckles, and roller 17.8.1.3 Visor condition and cleanliness 17.8.1.4 Integral nose cup condition and intactness including inhalation valve 17.8.1.5 Speech diaphragm condi

tion 17.8.2 After use: 17.8.2.1 Decontaminate the mask as per manufacturer’s instruction 17.8.2.2 Dry the face mask and wipe it properly 17.8.2.3 Check the face piece for any damage, missing or malfunction components 17.8.2.4 Store the mask properly for future use 17.8.3 System assem bly (positive pressure) : 17.8.3.1 Remove the seal plugs from both ends of the breathing tube. 17.8.3.2 Inspect the valve seat inside face piece for any damage or missing. 17.8.3.3 Thread the male end of breathing tube into the female thread of the face piece until hand tight . 17.8.3.4 Inspec t the gasket inside of the female end of breathing tube, do not use if the gasket is damaged or missing . 17.8.3.5 Attach the female end of breathing tube into the blower assembly until hand tight . 17.8.4 System assembly (negative pressure) : 17.8.4.1 Check the cartridge for expirat ory date 17.8.4.2 If protective seal is damaged do not use the cartridge 17.8.4.3 The presence of snub cup is important

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 395 17.8.4.4 Check the filtration media at the facemask and the blanking plug must be correctly connected to the face piece. 17.8.4.5 Remove the protective seal of the filter 17.8.4.6 Thread the filtration media to the left or right air inlet connector of the face mask until a hand tight seal is achieved. 17.8.4.7 Ensure the blanking plug is installed in the opposite air inlet connector. 17.8.5 Donning of the facemask : 17.8.5.1 Loosen the head harness . 17.8.5.2 D on the face mask by first placing the jaw end on your face, then pull the head harness over the head. 17.8.5.3 Starting with the lower straps on both sides, adjust the head harness evenly so that the cradle lies centred on the back of the head. 17.8.5.4 Fit check the face m ask for proper seal by closing the air inlet opening by hand, create a vacuum to make the mask press onto user face if does not press readjust the head harness. 17.8.6 Doffing of the facemask : 17.8.6.1 Loosen the head harness 17.8.6.2 Pull the head harness over the head 17.8.6.3 After use, the face mask must be decontaminated, maintained, and stored properly. 17.8.7 Failure to fit the respi

rator and face piece to the user may result in limited or no respiratory protection and may expose the wearer to substances which can cause serious personal inj ury or death. 18. Water heater storage : 18.1 The water heater injection system should be drained, cleaned, and dried after each use before storage . 18.2 If it is to be stored more than two months drain fuel from the burner unit. 18.3 Solution unit: 18.3.1 Empty and wash the solutio n container, fill with clean water 18.3.2 Open the wash output valve and flush the system with clean water 3 - 5 minutes. 18.3.3 Disconnect the feed tube from the container 18.3.4 Empty and dry the solution container 18.3.5 Reconnect the fuel tube to the container 18.4 Blow out procedure : 18.4.1 Disconnect water supply 18.4.2 Open rinse valve (lower right hand with blue tip) 18.4.3 Close wash valve (lower left hand with red tip) 18.4.4 Connect air supply with proper air fitting to air valve on the water supply line. 18.4.5 Allow sufficient time for water to drain out of the rinse valve until no visible flow is present.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 396 18.4.6 Open wash valve (lower left - hand side with red tip) 18.4.7 Open filter valve 18.4.8 Close rinse valve (lower right - hand side with blue tip) 18.4.9 Allow sufficient time for water to drain out of the wash valve until no v isible flow is present. 18.5 Solution Injector : 18.5.1 Unscrew the small black retaining nut at the button of the injection system . 18.5.2 Carefully pull down and remove the fuel tube assembly from the injection system. 18.5.3 Depress the black button on top of the blue injector housing; allow all water to drain. 18.5.4 Remove water from the fuel tube assembly, grasp the solution container and twirl it rapidly in a circular motion (do in care). 18.5.5 Unscrew the blue body cover form the injection, clean, and dry the interior of the ho using and exposed piston. 18.5.6 Reattach tube assembly to injection system 18.6 Burner unit : 18.6.1 Rinse with clean water sparingly and wipe down with clean water . 18.6.2 Do not put water over or inside the heater chamber . 18.6.3 Allow water heater to dry for 24 hours in an open area . 18.6.4 If necessary, to purge all fuel from the burner, follow thes

e steps: 18.6.5 Disconnect the feed tube inside the fuel can from the container cap assembly. 18.6.6 Run the heater until the burner shuts down. 18.7 SAFETY NOTES: 18.7.1 Never put hands or face over the exhaust opening on the Heater Stack. 18.7.2 Never put any item or material down into the Heater Stack. 18.7.3 Never touch the Heater Stack Shroud or Heat Chamber during burner operation. 18.7.4 Always lift the Heater by its handles and hand holders over the wheels. 18.7.5 Always pack and sto re the Heater in an upright position, never on its side. 18.7.6 Never adjust the set points on the Burner Thermostat controller. 18.7.7 Never cover or limit operation of the Pressure Relief Valve during operation .
