Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 399

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 400 SOP 4.11 . A .1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11 . A .1 DAILY LIFEFLIGHT OP ERATIONS Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMC AS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding daily operations from Al Udeid Air Base, when participating in the LifeFlight Program . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or aeroplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar. 2. The Consultant Effe ctiveness will monitor staff members’ compliance with this procedure . 3. Leaving the LifeFlight hangar at Doha Air Base : 3.1 Do not leave the hangar premises while on active duty unless deploying on a mission/ tasking . 3.2 In extremis, one crew member may be permitte d to leave the hangar and go to the maintenance hangar if necessary. 3.2.1 Always inform the second crew member, the pilots and the flight coordin

ator and always remain contactable by radio and mobile phone. 3.2.2 One crew member shall remain in the hangar at all tim es to ensure no delay in flight activation. 3.3 In the event of an aircraft being withdrawn from service due to maintenance, equipment & crew shortages, or weather; 3.3.1 Notify the Consultant Effectiveness and SME: LifeFlight immediately . 3.3.2 The Medical Flight Crew sh all be deployed on the LifeFlight Charlie response vehicle for the remainder of their shift or until the aircraft is made available for service. 3.3.3 Under such circumstances, staff are permitted to leave Al Udeid base to fulfil their duty subject to approval by the Consultant Effectiveness . 4. Standing down of aircraft : 4.1 In the event of the aircraft being stood down due to maintenance issues, staffing issues or weather, the following procedure is to be followed: 4.1.1 Notify the Consultant Effectiveness, SME: LifeFlight immediately and the Senior Operations Manager (SOM) on call, who shall subsequently notify the Executive Team Member on call . 4.1.2 Notify the Flight Coordinator to update the dispatch system in NCC. 4.2 Aircraft out - of - service (OOS) due to inclement weather for mor

e than one (1) hour : 4.2.1 The crew from the 2nd on - call helicopter shall deploy the LifeFlight Charlie unit until such time that the weat her improves . 4.2.2 The Flight Coordinator shall obtain regular updates from the pilots as well as from online weather services every 30 minutes and record this information on the Flight Coordinator report. 4.2.3 One helicopter shall always remain in service in case o f an Offshore or International Retrieval mission .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 401 4.3 Aircraft OOS due to repairs and maintenance: 4.3.1 The crew shall man the LifeFlight Charlie unit until such time that the aircraft is reported by the Maintenance Engineers to be back in service . 4.3.2 The Flight Coord inator shall get updates from the pilots on an hourly basis and update the Consultant Effectiveness, who shall subsequently update the SOM on call . 4.3.3 Should the aircraft be out of service for consecutive shifts, duty staff should alternate CHLF deployments t o distribute the duties fairly . 4.4 Aircraft OOS due to staffing issues: 4.4.1 Make all attempts to find replacement medical staff or pilots to fulfil the necessary staff shortage. 4.4.2 In the case of medical crew shortage, the on - duty team member shall be reassigned to other duties, as deemed appropriate by the Consultant Effectiveness, until the crew shortage has been resolved. 4.4.3 In the case of pilot shortage, the medical crew will man the LifeFlight Charlie ground unit until a replacement pilot is found . 5. Re - fuelling of A ircraft : 5.1 Standard refuelling of the aircraft: 5.1.1 Shall be conducted by the Maintenance Engineer

s in conjunction with the Qatar Emiri Air Force (QEAF) refuelling staff at Doha Air Base . 5.1.2 During this time, the aircraft shall be shut down and all staff will remai n outside of the aircraft. 5.2 Refuelling of the aircraft with LifeFlight crew on board, with engines running: 5.2.1 Both medical crews shall exit the aircraft in communication with the pilots and assist in safely securing the aircraft during refuelling. 5.2.2 Once refuel ling is complete, medical crews shall enter the aircraft under the direction of the pilots. 5.3 Refuelling of the aircraft with a patient on board: 5.3.1 The door closest to the fuel tank shall remain closed and the door opposite it shall be unlatched but remain closed due to noise and fumes. 5.3.2 At least one medical crew (appropriate to the level of medical care required for the patient) shall be unrestrained and remain with the patient at all times. 5.3.3 The second medical crew shall be outside the aircraft to ass ist in securing the aircraft during refuelling. 5.3.4 Entry and exit of the aircraft shall be under direction of the pilots. 5.3.5 Due to the high risks involved when refuelling with a patient on board the helicopter, inform the F
light Coordinator immediately to notif y the SME: LifeFlight as well as the SOM on call .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 402 SOP 4.11.A.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.2 HELICOPTER OPERATIONS AT HAMAD GENERAL HOSPITAL Implementation Date 14 August 2018 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members and other role players with guidance and relevant information regarding the safe operation of multiple helicopters approaching Hamad General Hospital (HGH) simultaneously. Responsibility ALL LIFEFLIGHT STAFF & OTHER HELICOPTER OPERATORS 1. ABBREVIATIONS : 1.1 FATO: Final Approach and Take - Off area 1.2 HE : (HOTEL ECHO) Hamad Emergency West elevated helipad 1.3 HG : (HOTEL GOLF) Hamad General East elevated helipad 1.4 HGH : Hamad General Hospital 1.5 HMCAS : Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service 1.6 NCC : National Command Centre, State of Qatar 1.7 Stand : A parking area for aircraft at a heliport 2. This procedure shall apply to any situation or instance where more than one helicopter i s approaching Hamad General Hospital with the intention of delivering patients to or transporting patients from the hospital. 3. Any helicopter operator i

n the State of Qatar wishing to utilise the helipads at HGH shall comply with this procedure. 4. The SME: Li feFlight will be responsible for monitoring compliance with the Standard Operating Procedure. 5. The LifeFlight Flight Coordinator in the NCC shall coordinate all helicopter operations into and out of HE. 6. HE shall be the default helipad for ALL helicopter tra ffic. 7. HE has 1 FATO and 1 Stand. The first aircraft to land at HE, will vacate the FATO to the helicopter Stand. 8. The LifeFlight Flight Coordinator shall in the first instance request a ground ambulance to be dispatched timeously to the correct helipad fro m the HCC Dispatcher to assist the flight crew with the transfer of the patient to Hamad Hospital Facilities. 9. Should the HCC report that there is no available IFT resources, the LifeFlight Flight Coordinator shall in the second instance request a ground am bulance from the NCC Dispatcher to assist with the patient transfer. 10. As per the LifeFlight HOTEL ECHO Helipad Activation Process, Romeo 1 shall be informed by either the HCC or NCC Dispatcher to support the responding ground ambulance to access the helipad . See Appendix 4.11.A.2 . 11. The LifeFlight

Flight Coordinator shall communicate with the responding ground ambulance personnel to don full personal protective equipment and not to exit the waiting area without instruction from the Flight Crew. 12. The Flight Coordinator shall ensure that approved ground fire safety procedures are activated for every helicopter landing and take - off at designated helipads .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 403 SOP 4.11 .A.3 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.3 HELICOPTER OPERATIN G SITES Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding helicopter operating sites within the State of Qatar when participating in the LifeFlight Program. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program. 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff members’ compliance with this procedure . 3. Day Operations: 3.1 All designated Helicopter Operating Sites (HOS) shall be available for use unless prio r ‘out of service’ notification has been given or the sites are marked for restricted access. See SOP Appendix 4.7.B - 1 . 3.2 Adhere to pre - notification for these sites, as stated in the HOS document. 4. Night Operations: 4.1 Only dedicated night HOS shall be utilized as stated in the relevant HOS document. 4.2 Adhere to pre - not

ification for these sites, as stated in the HOS document. 5. HOS Document: 5.1 The HOS document shall be updated regularly and distributed by SME: Life Flight whenever changes to the status of HOS sites occur or new sites are added . 5.2 Any additional HOS shall be decided upon by senior management and either accepted or rejected in accordance with HOS standards and approval by the relevant stakeholders .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 404 SOP 4.11.A.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.4 FLIGHT FOLL OWING Implementation Date 14 August 2018 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding communications, reporting and emergency procedures during flight following, when participating in the LifeFlight Program. Responsibility ALL AEROMEDICAL STAF F 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program. 2. In conjunction with key stakeholders from partner agencies and HMCAS National Command Centre Operations Officer, the SME: LifeFlight is responsible for ensuring that a robust system of flight following is maintained. 3. This procedure interfaces with the Qatar Emiri Air Force (QEAF) Operating Procedure 60 – 2 and Qatar Air Traffic Control procedures. 4. On a day - to - day basis, the SME: LifeFlight shall delegate the responsibili ty of flight following to the Flight Coordinator. 5. Definitions : 5.1 Landing Zone (LZ) : Helicopter Landing S

ite set up on an ad hoc basis, close to the scene of an incident. 5.2 Helicopter Operating Site (HOS) : Permanent, not purpose - built structure pre - designated for use as a temporary Helicopter Landing Site as and when required e.g. sports pitches and vehicle weigh stations. 5.3 Heliport : Permanent structure, purpose - built for helicopter landing, take - off, and related activities. 5.4 NCC : National Command Centre of the State of Qatar containing the primary communications centre for the HMCAS. 5.5 Lost Communications : The loss of the primary means of communication between the NCC and an aircraft for a period of 5 minutes or more. 5.6 Flight Following : The process of tracking the movement and activity of an aircraft from activation through to end of the mission. 5.7 Aircraft Overdue : The time at which 15 minutes have elapsed since the ETA at a given location or reporting time. 5.8 Incident/Accident Procedure : The actions to b e taken when an aircraft is classed as overdue or is anticipated to be overdue as a result of other evidence (e.g. failure to establish communications using the ‘lost communications’ procedure and/or evidence from SKYTRAC satellite tracking system su

ggesti ng that aircraft has been subject to an incident or accident). 5.9 LifeFlight Flight Coordinator (FC) : The Flight Coordinator operates in conjunction with the HMCAS Dispatch team to provide a seamless service on a 24 - hour basis. 6. Actions at commencement of shif t : 6.1 The Pilot In Command (PIC) shall inform all aircraft crew of pertinent Notices to Airmen (NOTAMs) as part of the shift brief. 6.2 A roster of Persons on Board (POB) shall be transmitted to the FC.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 405 7. LifeFlight Tasking Likelihood : 7.1 Zulu case (as per dispatch ma trix) within agreed operational geographical response area. 7.2 HMCAS Ground Unit requesting assistance from LifeFlight aircraft. 7.3 Major Incident Response 7.4 Non - emergency events such as training exercises and Public Relations events, etc., based on the submission of a formal Helicopter Tasking Request. 8. Emergency Call and Dispatch : 8.1 LifeFlight shall be dispatched to emergency calls as per dispatch matrix ( SOP 3.3 ). 8.2 The FC shall dispatch available LifeFlight assets accordingly, as detailed below. 8.3 During the call receipt and dispatch phase, all relevant information shall be captured within the existing Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) system. 9. Start - up and take off of the LifeFlight Aircraft : 9.1 Maintain a ‘sterile cockpit’ throughout the start - up and take - off phase. 9.2 Irrespective of the nature of the mission, once the decision has been made to task a helicopter, the following actions shall be taken: 9.2.1 Flight Coordinator : 9.2.1.1 Notify QEAF Engineering personnel of tasking 9.2.1.2 Confirm location and Grid Referen

ce of the patient/incident/event to which the aircraft is being dispatched. 9.2.1.3 Notify aircraft of Grid Reference of incident/event 9.2.1.4 Notify Air Traffic Control of tasking 9.2.1.5 Logs time of aircraft departure from Base and during mission. 9.2.1.6 Notify appropria te personnel at Heliports/HOS that are likely to be used (enables the appropriate action to be taken and agencies to be present). 9.2.2 Aircraft Crew : 9.2.2.1 Transmit ‘start - up’ radio call 9.2.2.2 Transmit ‘taxi’ radio call 9.2.2.3 Transmit ‘lifting’ radio call 10. Aircraft Airborne : 10.1 Once the aircraft is airborne the following actions shall be taken: 10.1.1 Aircraft Crew : 10.1.1.1 Transmit ‘airborne’ radio call, including ETA, fuel endurance and POB. 10.1.1.2 Transmit flight report every 10 minutes – to include Position, ETA and ‘Ops Normal’ message (or details of any variance from ‘Ops normal’). 10.1.1.3 Transmit ‘scene visual’ radio call once on station over the destination LZ/ scene. 10.1.1.4 Establish communications with Ground Unit(s) as appropriate using TETRA Network on the ‘AirMed - 1’ talk group.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 406 10.1.2 Flight Coordinator : 10.1.2.1 Log details of all calls, flight reports and log SKYTRAC information every 10 minutes. 10.1.2.2 Transmit details of LZ/HOS including hazards, suggested direction of approach, etc. to the aircraft. 10.1.2.3 Transmit relevant details of patient/incident to the ai rcraft. 10.1.3 Coordinating Ground Unit (usually a Delta call - sign) : 10.1.3.1 Establish communications with Aircraft on ‘AirMed 1’ talk group. 10.1.3.2 Notify aircraft of hazards, wind direction, possible approach etc. (either by direct transmission or relayed through the FC). 10.1.3.3 Inform of location of rendezvous points. 10.1.3.4 Coordinate ground transport movement at the scene (in conjunction with other agencies). 10.1.3.5 Transmit updates from scene as necessary. 11. Final Approach, Landing and Loading : 11.1 Maintain a ‘sterile cockpit’ throughout the fin al approach and landing phase however, radio silence may be broken if critical, life - saving information need to be communicated (e.g. unsafe LZ conditions whilst the aircraft is on short finals). 11.2 The aircraft crew shall transmit the following radio calls

a s appropriate and shall be logged by the FC: 11.2.1 ‘Finals’ 11.2.2 ‘Landed’ 11.2.3 ‘Hot Load’ or ‘Shutting Down’ 12. Subsequent mission activities : 12.1 For the remainder of the mission, all subsequent take - off, flight legs and landings shall be managed using the reporting/logging pro cedures highlighted at sections 6 - 8 above. In addition to this, the following actions shall be taken: 12.1.1 Flight Coordinator Actions : 12.1.1.1 Transmit details of subsequent LZ/HOS Sites/Heliport to be used. 12.1.1.2 Record take - off time from scene/pick up point. 12.1.1.3 Record landing time at drop off point/receiving facility. 12.1.1.4 Record take - off time from drop off point/receiving facility. 12.1.1.5 Record landing time back at base. 12.1.1.6 Record the time the helicopter is available again (after refuelling & restocking). 12.1.2 Gain consent to transport . 12.1.2.1 The patient, or a relative with authority to act on their behalf, must give their consent to flight. 12.1.2.2 Companions to paediatric patients are permitted following a risk assessment by the Pilot in Command in collaboration with the medical crew.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 407 12.1.2.3 Where a patient or relative refuses to be flown against the advice of the senior clinician present, they are to sign the HMCAS ePCR to indicate refusal. Refer to SOP 4.8.D . They may be discharged at scene or be transported to rec eiving facility by a Ground Unit if flight is refused. 12.1.2.4 In the case of violent/combative patients or aggressive companions, a risk assessment shall be made by the Pilot in Command in collaboration with the medical crew. Transport may be refused if the patie nt is deemed to pose a threat to the aircraft and/or crew. 12.1.2.5 Under no circumstances will any patients with known communicable infections/diseases be transported in any LifeFlight aircraft. 12.1.3 Notification of Receiving Facility : 12.1.3.1 Refer to SOP 4.8.A for selecting appropriate receiving health facilities. 12.1.3.2 The FC shall notify the receiving facility (either directly or via the NCC Clinical Desk) of the patient’s details and other pertinent information, including in - flight updates and shall co nfirm bed availability. 12.1.3.3 Aircraft crew shall transmit any changes to current ETA, fuel endurance a

nd POB as changes occur. 13. End of Mission : 13.1 The mission ends once the aircraft has returned to base and is shut down. 13.2 When an aircraft is re - tasked before returni ng to base, end of mission is considered to be the time at which the aircraft takes off from its last landing point, or the time at which the aircraft switches to the new mission (if no patient is on board). 14. Incident and Accident : 14.1 In the event of an aircra ft incident (lost communications and aircraft overdue) or an aircraft accident ( See Figure 1 below ): 14.1.1 If 5 minutes after contact window has expired, the FC shall : 14.1.1.1 Attempt to contact the aircraft using alternative communications means (aircraft GSM telephone, aircraft Satellite phone, SkyMail application, aircrew personal mobile phones, HMCAS resources on scene at aircraft’s destination). 14.1.1.2 Simultaneously check SKYTRAC and inform Operations Officer: NCC. 14.1.1.3 Notify the SME: LifeFlight with the following br ief: 14.1.1.3.1 Aircraft identification 14.1.1.3.2 Last known position 14.1.1.3.3 Direction of Travel 14.1.1.3.4 Names of POB 14.1.1.3.5 Phase of mission and last fuel endurance reported 14.1.1.3.6 Actions taken to

locate 14.1.2 If 15 minutes after contact window has expired the FC shall : 14.1.2.1 Inform destination LZ and confirm any visual contact with aircraft. 14.1.2.2 Notify the SME: LifeFlight and Operations Officer: NCC.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 408 14.1.2.3 Dispatch second call LifeFlight aircraft for aerial reconnaissance of last known position as per SKYTRAC. 14.1.2.4 Under guidance from the SME: LifeFlight, notify the following agencies: 14.1.2.4.1 Qatar Armed Forces General Headquarters (GHQ). 14.1.2.4.2 QEAF Air Operations Command (AOC) – requesting Search and Rescue activation. 14.1.2.4.3 Qatar Police Department 14.1.2.4.4 Qatar Civil Defence 14.1.2.4.5 Air Traffic Control Services at both Doha International Airport and Hamad International Airport. 14.1.2.4.6 Qatar Civil Aviation Authority 14.1.2.5 Upon notification and briefing, the SME: LifeFlight shall notify the HMCAS Executive Management team and brief them accordingly. 15. Aircraft Accident/Prolonged Overdue Procedure : 15.1 When an accident is confirmed, or an aircraft has been overdue for a period of more than thirty minutes, the following action shall be taken : 15.1.1 Compile hard copy transcripts of all reports, recordings and documentation relating to the incident/accident to include the following : 15.1.1.1 ProQA screenshot printouts 15.1.1.2 CAD screenshot printouts 15.1.1.3 SKYTRAC tracking

logs of the last mission for the relevant aircraft. 15.1.1.4 Recordings of all data and voice transmissions as well as relevant telephone calls. 15.1.1.5 Relevant screenshots from the Qatar Geo - Portal Ambulance Response System. 15.1.1.6 POB roster with emergency contact details for relatives. 15.1.2 An immediate blanket embargo shall be imposed on ALL personnel making unofficial communication (written, spoken and electronic) to the public (including friends and family) and the media (including TV companies, radio companies, newspapers, social media, w ebsites, and blogs). 15.2 Once wreckage has been discovered, response to the incident shall take place as appropriate . The QEAF shall lead scene management and investigation. 15.3 Where Next of Kin are resident outside the State of Qatar, notification of incident wi ll be facilitated via the staff member’s Embassy. 15.4 Once Next of Kin have been informed, the Executive Director: HMCAS will make a statement to the media followed by further statements to the HMCAS internal audience. 16. Incident/Accident Drills : 16.1 Action drills s hall be conducted on a quarterly basis to practice the Incident/Accident Plan and shall involve the
following stakeholders: 16.1.1 Pilots 16.1.2 Medical Crew
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 409 16.1.3 NCC Personnel 16.1.4 QEAF representatives 16.1.5 HMC AS Administrative Personnel

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 411 SOP 4.11 .A.5 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.5 LIFEFLIGHT CREW CON FIGURATION & SCHEDUL ING Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMC AS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding the configuration of LifeFlight crew and scheduling when participating in the LifeFlight Program. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or aeroplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar . 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff fitness levels as well as their general health and compliance with this procedure . 3. LifeFlight Crew Configuration : 3.1 All medical flights shall maintain a minimum of two medical attendants with at least one Critical Care Paramedic (CCP ). 3.2 All LifeFlight crew shall adhere to active flight crew requirements and must be deemed fit to fly at all t

imes ( SOP 4.11.B.3 ). 3.3 In an emergency and on an unanticipated, infrequent basis, non - schedu led or ad hoc LifeFlight staff may be added as an additional medical team member only if they meet prescribed flight crew requirements ( SOP 4.11.B.2 ) and are deemed fit to fly. ( SOP 4.11.B.3 ) . 3.4 HMCAS Managers may be scheduled to work as ad hoc crew provided that they meet prescribed flight crew requirements ( SOP 4.11.B.2 ) and are deemed fit to fly . ( SOP 4.11.B.3 ) . 4. International Retrieval Missio ns : 4.1 There shall always be a minimum of two CCPs as part of the medical team. 4.2 All staff shall adhere to Flight and Duty times. ( SOP 4.11.B.3 ) . 4.3 Doctors seconded to LifeFlight for International Retrieval Missions shall: 4.3.1 Adhere to prescribed flight crew requirements ( SOP 4.11.B.2 ); 4.3.2 Complete the prescribed Specialized High Acuity Adult Retrieval training program; 4.3.3 Be deemed fit to fly ( SOP 4.11.B.3 ) . 5. Duty Schedule : 5.1 Adhere to the Flight and Duty hours stipulated in ( SOP 4.11.B.3 ). 5.2 A Day Shift shall be scheduled from 04h45 to 16h45 and a Night Shift from 16h45 to 04h45. 5.3 Do not exceed 16 hours continuous duty in a 24 - hour period

unless attending a late call - out. 5.4 The Workforce Management Department designee shall notify (in writing) all LifeFlight staff if there are any changes to shift times with a minimum of 48 hours notice. 5.5 The Workforce Management Department designee shall produce the LifeFlight roster and shall be approved by the Consultant Effectiveness. 5.6 The roster shall be completed and distributed to staff at least two weeks in advance. 5.7 A minimum of two Flight CCP’s (F - CCP) and two Flight AP’s (F - AP) shall be scheduled per shift to man the two aircraft on duty.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 412 5.8 One F - AP will be scheduled per shift to perform Flight Coordinator duties within the NCC. 5.9 Medical crew shall be scheduled on a four - day - on, four - day - off rotation. 5.10 Over - time shifts may be undertaken in line with prescribed res t periods and duty limitations. Written approval must be obtained from the Consultant Effectiveness to ensure duty restrictions are adhered to. 5.11 On request, married couples in the LifeFlight program may be scheduled in the same team. 5.12 LifeFlight crew shall b e scheduled alternating shifts on the LifeFlight aircraft and on Charlie ground units to ensure skill maintenance, as operational coverage needs dictates .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 413 SOP 4.11 .A.6 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.6 GENERAL & CLINICAL RESPONSIBILITIES DUR ING A LIFEFLIGHT SHIFT Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding general and clinical responsibilities, when participating in the LifeFlight Program. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or aeroplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar . 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff members compliance with this procedure 3. Checklists : 3.1 Complete the prescribed checklists (according to the associated technical standard) during every operational duty: 3.1.1 Complete a Shift checklist for the aircraft and/ or the LifeFlight Charlie vehicle(s) at the commencement of every 12 - hour shift period. 3.1.2 If scheduled on a day dut

y on the last Thursday of the month, complete a monthly comprehensive expiry date checklist. 3.2 Submit all checklists daily, via the predetermined checklist drop - off location or electronically to the Production Supervisor: LifeFlight . 4. Patient Care Continuity and Reporting : 4.1 Provide ethical, respectful, and confidential patient care to all patients as prescribed by the HMCAS Clinical Practice Guidelines. 4.2 Transfer of patient care from the medical team on the aircraft shall be to a medical team at the receiving hospital. 4.3 For general patient handover and en dorsement procedures, refer to SOP 4.8.B . 4.4 For appropriate recording of patient care and handling of documentation, refer to SOP 4.9.A . 4.5 Complete all other documentation included in continu ous quality and assessment as required.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 414 SOP 4.11 .A.7 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.7 DECEASED PATIENT ON LIFEFLIGHT Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding the deceased patient whilst participating in the LifeFlight program. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFEFLIG HT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or aeroplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar. 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff compliance with this procedure. 3. Death in Flight: 3.1 In the event that a death occurs during the transportation of a patient in the LifeFlight aircraft: 3.1.1 Notify the Flight Coordinator immediately who shall notify the Co nsultant Effectiveness on duty. 3.1.2 Continue flying to the receiving facility 3.1.3 Handover the deceased to the necessary staff at hospital as per SOP 4.8.B . 4. Deceas

ed patient on scene with no medical intervention by HMCAS/L ifeFlight: 4.1 In the event that there is a deceased patient on scene who did not receive any medical intervention, follow standard HMCAS procedures. 4.2 The Police shall remove the deceased from the scene. 4.3 In rare instances such as a deceased patient in an offsho re location, refer to the SME: LifeFlight or Consultant Effectiveness for advice. 5. Deceased patient on scene with medical intervention by HMCAS/LifeFlight: 5.1 In the event that a patient has received medical interventions by HMC AS and/or LifeFlight medical cr ew and has been declared dead on scene: 5.1.1 The ground ambulance crew shall move the deceased to hospital. 5.1.2 If required, the LifeFlight medical crew shall complete all necessary documentation and handover such to the ground ambulance crew. 5.1.3 Notify the Flight Coo rdinator if LifeFlight staff are the only medical crew on scene and await further instructions from the Consultant Effectiveness on duty .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 415 SOP 4.11 .A.8 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.8 NON - EMERGEN CY AND AD HOC LIFEFL IGHT MISSIONS Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To outline the procedures for LifeFlight helicopter operations when tasked with non - emergency, ad hoc missions including training and public relations missions. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or aeroplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar. 2. The SME; LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff compliance with this procedure . 3. Definitions: 3.1 Non - Emergency Ad Hoc Mission : Any LifeFlight mission not dispatched by the NCC as part of an emergency medical response. 3.2 Training Mission : Any mission undertak en for the purpose of training LifeFlight crew members or any other staff associated with LifeFlight operations. This also includes participation

of LifeFlight helicopters in joint training exercises with other agencies. 3.3 Event Standby Missions : Any mission undertaken to provide medical coverage for a pre - arranged event. 3.4 Public Relations Mission : Any mission undertaken in support of HMCAS Public Relations Department or independently to promote the LifeFlight program. 3.5 Ad Hoc Landing Zone : Any Landing Zone not used as part of the normal operations of the LifeFlight program. These are typically located close to the event, exercise or PR campaign and are typically unmarked. 4. Requests : 4.1 All requests for the involvement/participation of LifeFlight helicopters or crew in Public Relations or marketing activities shall be directed, in writing, to the Executive Director: HMCAS. 4.2 All other requests for involvement/participation of LifeFlight helicopters or crew must be directed, in writing, to the SME: LifeFlight. 4.3 These req uests shall be reviewed subject to the following criteria: 4.3.1 Relevance 4.3.2 Aircraft availability 4.3.3 Staff availability 4.3.4 Safety restrictions 5. Authorizations : 5.1 The Executive Director: HMCAS shall authorize all non - emergency, ad hoc missions related to public relations
or marketing. 5.2 The Assistant Executive Director of Critical Care Services shall authorize all non - emergency, ad hoc missions related to training, special events or orientation flights.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 416 6. LifeFlight Tasking : 6.1 Following receipt of a letter from the r equesting agency/organization or an internal request via the LifeFlight Tasking Request Form (Form No: HMCAS/A/LF/2), the SME: LifeFlight or a delegated representative shall: 6.1.1 Meet with the requesting agency/organization at the site and identify potential a d hoc landing zones with photos (where applicable), GPS coordinates and potential hazards around the landing zone. 6.1.2 Complete the HMCAS/A/LF/1 form 6.1.3 Authorize tasking as appropriate 6.1.4 Distribute authorized tasking (in PDF format) to predetermined distribution l ist via e - mail. 6.1.5 Brief (telephonic/in person) the Flight Commander: Training, 23 (EMS) Squadron (QEAF) where applicable. 6.1.6 Brief (telephonic/in person) the HMCAS Flight Coordinator, Pilot in Command and medical flight crew executing the tasking. 6.1.7 Confirm any a dditional arrangements (e.g. other HMCAS resource deployment, involvement of other agencies, etc.) 6.1.8 Execute tasking mission 6.1.9 Debrief (telephonic/in person) the Pilot in Command and medical flight crew involved. 6.1.10 Feedback to HMCAS Senio

r Management as and when required. 7. Radio Communications during Non - Emergency/Ad Hoc Missions : 7.1 Maintain standard radio communications and flight following between crew and Flight Coordinator. 7.2 For some special events & training exercises that require the monitoring of additional T ETRA radio channels, utilize a portable TETRA radio, bearing in mind that airborne communications on this radio may not be possible .
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 417 Form No: HMCAS/A/LF/2

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 418 SOP 4.11 .A.9 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.9 LEAVE AND DUTY EXCH ANGE ON LIFEFLIGHT Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding annual leave, sick leave, casual leave, injury on duty and duty exchanges whilst on the LifeFlight program. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program. 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff compliance with this procedure. 3. Annual Leave : 3.1 An Annual Leave Planner shall be projected by the Workforce Management Department designee and approved by Consul tant Effectiveness and Operations Manager: Critical Care Services. 3.2 No more than two CCP’s and two FAP’s shall be permitted leave within the same month to ensure adequate staff coverage for duties, unless approved by the Consultant Effectiveness. 3.3 Submit all written leave applications to the

Consultant Effectiveness no less than two months prior to leave dates requested . 3.4 The Consultant Effectiveness shall review the leave request and approve/refuse the application within 7 days. 3.5 Leave applications may be perm itted within a shorter notice period if pre - approved by the Consultant Effectiveness. 3.6 Emergency leave may be approved at short notice at the discretion of the Consultant Effectiveness. 4. Casual Leave : 4.1 Casual leave requests shall be permitted if another staff member is able to fulfil the requested duty (depending on operational availability). 4.2 A leave request form shall be submitted to the Consultant Effectiveness no less than 7 days prior to date requested. 4.3 The Consultant Effectiveness shall review the leave r equest and approve/refuse the application within 2 days. 5. Sick Leave: 5.1 Please refer to general sick leave procedures in SOP 1.3 . 5.2 Staff shall inform the Workforce Management Department designee of the outcome of their visit i.e. fit for work or booked off duty and expected duration. 6. Injury on Duty : 6.1 Immediately notify the Flight Coordinator if an injury has been sustained whilst on duty. 6.2 The Flight Coordinator
shall immediately inform the Consultant Effectiveness and the Operations Manager: CCS on call. 6.3 Follow sick leave procedure detailed in SOP 1.3 .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 419 7. Duty Exchanges : 7.1 No more than three duty exchanges per month shall be permitted. 7.2 Duty exchanges shall not be permitted for the shift cycle immediately before o r after approved annual leave dates. 7.3 Duty exchange forms shall be completed and submitted electronically to the Consultant Effectiveness with a minimum of 3 days notice prior to the shift requested 7.4 The Consultant Effectiveness shall approve/refuse the requ est and notify the Workforce Management Department accordingly .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 420 SOP 4.11 .A.10 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.10 MANAGEMENT OF BARIA TRIC PATIENTS BY LIF EFLIGHT Implementation Date 24 October 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information to facilitate appropriate transportation and triage of bariatric patients by LifeFlight . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or aeroplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar. 2. Whilst there are varied definitions of Bariatric; in the Aeromedical environment, the classification is applied to any person whose weight or physical dimensions exceed the capability of standard equ ipment used by the HMC Ambulance Service. 3. The procedure ensures that the Medical Flight Crew evaluates the capabilities of the stretcher system being used in the LifeFlight aircraft to determine its appropriateness for each

patient’s transportation. 4. Due to the logistical challenges with managing these patients, these missions are commonly longer than the non - bariatric transportation. 5. This procedure should be read in conjunction with the Transfer and Retrieval Services Bariatric Transfers SOP 4.8.T . 6. LifeFlight Bariatric Patient Decision Tool: 6.1 The purpose of the tool is to provide the LifeFlight Medical Crew with adequate information as to the configuration and capabilities within the patient compartment of the AW139 aircraft. 6.2 Maximum patient length – consider the actual length of the aircraft stretcher: 1980mm. 6.3 Maximum patient shoulder width – ensure that the patient can be secured in the aircraft and does not present an egress hazard to the crew (see fig A). 6.3.1 Figure A 6.4 Maximum length of safety belts – make conservative judgements when securing the patient onto the stretcher (see fig B ).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 421 6.5 Weight and balance calculations are absolute to ensure Category - A performance at elevated heliports and patient dimensions should not compromise flight safety . 6.6 The Pilot in Command assumes final authority for all safety and operational decisions . 6.6.1 Figure B 7. Bariatric Unit Activation: 7.1 Patients exceeding the equipment or aircraft limitations should be referred to the National Command Centre for Bariatric Unit dispatch (as per SOP 4.8.T ). 7.2 Whilst awaiting the appropriate assistance, the patient must receive all necessary care and reassurance as outlined in the HMCAS Clinical Practice G uidelines. 7.3 The patient's delay in transportation must be professionally communicated, explaining the safety limitation within the aircraft, with the patient or responsible person .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 422 SOP 4.11 .A.11 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11 . A.11 LIFEFLIGHT HOT LOAD ING PROCEDURE Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding LifeFlight hot loading procedures. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program. 2. The procedure outlines fundamental safety considerations when undertaking Hot Load activities. 3. The Flight CCP shall be responsible for ensuring overall scene safety and compliance with the Hot Load procedure. 4. The SME: will be responsible for monitoring staff c ompliance with this procedure. 5. Normal operating procedure shall be to load and unload patients with the aircraft engines shut down. 6. Definition of Hot Load : In certain circumstances, it might be necessary to load a patient while the rotors are turning, and the Pilot in Command maintains power to the main and tail rotors. This i

s called a “Hot Load” and may need to be performed due to: 6.1 Low aircraft voltage after a short transit 6.2 Low ambient temperature which may affect restart 6.3 The high ambient temperature requ iring a long period (25 to 30 minutes) for the engines to cool sufficiently to be restarted. 7. The left side stretcher will be the default Hot Load stretcher, unless circumstances dictate otherwise and have been agreed by the crew prior to landing. 8. Upon arri val at the designated landing zone, the LifeFlight CCP shall exit the aircraft and go outside the rotor disk to the on - scene personnel while the pilot maintains power to the rotors. 8.1 The LifeFlight AP shall exit the aircraft to the 2 o’ clock position outsi de the rotor disc to provide safety, with direct sight of the tail rotor and provide information to the Pilot in Command. 8.2 Should the Flight AP be required to assist the patient, the Non - Flying Pilot will exit the aircraft to the 10 o’ clock position to pro vide scene safety. 8.3 At all times, there shall be communication between the Pilots and medical crew utilizing the Polycon® or TETRA® radio systems. 8.4 No civilian bystanders will approach or assist in transferri

ng the patient to the aircraft. 8.5 Loading and unload ing must be carried out under the supervision of the Non - Flying Pilot. Great care must be taken in these circumstances to ensure that in the "heat of the moment" loading personnel and/or onlookers do not approach the tail rotor without supervision. 8.6 Only ap propriately briefed HMC Ambulance Service personnel with appropriate PPE (eye and hearing protection) will assist with the manual handling of the patient to the aircraft. 8.7 The duty medical flight crew shall ensure that all patients, whenever possible, wear hearing protection. (As a general rule, patients will not be on the headset intercom system). 9. Due to risk of injury, Pilots should not participate with the lifting of patients. If Pilot assistance becomes necessary however, then only the Co - Pilot should assist.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 423 10. In certain circumstances, it may be necessary to land and deploy the medical crew and primary response equipment. The aircraft will then take off and remain in a safe orbit or reposition the aircraft to a safe location away from the scene, a waiting further instructions. This procedure may need to be performed due to: 10.1 Unpredictable traffic or bystander conditions 10.2 Unstable landing surface 10.3 Remote location of scene from acceptable access

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 424 SOP 4.11 .A.12 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Servic e Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.12 LIFEF LIGHT CONFIGURATION & PASSENGER SAFETY Implementation Date 24 October 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information on the aircraft configuration and passenger safety information for operational service with LifeFlight. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. Medical Flight Crew members and any HMC staff member seeking to serve on LifeFlight will comply with this procedure. 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff flight crew status and compliance with this procedure. 3. Aircraft Configuration : 3.1 1 - Patient configuration : (Staff orientation, Retrieval mission or Offs hore deployment): 3.1.1 Consists of 1 aircraft stretcher and 2 attendant seats as a minimum. 3.1.2 As per the supplement to the AB/AW 139 Rotorcraft Flight Manual, up to 4 attendant seats may be accommodated. 3.2 2 - Patient Configuration : (Routine LifeFlight Operational Model): 3.2.1 Consists of 2 aircraft stretcher

s and 2 attendant seats as a maximum. 3.2.2 As per the supplement to the AB/AW 139 Rotorcraft Flight Manual, up to 2 attendant seats may be accommodated. 3.2.3 LifeFlight operations shall not transport more than 1 patient in the 1 - patient configuration, or more than 2 patients in the 2 - patient configuration. 4. Passenger/Patient Safety Briefing : 4.1 LifeFlight Medical Crew shall provide a safety briefing to all passengers and competent patients prior to departure including: 4.1.1 S eatbelts (o peration and fastened for take - off, taxiing & landing) 4.1.2 A ction in case of passenger discomfort 4.1.3 F ire Extinguisher (location and operation) 4.1.4 E mergencies (exit and evacuation plan) 4.1.5 T alking (patients will generally not be on headset, use of ear defenders for excess noise) 4.1.6 Y our questions (encourage patient/passenger to speak if they feel ill or nervous) 4.1.7 Use quick reference card for hot loading or language barrier. 5. Patient Escorts and Accompaniment : 5.1 Escorts are not routinely transported unless in e xceptional circumstances following a risk assessment by the Pilot in Command in collaboration with the medical crew. 5.2 Deviation from standard oper
ating procedure requires escalation to the NCC and SME: LifeFlight.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 425 5.3 Companions to paediatric patients are permi tted following a risk assessment by the Pilot in Command in collaboration with the medical crew .
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 426

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 427 SOP 4.11 .A.13 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.11.A.13 LATEX ALLERGY PRECA UTIONS Implementation Date 24 October 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with the guidance and relevant information regarding latex allergies of staff and patients. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL STAF F 1. Staff and latex allergy : 1.1 All staff shall notify their immediate supervisor of any personal allergies, including latex allergy. 1.2 A record of staff allergies shall be noted in their personnel files. 1.3 Staff with latex allergies shall inform fellow crew members to prevent accidental latex contact during the shift . 2. Patients and latex allergy : 2.1 Obtain an appropriate history during routine clinical assessment to identify the risk of latex allergy. 2.2 A latex allergy shall be noted by all medical staff managing t he patient and annotated on the ePCR and handed over to hospital staff on arrival at the hospital prior to any staff being in contact with the patient. 2.3 When possible, every effort must be made to obtain an allergy histor

y for unconscious patients. 3. Equipmen t : 3.1 A list of equipment containing latex shall be made available to staff and in - patient treating vehicles, including aircraft. 3.2 Latex free gloves shall be available for use by staff with latex allergy and on patients with latex allergy. 3.3 All staff shall be v igilant of which equipment is safe to use on patients with latex allergy. 3.4 Be prepared and follow the relevant Clinical Practice Guideline in the event of a latex allergic reaction.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 428 SOP 4.11 .A.14 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.A.14 LIFEFLIGHT OFFSHORE ACTIVATION AND OPERA TIONS Implementation Date 13 March 2018 Purpose To provide LifeFlight personnel with safety performance goals necessary to assure safe offshore helicopter operations. The framework is aligned with QEAF (Qatar Emiri Air Force) standards for safe mission pre - flight planning and preparation . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. Background and Purpose : 1.1 This regularly receives requests to transport patients from offshore areas including oil rigs, vessels and islands to hospital. Due to the limited access, these cases are serviced by LifeFlight. 1.2 This SOP provides the procedures for LifeFlight offshore missions activation and coordination specific the LifeFlight Coordinator and operational LifeFlight staff and outlines fundamental pre - flight planning and safety considerations when undertaking offshore operations. 1.3 This SOP should be re ad together with SOP 3.24 which sets out the procedures relatin

g to the NCC call - taking and dispatch of offshore cases. 2. Definitions : 2.1 A7S stands for “Alpha 7 Sierra” which is the Joint Operations Centre operated by Qatar Energy and, among other disaster response responsibilities, is responsible for coordinating offshore medical evacuations. 2.2 LFC means LifeFlight Coordinator 2.3 DTL means Distribution Team Leader 2.4 PiC means Pilot in Command 3. LifeFlight Activation and Follow ing : 3.1 In accordance with SOP 3.24 , the LFC will be notified as soon as possible of an incoming offshore case from A7S. 3.1.1 The LFC must immediately proceed to their console, add a note in NJM that they were notified of the case, open the LifeFlight@hamad.qa mailbox and monitor the inbox for the Offshore Retrieval Request Form from A7S. 3.1.2 The LFC must advise the dispatcher of which aircraft to assign to the case. 3.1.3 The LFC must notify the assigned LifeFlight crew of the incoming case providing the general location (specific locations should only be provided once confirmed with A7S). 3.1.4 The LFC must open DaWinci and locate the vessel using the helideck code and name provided in the Offshore Retrieval Request form. The location of the vessel s

hould be monitored until LF report on - station. 3.1.5 The LFC must assess the flight conditions including weather, visibility, wind and any other conditions that may inhibit safe mission activation or impose unreasonable risk to the aircraft or crew. If necessary, the LFC should discuss any concerns with the PiC. 3.2 In accordance with SOP 3.24 , once the NCC call - taker has completed ProQA interrogation with the A7S caller, they will endorse the case to the LFC directly or through the DTL. 3.2.1 The LFC must immediately update the LF call - sign timestamp to “Received” in NJM.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 429 3.2.2 The LFC must then call A7S, request to speak to the sender of the Offshore Retrieval Request Form (as named on the form) and confirm the below details, by reading them back to the A7S caller: 3.2.2.1 Specific helideck name, code and GPS coordinates, and confirm this is the location of the patient. 3.2.2.2 Nearest refuelling point (name, code, and GPS coordinates). 3.2.2.3 Confirm if there is any infectious disease or contamination. If there is hazardous material contamination, they must confirm the patient is undergoing decontaminati on procedures and advise A7S we are unable to transport patients contaminated with hazardous materials. 3.2.3 After completing the phone call with A7S (3.2.2 above) the LFC will send the Offshore Retrieval Request Form to the LifeFlight CCA on MS Teams and conta ct the crew on Airmed 1 to dispatch the aircraft, confirming the case and ensuring the flight crew have received the Offshore Retrieval Request Form in MS Teams. 3.2.3.1 The LFC must update the NJM timestamp of the aircraft to “LF - Activated” and update the DTL. T he LFC should ensure to update NJM timestamps and notes thro

ughout the mission with all flight tracking information. 3.2.4 Once the LFC knows the ETA of the aircraft to the offshore helideck they must note it in NJM, update the DTL and call A7S back advising the m of the ETA. If necessary, the LFC may now obtain further patient information which can be provided to the medical team. 3.3 The LFC must continue to track the progress of the mission confirming mission status and aircraft location at least every 10 - minutes and continue further mission coordination following SOP 4.11.A.4. 3.3.1 Due to limited TETRA® network coverage, radio transmissions should be attempted at least every 10 (ten) minutes or when possible to indicate “Ops Normal”. Alternative communication methods, as guided by management, may be used where TETRA® network coverage is ineffective, to confirm mission and crew status. 3.3.2 The LFC shall monitor the aircraft with SKYTRAC® and confirm arrival and departure times with the Helicopter Landing Officer [HLO] of th e platform/vessel. 4. Pre - flight Planning and Aircraft Preparation : 4.1 Flight planning is the responsibility of the PiC and should include: 4.1.1 Consideration will be given to the weather, visibility and any

known hazards that may impose unnecessary risk to the missi on or crew. 4.1.2 Estimation of flight time and estimated ground idle time with consideration of refuelling requirements. 4.1.3 Refuelling, if required, should be planned for the outbound flight in which the patient is not onboard the aircraft. If this is not possibl e, the Medical Crew may disembark at the destination Helideck, allowing refuelling to occur during patient assessment and packaging. 4.2 There will be a mission safety briefing given by the PiC, or his delegate, and attended by all flight crew for the mission. 4.3 Aircraft preparation should be planned and completed as soon as notified of the offshore mission as follows: 4.3.1 For confirmed 1 (one) patient evacuation:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 430 4.3.1.1 Remove the right stretcher to ensure ease of emergency egress during aircraft ditching. 4.3.1.2 Secure the primary response bag on the right, aft of the door, with the provided cargo net. 4.3.2 For confirmed 2 (two) patient evacuation: 4.3.2.1 Ensure both stretchers are correctly installed, and the right stretcher is free of all equipment to ensure ease of emergency egress d uring aircraft ditching. 4.3.3 Life vests, for the crew, must be donned prior to boarding the aircraft. Patient life vests must be donned prior to patient loading and if possible, deployment and emergency procedures must be explained to the patient prior to boar ding the aircraft. 5. Offshore Helideck Operations : 5.1 Upon arrival at the designated landing zone, the Medical Crew shall exit the aircraft under instruction of the HLO whilst the pilot maintains power to the rotors. 5.2 The Medical Crew shall follow the instructio ns of the HLO to access the patient(s) and return to the aircraft under his supervision. 5.3 At all times, there shall be communication between the Pilots and Medical Crew utilizing the Polycon® or TETRA® radio system

s. The HLO may also relay communications be tween the Medical Crew and Pilots. 5.4 The Medical Crew must ensure the patient life vest has been donned prior to entering the aircraft. 5.5 The left stretcher will be the default Hot Load stretcher, unless circumstances dictate otherwise or as guided by the PiC. 5.6 The Medical Crew shall ensure that all patients, whenever possible, wear hearing protection. (As a general rule, patients will not be on the headset intercom system). 5.7 In certain circumstances, it may be necessary to land and deploy the Medical Crew and pr imary response equipment. The aircraft will then take - off and remain in a safe orbit or reposition to a nearby refuelling station and, when appropriate, return and collect the Medical Crew and patient(s ) .
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 431 Call Intake Process - A7S Offshore LifeFlight Cases

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 432 SOP 4.11 .A.15 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 4.11.A.15 LIFEFLIGHT SELF DISP ATCH Implementation Date 01 September 2022 Purpose To ensure appropriate activation of emergency resources, when requested directly by Military or external agencies. Responsibility ALL LIFEFLIGHT AND N CC PERSONNEL 1. HMCAS may be notified of an incident/patient directly by other agencies including but not limited to the police, civil defense, military, etc. 2. HMCAS and QEAF have agreed to self - dispatch LifeFlight at Military request to ensure rapid deployment of lifesaving assistance to all military personnel within the State of Qatar and it s territorial waters. 3. The request is facilitated by the Officer in Command of 23 Squadron of Wing 3 at Doha Air Base. The 23 Squadron OC has the authority to task the aircraft as the point of contact to ensure dispatch without undue delay. 4. This is for var ious scenarios including parachute accidents, ejection of pilots over water/land, SAR operations for downed aircraft and incidents during live - fire exerc

ises that are otherwise classified to military security clearance only. 5. As with all missions, a pre - fl ight briefing to determine the safety of all personnel for the mission must be undertaken. 6. The medical crew shall inform the LifeFlight Flight Coordinator during activation to open a CFS with information to follow. 7. ALWAYS notify the NCC when LifeFlight is responding to an incident. 8. When the Flight Coordinator receives an emergency call directly from any source other than the NCC, the following procedure applies: 8.1 Immediately notify the dispatcher of the approximate locati on of the call while proceeding to the incident. 8.2 Notify the SME: LifeFlight of the mission tasking 8.3 Notify the Operations Officer NCC, Oscar Command and the Consultant CCP Effectiveness on duty if there is a need to dispatch other HMCAS or partner agencies to the incident. 8.4 The Flight Coordinator shall undertake routine flight following and the air crew will communicate using the routine communication procedure for the various phases of flight, including: Airborne, POB, endurance and ETA. 8.5 On arrival at the sc ene, LifeFlight personnel are to follow routine procedures as if activated
directly by the NCC .
