Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 434 SOP 4.11.B : LifeFlight Health & Safety
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 435

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 436 SOP 4.11 .B.1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11 . B .1 USE OF MEDICAL CREW RESTRAINTS Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding the proper use of medical crew restraints whilst on the LifeFlight program. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program. 2. The SME: Lifeflight will be responsible for monitoring staff compliance with this procedure. 3. Operating as Medical Crew : 3.1 Medical personnel shall be secured at all times during take - off and landing. 3.2 Medical personnel shall inform the pilo ts whenever there is a situation that requires temporary removal of their restraints. 3.3 Once the task is completed, the medical personnel shall inform the pilots that they are re - secured in their restraints. 4. Operating as Crewman : 4.1 In the event of LifeFlight p ersonnel being required to perform crewman

type duties they may be permitted by the pilots to be unrestrained from the seat. 4.2 In this case, wear a safety harness that is attached to the aircraft hard point at all times during these operations. 4.3 Crewman safet y harnesses are not a substitute for the approved, installed restraining system on the helicopter seats and may never be used as a substitute restraint system.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 437 SOP 4.11 .B.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.B.2 PHYSICAL FITNESS & ASSESSMENT PROCEDURE Implementation Date 24 October 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information on the physical standards and training to be completed prior to and throughout operational service with LifeFlight. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. Medical Flight Crew members and any HMC staff member seeking to serve on LifeFlight will comply with this procedure. 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff flight crew status and compliance with this procedure . 3. Fitness Evaluation : 3.1 Any HMCAS staff member involved in or wishing to be involved in the LifeFlight Program must pass an initial f itness evaluation and a minimum of six monthly reassessments thereafter. 3.2 Fitness evaluations will be conducted every 3 months with assessment dates to be announced via staff memoranda. 3.3 LifeFlight staff shall not exceed 6 months between LifeFlight fitness c urrency evaluatio

ns. 4. Fitness Test Components : 4.1 The LifeFlight Physical Fitness Assessment is a physical assessment used to measure an individual’s upper body strength, cardiovascular fitness, and competence in both land and water - based activity. 4.2 The test co mprises 3 aquatic events, conducted either in a swimming pool or open water: 4.2.1 100m Freestyle swim 4.2.2 10m Underwater swim 4.2.3 10 Minutes treading water 4.3 And 2 land events: 4.3.1 2.4km Run 4.3.2 100m Stretcher carry with 80kg load 5. Helicopter Medical Transport Course (HMTC) : 5.1 Prior to starting operations on the aircraft, all LifeFlight staff shall complete the HMTC, presented by the HMCAS Education Department. 5.2 Annual refresher courses shall be undertaken to maintain currency . 6. Crew Resource Management Course (CRMC) : 6.1 Prior to sta rting operations on the aircraft, all LifeFlight staff shall complete a CRMC at an off - site accredited institution. 6.2 Annual refresher courses shall be attended to maintain currency. 7. Helicopter Underwater Escape Training (HUET) & Helicopter Emergency Escape Device (HEED) Training :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 438 7.1 Prior to starting operations on the aircraft, all LifeFlight staff shall complete HUET and HEED training at an off - site accredited institution. 7.2 HUET and HEED certification is valid for 4 years from completion of the course. 7.3 Annual r efresher courses shall be attended to maintain currency. 8. Clinical and Technical Skills maintenance : 8.1 All LifeFlight staff shall undertake appropriate clinical refresher courses held by the HMCAS as set out in the HMCAS Training Plan, including annual HMCAS infection control training. 8.2 All LifeFlight staff shall undertake annual refresher courses in infection control held by HMCAS Infection Control Department. 8.3 All LifeFlight staff shall undertake a minimum of two (2) x 12 - hour operational shifts on the aircraf t per month in order to maintain active Flight Crew status. 9. Pregnancy : 9.1 Flight duties while pregnant are allowed unless 20 weeks gestation has been exceeded. 10. New pregnancy of female LifeFlight staff shall be reported to the SME: LifeFlight as soon as possib le. A letter from her Gynaecologist confirming pregnancy will be required as soon as pos
sible. Pertinent medical history or complications during previous pregnancies which may affect the ability to fly should be contained in the letter .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 439 SOP 4.11 .B.3 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.B.3 FITNESS TO FLY Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding their medical fitness when participating in the LifeFlight Program. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or aeroplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar. 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff members’ fitness levels as well as their general health and compliance with this procedure. 3. Flight and Duty Hours : 3.1 LifeFlight shifts may not exceed 14 hours without authorization from the Consultant Effec tiveness. 3.2 The rest period prior to commencement of LifeFlight duties shall not be less than 10 hours. No other duties, meetings, training, etc. may be undertaken within the 10 hours preceding the Lif

eFlight activity (i.e. LF Shifts, LF Training, Events, et c.). 3.3 Medical Flight Crew must have adequate rest and sleep in the preceding 24 hours before a LifeFlight shift. 3.4 Medical Flight Crew may not work more than 6 consecutive shifts without at least one full day off or work more than 60 hours per week. 3.5 Medical F light Crew members should not donate blood within 72 hours of a flight duty. 3.6 Medical Flight Crew members must not undertake any flying duties within 24 hours after Open Water Scuba Diving. 4. Unfit To Fly : 4.1 No individual shall act as a member of the crew of a HMCAS LifeFlight helicopter, if for any reason, their physical or mental condition is such that it could endanger the safety of the aircraft, the crew, or the patients. 4.2 Flight crew members who have concerns about the fitness of any of their fellow crew mem bers on duty are strongly encouraged to contact the Consultant Effectiveness on duty or the SME: LifeFlight and report their concerns and should avoid entering into arguments with fellow crew members about the same. 4.3 Flight crew members are not obliged to f ly with any other crew member who is perceptibly unfit to perform flying duties. 4.

4 HMCAS staff members who are suffering from any sickness/injury that is contagious or potentially debilitating should not undertake flying duties. Personnel that are sick or in jured must report to the Staff Clinic or a suitable alternative Medical Facility. A medical report/sick note shall be submitted to the SME: LifeFlight who will decide whether the staff member is cleared for flying duties or not. 4.5 Individuals prone to motion sickness should not undertake flight duties.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 440 5. Medications : 5.1 Medications that impair judgement and/or cause drowsiness should not be taken prior to or during flight duty time. Examples of these types of medication include (but are not limited to) the following: 5.1.1 Antihistamines 5.1.2 Alpha - adrenergic blocking agents 5.1.3 Antidepressants 5.1.4 Anti - emetics 5.1.5 Codeine based analgesia 5.2 Enquiries regarding medication and fitness to fly are to be directed to the SME: LifeFlight. 6. Attributes required to function safely and effectiv ely in an aeromedical environment : 6.1 Fatigue and flight operations: 6.1.1 The following warning signs should alert flight crew members of a fatigue - related dangerous situation: 6.1.1.1 Eyes going in and out of focus 6.1.1.2 Head bobs involuntarily 6.1.1.3 Persistent yawning 6.1.1.4 Wandering or poorly organized thoughts 6.1.1.5 Spotty near - term memory 6.1.1.6 Missed or erroneous performance of routine procedures 6.2 Situational awareness: 6.2.1 For a HEMS pilot or flight crew member, situational awareness involves having a mental picture of the existing inter - relationship of location, flight conditions, config

uration, and energy state of the aircraft as well as any other factors that could potentially affect its safety such as: 6.2.1.1 proximate terrain; 6.2.1.2 obstructions; 6.2.1.3 airspace reservations; 6.2.1.4 landing zone infringements; and 6.2.1.5 weather systems 6.2.2 The flight crew shall have an understanding of the current state and dynamics of the aeromedical system and shall be able to anticipate future change and developments. 6.2.3 In terms of situational awareness, medi cal flight crew shall: 6.2.3.1 Extract information from the environment; 6.2.3.2 Integrate this information with relevant internal knowledge to create a mental picture of the current situation; 6.2.3.3 Use this picture to direct further perceptual exploration in a continual perce ptual cycle; and 6.2.3.4 Anticipate future events

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 441 6.3 Flight crew members may not fly if their actual body size prevents the helicopter’s seat belts & restraining system from securing them to the seat. 6.4 Flight crew members must demonstrate their ability to lift and car ry a stretcher with a minimum load of 80kg .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 442 SOP 4.11 .B.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.B.4 CLEANING OF LIFEFLI GHT (LF) HELICOPTERS Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding the cleaning of all operational LF helicopters. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFEFLIGHT STAFF 1. SCHEDULED CLEANING & DECONTAMINATION : 1.1 Daily cleaning: 1.1.1 At the start of each shift the Medical Flight Crew on duty will inspect the interior of the helicopter for cleanliness. 1.1.2 Basic cleaning and dusting shall be performed by the Medical Flight Crew on duty (e.g. removal of sand from the floor). 1.1.3 The Medical Flight Crew on duty shall perform cleaning and disinfection of both the equipment and other frequently - touched surfaces after each patient transportation, using HMC - approved dis infectant wipes. 1.1.4 If any bio - hazardous contamination of the aircraft is found (typically after the transportation of patients), the aircraft should be cleaned on site, if at Hamad Emergency Helipad (

“Hotel Echo ” ) or contact the designated Ambulance Attendan ts to facilitate deep cleaning and simple decontamination at the LifeFlight Base. 1.1.5 Depending on the extent of contamination, the helicopter may be booked Out of Service for the duration of the cleaning task. 1.2 Weekly cleaning: 1.2.1 All operational LF helicopters w ill undergo social cleaning and simple decontamination twice a week on Sundays and Wednesdays at 0700 H . 1.2.2 The designated ambulance attendants will perform these cleaning tasks at the LifeFlight Hangar at Doha Air Base . 1.2.3 The Flight Coordinator in the NCC will coordinate with all the role players to facilitate the cleaning task. 1.3 Monthly deep cleaning: 1.3.1 All operational LF helicopters will have their Medical Interiors completely removed for cleaning and disinfecting once per month . 1.3.2 The AgustaWestland engineers at Doha Air Base will perform the removal and re - fitment of the Medical Interiors . 1.3.3 The removed Interiors will be cleaned and di sinfected by the Ambulance Attendants. 2. CLEANING RESTRICTIONS : 2.1 Detergents and cleaning solutions : 2.1.1 Only HMC approved detergents and cleaning solutions may be utilized for clean
ing of LF helicopters and equipment. 2.2 Equipment removal :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 443 2.2.1 No equipment may be remove d from operational LifeFlight helicopters for cleaning purposes by anyone other than LifeFlight Medical Crew Members. 2.2.2 All equipment handed in to the HMCAS Production Department for cleaning has to be recorded in the equipment book and signed for by a repre sentative from the HMCAS Production Department. 2.2.3 If equipment must be removed for a prolonged period, loan equipment should be obtained from HMCAS Production Department to ensure continuity of the LifeFlight service.
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 444
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 445 SOP 4.11.C: LifeFlight Equipment
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 446

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 447 SOP 4.11.C.1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11 . C .1 LIFEFLIGHT PERSONAL PROTECTIVE EQUIPMEN T Implementation Date 24 October 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding the proper use, storage, and care of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and related items within the HMCAS. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program. 2. The SME: Lifeflight will be responsible for monitoring staff members’ compliance with this procedure. 3. Aeromedical crew members shall b e issued with the following PPE: 3.1 NOMEX® Flight Suit and NOMEX® Flying Gloves : 3.1.1 Do not make any alterations to the issued flight suits without approval from the OM. 3.1.2 Wear the Nomex® suit on all helicopter flights. 3.1.3 Always wear the standard shoulder rank insign ia on the flight suit at all times. 3.1.4 Fasten all suit closures during use 3.1.5 Clean Nomex® garme

nts either at home, by industrial laundry or dry cleaning. 3.1.6 Use NOMEX® Flying Gloves during helicopter operations only. 3.2 HAIX AIRPOWER® Boots : 3.2.1 Always wear issued boots when performing helicopter duties to ensure adequate ankle support and toe protection. 3.2.2 Staff who have not been issued with HAIX boots may wear other boots if approved by the SME: Lifeflight . Only black boots may be worn with flight suits 3.2.3 Keep boots should clean at all times. 3.2.4 Avoid using flammable cleaning/polishing products on boots. 3.3 MSA® GALLET LH250 Helicopter Helmet : 3.3.1 Ensure that the helmet fits comfortably & snugly. 3.3.2 Attach only supplied, approved headlamps/accessories to the helmet. 3.3.3 Do not attach/tie/pa ste any other item in any way to the helmet 3.3.4 Graphics, paint, artwork, stickers, writing or any markings of any kind shall not be allowed on helmets except the official, supplied HMCAS markings. 3.3.5 Do not drill/make any holes into any part of the helmet. 3.3.6 Clean and preserve helmets in strict adherence to the manufacturer’s instructions. 3.3.7 Helmets will be inspected every three months by the SME: Lifeflight in strict adherence to the manufactur

er’s warranty recommendations. Report any damage or defects to any helmet immediately. 3.3.8 If a helmet is dropped from a height greater than one (1) meter, report it immediately, even if no damage is apparent as the integrity of the helmet’s shell may be compromised.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 448 3.3.9 Keep the helmet visor (smoked or clear depending on time of day) in the lowered position during all helicopter operations especially when helicopter doors are open while the engines are running or during flight. 3.3.10 Tighten the chin strap and fasten securely for the duration of wear. 3.3.11 Store helmets in the provided helmet bag (to protect it from dust), inside the provided carry bag to protect from damage. 3.3.12 Store helmet away from direct sunlight, preferably in an environment with an ambient temperature less than 30° C and relative humidity < 65%. 3.4 Custom - made/Disposable Hearing Protection : 3.4.1 ALWAYS wear hearing protection when working on the Apron at Doha Air Base. 3.4.2 ALWAYS wear hearing protection (helmet or plugs) when working around any running helicopter, primarily when operating in a ground - supporting role. 3.5 Rescue Knives and Tools : 3.5.1 The supplied personal rescue tools are to be carried at all times during flying operations. 3.6 BLACK 5.11® Mission Ready 2.0 Bag : 3.6.1 Store all supplied PPE in this bag during transportation and when not in use. 3.6.2 PPE equipment may n

ot be stored o r transported in any other bag or case. 3.6.3 Do not use this bag for any other purpose. 3.7 LSC® QUAD LOCK Aviation Safety Belt : 3.7.1 Wear the safety belt in all circumstances where one or both crew members are required to operate on - board an airborne helicopter with op en doors and crew members not strapped into seats (e.g. during pattering or HSAR operations). 3.7.2 Wear the safety belt above the hips and adjust for a tight fit, ensuring that both buckles are latched. 3.7.3 Attach the tether to a secure approved load - rated aircraft fitting and verify the safety hook is secured and locked . 3.7.4 Adjust the tether length to remove excess slack to prevent inadvertently exiting out aircraft. 3.7.5 Maintain and inspect safety belts in strict adherence to the manufacturer’s instructions. 3.8 SWITLIK X - BA CK MOLLE® Air Crew Vest : 3.8.1 DONNING INSTRUCTIONS : 3.8.1.1 Put vest on with zipper and buckle in front (Do not wear life vest under clothing) . 3.8.1.2 Zip vest up 3.8.1.3 Fasten snap buckle 3.8.1.4 Adjust “X” straps for proper fit 3.8.1.5 Fold excess waist strap, as necessary, and secure in strap st owage pockets. 3.8.2 INFLATION & USE : 3.8.2.1 Do not inf
late life vest while inside aircraft.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 449 3.8.2.2 After exiting the aircraft, pull sharply down on the inflation beaded handles. 3.8.2.3 To access oral inflation tube, lift the lower cover flap on the front cover. 3.8.2.4 Emergency strobe will illuminate on contact with water. 3.8.3 MAINTENANCE & CARE – Always inspect life vest prior to donning: 3.8.3.1 Check for damage to the outer covering, supporting vest material and straps. If any damage is evident, the vest should be inspected by authorised service centre. 3.8.3.2 Check to ensure that the zip on the outer covering is secure all along the side and that the ends are secure with Velcro fastener. 3.8.3.3 Check to ensure that the inflation lanyard is in place at the bottom of the vest, secured with Velcro fastene r in its proper location. 3.9 HEED 3® Helicopter Emergency Egress Device : 3.9.1 The device will be attached to the Switlik X - Back Air Crew Vest using the mole attachment. 3.9.2 PRE - USE CHECK: 3.9.2.1 Check Pressure Indicator - Dial Gauge Indicator needle is within green zone (ran ge from 2600 to 3000 psi). 3.9.2.2 Check for obvious physical damage, broken or loose parts. 3.9.3 TO USE THE HEED:

3.9.3.1 Pull D - ring on flap of Holster to open. 3.9.3.2 Grab HEED regulator head and pull out of the Holster. This action will release the Mouthpiece Cover. 3.9.3.3 Place the HEE D regulator into user’s mouth. 3.9.3.4 If the system is used underwater press purge button or exhale sharply to expel water from the regulator prior to inhalation. 3.9.3.5 Continue to inhale and exhale to a safe and normal rate of ascent. 3.9.4 NOTE: 3.9.4.1 The HEED utilizes a balance d regulator which means it will provide air in any orientation including the regulator being upside down or sideways. 4. Staff members shall individually be responsible for the safekeeping and basic care of their own, issued Personal Protective Equipment. 5. All issued Personal Protective Equipment MUST be present prior to commencing LifeFlight duties.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 450 SOP 4.11.C.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.C.2 CARE OF PERSONAL PR OTECTIVE EQUIPMENT Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMCAS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding the proper care and cleaning of Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) and related items within the HMCAS. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFE FLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member undertaking duties within the LifeFlight program. 2. The SME: LifeFlight will be responsible for monitoring staff compliance with this procedure. 3. Care of NOMEX® Flight Suits : 3.1 Read and carefully follow manufacturer’s laundering instructions. 3.2 Launder Nomex® garments separately from other garments to avoid the transfer of flammable fibres or other materi als. 3.3 Hand or machine wash in water not exceeding 60°C using a good proprietary washing powder or detergent that does not contain optical whiteners or high levels of oxidizing agents. The best results are given b

y using a liquid detergent with near - neutral pH and water with minimum alkaline content. 3.4 Do not use pure soap powders as these may affect the flame retardant properties of the garment. 3.5 The machine cycle should not exceed 1 hour and do not exceed 2/3 of the machine capacity. 3.6 Ensure that the rinse cyc le is sufficient to remove all residual soap/detergents. 3.7 DO NOT USE BLEACH, PEROXIDE, OR CAUSTIC WASHING AGENTS . 3.8 Preferably line dry, not in direct sunlight and hang inside out to avoid fading of the outer surface. 3.9 The garment may be tumble - dried at low he at 3.10 If required, iron with a medium/hot iron 3.11 The garment can be dry - cleaned 3.12 Store Nomex® garments in a dry place away from heat and direct sunlight. 4. Care of NOMEX® Flying Gloves : 4.1 Wear Nomex® gloves at all times during missions unless patient examination glo ves are indicated for patient treatment. 4.2 Do not store bundled/ folded tightly into pockets but rather stored flat (e.g. inside helmet) to avoid creation of permanent creases in the material. 4.3 Avoid contact with oils and fuel as this will degrade the leather and Nomex® material. 4.4 Wash only as per Nomex® manufacturer’s re
commendations as described for Nomex® flight suits in 3.1 to 3.12. 5. Care of Flying Boots : 5.1 Read and carefully follow the manufacturer’s usage, care, and cleaning instructions.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 451 5.2 Hand wash wi th a shoe brush or other soft brush and warm water (at a temperature no higher than 40°C). Scrub lightly with the wet brush to remove dirt and other soils. 5.3 Air dry protective footwear slowly, never with forced hot air or on a heat source. A boot dryer is a cceptable. 5.4 Remove the insole and stand it up inside the boot to allow the absorbent insole to dry without being misplaced. 5.5 Keep out of direct sunlight and away from open windows, hot ovens, or radiators. 5.6 Apply a professional grade silicone - based shoe polis h. Those that contain petroleum - based products are flammable and therefore unsuitable. 6. Care of MSA® GALLET LH250 Helicopter Helmet : 6.1 Wear personal issue perspiration absorbing Nomex® skull cap under the helmet to improve the hygiene inside the helmet and pr olong the inner lining’s life. 7. Care of Custom - made Hearing Protection : 7.1 Read and carefully follow the manufacturer’s usage, care, and cleaning instructions. 8. Underwear : 8.1 All clothing (T - shirts & underwear) worn underneath the Flight Suit shall be wool, Nomex® or 100% cotton (i.e. no synthetics materia

ls e.g. Lycra, spandex, polyester, etc.). 8.2 Wear only plain white and navy blue T - shirts underneath the flight suit with no visible brands or logos. 8.3 Do not wear any metal - containing underwear (e.g. underwire bras, e tc.). 8.4 Wear only wool or Nomex® socks. 9. Jewellery : 9.1 Do not wear any loose or exposed jewellery during helicopter operations (hoop earing, bangles, bracelets etc). 10. Care of CAMELBAK® Rehydration Pack : 10.1 The issued CAMELBAK® is designed for use during missions for rehydration purposes of individual crew members. 10.2 Take care to avoid cross contamination . 10.3 Crew members may fill the hydration bladder with any fluids they choose, although HMCAS will not replace hydration bladders or piping damaged by fluids ot her than water. 11. Care of Rescue Knives and Tools : 11.1 The Leatherman® Wave and Gerber® Folding Tool are provided as utility equipment during helicopter operations and shall be carried at all times during flying operations. 11.2 Store the tools in secure pockets which are available on the flight suit or the Camel Back®. 12. Repair and Replacement of Damaged or Lost PPE : 12.1 Repair/replacement of damaged equipment may take extended

periods of time (especially custom - made flight suits) since most of the PPE is impor ted. 12.2 Any PPE damaged/destroyed/lost by user negligence, misuse or abuse shall be repaired/replaced at the user's own expense. 12.3 Any PPE found abandoned, abused, or stored incorrectly will be confiscated by management.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 452 12.4 Report lost or stolen PPE immediately to the SME: LifeFlight and register a case with the Police. 12.5 Complete an Occurrence/Variance/Accident report for any component of PPE that is damaged or lost. Each item must be reported in separate OVA’s. 12.6 All issued PPE must be returned to the Senior Producti on Supervisor at the end of employment as part of the Termination and Separation process, the cost of any outstanding PPE will be recovered from the employees end of service settlement. 13. Standard Issued PPE to be Returned at the end of Service : 13.1 MSA Gallet L H250 Helmet 13.2 Remix Pro Headlamp 13.3 Nomex 13.4 Flight suit 13.5 LF Jacket 13.6 Nomex Flight Gloves 13.7 Rescue Gloves 13.8 Leatherman Folding Tool 13.9 Gerber Knife 13.10 Leatherman Raptor Rescue Shears 13.11 Camelbak 13.12 HAIX Boots 13.13 HDS2 Tetra Earpiece

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 453 SOP 4.11 .C.3 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations (LifeFlight) Procedure 4.11.C.3 MEDICAL EQUIPMENT, CONSUMABLES AND ASSE T MANAGEMENT Implementation Date 01 April 2017 Purpose To provide HMC AS staff members with guidance and relevant information regarding the management of medical equipment to ensure the safe and effective delivery of the LifeFlight service. Policy Ref.: SA 1056 AS Medical Equipment Management Plan . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL LIFEFLIGHT STAFF 1. This procedure will apply to any HMCAS staff member or Medical Flight Crew member actively involved in the LifeFlight program or seconded to fly on any other helicopter or airplane as part of their official duties within the State of Qatar. 2. The SME: LifeFlight shall delegate to the Production Supervisor the task of tracking equipment's location and maintaining a database of equipment availability and due dates for service and repair. 3. The Production Supervisor shall be responsible for e nsuring that sufficient items of medical equipment are serviceable and available for use i

n order to support the operational effectiveness of the LifeFlight Service. 4. Definitions : 4.1 Medical Equipment : Non - consumable items of equipment used for patient care, i ncluding, but not limited to: electrical medical equipment (electro - med) and equipment used for immobilization, transport, and extrication of patients. 4.2 User group : Personnel employed within the Emergency Aeromedical Service who operate point - of - care medica l equipment items. 4.3 Equipment Support : The process of servicing, maintenance, and repair of front - line medical equipment. 4.4 Bio Medical Engineering (BioMed) : The internal provider of Equipment Support services within Hamad Medical Corporation. 4.5 Decontamination : The process of cleaning an item of equipment to ensure that it is free of blood, body fluids and other bio - contaminants. 4.6 Asset management is the process by which the SME: LifeFlight and other members of the User group are aware of the location and status of all medical equipment. 5. Ground Transportation Platforms : 5.1 The Production Supervisor shall be responsible for ensuring that vehicles are presented for servicing and repair when required, in line with the existi

ng HMCAS fleet management system. This respon sibility may be delegated to a capable member of staff. 5.2 Users of these vehicles shall thoroughly inspect these vehicles using the prescribed vehicle checklists in line with SOP 4.2.A . 6. Air Ambulances : 6.1 The Qatar Emiri Air Force (QEAF) shall manage the servicing and repair of air ambulances through AgustaWestland. 6.2 Flight Medical Crew shall be responsible for conducting daily checks of medical equipment and installations and a general check of the serviceability of the side doors, seats, and crew safety harnesses.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 454 6.3 Report faults and problems immediately to the Pilot in Command or the Operations Officer. 7. Extrication and immobilization equipment : 7.1 Check all extrication and immobilization equipment daily for serviceability and completeness. 7.2 Immediately report any damaged, faulty, lost or worn items to the Production Supervisor. 7.3 The Production Supervisor shall arrange for the item to be replaced as soon as possible through HMCAS Production Department. 7.4 NEVER us e any faulty items of equipment on a patient. 8. Electric Medical Equipment (ELECTRO - MED): 8.1 All electro - med equipment items shall be approved for use by BioMed and formally brought into service. 8.2 All BioMed approved equipment shall display a yellow barcoded lab el with a unique identifying HBE number. Other labels that may be affixed include warranty labels and ‘next test due’ labels. 8.3 NEVER use any item of electro - med equipment if it does not display BioMed approval . 9. Trials and Demonstration Equipment : 9.1 Equipment being trialed/tested for fitness purposes shall also be subject to BioMed approval. 9.2 A blue ‘Demo Unit’ label shall

be affixed to all trial equipment. 10. User Training and Competence : 10.1 The SME: LifeFlight shall ensure that all personnel are trained in the use of all serviceable items of equipment. 10.2 Individual personnel shall demonstrate competence in the use of serviceable equipment. 10.3 A copy of competency certificates shall be retained in the Users Personnel File. 10.4 Training on newly acquired equipment wil l be facilitated through the HMCAS Training Department. 10.5 The SME: LifeFlight shall ensure that any internal competence training provided on in - service equipment complies with the equipment manufacturers’ recommendations. 11. User Checks and Maintenance : 11.1 The Use r Group shall ensure that electro - med equipment is maintained in a serviceable condition and that daily pre and post use checks are carried out in accordance with the manufacturers’ recommendations. 11.2 User check sheets shall be retained on file for a period of 5 years. 11.3 Maintain batteries in a charged state and account for ancillary items of equipment (charging units, power cables, ECG electrodes, BP Cuffs etc.) at the beginning and end of each shift. Ensure that such items are recovered when handin

g over a pa tient to a receiving facility. 11.4 Complete the daily check sheet for each item of electro - med equipment. 11.5 Conduct a rapid trouble - shooting assessment if an item of equipment is found to be non - functional (e.g. check power source and battery status, ensure lead s and cables fully plugged in). If the fault cannot be rapidly rectified, source a replacement item of equipment from the LifeFlight Store. 11.6 Decontaminate faulty electro - med equipment using alcohol or non - caustic detergent before being passed to the HMCAS P roduction Department for repair arrangements with BioMed.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 455 11.7 NEVER place a faulty item of electro - med equipment on a shelf where it may be inadvertently re - used . 11.8 NEVER cannibalize any item of electromedical equipment (ancillaries taken for use on another item of equipment). Actions such as this have the potential to cause Serious Untoward Incidents resulting in death or injury to a patient. DISCIPLINARY ACTION MAY BE TAKEN AGAINST PERSONNEL FOUND TO BE IN VIOLATION OF THIS INSTRUCTION . 12. Pre - packed medical/surgical kits : 12.1 Place opened pre - packed kits in the allocated box inside Doha Air Base Medical Store Room or at the relevant areas at HGH or HMCAS main stores for re - packing. 12.2 Partially used kits are not to be discarded unless soiled. 13. Medical Stock Maintenance : 13.1 Maintain stock levels in accordance with the maximum and minimum levels designated by the SME: LifeFlight. 13.2 Communicate replenishment requests to the Flight Coordinator who shall ensure that the stock (including non - medical items e.g. water) is ordered and ready to be transported to Doha Air Base with the oncoming duty personnel. 13.3 Transport partially used prepacked k

its and other non - medical items (e.g. empty water bottles) to the main HMCAS stores at the end of the shift. 14. Re - stocking of Equipmen t and Sundries ( excluding controlled medications ): 14.1 Replace portable oxygen cylinders from the HGH A&E Ambulance Attendant Store or at the HMCAS main stores. 14.2 If a patient is transported to HGH, replenish all prepacks and oxygen cylinders from the stores at the A&E Ambulance Attendant Store. 14.3 If a patient is transported to Heart Hospital or PEC Al Sadd, replenish all prepacks from the HMCAS main stores. 14.4 If a patient is transported to any other facility, replenish all prepacks from the Doha Air Base LifeFlight store room. 14.5 If a patient is treated on scene and not transported, replenish all stock used from the Medical store - room at the Doha Air Base LifeFlight Hangar upon return. 15. Replenishment of Controlled Medications : 15.1 All controlled medications are only availab le from the HMCAS main stores. Please see SOP 4.3.C for specific controlled medications procedures. 15.2 If a patient has been treated and transported to hospital, complete all necessary paperwork, and restock any disposables at the relevant area as de

tailed above. 15.3 If at HGH, use the Ambulance Attendant vehicle to drive to the HMCAS main stores to restock controlled medications. 15.4 If the patient is not transported to HGH and restocking is to be conducted at the HMCAS main stores, then restock all equipment and sundries together with controlled medications .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 457 SOP 5 .1 : Retrieval Services (RS): General
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 458

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 459 SOP 5.1 .A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector HMCAS Retrieval Services (RS) Procedure 5.1 .A RETRIEVAL SERVICES D RIVING Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To provide guidance on driving for Retrieval Services operations . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVICES O PERATIONAL STAFF 1. Background: 1.1 This SOP complements HMCAS SOP 4.2.A and SOP 4.6.A . Instead of replacing the provisions made within these publications, it expands upon certain areas with reference to the unique mission sets that are conducted by Retrieval Services. 2. Implications for RS Drivers: 2.1 RS drivers must comply with the following instructions: 2.1.1 ‘Priority 1’ driving techniques must only be used under extreme circumstances (such as when a patient suddenly and unexpectedly deteriorates in - transit). 2.1.2 Careful driving at moderate speeds will benefit the patient most and must be the normal driving technique applied even for the most high ac uity RS patients. 2.1.3 The decision for the driver to drive under Priority 1 conditions must be informed by the patient’s condition and rests wit

h the senior clinician present. HMCAS SOP 4.6.A gives specific instructions for ‘Priority 1’ driving including the direction that no HMCAS ambulance may exceed the stated speed limit by more than 20 km/h. These instructions must be adhered to. 2.1.4 The ‘box body’ shape of the ICU Ambulances hinders the situational awareness of the driver. The co - dr iver must act at all times to aid the driver’s situational awareness (pointing out hazards etc.) and must always dismount to guide the driver in reversing. 3. Vehicle Familiarization and Licensing: 3.1 Line Managers are responsible for ensuring that all driving p ersonnel have a current drivers’ license that is appropriate to the vehicle being driven. 3.2 Individuals are responsible for reporting any health changes or personal circumstances that may affect their ability to or competence in driving HMCAS RS vehicles. 3.3 Al l drivers must undergo familiarization training and be assessed as competent in the operation of the vehicle being driven. 3.4 Personnel employed on driving duties in units that do not deploy daily must conduct two hours of practical driving per shift to maint ain currency and familiarity with the vehicle .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 460 SOP 5. 1.B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.1.B MANAGEMENT OF EMERGE NCY PATIENTS BY RETR IEVAL UNITS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To provide guidance on management of Emergency Patients encountered by Retrieval Services personnel . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC ES OPERATIONAL STAFF 1. Situation : 1.1 Retrieval Services crews may be faced with Emergency Patients. It is anticipated that this will be as a result of: 1.1.1 Unexpected and sudden deterioration in the condition of non - emergency patients being transported. 1.1.2 ‘Flagging down’ of Retrieval Services ambulances by members of the public a t the scene of an RTA or medical emergency. 1.1.3 RTA involving (or in close proximity to) a Retrieval Services vehicle. 2. Priority : 2.1 When faced with Emergency Patients, Retrieval Services crews must take immediate action and administer treatment as per Ambulance Service CPG’s. 3. Communication : 3.1 Following/during immediate action, the Retrieval Services crew must notify the HCC that they have an emerg

ency patient. The HCC will notify the crew to switch to a suitable NCC channel. 3.2 The Retrieval Services crew mu st communicate a situation report to NCC on switching channels, this must include: 3.2.1 Unit call sign 3.2.2 Location 3.2.3 Nature of the emergency 3.2.4 Number of patients and priority (including QEWS score) 3.2.5 Treatment given/in progress 3.2.6 Back up requested 3.3 NCC, in conjunction with the Clinical Desk, will make a decision on whether the crew should start or continue transport to the appropriate facility. Additional resources will be dispatched as required taking level of care, distance to receiving facility, ability t o drive Priority 1 into account. 4. Handover and Responsibility : 4.1 If Emergency Ambulance Services assets are not available or not required, Emergency Patients will be transported to an appropriate facility by a Retrieval Services crew with NCC approval. If req uired, a CCP will accompany the patient. 4.2 Emergency Patients may be transported by a Retrieval Services crew, provided the unit can provide a level of care commensurate with that which can be provided by the appropriate Emergency Ambulance Service crew.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 461 5. C ommensurate level of care : 5.1 Emergency Patients may only be transported to hospital by Retrieval Services if it meets the following criteria: 5.1.1 The unit has Ambulance Paramedic capability. 5.1.2 Capable of providing full spinal immobilization if clinically indicated . 5.1.3 Has a fitted stretcher. 5.2 No patient shall experience a lesser standard of care as a result of them being transported by a Retrieval Services Unit in lieu of an Emergency Ambulance Service Unit .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 462 SOP 5.1.C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.1.C AIRPORT OPERATIONS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To provide guidance on the general principals to be followed when operating at airports . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC ES OPERATIONAL STAFF 1. Background 1.1 Retrieval Services personnel from all service offerings are involved in the transfer of patients into and out of Qatar via Commercial, Military and Official aircraft at the following strategic air transport hubs: 1.1.1 Hamad International Airport 1.1.2 Doha International Airport 1.1.3 Al Udeid Air Base 2. Access : 2.1 Retrieval Services personnel entering strategic air transport hubs in the course of their duties do so entirely at the discretion o f the operating authority (Hamad International Airport and Qatar Emiri Air Force). 2.2 Personnel not in possession of access passes to these establishments may be granted temporary access via the following personnel (accessed through the management chain): 2.2.1 Al Udeid Air Base/Doha International Airport: Seni

or Supervisor for Public Relations. 2.2.2 Hamad International Airport: Senior Operations Manager for Emergency Service. 2.3 No RS staff member is to use their access cards for any personal gain, e.g purchase discounts, access to passenger terminals without official business, etc. 3. Security Considerations : 3.1 Hamad International Airport and Al Udeid Air Base are key installations within the State of Qatar. Access by Retrieval Services personnel is limited to those areas that are essential to the execution of their duties. 3.2 Photography in all forms, including the capture of still and video footage by smart phones is strictly prohibited in these locations without authority. 3.3 Retrieval Services personnel, vehicles, patients, and relatives may be subjected to search by security personnel, including metal detection and X - Ray. All personnel are expected to comply. 3.4 Each location will be entered via a pre - arranged access point. 4. Immigration : 4.1 Personnel leaving national terr itory of the State of Qatar on duty must be in possession of the following documentation: 4.1.1 Passport 4.1.2 Qatar ID 4.1.3 Valid exit permit from Ministry of the Interior of the State of Qatar
if applicable. 4.1.4 Valid entry visa for the flight destination

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 463 4.2 When required, exit permits will usually be arranged through the Senior Operations Manager for Emergency Service. If not available, the request will be escalated through RS management. 4.3 All RS staff traveling abroad, officially or unofficially, should be doing so without thei r HIA, DIA, Al Udeid Air Base. All access cards should be handed to Sierra Retrieval prior to departure. 5. Airside Operations : 5.1 When HMCAS personnel and vehicles are operating on ‘airside’, the following precautions must be taken without fail: 5.1.1 Do not proceed onto the apron without permission from Airport/Airbase staff. 5.1.2 Vehicles are not to exceed 25 km/h and must be driven on the margins of the apron. 5.1.3 For purposes of loading and unloading patients, vehicles must be parked a minimum of 25 meters from the aircraf t unless using a spotter. 5.1.4 When driven ‘airside’, HMCAS vehicles must display dipped headlights, four - way hazard warning lights. 5.1.5 Personnel are to wear PPE including high visibility/reflective vests. Hearing protection is to be worn in the presence of runnin g aircraft engines. Hats are not to be wo

rn. 5.1.6 Personnel must not walk under aircraft wings, approaching aircraft as directed by the Air Load Master and/or ground staff. 5.1.7 Rotary wing aircraft should only be approached once the aircraft engine has shut down un less briefed otherwise. In such case safety precautions should be used as briefed. 5.1.8 Loose articles must be secured and not permitted to present a foreign object damage (FOD) threat to the aircraft. 5.1.9 Smoking of any description is strictly prohibited. 6. Document ation : 6.1 The following documents must be presented to Ground Staff/Air Movements staff: 6.1.1 Passenger manifest 6.1.2 Medical equipment manifest including details of Dangerous Air Cargo. 6.1.3 Baggage manifest 6.1.4 Passports of patient and accompanying medical personnel. 6.1.5 Pharmaceuticals list 6.1.6 Letters of authorization for carriage of controlled medications 6.2 A copy of the passenger list (staff and patients) must be lodged with the Clinical Coordinator in either paper or electronic format prior to departure. 6.3 Authorit y to emplane and/or patient medical report must be obtained from the International Medical Affairs Office of the Ministry of Public Health prior to departure

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 467 SOP 5. 2 .A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Scheduled Ambulance Service (SAS) Procedure 5. 2 .A SCHEDULED AMBULANCE SERVICE ( SA S) SUPERVISOR DUTIES Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure Supervisor Duties are adhered to . Responsibility SA S SUPERVISORS 1. Day Shift Duties : 1.1 The SA S Supervisor will start duty at 05 0 0hrs daily at which point a handover will be conducted between the night and day shift personnel to confirm the following: 1.1.1 Vehicles available for day shift 1.1.2 Staff available for day shift 1.1.3 Staff sickness 1.1.4 Operational matters that have arisen during the night that warrant escalation (complaints, incidents, accidents). 1.1.5 Any ongoing incidents 1.2 Following takeover of duty, the Day Shift Supervisor will move to the Fahad Bin Jassim Renal Unit (FBJ) and conduct the following activities: 1.2.1 Troubleshooting of problems, issues, or complaints 1.2.2 Assisting with heavy patients 1.2.3 Random inspection of crews and ambulances for the fo llowing: 1.2.3.1 Cleanliness (interior/exterior) 1.2.3.2 Completion of vehicle a

nd equipment checks . 1.2.3.3 Staff turnout and appearance (including carriage of Corporate ID) . 1.2.4 Unless summoned to assist elsewhere, the SA S Supervisor will remain at FBJ until next Dialysis change ov er . 1.3 T he SA S Supervisor will relocate to Hamad General Hospital Outpatient Department (OPD), where he will continue to undertake the troubleshooting, assistance and inspection activities highlighted above, returning to FBJ at 1000hrs to continue the same un til 1200hrs. 1.4 The SA S Supervisor may be called to assist with problems encountered by any SA S crew within Doha city limits. 1.5 Incidents occurring outside of Doha (North of Umm Slal Mohammed intersection, Duhail and Qatar University, West of Al Shamal Road/Doh a Expressway and South/South - West of F Ring Road) will be referred to the 999 Delta from the nearest hub. 1.6 The SA S Supervisor will engage in routine administration tasks, including completion of the daily schedule and responding to emails, while continuing to act as the focal point for all issues involving front - line units and working in collaboration with Operations Production to resolve problems. 1.7 The SA S Supervisor will work to activate the

units for the night shift and hand over any live issues to the onc oming Night Shift SA S Supervisor. 1.7.1 The day - night handover takes the same form as the handover received at the start of the day shift.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 468 2. Night Shift Duties: 2.1 The SA S Supervisor for the night shift will start duty at 17 0 0hrs at which point the handover will be c onducted. 2.2 Following takeover of duty, the SA S Supervisor will complete the process of activating units for the night shift. 2.3 The SA S Supervisor will act as the focal point for all SA S matters throughout the night, monitoring the activities of SA S Units on shift. 2.4 T he SA S Supervisor will finalize the daily roster and forward it via email to the following personnel: 2.4.1.1 Operations Officer: Scheduled Ambulance Services. 2.4.1.2 Senior Operations Manager: Scheduled Ambulance Services. 2.5 The SA S Supervisor will conduct random visits to crews on shift, checking the following: 2.5.1.1 Cleanliness of ambulances (interior and exterior). 2.5.1.2 Completion of vehicle and equipment checks. 2.5.1.3 Staff turnout a nd appearance (including carriage of Corporate ID and Personal Protective Equipment ). 3. The SA S Supervisor will complete the daily report and conduct a review of the day’s e PCR ’ s, checking each for legibility and fullness of completion. 4. The SA S Supervisor

wi ll work in collaboration with Operations Production to resolve any issues ahead of the day ahead. 5. The SA S Supervisor will begin the activation of units for the coming day shift before handing over duty to the oncoming Day Shift SA S Supervisor at 05 0 0hrs .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 473 SOP 5.3.A Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Scheduled Ambulance Service (SAS) and Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.3.A EMERGENCY TRAN SFERS FROM HGH ED TO HEART HOSPITAL Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide guidance on HMCAS emergent transfers from HGH ED to Heart Hospital procedures, roles, and responsibilities . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC E, SCHEDULED AMBULAN CE SERVICE AND HCC COMMS PERSONNEL 1. Role Description : 1.1 HM CAS will provide a double paramedic crewed ambulance for the emergency inter - facility transfer of a STEMI patient from Hamad General Hospital to the Heart Hospital. 2. Equipment : 2.1 Standard Ambulance equipment shall be provided by HMCAS. Any additional equipment must be supplied by the transferring facility. 3. Dispatched Ambulance : 3.1 The first available ambulance (either Emergency Ambulance Service or Scheduled Ambulance Service will be dispatched. Benchmark time from dispatch to arrival at the referring facility for an unstable STEMI patient going for PCI is less than 10 minutes. 3.2 The first availab

le ambulance (either Emergency Ambu lance Service or Scheduled Ambulance Service) will be dispatched. Benchmark Time from dispatch to arrival at the referring facility for a Stable STEMI patient going for admission in specialist facility for further care is less than 30 minutes. 3.3 Dispatch wil l be coordinated by the Healthcare Coordination Center in conjunction (where an interfacility ambulance is not available) with the National Command Center. 4. Hospital to Hospital Communication : 4.1 The referring hospital will contact the Healthcare Coordination Centre to request an ambulance for transfer. 5. Ambulance Activation : 5.1 The Physician in charge of the case at the referring facility will determine the acuity of the transfer, based upon the following criteria: 5.1.1 Patients ventilated, on continuous medication infusion or otherwise unstable requires augmentation by the referring facility staff. The responding ambulance will report directly to the HGH Emergency Department. 5.1.2 Patients that are stable but have been diagnosed with a STEMI (ST segment elevat ion myocardial infarction) will be transferred by the AP crew without escort. The ambulance will report directly to the

HGH Emergency Department . 5.1.3 Patients not ventilated, on medication infusions ( within AP scope of practice) or who are otherwise stable, require augmentation by the receiving facility staff will be transported by the AP crew. 6. Completion o f Transfer : 6.1 At completion of transfer HMCAS will not return the hospital transfer team or equipment . 7. Logistics :
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 474 7.1 HMCAS will be responsible for post - use cleaning of the ambulance and fault reporting .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 475 SOP 5.3.B Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Schedule Ambulance Service (SAS) and Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.3.B PAEDIATRIC EMERGENCY CENTRE (PEC) TRANSF ERS Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide guidance on HMCAS PEC unit activation procedure, roles, and responsibilities . Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL STAF F AND HCC COMMS PERS ONNEL 1. Role Description : 1.1 PEC Transfers are conducted by Scheduled Ambulance Services in conjunction with specialist personnel from the PEC hospital for the purpose of transporting paediatric patients between fixed facilities within the State of Qatar. 2. Crew Composition : 2.1 HMCAS will provide two Ambulance Paramedics who will be augmented by a transfer team of hospital specialists. 2.2 The escorting clinicians are employees of Hamed Medical Corporation and are responsible for the continuation of patient care. 3. Equipme nt : 3.1 Standard primary response equipment shall be provided by HMCAS. Any additional equipment must be supplied by the transferring facility. 4. Hospital to Hospital Communication

: 4.1 Before activating the ambulance service, the patient will have been referred to the receiving facility and the referral accepted. 4.2 All communication with the receiving facility will be carried out by the referring facility. 5. PEC Unit Activation : 5.1 The benchmark time from dispatch to arrival at the referring facility for unstable patients is less than 10 minutes. 5.2 The benchmark time from dispatch to arrival at the referring facility for stable patients is less than 20 - 45 minutes. 5.3 The Physician in charge of the case at the referring facility will determine the acuity of the transfer, based upon the following criteria: 5.3.1 Patients ventilated, on continuous medication infusion or otherwise unstable requires augmentation by the referring facility staff. 5.3.2 Patients that are stable but being transferred to OPD for testing require a sta ff nurse from the referring facility. 5.3.3 Patients not ventilated, on medication infusions within an AP scope of practice or who are otherwise stable . These patients will be transported by the AP crew. 5.3.4 Patients not ventilated, on medication infusions outside t he AP scope of practice (will require the Treating Clinicia
n doctor to validate the need of the infusion for transport or alternatively the referring facility to provide a nurse escort to monitor the infusion ). 5.3.5 Refer to SOP 3.22 HCC Call Takin g.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 476 6. Completion o f Transfer : 6.1 At completion of transfer, HMCAS will only return hospital transfer staff (PEC Al Sadd only) and equipment if: 6.1.1 There are no other PEC calls pending 6.1.2 HMCAS is not in escalation 6.1.3 HCC approves the return of st aff 7. Logistics : 7.1 HMCAS will be responsible for post - use cleaning of the ambulance and fault reporting .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 477 SOP 5.3.C Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Scheduled Ambulance Service (SAS) and Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.3.C NEONATAL INTEN SIVE CARE UNIT (NICU ) TRANSFERS Implementation Date 25 February 2020 Purpose To provide guidance on HMCAS NICU Unit activation procedure, roles, and responsibilities. Responsibility ALL OPERATIONAL STAF F AND HCC COMMS PERS ONNEL 1. Role Description : 1.1 NICU Transfers are conducted by Scheduled Ambulance Service and/or Retrieval Services in conjunction with specialist personnel from Women’s Hospital NICU for the purpose of transporting high acuity neonatal patients between fixed facilities. 2. Crew Composition : 2.1 HMCAS shall provide two Ambulance Paramedics who will be augmented by a transfer team of hospital specialists. 2.2 The escorting clinicians are employees of Hamed Medical Corporation and are responsible for the continuation of patient care. 3. Equipment : 3.1 Women’s Hospital NICU shall provide a ‘ParAid’ stretcher, neonata l incubator, and all medical consumables. Any stretchers must be removed from the ambulan

ce prior to arrival at Report Point. 4. Hospital to Hospital Communication : 4.1 Before activating the NICU unit, the patient will have been referred to and accepted by the re ceiving facility. 4.2 All communication with the receiving facility will be carried out by the referring facility. 5. NICU Unit Activation : 5.1 Benchmark time from dispatch to arrival at the referring facility (or pickup point at Women’s Hospital Emergency Department ) is less than 45 minutes. 5.2 The HMCAS NICU Unit will report to the referring facility based upon the following criteria: 5.2.1 When a patient is being transferred from the referring facility, using the referring facility staff and equipment, the NICU Unit will re port directly to the referring facility and meet the transferring staff. 5.2.2 When a patient is being transferred from the referring facility, using the NICU team and equipment from Women’s Hospital, the HMCAS NICU Unit will report to the Women’s Hospital Emerg ency Department entrance and meet with the transferring staff. 5.3 Activation decision: 5.3.1 Activation will be initiated on the order of the Neonatologist on - call. 5.3.2 Refer to SOP 3.22 HCC Call Taking 6. Completion of
Transfer : 6.1 At co mpletion of transfer, HMCAS will return hospital transfer team to Women’s Hospital Emergency Department or referring hospital if staff and equipment is not from Women’s

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 478 Hospital. The NICU ‘ParAid’ stretcher, incubator and associated medical equipment will be removed. 7. Logistics : 7.1 HMCAS will be responsible for post - use cleaning of the ambulance and fault reporting. Women’s Hospital NICU will be responsible for reconstituting and post use checks of the specialist equipment and ParAid stretcher. 8. Hours of Service : 8.1 Until the service has sufficient capacity to provide a dedicated vehicle, the following platforms will be used as available in the following order: 8.1.1 IFT Unit 8.1.2 Airport Unit 8.1.3 PEC Unit 8.1.4 Bariatric Unit 8.1.5 Retrieval Services Unit

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 479 SOP 5.3.E Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 5.3.E AMBULATORY P ATIENT T RANSPORT TO C ARRY - O UT I NTERFACILITY T RANSFERS Implementation Date 01 January 2024 Purpose To provide guidance on HMCAS transfers of patients between HMC Medical Facilities, and Roles and Responsibilities . Responsibility ALL HMCAS OPERATIONA L STAFF 1. Purpose: 1.1 HMCAS will provide a single/double paramedic crewed vehicle for Interfacility Transfers of Stable Ambulatory/Stable wheelchair patients who has been identified by the referring facility staff. 1.2 This may include patients going to Out - Patient Departments (OPD) for further investigation (physiotherapy, radiology, ophthalmology, ultra - sound, dentistry, podiatry etc..), for follow - up consultation, for hospital admission and/or continuity of care. 2. Equipment : 2.1 The Specialised Patient Carrier (SPC) will carry basic lifesa ving equipment i.e. Portable Oxygen, Lifepak 1000, Basic Response Bag with a Bag Valve Mask. 2.2 The SPC’s will carry a wheelchair to ensure smooth facilitation of a pat

ient from a hospital bed to the vehicle or from the vehicle to a hospital bed. 2.3 The SPC’s wi ll have a Mobile Data Terminal (MDT), to ensure HMCAS monitors location and timing (time of dispatch till available) ensuring swift turnaround of AS resources and availability to service the next call. 2.4 The SPC’s will have an Electronic Patient Care Report (EPCR), to ensure continuity of care and clinical documenting for patient transport: 2.4.1 Patients transported by SPC will have a single set of vital signs recorded for every patient encounter. 2.4.2 EPCR clinical documents for patients transported in SPC will be exc lude from HMCAS Clinical Documenting Audit. 3. Scope : 3.1 To ensure safe transport of stable ambulatory/wheelchair patients who do not require an Ambulance to transport them between HMC Healthcare facilities. 4. Definitions : 4.1 Specialised Patient Carrier : The procurement of these vehicles is to transport the Stable Ambulatory/Wheelchair patient. These vehicles comes fully fitted to maintain service delivery. 4.2 Stable patient : A patient with vital signs within normal clinical parameters for that age group. 4.3 Ambulat ory patient : A patient who can perform acti
vities of daily living by themselves i.e. without assistance of a nursing aid/allied healthcare worker (refer to Annexure A or Annexure B). 5. Procedure : 5.1 Hospital to Hospital Communication

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 480 5.1.1 The referring facility will contact the Healthcare Coordination Center (HCC) on 16099 to request a booking slot for Interfacility transfer. 5.1.2 HCC Call Taker can with the use of Annexure A or Annexure B, interrogate the call to ascertain the Right Resource to the Right Patient at the R ight Time. 5.1.3 The premise of a nurse contacting the HCC for a booking slot is considered after both facilities have agree to transport and receive the patient accordingly. 6. Ambulance Activation : 6.1 The Healthcare worker taking clinical care of the patient, will c all the Healthcare Coordination Center (HCC) on 16099 and answer all of Computer Aided Dispatch (CAD) questions. 6.2 If the patient meets both definitions of being a Stable Ambulatory/wheelchair patient, then SPC unit can be used to transport the patient safel y to his/her appointment. 6.3 Inclusion criteria for Adult and Pediatric patients: 6.3.1 Patients who have an IV cannula with no attachments (Adult and Pediatric). 6.3.2 Patients who have a CVP in - situ with no attachments (Adult). 6.3.3 Pediatric patients being carried by paren t/care giver. 6.3.4 Pediat

ric patients who are not carried but can safely be transported in a child car seat. 7. Logistics : 7.1 HMCAS will be responsible for pre - and post - cleaning of the minivans and fault reporting. 8. Hours of Service : 8.1 The SPC units will be in service 12 hours a day, 5 days a week (subject to change based on demand ) .
