Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 484 SOP 5.4: Retrieval Services SOP 5.4.A Retrieval Services Operations
Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 485

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 486 SOP 5.4.A.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.A.2 HIGH ACUITY ADULT TR ANSFERS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose Effective end to end management of SHAARP Transfers . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC E AND HCC PERSONNEL 1. Role Description : 1.1 High Acuity Adult Transfers are conducted by Retrieval Service in conjunction with specialist personnel from Hamad General Hospital MICU for the purpose of transporting high acuity adult patients between fixed facilities within Qatar. 2. Crew Composition : 2.1 HMCAS will provide 2 x Ambulance Paramedics and X1 Critical Care Para medic who will be augmented by a Hamad Medical Corporation Physician from the MICU HGH. 3. Equipment : 3.1 HMCAS will provide all necessary equipment with a specialized ParAid trolley for all High Acuity Transfer cases. 4. Activation time : 4.1 Retrieval Service crew and ICU Ambulance will be ready to deploy within 2 hours of activation. 5. Activation decision : 5.1 Activation will be initiated on the order of the receiving ICU Physician togethe

r with the on - call Retrieval Doctor. 6. Means of Activation : 6.1 High Acuity transfers will be activated by telephone call to the Retrievals desk from the receiving Physician. 7. Logistics : 7.1 HMCAS will be responsible for post - use cleaning of the ambulance, fault reporting, and for reconstituting and post use checks of the specialist equipment and ParA id stretcher. 8. Day Service : 8.1 Until the service has sufficient capacity to provide a 2nd dedicated vehicle and enough manpower for 24/7 coverage, the Retrieval Team will provide a service between the hours of 10:00 and 22:00. 9. High Acuity Transfer Criteria : 9.1 High Acuity Transfer: 9.1.1 Scheduled transfer of patients between intensive care units. 9.2 Hospital Transfer: 9.2.1 Unscheduled transfer of a Level 1 - 3 patients between hospitals requiring a response time appropriate to the patient’s condition. Team consist of 2 Ambulance Paramedics and an appropriate hospital escort as required. Equipment provided by the hospital. 9.2.2 Unscheduled Hospital transfers shall only be authorized by the Retrieval Consultant. 9.3 Retrieval Services shall not service a case from a hospital to an OPD.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 487 10. On - call roster : 10.1 Each month the MICU Doctors shall provide the Retrieval Service with the monthly on - call list for High Acuity Transfers. In the event of a Doctor On - Call being unable perform his duties, the Operations Manager – CCS is to be notifie d in advance .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 488 SOP 5.4.A.3.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.A.3.2 COMMERCIAL FLIGHT OP ERATIONS: REPATRIATI ON TO QATAR UPON COMMERCIAL AIRC RAFT Implementation Date 13 March 2018 Purpose To standardize the approach to mission planning and execution for the Repatriation of patients from international destinations to Qatar upon commercial aircraft . Responsibility ALL HMCAS RETRIEVAL SERVICES PERSONNEL 1. General process pertaining to the transport of patients upon commercial aircraft : 1.1 The request for Retrieval Service (RS) escort for the repatriation of patients from International Destination to Qatar upon commercial aircraft may come from a number of sources. Regardless of the source of financial approval, the process outlined in this SOP must be followed to ensure the safe transport of patients. 2. Communication with External Parties/Agencies : 2.1 All communication with external parties /agencies should ideally be via the Retrievals Desk. 2.2 All internal and external email communication should include the “Retrieval Service Mi

ssion Coordination Team” email group members: 2.2.1 Retrievals Desk 2.2.2 Hamad General Hospital MICU Retrieval Consultant 2.2.3 Operat ions Manager - CCS 2.2.4 Clinical Coordinator 2.2.5 Sierra Retrieval (all) 2.3 Direct communication with external agencies may be conducted by Retrieval Service team members in instances where complex details need to be explained. 2.4 If communication with an external party i s required to be held without involving the Retrievals Desk, a summary email summarizing the outcome of the communication is required to be sent to the Retrieval Service Mission Coordination Team mail group. 3. Recording of Communication : 3.1 All communication wi th internal (HMCAS) or external parties/sponsors is to be recorded. 3.2 All paper records must be scanned and filed. 3.3 All electronic documents must be emailed to the Retrievals Desk to be saved on to the CAD. 3.4 Any information passed via phone must be put into email and sent to the receiving person(s) as soon as possible after the phone conversation. 3.4.1 All RS personnel are to encourage external parties to do this also. 3.4.2 If an external party is unable to provide written confirmation via email/fax or hard

copy, it is the RS member’s responsibility to put the details into email and send it to the relevant person for confirmation. 4. Mission Sponsorship/Financial Responsibilities : 4.1 Financial sponsorship of the patient’s treatment, travel and medical escort costs may be from (but not limited to) the following parties: 4.1.1 Hamad Committee for Treatment Abroad (HCTA)

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 489 4.1.2 International Medical Affairs Office (IMAO) 4.1.3 Supreme Committee for Treatment Abroad (SCTA) 4.1.4 Ministerial Committee for Treatment Abroad (MCTA) 4.1.5 Third Party 4.2 The reques ting party is responsible for all financial costs repatriating (see point 4.1) the patient including: 4.2.1 Flight tickets of escorting medical staff 4.2.2 The payment of excess baggage 4.3 Any agreement for HMCAS to cover costs requires authorization of the AED – Clinica l Services. 5. Repatriation Mission Request Notification : 5.1 Notification of a requirement for RS medical escort, regardless of source, must be made in writing to the Retrieval Desk. This may be via email, fax or hand delivered letter. 5.2 Any documents received by hand or fax must be scanned and sent to the retrievals desk by email for filing. 5.3 Any member of the HMC Ambulance Service, who receives a request for RS escort on commercial aircraft, must be passed to the Retrievals Desk. 5.4 The Retrieval Desk will automatica lly assign a “GOAL ID” upon receipt of notification of any patient who is intended to travel with Retrieval Services. 6. Clinical Approval

: 6.1 Upon receipt of the request for escort, the Clinical Coordinator shall gather all pertinent clinical information regar ding the patient. If available details are not sufficient, this may require a conference call to the treating physician. 6.1.1 Conference call should include Retrieval Consultant or designee and the treating physician abroad. 6.1.2 The Retrieval Consultant may assign the Clinical Coordinator to do the conference call and report back to him with clinical details via email. 6.2 The Retrieval Consultant will provide provisional clinical approval of RS personnel to escort the patient. 6.3 After review of the clinical information presented by the Clinical Coordinator, and the patient is suitable to travel upon a commercial aircraft, th e Retrieval Consultant will provide the following information to the requesting party via email: 6.3.1 The recommended skill level of care required to manage the patient during the flight (e.g. personnel skilled in the use of ventilator and capable of intensive care support). And/or: 6.3.2 The recommended team composition to manage the patient during the flight. 6.3.3 The type of seat configuration required to manage the patient (i

f required). 6.3.4 The Retrieval Consultant may delegate the responsibility of this correspondence to the Clinical Coordinator or other RS team member. 6.4 The Retrieval Consultant may deny further RS involvement in the escort of patients if:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 490 6.4.1 The patient is defined as “low risk”. i.e. the patients’ clinical condition does not require Critical Care Paramedic l evel of care. 6.4.2 The patient is not “fit to fly”. i.e. the patients’ clinical condition makes the patient unsuitable for travel upon commercial aircraft. 6.4.3 The case is assigned to a different department e.g. Heart Hospital or Trauma ICU. 6.5 If the patient is “not fit to fly” the Retrieval Consultant will send the rationale to the Mission Sponsor with a recommendation of how to proceed with the patient’s transport (e.g. implement stabilization measures and reassess at a later date/change of destination/arrange for p rivate air ambulance). 6.6 Patients may require liaison between the Retrieval Consultant and other specialists in order to prepare the patient for the flight/implement stabilization measures. 6.6.1 The Retrieval Consultant may send the required correspondence with these persons directly or delegate this responsibility to the Clinical Coordinator or other RS team members. 6.7 If the patient is “fit to fly” but not suitable for Commercial aircraft (e.g. bariatric) then the Retri

eval Consultant together with RS team, shall make a recommendation on what aircraft type to use to the requesting committee. 7. Medical Information Form (MEDIF): 7.1 The current treating physician abroad shall be responsible for completing a MEDIF accurately and then send it to Qatar Airways. See Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 1 . 7.2 The current treating physician who completes the MEDIF shall also send a copy of the MEDIF to Retrievals Desk. Retrievals Desk shall liaise with the treating physician to email a copy. 7.3 MEDIFs should be completed within the given timeframe requested by the airline. 7.4 No MEDIF is to be provided to a patient or family member without it first being sent to the airline. 7.5 Retrieval Consultant should be notified immediately once a MEDIF has been c ompleted by the treating physician and be given the MEDIF Number. 8. Mission Sponsor Approval : 8.1 The requesting party will confirm that the requirements for RS escort have been met (e.g. team composition and/or other specific requirements). 8.2 If the requesting pa rty does not meet the requirements for RS escort (e.g. approval of a team less than the Retrieval Consultant recommended team composition), the Retrie

val Consultant, Clinical Coordinator, Sierra Retrieval, and Operations Manager - CCS will jointly agree ho w the escort will proceed. 9. Mission Preparation: Initial Patient Visit : 9.1 Initial patient visit will be the conference call between Retrieval Consultant and the treating physician abroad. 9.2 All clinical details will be emailed to the Clinical Coordinator to keep record. 9.3 When a flight date has been confirmed, a conference call should be conducted at least 2 days prior to flight to ensure the patient is still clinically “fit for flight”. 9.4 Any change in clinical condition should then be emailed to the requesting party. 10. Mission Preparation: Logistics :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 491 10.1 Sierra Retrieval will work with the Mission Sponsor to organize the booking of flights. 10.1.1 Sierra Retrieval will inform the sponsoring party of available flight dates and names. 10.1.2 Return flights shall be boo ked in accordance with the agreed planned date to return the patient to Qatar. 10.2 Where required (and if not already assigned), Sierra Retrieval will request from the sponsoring party the names of additional medical escorts (Doctors, Nurses and Respiratory Th erapists etc.) who shall escort the patient. 10.3 Sierra Retrieval will coordinate preparation of the required equipment for the mission. 11. Mission Communications : 11.1 The Retrievals Desk will start a Mission Microsoft TEAMS group for the mission prior to the Pre - Fli ght visit being conducted. 11.1.1 The TEAMS Group will be titled with the GOAL ID of the patient, the point of origin and the destination of the flight. 11.1.2 The group will include the following members: 11.1.2.1 All mission medical crew 11.1.2.2 Sierra Retrieval 11.1.2.3 Clinical Coordinator 11.1.2.4 Retrieval Consultant 11.1.2.5 Operations Manager - CCS 12. Mission Prepa

ration: Pre - flight patient visit : 12.1 A final conference call will be conducted 2 days prior to team departure from Qatar. 12.2 The same criteria outlined in point 9 above will be reassessed. 12.3 If the patie nt has deteriorated and is therefore not “fit to fly” the Retrieval Consultant will notify the requesting committee if the case is to be postponed or cancelled due to the patient’s condition. 12.4 Sierra Retrieval will inform the Retrieval Service Mission Coord ination Team and the Retrieval Consultant of any operational changes which require the cancellation or postponement of the flight. 12.5 The Retrievals Desk will notify the sponsoring party of any requirement to cancel or postpone the mission. 12.6 I final call needs to be made by the Retrievals Desk to the referring facility 3 hours prior to the team departing from Doha. If the patient has then deteriorated this will give the team enough time to cancel the flight and put the mission on hold until the patient has been stabilized. 13. Mission Preparation: Receiving Facility Confirmation : 13.1 The Retrievals Desk must make contact with the receiving facility to ensure the patient is expected, prior to the team’s depa

rture from the referring destination. 13.2 The Retrievals Desk w ill email HGH Bed Management all patient details and relevant clinical records to ensure a bed has been booked in an appropriate unit. 13.3 The Retrievals Desk will notify mission TEAMS group that the receiving facility has confirmed they are expecting the pati ent. 14. Mission Preparation: Medical Attaché Confirmation :

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 492 14.1 The Retrievals Desk must make contact directly with the Medical Attaché (or assigned attaché coordinator) and outline any specific transport requirements. 14.1.1 The Medical Attaché Request document template (RS.O.0009) should be used for this purpose. See Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 2 . 14.2 Telephonic or email confirmation from the attaché must have been received by the Retrievals Desk prior to departure from Doha. 14.3 The Retr ievals Desk will notify the mission TEAMS group that confirmation has been received. 15. Mission Preparation: Emergency Contact Details : 15.1 The Retrievals Desk will ensure that all medical crew members accompanying the patient outside of RS have completed the Eme rgency Contact Details form (RS.O.0006) prior to departure. See Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 3 . 16. Mission Approval: Mission Tasking document : 16.1 A Tasking document (RS 2016 01 Ver 1.2) is required to be completed for every commercial expatriation mission. See Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 4 . 16.2 The Retrievals Desk is responsible for ensuring the Tasking Form is completed and may contact any person within and outside of HMCAS in order to at

tain all the required information. 16.2.1 Before contacting the Mission Sponsor, the Retrievals Desk should first contact Sierra Retrieval to attain the required details. 16.3 The Tasking document shall be submitted a minimum of 24 hours prior, where possible, to the flight departure time. 16.4 The Tasking document is to be submitted to the Operations Manager – CCS or designee for approval. 16.5 Operations Manager – CCS or designee will email the approved Tasking Form to the Retrievals Desk. 16.6 Retrievals Desk shall email the Task ing document to AED - Clinical Services, SOM – CCS, Ops Manager – CCS, and SME Clinical Desk. 17. High Risk Mission Considerations : 17.1 Patients who are deemed to be at high risk of deteriorating during flight, but due to outside influence will continue to travel th e following is to be conducted. 17.1.1 The Operations Manager - CCS on - call must be notified. 17.1.2 The patient and/or family must be informed of the risks of flight. 17.1.3 The Retrieval Consultant (or designee) must confirm with the patient and family that the flight can’t be diverted. 17.1.4 The patient (or authorised family member) must sign the Acknowledgement of Agreement on High

Risk Travel (See Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 7 ). 17.1.5 The Retrieval Consultant will inform the carryin g airline of the patient’s condition and any requirements for in flight diversion. 17.1.6 Confirmation must be obtained from the carrying airline stating that they have agreed that the patient may still travel on board the flight. 18. Mission Team Leader Pre - Departur e Responsibilities : 18.1 A designated Team Leader will be appointed once the mission has been confirmed and the team selected.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 493 18.2 The Mission Team Leader is required to provide a timeline for the major mission steps: 18.2.1 Time of team rendezvous (and location) 18.2.2 Time of departure to airport – HIA 18.2.3 Time of arrival at the airport – Destination 18.2.4 Approximate time of customs/immigration clearance at destination (in destination time) 18.2.5 Time at Hotel 18.2.6 Time at patient side 18.2.7 Time departing patient to Airport 18.2.8 Wheels up time 18.2.9 Time at dest ination Doha 18.3 The Mission Team Leader should also outline the following requirements to the Retrievals Desk: 18.3.1 Ambulance requirements (both local and at destination) 18.3.2 Destination transport requirements from the Medical Attaché 18.3.3 Any additional requirements 18.4 The M ission Team Leader is required to provide the following information to the Retrievals Desk: 18.4.1 Hotel/Accommodation details 18.5 The Mission Team Leader should also assist the Retrievals Desk in ensuring all other mission team members (both RS and non - RS members) h ave completed their responsibilities outlined in point 19 below. 19. Mission Team Members Pre - Departure Responsibili

ties : 19.1 Mission Team Members must provide the following information to the Retrievals Desk: 19.1.1 Provide the Retrievals Desk their Hotel/Accommodation details. 19.1.2 Provide the Retrievals Desk their return flight booking details. 19.2 The Retrievals Desk is responsible for ensuring that these details are attained prior to departure. 19.3 These details should be entered into the mission TEAMS group. 20. Mission Pre - departure Tasks : 20.1 The Retrieval Desk will arrange local ambulances as required. 20.2 Sierra Retrieval will ensure all equipment is prepared. 21. Mission Execution : Mission Communications: 21.1 The mission team leader shall ensure the following notifications a re put on the mission TEAMS group: 21.1.1 At team rendezvous location 21.1.2 Departing to Airport 21.1.3 At airport 21.1.4 Cleared Immigration/Customs

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 494 21.1.5 At Gate 21.1.6 On Board Aircraft 21.1.7 Departing/Wheels up 21.1.8 Arrived at Destination/Wheels Down 21.1.9 Disembarked aircraft 21.1.10 At Hotel 21.1.11 Departing Hotel for p atient 21.1.12 At patient side 21.1.13 Loading patient into ambulance 21.1.14 Departing for Airport 21.1.15 Awaiting Boarding 21.1.16 Boarded aircraft, patient loaded 21.1.17 Wheels up 21.1.18 Wheels Down 21.1.19 Cleared Customs/Immigration 21.1.20 With HMCAS Ambulance, Departing for HGH 21.1.21 At HGH, patient handover being completed 21.1.22 Retrieval Service Mission Complete (once all equipment is returned) 21.2 The Retrieval Desk will continue to monitor the status of all members until all members have returned to Qatar. 21.2.1 The Retrieval Desk will notify “Mission Complete: All Team Members have returned to Qatar” once this has occurred. 22. Mission Execution : Safety Checks: 22.1 Safety checks need to be conducted by each mission member. 22.2 The mission team leader is responsible for informing non - Retrieval Service members of safety check requirements. 22.3 E ach team member should notify on the TEAMS group “Safety Check”. 22.

4 Safety Checks should be conducted every 24 hours until mission is completed. 22.5 Safety Check times can be adjusted by the team member as required provided the time of the next safety check in ti me is stated on the mission TEAMS group. 22.6 The Retrieval Desk is responsible for monitoring that all medical team members have conducted their safety checks at the designated time. 22.7 If a team member fails to check in: 22.7.1 The Retrievals desk must request a safety check update from the team member. 22.7.2 If the TEAMS message has not been delivered successfully (two “grey - ticks” on the message) the Retrievals Desk must send an SMS to the team member. 22.7.3 After 30 minutes of no contact, the Retrievals desk must call the team m ember on their mobile.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 495 22.7.4 If no contact is made, the Retrievals Desk must call the team members Hotel/Accommodation phone number. – A message should be left with the hotel reception if they are unable to contact. 22.7.5 If still no contact is made, the Retrievals De sk should notify Sierra Retrieval On call. 22.7.6 The Sierra Retrieval on call shall then escalate to the Operations Manager on call. 23. Mission Documentation : 23.1 Upon completion of the mission, the Mission Team Leader is to ensure that the EPCR is complete and that th e case is closed. 23.1.1 All mission documentation should be returned and no documentation are to remain in the possession of the clinical team. 23.1.2 Post Mission Case Reviews and Audits will be conducted in coordination with the Quality and Patient Safety Team . 24. Appendices : 24.1 Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 1: Medical Information Form (MEDIF) 24.2 Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 2: RS.O.0009 Medical Attaché Form 24.3 Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 3: RS.O.0006 Emergency Contact Details Form 24.4 Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 4: RS 2016 01 Ver 1.2 Mission Tasking Form 24.5 Appendix 5.4.A.3.2 - 7: CL711 Acknowledgement of Agreement on Hi
gh Risk Travel

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 496 SOP 5.4.A.8 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.A.8 EXTRACORPOREAL MEMBR ANE OXYGENATION (ECM O) PATIENT TRANSFERS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose Effective end to end management of Retrieval Transfers . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC E PERSONNEL 1. Role Description : 1.1 ECMO Transfers are conducted by Retrieval Service in conjunction with specialist personnel from Hamad Hospital MICU for the purpose of transporting ECMO patients between fixed facilities within the Sovereign Territory of the State of Qatar. 2. Crew Composition : 2.1 HMCAS will provide 2 x Ambulance Paramedics, augmented by a transfer team of hospital specialists and x 1 Retrieval Service CCP. 2.2 Sierra Retrieval shall be part of the team to ensure adequate transportation of Hospital ECMO Team and ECMO Equipment to the referring fac ility. 2.3 The Hamad Medical Corporation Hospital ECMO Team consist of x1 ECMO Consultant, x1 ECMO Nurse and x1 Perfusionist. 3. Equipment : 3.1 Retrieval Services will provide a ‘ParAid’ stretcher, ICU Ambulance

, and monitoring equipment. HGH MICU will provide ECMO S pecific equipment. 4. Activation time : 4.1 Retrieval Service crew and ambulance will be ready to begin the transfer within 90 minutes of activation. Rapid Response activation of initial team by Sierra Retrieval. Collect hospital team and move to referring facilit y. ICU Ambulance and rest of the team follow under 90 min with the rest of the equipment. 5. Activation decision : 5.1 Activation will be initiated on the order of the ECMO Consultant on - call. 6. Means of Activation : 6.1 ECMO transfers will be by telephone call to HCC Co mmunications Retrieval Desk. 7. Report Point : 7.1 The report point for pick - up of ECMO transfer team will be outside Hamad Hospital Accident and Emergency Department. 8. Logistics : 8.1 HMCAS will be responsible for post - use cleaning of the ambulance and fault reporting. HGH will be responsible for reconstituting and post use checks of the specialist equipment. 9. Daytime Service Provision : 9.1 Until the service has sufficient capacity to provide a dedicated vehicle between 07:00 and 19:00 the ECMO service will be provided by the Retrieval ambulance. 9.2 In case the Retrieval Ambulance is n
ot available, an ICU type IFT ambulance will be used. 9.3 Call Activation:

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 497 9.3.1 ECMO Consultant calls Retrievals Desk (RD) and activates ECMO case. 9.3.2 Retrievals Desk will inform Production and C linical Coordinator to activate Retrieval Vehicle and team. 9.3.3 Sierra Retrieval immediately goes to HGH ED to meet ECMO Team and proceed to referring facility. 9.3.4 Retrievals Team to be at HGH ED within 45min of activation to load equipment and go through pre - bri ef and checklist. 9.3.5 Rest of team to be ready to leave HGH ED within 90min of activation to referring facility. 10. Night Service Provision : 10.1 Until the service has sufficient capacity, ECMO calls at night will be serviced by Retrieval unit, subject to staff availa bility and Retrieval unit activity. 10.2 Activations outside of Retrieval unit operations should be escalated to the Ops Manager – CCS .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 498 SOP 5.4.A.9 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.A.9 AEROMEDICAL TRANSPOR T OF BARIATRIC PATIE NTS USING QEAF AIRCRAFT Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose Notify staff of the steps required to transport Bariatric Patients on QEAF Strategic Transport Aircraft . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC E PERSONNEL 1. Eligibility : 1.1 Subject to approval from the Ministry of Public Health, Bariatric patients that cannot be transported on commercial flights will be transported by HMCAS Retrieval Service using military aircraft. 2. Service Provision : 2.1 HMCAS Retrieval Service will provide a bedside to bedside service from home or hospital to receiving facility using ground and air transport platforms. 3. Vehicles : 3.1 By definition, these patients are too large to fit into standard ambulances. In the absence of dedicated ‘super - bariatric’ ambulances, the following vehicles are required to transport bariatric patients. 3.1.1 ICU Ambulance : For carriage of medical equipment and gases 3.1.2 Catering truck : For transport of

patient and bari atric bed 3.1.3 Low loader truck : For transportation of Fork Lift 3.1.4 Fork Lift : For loading of patient into catering truck 3.1.5 Sierra Retrieval vehicle : For transport of Operations Production staff and additional personnel as required. 3.1.6 Green Bus : For transport of additional hospital staff to Al Udeid Air Base. 3.1.7 Panel Van : For transportation of additional equipment if required. 4. Operational Phases : 4.1 Pre - assessment of patient by a competent medical authority (Medical Director/ICU Consultant). 4.2 Assessment of access and eg ress at patient’s home 4.3 Route reconnaissance from patient’s home to hospital 4.4 Admission to hospital for pre - flight work up (bed to be booked) 4.5 Arrangement of bed at receiving facility 4.6 Arrangement of flight, visas and exit permits for patient, escorting relatives, and staff. 4.7 Movement to hospital 4.8 Pre - flight work up 4.9 Movement to Al Udeid Air Base 4.10 Aeromedical transport 4.11 Handover to receiving hospital team

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 499 4.12 Transport to receiving facility 4.13 Recovery of team and equipment to Doha 5. Communications : 5.1 Command a nd Control will be executed through Channel Tango 4. 5.2 Standard call signs will be used. Additional non - standard call - signs will be agreed during mission planning. 6. Command and Control : 6.1 Mission Team Leader will be responsible for all aspects of tactical comma nd and control. 7. Recording and Reporting : 7.1 Retrievals Desk will be responsible for tracking and recording mission activity and providing situational updates to all call signs. 8. Escalation : 8.1 Problems and untoward events that cannot be resolved at the tactical l evel must be escalated by the team leader to Sierra Retrieval on call or Ops Manager - CCS. 9. Further Guidance : 9.1 These missions are sufficiently complex to require detailed planning and briefing and firm command and control throughout all phases of execution. Further guidance is contained in Retrieval Services Standing Contingency Plan for Aeromedical Transport of Bariatric Patients by Qatar Emiri Air Force Strategic Transport Assets .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 500 SOP 5.4.A.10 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.A.10 AEROMEDICAL LOGISTI C PLANNING CONSIDERA TIONS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To provide guidance for planning logistic aspects of aeromedical missions . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC E FW FLIGHT PERSONNE L 1. Team Leader : 1.1 Retrieval Service Critical Care Paramedics are responsible for planning logistic aspects of international aeromedical missions. This SOP gives guidance on considerations that inform logistic planning. 2. General Principles : 2.1 Extended duration Inter - Facility Missions are to be planned as bedside to bedside transports with the logistic support plan conforming to the operational plan. The principles of logistic sup port are: 2.1.1 Simplicity . The more complex the logistic support plan is, the more likely it is to fail. 2.1.2 Conformity . The logistic plan must conform to and synchronize with the operational plan. 2.1.3 Economy . Manpower and equipment are limited resources; the logistic support plan should be as lean as possib

le, avoiding unnecessary use of staff, time, space, and resources. 2.1.4 Flexibility . The logistic plan must be flexible enough to deal with unexpected demands and changes to the operational plan. 2.1.5 Foresight . The logistic plan must anticipate likely eventualities resulting from the operational plan. 2.1.6 Robustness . The logistic plan must have in - built resilience to withstand increased demand. 3. Logistic Considerations : 3.1 Logistic considerations to support Inter - Facility Missions mu st take account of the ‘4Ds’ of Logistic Support: 3.1.1 Destination : 3.1.1.1 The following factors can be considered under ‘Destination’: 3.1.1.1.1 What is the destination of the mission? 3.1.1.1.2 What is the climate/temperature? 3.1.1.1.3 What local healthcare provision can be used? 3.1.1.1.4 Where is the receiving facility? 3.1.1.1.5 Where is the most appropriate Airport of Disembarkation in relation to the receiving facility? 3.1.1.1.6 What are the best routes for ground transport – in Qatar, at destination? 3.1.1.1.7 Are there any endemic diseases/health warnings in force at the destination?

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 501 3.1.1.1.8 What airports might be used enroute for Technical Stops or Emergency Diverts? 3.1.2 Duration : 3.1.2.1 The following factors can be considered under ‘Duration’: 3.1.2.1.1 What is the expected total mission duration? 3.1.2.1.2 What is the flying time/journey time? 3.1.2.1.3 What is the expected time of each leg of the journey? 3.1.2.1.4 What is the expected duration of planned Technical Stops? 3.1.2.1.5 What is the expected duration of any delays (e.g. clearing immigration/customs etc. 3.1.2.1.6 What is the potential impact of duration on cold - chain items? 3.1.3 Distance : 3.1.3.1 The following factors can be considered under ‘Distance’: 3.1.3.1.1 Transit times between facilities in destination country and within the State of Qatar. 3.1.3.1.2 Location of Emergency Departments on route in case of emergency stops during road moves. 3.1.3.1.3 Fuel requirements for road moves. 3.1.3.1.4 Total mission time and safe working hours – need for additional team and pre - positioning for multi - sector journeys. 3.1.3.1.5 Traffic conditions and requirement for additional escort. 3.1.4 Demand : The following factors can

be considered under ‘Demand’: 3.1.4.1 Number of patients and level of care required 3.1.4.2 Additional standard equipment requirements (more of the same) 3.1.4.3 Specialist equipment requirements (e.g. NICU/Bariatric eqpt) 3.1.4.4 Medical gas requirements 3.1.4.5 IV Fluids 3.1.4.6 Non - standard pharmaceuticals 3.1.4.7 Medical consumables 3.1.4.8 Dangerous Air Cargo 3.1.4.9 Accommodation, feeding and transport requirements for personnel. 4. Medical Logistics : 4.1 For medical logistic planning, the ‘4C’s can be asked of each of the ‘4C’s: 4.1.1 Capacity : 4.1.1.1 Is there a bed available at the receiving facility? If not, are there alternative facilities that can accept the patient? 4.1.1.2 What transport platform is required to accommodate the patient, the team(s) and accompanying family members?

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 502 4.1.1.3 Is there a requirement for additional vehicles for elements of the journey (e.g . panel van, green bus etc.)? 4.1.2 Capability : 4.1.2.1 Do the transporting platforms and the receiving facility have capability required to meet the needs of the patient? 4.1.2.2 What is the skillset and team composition required to provide optimum care for the patient? 4.1.3 Contin uity : 4.1.3.1 The mission is to be planned as a bedside to bedside transport. 4.1.3.2 Is there a need for technical stops at facilities enroute? 4.1.3.3 Is there a need for additional teams or pre - positioned teams? 4.1.4 Convenience : 4.1.4.1 Are there closer facilities that offer the same capa bility for the patient with reduced risk associated with longer distances to be travelled? 4.1.4.2 Does a particular facility offer greater convenience for the patient? 5. Logistic Push/Pull : 5.1 Once initiated, Retrievals Service operations must be self - sufficient. This implies a degree of ‘over provision’ to be able to cater for unexpected events and delays. The governing principle is that on routine aeromedical missions, sufficient stocks are carried t

o cater for 24 hours of operation. 5.2 At the end o f each mission, replenishment will be provided based on stocks consumed. 6. Cargo Manifesting : 6.1 As the Retrievals Service matures, all standard medical modules are to be weighed and manifested to allow Air Load Masters, ground staff and aircrew to complete wei ght and balance reports. 7. Dangerous Air Cargo : 7.1 A list of Dangerous Air Cargo is to be provided 8. Prohibited items : 8.1 Prohibited items are not to be carried 9. IT Equipment : 9.1 Regardless of destination, all IT equipment, including cell phones, is to be fully charged at commencement of the mission. 10. Transport of vehicles : 10.1 If vehicles are to be transported on military transport aircraft, fuel tanks are to be ¼ full and the condition of tires inspected to ensure that there is no damage and no punctures. Vehicles will be e mbarked under the strict control of the Air Load Master. 11. Controlled Medications : 11.1 Personnel carrying controlled medications must be in possession of an official letter from the Ministry of Public Health stating their qualification and requirement to carry t hem. Utmost care must be taken of controlled medications, including s
afeguarding them from theft, loss, or

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 503 damage. Controlled Medications may only be carried in the box provided by Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service. 12. Passenger Manifest : 12.1 A passenge r manifest must be completed to include details of medical personnel, patients and relatives/friends accompanying and Passport, Qatar ID number and Residence Permit and Exit/Entry visa details .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 504 SOP 5.4.A.13 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.A.13 EMERGENCY RATION PA CKS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To provide guidance on direction on the use of emergency ration packs. Responsibility RETRIEVAL SERVICE AE ROMEDICAL PERSONNEL 1. Purpose : 1.1 Emergency Rations are provided to Retrieval Service personnel undertaking aeromedical retrievals on the following platforms: 1.1.1 Qatar Emiri Air Force fixed wing aircraft 1.1.2 LifeFlight AW139 rotary wing aircraft 1.2 These rations are for emergency use only and are not to be consumed as routine . Examples of emergency situations include: 1.2.1 Aircraft out of service in mission with delayed return 1.2.2 Aircraft crash leading to a survival situation 1.3 The followi ng do not constitute emergency situations: 1.3.1 Delay in departure from Airport of Embarkation in Qatar 2. Ration Contents : 2.1 The emergency ration provides: 2.1.1 2 x ‘Meals Ready to Eat’ (MRE) per person 2.1.2 3 x litres of water per person 2.2 Each MRE contains sundry items and a self - heating

pack. 3. Use of the MRE : 3.1 All contents of the MRS can be eaten cold, but the main meal items taste better cooked. 3.2 Instructions for the use of the heater are included in the pack and are self - explanatory. 3.3 In the absence of a rock, the user wi ll need to use their discretion as to what constitutes a suitable “or something” (note, do not use flammable material). 3.4 MRE heaters should not be used on - board the aircraft. 4. Safety considerations : 4.1 Thermal injury can result from hot food and liquids. 4.2 MRE he aters are classed as ‘Dangerous Air Cargo’ and must not be from their packaging in - flight. 5. Routine meals : 5.1 As part of their personal mission preparation, staff should carry 2 x meals and sufficient water for 12 hours duration at their own expense. 5.2 Poor pers onal preparation does not constitute reasonable grounds for consumption of emergency rations.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 508 SOP 5.4.E.1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Service (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.E.1 RETRIEVAL SERVICE O PERATIONAL CURRENCY Implementation Date 13 March 2018 Purpose To ensure that operational staff are operationally competent, assessed and signed off before taking on any Retrieval Service Case/Mission . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC E STAFF 1. Purpose : 1.1 Retrieval Service is providing patient transport across multiple platforms locally and internationally. Critical Care Paramedics (CCP) and Ambulance Paramedic (AP) transporting patients within Retrieval Services shall be operationally signed off before performing duties. 2. Aeromedical Retrieval Operational Requirements : 2.1 All RS CCP’s and AP’s shall be orientated annually on the following aircrafts: 2.1.1 C130 2.1.2 C17 2.1.3 Qatar Airways Commercial Aircraft 2.2 All RS CCP’s shall be orientated annually on the following aircraft: 2.2.1 A7MED 2.2.2 AW139 2.3 For all Commercial/C130 and C17 missions, both CCP’s and AP’s assigned to their first mission will be paired with another experienced RS C

CP whom will assess the following: 2.3.1 The CCP demonstrates competence in leadership skills throughout the mission. 2.3.2 The CCP/AP clearly demonstrates knowledge of equipment, logistics and missio n specific requirements. 2.3.3 The CCP/AP clearly demonstrates adequate knowledge with regards to mission administrative matters i.e. Narcotic letter, SCH letter, MSDS Form, PCR/ePCR and MEDIF’S. 2.3.4 The CCP/AP demonstrates the ability and confidence to perform when experienced staff are not immediately available for consultation. 2.3.5 Ensures proper planning and preparation of the mission prior to take - off. 2.3.6 The CCP/AP understands and demonstrates the importance of continuous liaison with the family and/or patient during flight. 2.3.7 *Unless approved by Assistant Executive Director that the staff member can operate without being paired on his/her first mission. 2.4 Upon completion of the mission the experienced RS CCP shall complete the Operational Competency Sheet (Appendix A, nex t page) and report back to the Operations Manager - CCS on operational readiness or remedial action to be taken. 2.5 Visa requirements: 2.5.1 Retrieval Service Staff require entry to the followi
ng countries: 2.5.1.1 UK

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 509 2.5.1.2 Schengen zone 2.5.1.3 USA 2.5.2 Adhoc Visas might be required 2.5.3 Retriev al staff are responsible for their visas and are to ensure that they maintain the validity thereof. 2.5.4 Staff should keep their personnel file up to date on the shared drive with valid and updated documentation. 3. Ground Operational Requirements : 3.1 All CCP’s and AP’s will undergo orientation on all transport platforms together with experienced staff and Sierra Retrieval. 3.2 All newly joined staff shall work with experienced crew until they are operationally and clinically signed off by the Operations Manager. 3.3 All newly joined staff shall work a minimum of six months on the ground operations platforms before being assigned to an Aeromedical Retrieval, unless otherwise approved by the Operations Manager .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 519 SOP 5.4.F.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.F.4 MANAGEMENT AND ADMI NISTRATION OF BLOOD PRODUCTS Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure RS Critical Care Paramedics and Doctors manage HMCAS blood products as per established safety and best practice guidelines . Responsibility ALL RETRIEVAL SERVIC E FLIGHT STAFF 1. Provision of Blood Products : 1.1 HMC Blood Bank provides the following blood products for use by HMCAS Retrieval Service: 1.1.1 Two (2) x packs of three (3) units of ‘O’ Positive Red Blood Cells in an appropriate container. 2. Storage and Cold Chain Integrity : 2.1 The container will be capable of maintaining blood products at the required temperature for a period of up to 12 hours. 2.2 Staff must maintain cold chain integrity through sound management and monitorin g in accordance with SOP 5.4.G.3 . 3. Expansion of Provision : 3.1 HMC Blood Bank has the means to scale up the number of packs provided to a maximum of 10 packs upon request. 3.2 Other blood products can be provided on a case - by - ca

se basis, including but not limited to ‘O’ Negative blood and Fresh Frozen Plasma. 3.3 Requests for additional packs or provision of non - standard products may only be made by the senior clinician in charge of the case. 4. Procedure: 4.1 Requests are made as soon as possible after the requirement has been identified. 4.2 The requester must provide a clear statement of the blood products required and a pick - up time. 4.3 Persons collecting the blood products must be in possession of Corporate ID. 4.4 Whilst is it acknowledged that patient details may be scant or non - existent, all documentation must be completed as fully as possible. 4.5 At sign out, the collecting person must confirm that the content of the container is correct. 4.6 Blood products are to be handled appropriately including protection from heat and direct sunlight. 4.7 The container must not be opened until such time as the blood products are to be transfused. 4.8 The decision to transfuse rests with the senior clinician in charge of the case. 4.9 Prior to transfu sion, the patient’s serum haemoglobin must be checked using the iStat analyser. 4.10 Prior to transfusion, a 3 ml ‘type and screen’/’group and save’ spe

cimen must be obtained from the patient and stored in a purple top specimen tube. 4.11 The specimen must be labell ed with three of the following means of identification (name, HMC number, Date of Birth, QID number).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 520 4.12 Blood may only be transfused using a standard giving set incorporating a 170 – 200 micron filter. 4.13 Transfusion must be commenced within 12 hours of the con tainer packing time. 4.14 Units must be transfused within 4 hours of removing the blood unit from the container. 4.15 Details of the transfusion must be recorded as per policy in the Patient Care Report form and on other clinical records as required. 4.16 Adverse transfu sion reactions are to be documented in the Patient Care Report form and reported to the on - call Transfusion Physician on return. AERS Process with documentation 4.17 The patient’s condition must be monitored, with observations recorded every 10 minutes from com mencement of the transfusion until 30 minutes post transfusion. 4.18 Unused units, the container and specimens are to be returned to the Blood Bank at completion of the mission. 4.19 Details of the transfusion must be entered into the registry of blood products tran sfused during aeromedical missions .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 521 SOP 5.4.F.6 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Scheduled Ambulance Service (SAS) and Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.F.6 PATIENT HANDOVER & ENDORSEMENT Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To facilitate accurate transfer of information and safe and effective transition of care within HMCAS and to healthcare facilities . Responsibility ALL AMBULANCE SERVIC E STAFF & MEDICAL DOCTORS 1. Handover occurs every time the care for a patient is transferred from one healthcare provider to another, either within the Ambulance Service or to another healthcare facility (HCF). Every patient must be formally handed over and received though verbal and/or written communication. 2. For higher acuity cases, both the CCP/AP, as team leader, and the Doctor, as Clinical Leader, should receive report from the sending facility staff. 3. For higher acuity cases, both the CCP/AP and the Doctor should take part in giving the handover to the receiving facility. Be sure to include the receiving doctor as well as the receiving RN so that no patient information is missed, and pat

ient care is not compromised. 4. Use the following mnemonic to g uide verbal handover : I Identify the patients age, gender, and time of onset of complaint. M Describe the Mechanism of Injury or current Medical Complaint. I List the Injuries sustained (trauma patients) or information ab. S List the Signs and Symptoms. T List the Treatment given and response thereto. A List any Allergies the patient has. M List any Medications the patient is taking. B Summarize the patient’s Background , i.e. Past Medical History. O Describe any Other issues , e.g. social situation, organ donation. 4.1 On completion of handover, ensure that the receiving healthcare provider signs the Patient Care Report. 5. Pre - arrival notification for seriously ill or injured patients: 5.1 Because we are taking part in stable inter - facility transfers it is unnecessary to contact the Clinical Desk prior to arrival at the receiving facility . 5.2 The AP driving the ambulance should remain in contact with the control room as per HMCAS SOP 4.5.C . 5.3 If the patient’s condition deteriorates while in transit Clinical Desk should be contacted so that the receiving facility can be notified accordingly. 6. Upon arrival

at the HCF, the HMCAS treating clinician shall do the following: 6.1 Avoid using abbreviated words during the handover. 6.2 Identify and verbally confirm which receiving HCF doctor or nurse will accept responsibility for the continued care of the patient.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 522 6.3 Clearly identify self as the treating clinician. 6.4 Introduce the patient’s family/friends/colleagues and commence with the verbal handover of the patient. 6.5 Use the mnemonic “IMIST - AMBO” to guide the patient handover. 6.6 Assist the HCF doctor/nurse receiving the patient within clinical scope of practice. 6.7 Ensure efficient transfer of the patient to alternate monitoring and treatment devices, minimizing interruption of the clinical treatment being administered to the patient. 6.8 The HMCAS treating clinician should briefly re - evaluate the patient’s clinical presentation during the handover. If the patient’s clinical presentation or parameters differ significantly from the initia l handover then this could indicate a pre - morbid state and should be highlighted to the receiving doctor/nurse and documented accordingly in the “Treatment” section of the PCR. 6.9 Once the receiving HCF doctor or nurse has indicated that they are satisfied wi th the handover, ensure that they sign the PCR to acknowledge receipt and responsibility for the patient. If the HCF doctor or nurse’s name and HMC Staff number is illeg
ible then request clarification. 6.10 Remove HMCAS equipment that is not needed and notify t he NCC of unit availability accordingly .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 526 SOP 5.4.G.1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Schedule Ambulance Service (SAS) and Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.G.1 USE OF THE PARAID S TRETCHER Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To ensure safe and proper operation of the ParAid Stretcher. Responsibility ALL AMBULANCE SERVIC E PERSONNEL 1. The ParAid stretcher must only be used on the new WAS ambulances. Together, the ambulances and the ParAid stretchers are used to transport NICU, PICU and ICU SHAARP transfers. 2. At the shift start, ensure that the ParAid stretcher is checked and ready for service : 2.1 Assess the ParAid and equipment for soiling. 2.2 Where there is soiling from body - substances ( pooling or large splashes of body fluids on non - porous equipment or vehicle surfaces and any body fluids on porous equipment or vehicle surfaces ), clean the area following ambulance protocol ( SOP 2.3 ). 2.3 Check all equipment (e.g. suction, infusion drivers, etc.) that are attached to stretcher. 2.4 Check that oxygen cylinders are full or within acceptable range. 3. Loading patients onto Par

Aid Stretcher : 3.1 The ParAid stretcher should be placed parallel to the hospital stretcher. 3.2 Once all IV lines, ETT, Foley, etc. are secured, use a minimum of 4 people (5 people if patient has ETT) to slide patient onto the ParAid Stretcher. 3.3 Patient should be plac ed in a position of comfort, covered with blanket or blue sheet, and secured using seatbelts (e.g. chest, waist, and legs). Intubated patients must be temporarily disconnected from the ventilator to allow the seatbelt to be secured over the patient’s chest . 4. Securing Equipment to stretcher : 4.1 All electro medical devices must be secured to the stretcher prior to moving the patient. 4.1.1 Space Stations are to be secured to the vertical poles under the ParAid. Confirm that individual IV drivers and Syringe Drivers are locked into Space Stations. Ensure that Space Stations are plugged into power source. 4.1.2 Spare Monitor is to be secured in place and plugged into power source. 4.1.3 Ventilator should be mounted at the head of the stretcher with oxygen tubing running on the outside of the stretcher to the oxygen cylinders. 4.1.4 Primary monitor will be hung on the side of the stretcher until it is inside the

ambulance where it will be secured in the monitor cabinet. 4.1.5 Suction unit is to be secured with straps. 5. Loading the ParAid into the ambulance : 5.1 Stretcher must be loaded onto the lift head first. 5.2 All 4 brakes need to be locked prior to moving the lift up or down . 5.3 When the stretcher is locked in place inside the ambulance, main ParAid power must be plugged into ambulance wall outlet. 5.4 When the stretcher is locked into place inside the ambulance, portable oxygen must be switched to the main ambulance oxygen.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 527 6. Perform simple decontamination after every patient encounter: 6.1 Assess equipment and work surfaces for significant visible body - subs tances soiling; where found, exchange the equipment for reserve equipment. 6.2 Where there is no significant visible soiling, perform simple decontamination on all working surfaces and non - critical equipment (i.e. equipment that does not penetrate the skin and is not used on broken skin or mucous membranes ), including: 6.2.1 Monitor - defibrillator and attachments 6.2.2 Ventilator, Space Stations 6.2.3 Immobilization equipment 6.2.4 Stretcher and cabinet handles

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 528 SOP 5.4.G.2 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.G.2 SECURING OF EQUIPME NT DURING TRANSFER Implementation Date 01 October 2016 Purpose To provide guidance on direction on the carriage and stowage of equipment during patient transfers . Responsibility RETRIEVAL SERVICE CR ITICAL CARE PARAMEDI CS & TEAM LEADERS 1. Application : 1.1 This SOP applies to the securing of electro - medical equipment on all RS platforms to maximize patient and crew safety. 2. General Principles : 2.1 All equipment required for the retrieval is to be secured for transit using appropriate restraints and mounting brackets. 2.2 If mounting brackets are not available, they must be obtained from Operations Production. 2.3 Oxygen cylinders must be secured in the space provided on respective platforms prior to movement. 2.4 If the transfer is being conducted using a Stryker stretcher, oxygen cylinders must be secured to the stretcher to avoid being dropped. 3. Infusion Pumps and Syringe Drivers : 3.1 During transport o r movement, B - Braun infusion pumps mu

st either be housed in the charging unit and secured to a central pole on the stretcher using the standard bracket or secured safely in the space provided within the stretcher module. 3.2 When not in use, B - Braun infusion p umps must be housed in the charging unit. 4. Suction Units : 4.1 Suction units are to be secured in the space provided within the stretcher or mounted to a bracket on the ambulance during transportation. 5. Monitors and Defibrillators : 5.1 During transport or movement, m onitors and defibrillators must be secured to an appropriate bracket on the stretcher or securely attached to the mounting device available in the stretcher module. 5.2 When in use on a platform where a stretcher is not needed, the monitor should be secured sa fely to a fixed object with restraints or placed safely in an overhead compartment. 6. Ventilators : 6.1 If used during patient transport, ventilators are to be mounted on the appropriate retaining bracket of the stretcher being used. 6.2 Prior to movement of transpor t, staff must ensure that the ventilator is securely fitted to the bracket. 6.3 Where no bracket is available, the ventilator must be appropriately secured to a fixed obje
ct in a manner that will prevent unwanted movement and associated risk to the patient. 7. Stowage : 7.1 Medical equipment, packs and bags must be stowed safety during transportation.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 529 SOP 5.4.G.3 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.G.3 COLD CHAIN MEDICATION USE AND SIGN OUT BY RS CRITICAL CARE PARAMEDICS & DO CTORS Implementation Date 04 March 2020 Purpose To ensure RS Critical Care Paramedics and Doctors manage HMCAS cold chain medications as per established safety and best practice guidelines . Responsibility RETRIEVAL SERVICES C RITICAL CARE PARAMED ICS & DOCTORS 1. General Processes pertaining to ‘Cold Chain’ medications : 1.1 Critical Care Paramedics and Doctors working for Retrieval Services should follow an established ‘cold chain’ policy during the use and transport of medications requiring controlled refrigeration. This policy includes how to withdraw medications from the HMCAS medication refrigerator, packaging, and safe transport of ‘cold chain’ medications, re - stocking an d returning medications after use, and issues requiring escalation or reporting (such as the breach of temperature control range). (Ref. CL 6052). 2. Cold Chain Defined: 2.1 The ‘Cold Chain’ is a system of transporting and s

toring vaccines, medications, and reage nts within the safe temperature range of 2 to 8 degrees centigrade. The cold chain begins when the item is manufactured, continues through to the storage location, and ends with the use of the item. 2.2 All medications subject to refrigeration should be transp orted and stored via a ‘cold chain’ system whenever removed from HMCAS main pharmacy. 3. Management of ‘Cold Chain’ items: 3.1 Storage of Cold Chain Items: 3.1.1 All cold chain items should be stored in a refrigerator that is used exclusively for medications. 3.1.2 This refr igerator should be frost - free, regularly inspected and preferably a purpose - built medication refrigerator. 3.1.3 Cold chain items should NOT be stored in the inside door shelving as the temperature in this compartment become variable when the door is opened freq uently. 3.1.4 Overstocking of the refrigerator should be avoided, as there should be cool air circulating around the stock in the refrigerator. 3.2 Temperature inside refrigerator: 3.2.1 The refrigerator should be kept at between 2 and 8 degrees centigrade. 3.2.2 An electronic, recording thermometer should be placed in every fridge. 3.2.3 The temperature

records are to be recorded routinely as part of the continuous quality improvement program of HMCAS Pharmacy. 3.3 Ambient Temperature: 3.3.1 The ambient temperature of the room where medications are stored should be regulated to between 15 and 25 degrees centigrade and must be monitored and manually recorded each day in a temperature - monitoring sheet.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 530 4. Transportation of ‘Cold Chain’ items: 4.1 The following items are required to ensure the integrity of a cold chain system: 4.1.1 A refrigerator for storing medications 4.1.2 Cold chain monitors 4.1.3 A digital, electronic or mercury minimum/maximum thermometer and chart for daily temperature reading 4.1.4 Cold boxes for transporting medications 4.1.5 Ice packs to keep med ications cool 4.1.6 Material to separate ice packs from the medications (e.g. shredded paper, cardboard, bubble wrap or Styrofoam) 4.1.7 Colorimetric temperature control strips 4.1.8 ‘Cold Chain’ Register 4.2 All ‘cold chain’ items should be carefully packed and transported in insulated containers that contain ice packs. The ice packs should never come in direct contact with the ‘cold chain’ items as they may freeze the item. They should always have some insulation material between the item being transported and the ice pack. 4.3 Th e insulated container (cool box) should be: 4.3.1 Large enough to store items and icepacks during transport; 4.3.2 External surfaces need to be robust and durable; 4.3.3 Insulation thickness is at a minimum of 30 mm to
as much as 80 mm; 4.3.4 Lids should be tight fitting; 4.3.5 Have an externally readable thermometer/temperature indicator .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 531 4.4 When hand carrying ‘cold chain’ items over long distances or for prolonged periods of time, there is a risk of fluctuation in temperature and degradation of the integrity of the cold chain items: 4.4.1 It is essential that the coolers remain sealed during transport and only opened briefly when the medications are required for use; in addition, externally readable digital thermometers or colorimetric temperature indicators should be visible to the operator at all times. 4.4.2 The cooler should be kept out of direct exposure of extreme heat or cold sources, such as sunlight or heat transferrable surfaces. 4.4.3 The ‘cold chain’ items should be stored in a refrigerator wherever possible during transit (such as ve hicle medication fridges, aircraft refrigerators, hotel refrigerators, etc.) and maintain constant temperature control of between 2 to 8 degrees centigrade throughout the mission. Over prolonged missions, attempts should be made to refresh the cold packs i nside the cooler or refrigerate the entire cooler. 4.4.4 In addition to a thermometer, temperature control strips should be placed into every cold chain p

ackage prior to commencement of a mission. When the items are inspected prior to use/or arrives at the stora ge location, the temperature control strip is read to ensure the temperature has remained consistent during the transport. Records should be kept for each mission. 4.4.5 In the event of ‘cold chain items’ breaching the temperature range for periods greater than 60 minutes, the medications must be deemed unsafe (unless indicated otherwise by specific medication or manufacturer guidelines) and HMCAS pharmacy staff should be notified. 5. Procedure to withdraw ‘cold chain’ medications: 5.1 Retrieval Service Critical Care Pa ramedic or Doctor identify the cold chain medications and quantity required for the mission or withdraw a “cold chain prepack” and inform the HMCAS pharmacy staff. 5.2 The cooler, ice packs and insulation material are prepared and inspected by Retrieval Servic e staff. 5.3 For the cold item that is removed from fridge, the pharmacy staff stick a new label on the medication with a new expiry date determined by international guidelines. 5.4 The pharmacy/Retrieval Services staff remove the items from the fridge and place d irectly into the transport container. 5

.5 The items are appropriately spaced, insulated from direct contact with the ice bricks and a colorimetric temperature strip is inserted into the cooler prior to ensuring the cooler is properly sealed. 5.6 The externally mou nted thermometer is inspected for proper function and accuracy. 5.7 ‘Cold Chain’ items register is completed and counter signed by both staff. 6. Procedure to return ‘cold chain’ medications: 6.1 Retrieval Service staff informs the pharmacy staff that they have ‘cold chain’ items to return. 6.2 Retrieval Service staff advise the pharmacy staff on any breaches in the cold chain, medication use or ‘incidents’ that could compromise the items integrity. 6.3 Pharmacy staff inspects the externally mounted thermometer and note the d uration the items have been outside of a pharmacy controlled environment (such as occasions where medications accompany a mission for several days).

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 532 6.4 The pharmacy staff opens the cooler and notes any medication use, breakage or “incidents” that may compromi se item integrity (e.g. frozen amps, improper insulation, etc.). 6.5 Pharmacy staff inspects the colorimetric temperature strip inside the cooler to ensure temperature integrity was maintained throughout transit. 6.6 Medications are returned to the fridge or disca rded as necessary. 6.7 ‘Cold Chain’ items register is completed and counter signed by both staff. A requisition form is completed by the Retrieval Service staff for any items used. 6.8 Ice Bricks are returned to the freezer and cooler is placed in storage .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 533 SOP 5.4.G.4 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Retrieval Services (RS) Operations Procedure 5.4.G.4 CONTROLLED M EDICINES MANAGEMENT BY RS CRITICAL CARE PARAMEDICS & MEDICAL DOCTORS Implementation Date 04 March 2020 Purpose To ensure Critical Care Paramedics and Medical Doctors manage HMCAS approved controlled and non - controlled medications appropriately . Responsibility RETRIEVAL SERVICES C RITICAL CARE PARAMED ICS & MEDICAL DOCTORS 1. General Processes pertaining to Medications : 1.1 Critical Care Paramedics and Medical Doctors working for Retrieval Services should follow SOP 4.3.C and SOP 4.3 B in regard to all controlled and non - controlled medication use while in the country of Qatar. 1.2 This includes how to withdraw medications from the Controlled Medication Storage Case (CMSC), re - stocking of medica tions, returning medications after use, returning residual controlled medications and issues requiring escalation and reporting. 1.3 In line with Ministry of Public Health Policy, any used controlled medication ampoules or vials must be retained and returned to pha

rmacy as evidence. Where this is not possible (e.g. if the ampoule is inadvertently disposed of and cannot be recovered), an OVA is to be submitted by the CCP on their return. 1.4 Under no circumstances should staff expose themselves to the risk of a needle stick injury by attempting to recover used ampoules from a sharps box. 2. Management of Controlled medications outside of Qatar: Regional : 2.1 It is understood that controlled medications will need to be taken outside of Qatar due to various mission requirements. This includes but is not limited to; regional retrievals in both fixed and rotor - wing configurations as well as long distance fixed - wing retrievals to Europe, the UK, and the US. 2.2 Where fixed or rotor wing missions consist of an “out and back” (e.g. KSA retrieval) the controlled medication policy is identical to that of standard ground operations. See SOP 4.3.C . 3. Management of Controlled medications outside of Qatar: International : 3.1 Controlled medica tions will need to be taken outside of Qatar due to various mission requirements . This includes but is not limited to; 3.1.1 R egional retrievals in both fixed and rotor - wing configurations as well as long

distance fixed wing retrievals to Europe, the UK, and the US. 3.1.2 Where fixed - wing missions will be over an extended time (e.g. trips to the UK, US, or Europe) that the medical team will maintain their medications outside of the normal 12 - hours shift window. 3.2 The provider should ensure that he/she has removed the “Na rcotics Letter” from the CMSC with their name on it, ensured that it is in date, and brought with them during the mission. 3.3 Based on patient requirement non - controlled medications may be left aboard the Amiri - 10 aircraft or other HMCAS owned aircraft (this does not include any commercial flight platform) . Non - controlled medications should be secured with either a red pull tab or lock. 3.4 Controlled medications should ALWAYS travel with the provider who signed them out of the CMSC and NEVER be left on the aircra ft.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 534 3.5 Once in designated accommodation, the narcotics box should be locked and placed out of site, either in the provider’s suitcase or in the room safe.

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 538 SOP 6.1 Hamad Medical Corporation Ambulance Service Standard Operating Procedures Sector Ambulance Service Operations Procedure 6.1 STAFFING OF EVENTS A ND SEALINE CAMPING S EASON Implementation Date 26 March 2019 Purpose To outline which licensed clinical staff are eligible to attend high, medium, and low risk events . Responsibility ALL HMC AMBULANCE SE RVICE GROUP LICENSED AMBULANCE PARAMEDICS OR CRITIC AL CARE PARAMEDICS 1. Introduction : 1.1 HMCAS is required to ensure that optimal resources are provided to support events and that standards of care are maintained. Therefore, all events will be class ified as high , medium , or low risk . 1.2 HMCAS will identify which staff may or may not attend an event based on the risk posed b y the event to the general public (e.g. high - risk event such as Sealine) and/or risk to reputation of HMCAS (e.g. televised event) . 1.2.1 The choice of which staff may work at an event will be reflective of their clinical privileges and the level of risk of crit ical injury/illness that the event can reasonable be judged to pose. In addition, the risk to the reputation of HMCAS is also

an independent factor in identifying what staff may or may not attend an event in a patient facing capacity . 1.3 HMCAS scheduling department is responsible for ensuring an appropriate skill mix on events and that staff, with reduced privileges, are not deployed into roles requiring full privileges of either an ambulance paramedic or critical care paramedic without authoriz ation from an Executive Director and/or the Head of Professions or his/her designed deputy . 2. Risk Stratification of Event : 2.1 All events are graded either as being either high, medium, or low risk . 2.2 Frequently occurring types of events are listed in Table 1. 2.3 Ev ents not previously covered by HMCAS will be reviewed by the Events and Emergency Planning team and graded according to the prescribed risk assessment . 2.4 Events will be graded in accordance with the following principles : 2.4.1 Risk of injury/illness to public : 2.4.1.1 Likely requirement for care at or above the level of an ambulance paramedic . 2.4.2 Risk to reputation of HMCAS . 2.5 Only licensed critical care paramedics who hold full clinical privileges (level 4 privileges) , as a critical care paramedic, may undertake any duties as a

critical care paramedic . 2.5.1 The exception is where these staff are deployed in an operational managerial position or where these staff are deployed in an operational supportive but ‘non - patient facing role’ . 2.6 Ambulance paramedics who are allocated clinica l privileges as level 2 privileges or level 1 privileges , are not permitted to cover events designated as high risk (see Table 1) . 2.6.1 The exception is where these staff are deployed in an operational managerial position or where these staff are being deployed in an operational supportive ‘non - patient facing’ role (e.g. communications officer) .

Return to Table of Contents Version 4.4 – November 2024 Page 539 2.6.2 Or in a foot patrol first responder category . 2.7 With the exception of events deemed to be high risk, where both ambulance crew must hold privileges as an ambulance paramed ic (level 3 privileges) , the minimum staffing of an HMCAS ambulances is as follows : 2.7.1 One licensed ambulance paramedic with ambulance paramedic level 3 privileges (this clinician is deemed to be the senior clinician regardless of the rank of his colleague IF his colleague does not hold level 3 privileges . The second ambulance paramedic must hold, as a minimum, basic ambulance paramedic privileges (level 2 privileges) . 2.7.2 HMCAS clinicians and/or voluntary staff holding level 1 privileges are not permitted to work on a HMCAS ambulance as part of deployment staff at an event . 2.8 D HP licensed staff on light duties ARE NOT eligible for any events in any capacity without permission of an Executive Director and/or the Head of Professions or his/her designated deputy . 2.9 HMCAS clinicians with privileges as a first responder may only be deployed to medium and low risk events in th e following roles/capacity : 2.9.1 Foot patrol 2.9.2

Manning static first aid unit coverage 3. Privileging : 3.1 Clinical privileges are outlined in HMCAS policy . 4.
